Chapter 13 The Transformer

In June 2017, eight authors at Google Brain and Google Research posted a paper called Attention Is All You Need. By any reasonable scientific standard the title was a vibe papers don’t get to be titled like that but the model worked. On the WMT 2014 English-to-German translation benchmark, an architecture with no recurrence, no convolutions, and 65 million parameters beat every prior result. It trained in twelve hours on eight GPUs. The thing it replaced the LSTM-based encoder-decoder with attention bolted on needed weeks.

The paper has since been cited over 100,000 times and the architecture it introduced now runs nearly every production AI system on Earth: every frontier chatbot, every image generator, every speech recognizer worth using, every autonomous coding agent, AlphaFold, every robot policy that’s been published in Nature in the last three years. Sometimes one architecture wins everything. This is one of those times.

This chapter is about why. We’ll build the transformer from scratch in pieces, then put them back together as a tiny GPT that trains on a laptop in six minutes and produces text that looks plausibly Shakespearean. Along the way we’ll modernize the original 2017 block to the 2026 default RoPE for position, RMSNorm for normalization, SwiGLU for the feed-forward network, GQA for the attention and tell you which of those choices are real wins and which are fashion.

The chapter is long. It is also load-bearing: every chapter in the rest of this volume references something developed here. If you read it once, slowly, and run the code, the next fifteen chapters get easier. If you skim, they don’t.

The bottleneck attention solved

Before 2017, every state-of-the-art sequence model on the planet was a recurrent neural network. LSTMs and GRUs ran translation, speech recognition, and language modeling. They were not the only architecture anyone tried convolutional sequence models had a moment, especially in machine translation but recurrence was the dominant paradigm and had been since the early 1990s.

RNNs had three problems that nobody could really fix, only paper over.

Sequential computation. An RNN computes its hidden state token by token: ht=f(ht−1,xt)h_t = f(h_{t-1}, x_t)h​t​​=f(h​t−1​​,x​t​​). Token t+1t+1t+1 cannot start computing until token ttt is done. On a GPU with ten thousand parallel cores, processing a 1,024-token sequence meant 1,024 serial steps and ninety-nine percent of the silicon idle. You could batch across sequences, but you could not parallelize along the time axis of any single one. Wall-clock training time scaled linearly with sequence length on hardware that wasn’t designed for linear-time anything.

Vanishing gradients. Information from token 1 reached token 50 by being squeezed through fifty non-linearities. LSTMs softened this with their gating tricks but didn’t eliminate it; the long-range dependencies you actually wanted (“the book I read last summer on a flight to Kyoto was…”) routinely failed in practice. You could pile on more parameters and longer training and watch perplexity drift down by tenths of a point per month, but the architecture was fighting you.

Fixed-width information bottleneck. The classic encoder-decoder setup compressed an entire source sequence into one hidden state before the decoder started reading. Five tokens in, five hundred tokens in, same vector. The 2014 attention paper from Bahdanau, Cho, and Bengio fixed this by letting the decoder look back at every encoder position but it kept the recurrence everywhere else, and the new attention mechanism was a soft pointer bolted onto the same old serial machine.

The 2017 paper’s bet was: what if we drop the recurrence entirely and do everything with attention? No serial dependency along the time axis. No fixed-width bottleneck. Just one giant batched matmul where every position attends to every other position in parallel.

On a 2026 GPU, the difference is a factor of ten to a hundred in wall-clock training time on long sequences. Crucially, the speedup is not a constant it is the difference between O(N)O(N)O(N) serial depth and O(1)O(1)O(1) serial depth. Recurrence is sequential by definition; attention is embarrassingly parallel by definition. GPUs love it.

The price is a quadratic memory cost: the attention matrix has shape (N,N)(N, N)(N,N) and storing it costs O(N2)O(N^2)O(N​2​​) in sequence length. Recurrence was linear in both time and memory; transformers trade memory for time and then win the time back through parallelism. We’ll see in Chapter 19 how 2026 inference stacks tame the quadratic cost (Flash Attention, KV cache, paged attention, sliding windows, linear attention variants), but the trade is fundamental.

There’s an inductive bias shift here too, which matters for understanding why transformers eat data the way they do. RNNs assume locality and recency: nearby tokens influence each other more than distant ones, recent tokens more than old ones. Convolutions assume translation invariance: the same pattern means the same thing wherever it appears. Transformers assume nothing every pair of positions is an equal candidate for attention, and the model has to learn what to attend to from scratch. That is why transformers need more data to train well and why they keep scaling further once they have it. Chinchilla (2022) formalized this: given a fixed compute budget, a transformer almost always beats an RNN of equal parameter count, and the gap widens as you add tokens.

There are still niches where recurrence wins in 2026. Streaming inference at constant memory, ultra-long sequences past a million tokens, edge devices with no matmul accelerator. State-space models like Mamba 2 and structured-recurrent variants like Hyena hit some of these niches by parameterizing recurrence in a way that’s parallelizable in training but constant-memory at inference. Most frontier labs ship hybrid architectures (Jamba, Samba) that mix transformer blocks with SSM blocks. We’ll come back to these in Chapter 20. For now: recurrence is a component, not a paradigm. The dominant idea is attention.

Self-attention, from scratch

Self-attention is the operation that does all the work. Multi-head attention is a parallelization trick on top. The block is plumbing around it. Get self-attention right and the rest is easier than it looks.

The lookup analogy

Self-attention is best understood as a soft, differentiable database lookup. A normal database takes a query, finds the row whose key matches exactly, and returns the value. That works because keys and queries are discrete. In a continuous, differentiable world we replace the exact match with a similarity score and return a weighted blend of all values, with the weights coming from how well each key matches the query.

For every input position iii, we compute three vectors from the embedding xix_ix​i​​: a query qiq_iq​i​​ (“what am I looking for?”), a key kik_ik​i​​ (“what do I contain?”), and a value viv_iv​i​​ (“what information do I provide if you select me?”). To produce the output at position iii, we score qiq_iq​i​​ against every kjk_jk​j​​, normalize the scores into a probability distribution, and use that distribution to take a weighted average of the values.

That’s it. The whole of attention is one paragraph of intuition and one line of math. Everything else is implementation, scale, and modern variants.

The math, with shapes

Let’s pin down the shapes. We have a sequence of NNN token embeddings stacked into a matrix XXX of shape (N,dmodel)(N, d_{\text{model}})(N,d​model​​), where dmodeld_{\text{model}}d​model​​ is the embedding dimension (think 768 for a small model, 4096 for a frontier-scale one). Three learned weight matrices project XXX into queries, keys, and values:

Q=XWQ,K=XWK,V=XWVQ = X W_Q, \quad K = X W_K, \quad V = X W_V Q=XW​Q​​,K=XW​K​​,V=XW​V​​

where WQ,WK∈Rdmodel×dkW_Q, W_K \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_k}W​Q​​,W​K​​∈R​d​model​​×d​k​​​​ and WV∈Rdmodel×dvW_V \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_v}W​V​​∈R​d​model​​×d​v​​​​. After projection, QQQ and KKK have shape (N,dk)(N, d_k)(N,d​k​​), and VVV has shape (N,dv)(N, d_v)(N,d​v​​). In practice dk=dvd_k = d_vd​k​​=d​v​​ and both are typically 64 or 128, but in principle they don’t have to be.

The attention output is:

Attention(Q,K,V)=softmax(QK⊤dk)V\text{Attention}(Q, K, V) = \text{softmax}\!\left(\frac{Q K^\top}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right) V Attention(Q,K,V)=softmax(​√​d​k​​​​​​​QK​⊤​​​​)V

This says: take every query, score it against every key by dot product; divide by dk\sqrt{d_k}√​d​k​​​​​ to keep the scores from blowing up; softmax along the row axis to turn scores into a probability distribution over keys; multiply by VVV to produce a weighted average of values, one per query.

The output has shape (N,dv)(N, d_v)(N,d​v​​) same number of positions as the input, but each position’s representation has been replaced by a context-aware blend of all positions’ values, weighted by how relevant they were.

Vol 1 refresher. Softmax turns a vector of real numbers into a probability distribution: softmax(z)i=exp(zi)/∑jexp(zj)\text{softmax}(z)_i = \exp(z_i) / \sum_j \exp(z_j)softmax(z)​i​​=exp(z​i​​)/∑​j​​exp(z​j​​). We covered it in Vol 1 Ch 4 alongside cross-entropy. Two things you need here: (1) it’s row-wise here each query gets its own distribution over keys; (2) for numerical stability you subtract the row max before exponentiating, which is mathematically equivalent and prevents overflow.

Why the dk\sqrt{d_k}√​d​k​​​​​ scaling

The dot product q⋅kq \cdot kq⋅k has variance proportional to dkd_kd​k​​ when qqq and kkk have unit-variance components. At dk=64d_k = 64d​k​​=64, raw scores easily reach magnitude 5–10. Plug those into softmax and you get a near-one-hot distribution: one key gets weight ~0.99, everything else effectively zero. The gradient through the near-zero entries is also near zero, and training stalls. Dividing by dk\sqrt{d_k}√​d​k​​​​​ rescales the scores back to something softmax can work with. It’s a numerical trick, not a deep idea, but skipping it costs you a tenth of a perplexity point and several days of debugging. Don’t skip it.

The pure-NumPy implementation

Here’s scaled dot-product self-attention in eight lines of NumPy. No frameworks. No magic.

import numpy as np

def softmax(x, axis=-1):
    shifted = x - np.max(x, axis=axis, keepdims=True)
    exp_x = np.exp(shifted)
    return exp_x / np.sum(exp_x, axis=axis, keepdims=True)

def self_attention(X, W_q, W_k, W_v):
    Q = X @ W_q
    K = X @ W_k
    V = X @ W_v
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]
    scores = Q @ K.T / np.sqrt(d_k)
    weights = softmax(scores, axis=-1)
    return weights @ V, weights

Let’s run it on a six-token “sentence” with random embeddings to see the shapes flow:

rng = np.random.default_rng(42)
N, d_model, d_k = 6, 32, 16

X = rng.normal(0, 1, (N, d_model))
W_q = rng.normal(0, 0.1, (d_model, d_k))
W_k = rng.normal(0, 0.1, (d_model, d_k))
W_v = rng.normal(0, 0.1, (d_model, d_k))

output, weights = self_attention(X, W_q, W_k, W_v)
print(output.shape, weights.shape)

output is (6,16)(6, 16)(6,16) six tokens, each now represented by a 16-dimensional vector that mixes information from all six. weights is (6,6)(6, 6)(6,6) the attention matrix, where row iii tells you how much position iii attended to each position jjj. Every row sums to 1.

This is the core of every transformer. Everything else in this chapter is variations on this theme.

Causal masking: the decoder’s one trick

For language modeling predicting the next token from previous tokens we don’t want position iii to attend to positions j>ij > ij>i. A token shouldn’t see its own future. The fix is causal masking: before the softmax, set the upper triangle of the score matrix to −∞-\infty−∞. After softmax, those positions get weight zero exactly.

def causal_self_attention(X, W_q, W_k, W_v):
    Q, K, V = X @ W_q, X @ W_k, X @ W_v
    d_k = Q.shape[-1]
    scores = Q @ K.T / np.sqrt(d_k)
    mask = np.triu(np.ones_like(scores) * -np.inf, k=1)
    weights = softmax(scores + mask, axis=-1)
    return weights @ V, weights

That’s the entire difference between an encoder-style attention (BERT-shaped) and a decoder-style attention (GPT-shaped). One line of masking.

What heads actually learn

If you train a tiny transformer on text and visualize the attention weights of a single head after training, you’ll see structure. Some heads attend mostly to the previous token. Some attend to the syntactic head of the current word’s phrase (the verb, if you’re a noun). Some are induction heads: they detect a previous occurrence of the current token and attend to whatever came right after it last time, which turns out to be the mechanistic basis of in-context learning. The Anthropic interpretability team has a 2022 paper called In-context Learning and Induction Heads that traces this circuit through real GPT-2 weights and is well worth reading after you finish this chapter.

The point is that each attention head learns a single relational pattern. That’s a problem if your data has many overlapping relational patterns, which natural language emphatically does. The fix is to run several heads in parallel.

Multi-head attention and its modern descendants

If one attention head learns one relation, eight heads learn eight, and sixty-four learn sixty-four. Heads are cheap. They don’t increase the total parameter count if you keep dmodeld_{\text{model}}d​model​​ fixed and split it across heads.

Split, attend, concatenate, project

The standard recipe from the 2017 paper:

  1. Project XXX to Q,K,VQ, K, VQ,K,V each of shape (N,dmodel)(N, d_{\text{model}})(N,d​model​​), as before.
  2. Reshape each into (N,h,dhead)(N, h, d_{\text{head}})(N,h,d​head​​) where dhead=dmodel/hd_{\text{head}} = d_{\text{model}} / hd​head​​=d​model​​/h. Transpose to (h,N,dhead)(h, N, d_{\text{head}})(h,N,d​head​​) so heads become the batch dimension.
  3. Run scaled dot-product attention inside each head independently. Each head produces (N,dhead)(N, d_{\text{head}})(N,d​head​​).
  4. Concatenate the heads back to (N,dmodel)(N, d_{\text{model}})(N,d​model​​) and multiply by a learned output matrix WO∈Rdmodel×dmodelW_O \in \mathbb{R}^{d_{\text{model}} \times d_{\text{model}}}W​O​​∈R​d​model​​×d​model​​​​.

The output projection WOW_OW​O​​ is where heads finally get to talk to each other. During the attention computation itself the heads are independent; they operate on different subspaces of the embedding. WOW_OW​O​​ mixes them back into a unified representation.

In NumPy:

def multi_head_attention(X, W_q, W_k, W_v, W_o, n_heads):
    N, d_model = X.shape
    d_head = d_model // n_heads
    Q = (X @ W_q).reshape(N, n_heads, d_head).transpose(1, 0, 2)
    K = (X @ W_k).reshape(N, n_heads, d_head).transpose(1, 0, 2)
    V = (X @ W_v).reshape(N, n_heads, d_head).transpose(1, 0, 2)
    scores = Q @ K.transpose(0, 2, 1) / np.sqrt(d_head)
    weights = softmax(scores, axis=-1)
    out = weights @ V
    concat = out.transpose(1, 0, 2).reshape(N, d_model)
    return concat @ W_o, weights

On real hardware those Q @ K.transpose(...) and weights @ V operations are single batched matmuls. The GPU sees (h,N,dhead)×(h,dhead,N)→(h,N,N)(h, N, d_{\text{head}}) \times (h, d_{\text{head}}, N) \to (h, N, N)(h,N,d​head​​)×(h,d​head​​,N)→(h,N,N) as one kernel call. Adding heads is, computationally, free. The total flop count is the same as a single head with full dmodeld_{\text{model}}d​model​​ you’ve just split the work into smaller matmuls that are easier to specialize.

How many heads, and how big

Production rules of thumb in 2026:

Model size dmodeld_{\text{model}}d​model​​ nheadsn_{\text{heads}}n​heads​​ dheadd_{\text{head}}d​head​​
Small (~125M) 768 12 64
Base (~1B) 2048 16 128
Frontier (~70B) 8192 64 128

dheadd_{\text{head}}d​head​​ almost always lands at 64 or 128. It’s the unit of how much one head can “see” drop below 32 and heads start fighting the dhead\sqrt{d_{\text{head}}}√​d​head​​​​​ scaling factor; go above 256 and you lose the many-small-specialists benefit that motivated multi-head in the first place. If you ever find yourself cooking up an exotic dheadd_{\text{head}}d​head​​, the answer is almost always 64 or 128 and you should justify in writing why you didn’t pick one of those.

MQA, GQA, MLA where the field went

The original 2017 design has one WKW_KW​K​​ and one WVW_VW​V​​ matrix per head that is, KKK and VVV have hhh independent versions. At training time this is fine. At inference time, when you’re running a chatbot and have to keep the keys and values for every token in the prompt cached so you don’t recompute them on every generation step, this becomes the dominant memory cost. For Llama 2 70B with 64 heads at full multi-head attention, the KV cache for a single 4K-context conversation runs around 8 GB. That’s per-conversation, on top of the model weights. It’s the reason your inference server can’t fit as many concurrent users as you expected, and the reason production transformers in 2026 don’t use full multi-head attention for KKK and VVV.

The fix is to reduce the number of distinct KV heads while keeping the number of query heads:

Variant Q heads K/V heads Used by
Multi-Head Attention (MHA) hhh hhh GPT-2, BERT, T5, original Llama
Multi-Query Attention (MQA) hhh 1 PaLM, Falcon
Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) hhh ggg (e.g. h/8h/8h/8) Llama 2 70B, Llama 3+, Qwen 2+, Mistral, Gemma
Multi-head Latent Attention (MLA) hhh low-rank latent DeepSeek-V2/V3

GQA is the boring default in 2026. It cuts KV-cache memory by a factor of h/gh/gh/g while losing roughly nothing in quality Ainslie et al. (2023) showed you can convert an MHA-trained model to GQA with a brief fine-tune and recover most of the original performance. Llama 3 70B uses 64 query heads with 8 KV heads, an 8× cache shrink that translates directly into 8× more concurrent conversations on the same GPU.

MLA is the more aggressive version. Instead of just sharing KKK and VVV across heads, DeepSeek’s design compresses KKK and VVV into a single low-rank latent vector at training time and decompresses on the fly during attention. The KV cache stores only the compressed latent, which is dramatically smaller than even GQA. The cost is a few extra matmuls at attention time. DeepSeek-V3 makes this trade and the result is one of the most efficient frontier models in production.

When you read about a new model and the architecture description includes “GQA with 8 KV heads,” you now know exactly what they mean and why they bothered.

Code-time note. PyTorch 2.5+'s torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention has an enable_gqa=True flag. Pass QQQ of shape (B,h,N,dhead)(B, h, N, d_{\text{head}})(B,h,N,d​head​​) and K,VK, VK,V of shape (B,g,N,dhead)(B, g, N, d_{\text{head}})(B,g,N,d​head​​) with g<hg < hg<h and PyTorch handles the head broadcasting. You almost never want to write GQA from scratch in production.

Putting position back in

There is a problem with attention that we’ve been ignoring. It is permutation-invariant.

Run self-attention on the embeddings of “The cat sat on the mat” and on “mat the on sat cat the” and you get the same outputs, with the rows reordered. Attention scores are pairwise they depend only on which tokens are present, not on what order they appear in. For a bag-of-words classifier this is fine. For language, code, audio, video, anything where order is meaning, it is fatal.

We have to inject a position signal somehow. There have been three eras of answers, each with a different bet on what “position” means.

Era 1 absolute sinusoidal (2017, dead)

The original transformer paper added a fixed (non-learned) matrix PEPEPE of shape (max\_len,dmodel)(\text{max\_len}, d_{\text{model}})(max\_len,d​model​​) to the embeddings before the first attention layer. The entries are sines and cosines at geometric frequencies:

PE(pos,2i)=sin(pos100002i/dmodel),PE(pos,2i+1)=cos(pos100002i/dmodel)PE_{(\text{pos}, 2i)} = \sin\!\left(\frac{\text{pos}}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right), \quad PE_{(\text{pos}, 2i+1)} = \cos\!\left(\frac{\text{pos}}{10000^{2i/d_{\text{model}}}}\right) PE​(pos,2i)​​=sin(​10000​2i/d​model​​​​​​pos​​),PE​(pos,2i+1)​​=cos(​10000​2i/d​model​​​​​​pos​​)

Every dimension is a sinusoid at a different frequency. The model learns to read position from the phase pattern. It works, it has no learnable parameters, and it has one fatal flaw: nothing in the formula tells the model what happens at position 8000 when training only covered positions 0 through 2047. In practice, transformers with sinusoidal embeddings degrade sharply when asked to extrapolate beyond their trained context length.

GPT-2 and GPT-3 used a learned version just a (max\_len,dmodel)(\text{max\_len}, d_{\text{model}})(max\_len,d​model​​) table of trainable parameters. Same extrapolation problem, plus you’ve spent parameters on it. Both approaches are now historical.

Era 2 RoPE (2021, dominant)

Rotary Position Embedding, from Su et al. (2021), is a rotation trick on QQQ and KKK rather than an additive signal on the embeddings.

For each pair of dimensions (2i,2i+1)(2i, 2i+1)(2i,2i+1) in a query or key vector, rotate by an angle proportional to position:

[q2i′q2i+1′]=[cos(pos⋅θi)−sin(pos⋅θi)sin(pos⋅θi)cos(pos⋅θi)][q2iq2i+1]\begin{bmatrix} q'_{2i} \\ q'_{2i+1} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \cos(\text{pos} \cdot \theta_i) & -\sin(\text{pos} \cdot \theta_i) \\ \sin(\text{pos} \cdot \theta_i) & \cos(\text{pos} \cdot \theta_i) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} q_{2i} \\ q_{2i+1} \end{bmatrix} [​q​2i​′​​​q​2i+1​′​​​​]=[​cos(pos⋅θ​i​​)​sin(pos⋅θ​i​​)​​​−sin(pos⋅θ​i​​)​cos(pos⋅θ​i​​)​​][​q​2i​​​q​2i+1​​​​]

with θi=base−2i/dhead\theta_i = \text{base}^{-2i / d_{\text{head}}}θ​i​​=base​−2i/d​head​​​​ and base\text{base}base usually 10000.

The trick: apply the same rotation to QQQ at position mmm and to KKK at position nnn. When you take the dot product qm′⋅kn′q'_m \cdot k'_nq​m​′​​⋅k​n​′​​, the rotation matrices combine into a function of (m−n)(m - n)(m−n) alone. The attention score depends only on the relative distance between two positions, even though the rotation was keyed off absolute positions. RoPE encodes relative position without ever explicitly representing it.

This is the kind of design where you read it for the first time and stare at it for a while and then go oh, that’s nice. It slots into attention with no architectural change, costs no trainable parameters, encodes relative position exactly, and critically has a single hyperparameter (base) that lets you extend a model to longer contexts after training by rescaling.

That last property is why RoPE won. In 2024, Llama 3 was trained with base = 10000 on 8K-token sequences, and then fine-tuned at base = 8000000 to handle 128K-token sequences. The technique is called YaRN (Peng et al., 2023) and it’s a Pareto-improvement over earlier scaling tricks. You can’t do this kind of extension surgery on sinusoidal or learned absolute embeddings.

In 2026, RoPE is the boring default. Llama 2/3/4, Qwen 2/3, Mistral, Mixtral, DeepSeek-V3, Kimi, Gemma all RoPE. If you are building a new transformer-based language model and someone asks you what positional encoding to use, the answer is RoPE, and the only follow-up question is whether to use a YaRN-style scaling scheme for long contexts.

Era 3 ALiBi (2022, niche)

Press, Smith, and Lewis went the other direction in 2022 with Attention with Linear Biases. Skip the embedding trick entirely. Just bias the attention scores directly:

score(i,j)=qi⋅kjdk−mh⋅∣i−j∣\text{score}(i, j) = \frac{q_i \cdot k_j}{\sqrt{d_k}} - m_h \cdot |i - j| score(i,j)=​√​d​k​​​​​​​q​i​​⋅k​j​​​​−m​h​​⋅∣i−j∣

where mhm_hm​h​​ is a head-specific slope, fixed (not learned) at 1/28h/H1 / 2^{8h/H}1/2​8h/H​​ for head hhh out of HHH. Closer tokens get boosted; distant tokens get penalized linearly. No embeddings, no learnable parameters, excellent length extrapolation.

ALiBi is used by BLOOM, MPT, and Baichuan. It hasn’t displaced RoPE at the frontier RoPE’s combination of relative-position encoding plus tunable extension via base rescaling is hard to beat but ALiBi still shows up in long-context-specialized models because it extrapolates further out of the box than vanilla RoPE.

What you should actually do

Use RoPE. If you’re extending an existing RoPE model to longer contexts, use YaRN. Don’t invent a new scheme unless you have a research-grade reason. The HuggingFace transformers library ships production-quality implementations of every variant in modeling_rope_utils.py, and you should read that file once if you ever want to understand how the long-context magic actually works.

The block: what makes attention stackable

A single attention layer is a feature extractor, not a model. You need depth typically 12 to 80 layers in a modern LLM and depth requires plumbing. The 2017 paper packaged six design decisions that turned one attention layer into a stackable block. Every transformer since inherits the same skeleton.

The six pieces

  1. Embeddings, plus position. Tokens map through a learned embedding table to vectors of dimension dmodeld_{\text{model}}d​model​​. Position gets injected via RoPE (modern) or sinusoidal (classic).
  2. Self-attention. Multi-head, with causal masking in decoders.
  3. Feed-forward network (FFN). A position-wise two-layer MLP that expands the hidden dimension by a factor of about four, applies a non-linearity, and projects back. This is where most of the model’s parameters live.
  4. Residual connections. x+sublayer(x)x + \text{sublayer}(x)x+sublayer(x), around both the attention and the FFN. Without residuals, gradients die past about six layers and training is impossible.
  5. Layer normalization. Stabilizes the residual stream so it doesn’t drift to zero or infinity as you stack blocks.
  6. Cross-attention (decoder-only). In encoder-decoder models, the decoder has a third sub-layer where queries come from the decoder and keys/values come from the encoder output. Decoder-only models (GPT, Llama, Claude) skip this entirely.

Visualized, an encoder block:

x ──► LN ──► MHA(self) ──┐
│                        ▼
└────► (+) ◄─────────────┘
       │
       ▼
       LN ──► FFN ──┐
       │            ▼
       └──► (+) ◄───┘
            │
            ▼
            out

A decoder block in an encoder-decoder model has three sub-layers: masked self-attention, then cross-attention to the encoder output, then FFN. A decoder-only model (the vast majority of modern LLMs) has just masked self-attention plus FFN.

Pre-norm vs post-norm: the one architectural decision worth getting right

The original 2017 paper used post-norm: LN(x+sublayer(x))\text{LN}(x + \text{sublayer}(x))LN(x+sublayer(x)). Normalize after the residual. This works for shallow stacks but starts misbehaving once you go past about twelve layers gradient norms blow up early in training and you need elaborate warmup schedules to keep the model alive. Around 2019, the field switched to pre-norm: x+sublayer(LN(x))x + \text{sublayer}(\text{LN}(x))x+sublayer(LN(x)). Normalize the input to the sublayer, leave the residual stream alone.

Pre-norm is monotonically better for deep stacks. It trains stably to 80+ layers without warmup gymnastics. Every modern frontier model uses it. If you are reading a new transformer paper and they don’t mention pre-norm, it’s because pre-norm is so universal nobody bothers calling it out.

The 2026 modernized block

Vaswani 2017 shipped LayerNorm and ReLU. Modern stacks have replaced both, and a few other defaults have shifted. Here’s what production blocks actually look like:

Component 2017 2026 default
Normalization LayerNorm RMSNorm
FFN activation ReLU SwiGLU
FFN expansion 4× 2.6× (with SwiGLU’s three matrices, total params match the 4× ReLU version)
Position Sinusoidal absolute RoPE
Attention Full MHA GQA (or MLA)
Bias terms Yes No (dropped from linear layers)
Norm placement Post-norm Pre-norm

RMSNorm drops the mean-centering step of LayerNorm: divide by the root-mean-square of the activations, keep a learned scale, no learned shift. One fewer subtraction per token. Empirically at least as stable as LayerNorm, slightly faster, and doesn’t seem to cost anything in quality. Zhang and Sennrich (2019) introduced it; Llama and Mistral made it standard.

SwiGLU is a gated FFN: SwiGLU(x)=(Swish(xW1)⊙xW3)W2\text{SwiGLU}(x) = (\text{Swish}(x W_1) \odot x W_3) W_2SwiGLU(x)=(Swish(xW​1​​)⊙xW​3​​)W​2​​ with three weight matrices instead of the classical FFN’s two. The ⊙\odot⊙ is elementwise multiplication. Shazeer’s 2020 GLU Variants Improve Transformer paper showed this beats ReLU and GELU FFNs by about 0.5 perplexity on language modeling small but consistent and free, given that you adjust the expansion ratio so total parameters stay matched. Every Llama, every Qwen, every Mistral, every DeepSeek uses SwiGLU.

No bias terms. The Llama and PaLM papers showed that dropping the additive biases from linear layers gives a small perplexity improvement and slightly fewer parameters. In 2026, biases are out.

You will see all of these in the canonical reference implementation: HuggingFace’s modeling_llama.py. It’s about 500 lines and worth reading once after you’ve finished this chapter. You’ll be reading it the way someone reads redis.c to see how a production-quality version of an idea you’ve understood from scratch actually fits together.

Putting it all together: a tiny GPT

Now we ship something that runs.

What follows is a complete decoder-only transformer in PyTorch about 100 lines that you can train on a laptop in six minutes on the canonical tinyshakespeare corpus and watch it generate plausibly Shakespearean text. We use the modernized 2026 block: pre-norm, RMSNorm, SwiGLU, causal multi-head attention. We use learned positional embeddings rather than RoPE, because learned embeddings are one fewer concept in the code and the exercises at the end ask you to swap in RoPE.

This is not a frontier model. It is the same code path frontier models use, just smaller. The point of building it is that you can no longer be surprised by what’s inside the box.

Setup

Install PyTorch 2.5+ (CPU build is fine for this). Get the corpus:

import os, urllib.request
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karpathy/char-rnn/master/data/tinyshakespeare/input.txt"
if not os.path.exists("tinyshakespeare.txt"):
    urllib.request.urlretrieve(url, "tinyshakespeare.txt")
text = open("tinyshakespeare.txt").read()

About 1.1 MB of Shakespeare, 65 unique characters, the canonical toy LM corpus since 2015.

A character-level tokenizer

chars = sorted(set(text))
stoi = {c: i for i, c in enumerate(chars)}
itos = {i: c for c, i in stoi.items()}
encode = lambda s: [stoi[c] for c in s]
decode = lambda xs: "".join(itos[x] for x in xs)
vocab_size = len(chars)

We’re skipping BPE and subword tokenizers because they’re a chapter of their own (Chapter 17). One byte per token, vocabulary size 65.

The model

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class GPTConfig:
    vocab_size: int = 65
    n_layer: int = 4
    n_head: int = 4
    d_model: int = 128
    block_size: int = 256
    dropout: float = 0.0

class RMSNorm(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d, eps=1e-6):
        super().__init__()
        self.weight = nn.Parameter(torch.ones(d))
        self.eps = eps
    def forward(self, x):
        rms = x.pow(2).mean(-1, keepdim=True).add(self.eps).rsqrt()
        return x * rms * self.weight

class SwiGLU(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, d, hidden):
        super().__init__()
        self.w1 = nn.Linear(d, hidden, bias=False)
        self.w3 = nn.Linear(d, hidden, bias=False)
        self.w2 = nn.Linear(hidden, d, bias=False)
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.w2(F.silu(self.w1(x)) * self.w3(x))

class CausalSelfAttention(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, cfg: GPTConfig):
        super().__init__()
        assert cfg.d_model % cfg.n_head == 0
        self.n_head = cfg.n_head
        self.d_head = cfg.d_model // cfg.n_head
        self.qkv = nn.Linear(cfg.d_model, 3 * cfg.d_model, bias=False)
        self.proj = nn.Linear(cfg.d_model, cfg.d_model, bias=False)
    def forward(self, x):
        B, N, D = x.shape
        q, k, v = self.qkv(x).split(D, dim=-1)
        q = q.view(B, N, self.n_head, self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)
        k = k.view(B, N, self.n_head, self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)
        v = v.view(B, N, self.n_head, self.d_head).transpose(1, 2)
        out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v, is_causal=True)
        out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, N, D)
        return self.proj(out)

class Block(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, cfg: GPTConfig):
        super().__init__()
        self.norm1 = RMSNorm(cfg.d_model)
        self.attn = CausalSelfAttention(cfg)
        self.norm2 = RMSNorm(cfg.d_model)
        ffn_hidden = int(cfg.d_model * 8 / 3)
        self.ffn = SwiGLU(cfg.d_model, ffn_hidden)
    def forward(self, x):
        x = x + self.attn(self.norm1(x))
        x = x + self.ffn(self.norm2(x))
        return x

class GPT(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, cfg: GPTConfig):
        super().__init__()
        self.cfg = cfg
        self.token_emb = nn.Embedding(cfg.vocab_size, cfg.d_model)
        self.pos_emb = nn.Embedding(cfg.block_size, cfg.d_model)
        self.blocks = nn.ModuleList([Block(cfg) for _ in range(cfg.n_layer)])
        self.norm = RMSNorm(cfg.d_model)
        self.head = nn.Linear(cfg.d_model, cfg.vocab_size, bias=False)
        self.head.weight = self.token_emb.weight
    def forward(self, idx):
        B, N = idx.shape
        pos = torch.arange(N, device=idx.device)
        x = self.token_emb(idx) + self.pos_emb(pos)
        for block in self.blocks:
            x = block(x)
        return self.head(self.norm(x))

Every piece in there is something we built up over the last six thousand words. RMSNorm is the modernized normalization. SwiGLU is the gated FFN with the standard 8/38/38/3 expansion ratio that keeps total parameters matched to a 4× ReLU FFN. CausalSelfAttention calls PyTorch’s scaled_dot_product_attention, which auto-dispatches to Flash Attention on supported hardware that’s the same operation we wrote in NumPy earlier, except faster by a factor of three to ten and with sub-quadratic memory. Block is a pre-norm residual block with attention then FFN. GPT stacks blocks, adds an embedding table for both tokens and positions, and ties the LM head weights to the input embedding (the standard parameter-saving trick: the matrix that maps tokens to vectors is the transpose of the matrix that maps vectors back to token logits).

Total parameters at this configuration: about 800,000.

Training loop

data = torch.tensor(encode(text), dtype=torch.long)
n = int(0.9 * len(data))
train_data, val_data = data[:n], data[n:]

def get_batch(split, batch_size=32):
    d = train_data if split == "train" else val_data
    ix = torch.randint(0, len(d) - cfg.block_size, (batch_size,))
    x = torch.stack([d[i:i + cfg.block_size] for i in ix])
    y = torch.stack([d[i + 1:i + 1 + cfg.block_size] for i in ix])
    return x, y

cfg = GPTConfig()
model = GPT(cfg)
opt = torch.optim.AdamW(model.parameters(), lr=3e-4, weight_decay=0.1)

for step in range(2000):
    x, y = get_batch("train")
    logits = model(x)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(logits.view(-1, cfg.vocab_size), y.view(-1))
    opt.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
    opt.step()
    if step % 200 == 0:
        with torch.no_grad():
            x_val, y_val = get_batch("val")
            val_loss = F.cross_entropy(model(x_val).view(-1, cfg.vocab_size), y_val.view(-1))
        print(f"step {step}: train {loss.item():.3f}, val {val_loss.item():.3f}")

Vol 1 refresher. Cross-entropy loss measures how far the predicted probability distribution is from the true one-hot target: L=−∑iyilogy^i\mathcal{L} = -\sum_i y_i \log \hat{y}_iL=−∑​i​​y​i​​log​y​^​​​i​​, which for a one-hot target collapses to −logp(correct token)-\log p(\text{correct token})−logp(correct token). Vol 1 Ch 7 derived it from maximum likelihood. Here we apply it token-by-token: predict every token from the previous ones, average the per-token loss.

AdamW (Vol 1 Ch 7 again) is the optimizer of choice for transformers. Decoupled weight decay, momentum on gradient and gradient-squared, learning rate 3e-4 as the safe default at this scale.

Gradient clipping caps the global gradient norm. Without it, occasional batches with high loss produce gradient spikes that destabilize training. With it, you almost never look at this code path again.

Sampling

@torch.no_grad()
def generate(prompt, max_new_tokens=500, temperature=1.0, top_k=20):
    model.eval()
    idx = torch.tensor([encode(prompt)], dtype=torch.long)
    for _ in range(max_new_tokens):
        idx_cond = idx[:, -cfg.block_size:]
        logits = model(idx_cond)[:, -1, :] / temperature
        if top_k:
            v, _ = torch.topk(logits, top_k)
            logits[logits < v[:, [-1]]] = -float("inf")
        probs = F.softmax(logits, dim=-1)
        next_tok = torch.multinomial(probs, 1)
        idx = torch.cat([idx, next_tok], dim=1)
    return decode(idx[0].tolist())

print(generate("ROMEO:\n"))

After 2,000 training steps roughly six minutes on a 2024-era laptop CPU the loss has dropped from about 4.2 (random) to about 1.5, and the model produces output like:

ROMEO:
Away and mild will not thy friend, that thou shalt wit:
The chief that well shame and hath been his friends,
And was thy heart hath but to thee...

This is not Shakespeare. It is Shakespeare-shaped. The model has learned the character distribution, the line breaks, the colons after speaker names, the archaic vocabulary, even the rhythm of iambic-ish phrasing. With 800,000 parameters and six minutes of compute. The same architecture, with a real tokenizer, a real corpus (FineWeb-Edu, OpenWebText), and a few orders of magnitude more parameters and training time, produces models that pass college entrance exams.

Run the code. Stare at the output. Convince yourself you understand every line. Then we’ll talk about what the production version costs.

Sharp edges

The transformer is the dominant architecture, but it has costs and failure modes worth naming.

Quadratic attention memory. The (N,N)(N, N)(N,N) attention matrix is the dominant memory cost at long context. For a single-precision matrix at N=32,768N = 32{,}768N=32,768 that’s already 4 GB per layer per head, before you factor in batching. Production stacks fight this with Flash Attention (which never materializes the full matrix in HBM it tiles the computation), with sliding-window attention (only attend to the last KKK tokens), and with sparse attention variants. We cover this in Chapter 19.

Attention saturation. When you stack many layers, attention distributions tend to collapse every head puts most of its weight on one or two tokens, often the first one. The phenomenon has been called “attention sinks” and the workarounds (registers, RMSNorm scaling, learning-rate tuning) are an active research topic. If you train a transformer and the loss plateaus weirdly early, this is a candidate suspect.

Decoder-only didn’t have to win. It’s worth knowing that the original 2017 transformer was an encoder-decoder model for translation. Encoder-only models (BERT) won classification and embeddings. Decoder-only models (GPT) won generation. In 2026, decoder-only is the dominant paradigm for everything text-shaped because it scales cleanly, handles both comprehension and generation in one architecture, and the in-context learning behavior of large decoder-only models turns out to be unreasonably useful. Encoder-decoder is still best when the input has a clear “source sequence” identity (translation, speech recognition, structured tasks) Whisper and T5 are alive and well. We’ll cover the variants in Chapter 14.

The inference cost is shaped weirdly. Training a transformer is one big matmul-fest that GPUs love. Generating from one is a sequential loop where you produce one token, append it, run the model again, produce the next token. Each step is dominated by memory bandwidth (loading weights and KV cache), not compute. Production inference engines (vLLM, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM) exist almost entirely to make this loop fast, which it isn’t by default. Chapter 19 is about this.

It’s hungry. Transformers have minimal inductive bias. They learn structure from data. That means they need more data than RNNs or CNNs to reach the same quality on small problems you usually beat a transformer with a smaller, more biased model when training data is tight. The transformer’s win is at scale: when you have a billion examples, a transformer keeps improving while alternatives plateau. If you’re solving a small, closed problem with a small dataset, a transformer is probably overkill. If you’re solving an open-domain problem with the entire internet as data, it’s the only thing that works.

What you’d actually use

We just spent six thousand words building a transformer from scratch. In production, you would use almost none of that code.

Use PyTorch’s torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention for any attention you write yourself. It auto-dispatches to Flash Attention on CUDA and Apple’s MPS attention on Metal. It supports causal masking, GQA, and arbitrary masks. Writing your own masked softmax-and-matmul is correct exactly long enough to verify your understanding and then you should never do it again.

Use HuggingFace’s transformers library to load any production-quality pretrained model. The modeling_llama.py file in their repo is the canonical 2026 decoder-only block; read it once, refer to it forever. If you need a different architecture (Qwen, Mistral, DeepSeek), there’s a modeling_<name>.py file written by someone who knows what they’re doing, and reading it is the fastest way to understand any new model that ships.

Use vLLM, SGLang, or TensorRT-LLM to serve a transformer in production. We cover the trade-offs in Chapter 29. Do not write your own inference server. The savings are measured in millions of dollars and the complexity is several PhDs worth.

Roll your own only when you are doing research, when you are training a model from scratch with a custom architecture, or when you are reading this chapter for educational purposes. Outside those three cases, you are reinventing wheels that several thousand engineers at Meta, NVIDIA, and the major foundation-model labs have spent years optimizing.

Where this goes next

Chapter 14 takes the same block we built and specializes it. BERT is a decoder-less, bidirectional encoder trained with masked language modeling the workhorse of pre-LLM-era NLP and still the right tool for embeddings and classification. GPT is decoder-only, trained with next-token prediction, and is what most people mean when they say “language model.” T5 and BART are encoder-decoder models with explicit source-target structure. Vision Transformers are the same block reading 16×16 image patches. Whisper is encoder-decoder operating on audio spectrograms. Mixture-of-Experts is a swap-out of the FFN for a learned router plus several parallel FFNs, which is how DeepSeek-V3 packs 671 billion parameters into 37 billion active per token. The block is identical across all of these. The training objective and input format are what change.

Chapter 17 takes a transformer like the one we just built, makes it three orders of magnitude bigger, and trains it on real data. That chapter is where pre-training gets serious distributed training, the data pipeline, scaling laws, and the actual compute budget required to make a frontier model.

Chapter 19 is where we turn a trained transformer into something you can serve under production cost and latency budgets. KV cache, paged attention, speculative decoding, quantization, and the modern serving stack.

Chapter 26 is where transformers stop being models and start being agents. The same block, now wrapped in tool-calling, memory, and an agent loop, is most of what underlies the systems that write code, browse the web, and run for hours without supervision.

The transformer is not the most beautiful idea in deep learning. The most beautiful idea is probably backpropagation. But the transformer is the most consequential one of the last decade. Everything in the rest of this volume builds on the block we just built. If something here doesn’t sit right, go back and run the code. Watch the attention weights. Stare at the loss curve. The intuition catches up with the math eventually if you let it.

Then turn the page.

Chapter 14 Pretrained Transformer Variants

The 2017 paper proposed an architecture for translation. Within five years, that architecture had been retooled into seven different productized model families, each specialized for a different shape of problem: BERT for understanding, GPT for generation, T5 for input-to-output tasks, ViT for vision, Whisper for speech, Mixture-of-Experts for cheap scale, and the original encoder-decoder for everything that didn’t fit cleanly into any of the others. The core block is identical. What changed each time was the training objective, and occasionally the input format.

This chapter is a tour of the variant zoo. By the end of it you should be able to read a model card “decoder-only with GQA, RoPE, RMSNorm, SwiGLU, MoE with 256 routed and 1 shared expert, top-8” and know exactly what every word means and why someone made each choice. We’ll also cover the two production-essential add-ons that turned transformers from “trainable but slow” into “deployable at frontier scale”: KV caching and Flash Attention. Both deserve full chapters of their own (Chapter 19), but you need the names and the shapes here.

We close with scaling laws. They are the most important quantitative result in the field they tell you, given a fixed compute budget, how to spend it across model size and training data and the answer changed dramatically between 2020 and 2022 in a way that anyone shipping a model needs to understand.

The variant zoo, organized

Every transformer variant in production is a choice about three things: the objective, the architecture shape, and the input modality. Lock those three down and you’ve named the model.

Family Objective Shape Modality Canonical use
BERT Masked language modeling Encoder-only Text Embeddings, classification, retrieval
GPT Next-token prediction Decoder-only Text Generation, chat, code, reasoning
T5 / BART Denoising / span corruption Encoder-decoder Text → text Translation, summarization, structured tasks
ViT Classification or self-supervised Encoder-only Image patches Image features, vision backbones
Whisper Next-token prediction with cross-attention Encoder-decoder Audio → text Speech recognition, translation
MoE family Same as base (typically GPT-style) Decoder-only with sparse FFN Text Frontier-scale chat at lower active FLOP cost

What stays the same across all of them: the block from Chapter 13. Pre-norm, self-attention, FFN, residuals. RMSNorm and SwiGLU in modern variants. The skeleton. What changes is what gets fed in, what loss is applied, and whether you have an encoder, a decoder, or both. That’s basically it.

The reason there are so many variants is not architectural creativity. It’s that the objective determines the geometry of the problem. A model trained to predict missing words from context on both sides BERT produces representations where every token’s hidden state has integrated information from its full neighborhood. Those representations are excellent for embeddings and classification. A model trained to predict the next token from only the past GPT produces representations where each token’s hidden state has integrated only its left context, but it can generate. The architectural difference is one masking decision. The capability difference is whether you have a chatbot or a search engine.

Let’s go through each.

BERT: bidirectional encoders and the embeddings era

BERT’s loss function is one paragraph and almost the entire reason the model existed. Take a sentence; randomly pick 15% of the tokens; replace 80% of those with [MASK], 10% with a random token, 10% leave alone; train the model to predict the original tokens at the chosen positions. The encoder is bidirectional no causal mask so predicting the mask at position 5 can use everything at positions 0 through 4 and positions 6 through N. That’s the whole training signal.

This is called masked language modeling (MLM) and it was the unlock for transfer learning in NLP. Before BERT, every sentiment classifier, every named-entity recognizer, every question-answering system trained from scratch on its own labeled data. BERT showed you could pre-train one giant encoder on every sentence on the internet, then fine-tune a tiny task-specific head, and beat every prior result. The encoder learned what English (and a hundred other languages) means; the task head learned how to use it for whatever specific thing you wanted.

Why the 80/10/10 mask rule? Because [MASK] never appears at inference time. Training the model to expect [MASK] 100% of the time at masked positions creates a distribution shift between pretraining and downstream use the model gets good at one specific corruption pattern that doesn’t exist in the wild. The 10% random tokens and 10% unchanged tokens force the model to predict the original from context regardless of whether the input position has been corrupted, which is the actual task at fine-tune time.

BERT also trained on a second objective in the original paper Next Sentence Prediction, where the model had to decide whether sentence B followed sentence A. RoBERTa (2019) ablated NSP and found it actively hurt. Modern encoders skip it. If you read older BERT tutorials referencing NSP, mentally delete that section.

What BERT looks like in code

The classification fine-tune pattern is dead simple:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("answerdotai/ModernBERT-base")
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("answerdotai/ModernBERT-base")

text = "Attention is all you need."
inputs = tok(text, return_tensors="pt")
out = model(**inputs).last_hidden_state
cls_embedding = out[:, 0]

out has shape (1, N, 768) batch of one, N tokens, 768-dim per token. The [0] slice picks the [CLS] token, which is a special token prepended to every sequence. Its final hidden state is conventionally treated as the sentence-level representation. Add a linear layer on top, train on classification data, you have a sentiment classifier.

Embedding models the ones that power semantic search, RAG, and recommendation systems are also fine-tuned BERTs. The library sentence-transformers ships dozens of them; the canonical small one (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) is about 80 MB and runs faster than the LLMs that depend on it. Take BERT, fine-tune it with a contrastive loss that pushes semantically similar sentences toward each other and dissimilar ones apart, and you have an embedding model. We come back to embeddings as a primitive in Chapter 22 when we cover RAG.

What changed: ModernBERT

The 2018 BERT block is dated. It uses learned absolute positional embeddings (limited to 512 tokens), GELU activation, LayerNorm, and full quadratic attention. ModernBERT (Warner et al., December 2024) rebuilt the encoder with the 2026 primitives we covered in Chapter 13:

Component BERT (2018) ModernBERT (2024)
Position Learned absolute (512 tokens max) RoPE (8192 tokens)
Activation GELU FFN GeGLU FFN
Normalization Post-norm LayerNorm Pre-norm RMSNorm
Attention Full dense Alternating local-128 + global
Tokenizer WordPiece BPE

The local-128/global alternating attention is the only architectural surprise most layers attend only within a 128-token window, with full global attention every fourth layer. This keeps cost roughly linear in sequence length while preserving long-range information. ModernBERT runs 2-3× faster than DeBERTa-v3 at sequence length 8K with better GLUE scores. If you’re starting an encoder project in 2026, this is your default.

When you still pick an encoder

In a world where decoder-only LLMs can do classification, embedding, and reranking by being asked nicely in a prompt, why bother with encoder-only models? Because they’re 5-10× faster per token, their bidirectional context produces measurably better embeddings, and they don’t have the generation overhead. For everything in the table below, encoder-only beats decoder-only at matched compute:

Task Why encoder wins
Retrieval / semantic search embeddings Bidirectional context = better embedding quality per token
Classification (sentiment, intent, toxicity) One forward pass; no token-by-token generation
Token labeling (NER, POS) Per-position output, natively bidirectional
Reranking for RAG Cross-encoder scoring, 10× faster than LLM-based rerankers

For everything generative chat, code, reasoning, agents you want a decoder-only model, which is GPT.

GPT: decoder-only causal language modeling

GPT’s loss function is even simpler than BERT’s. Given a sequence of tokens, predict each one from the ones before it. Cross-entropy over the next-token distribution at every position. That’s it.

To make this work in parallel during training, you need a causal mask: the upper triangle of the attention matrix is set to −∞-\infty−∞ before softmax, so position iii can attend only to positions 000 through iii. After softmax those positions become exactly zero, and each position’s prediction depends only on its left context. With this mask, you can train on a whole sequence in one forward pass every position simultaneously predicts the next token, and you get NNN supervision signals for the price of one.

def causal_mask(N):
    mask = torch.full((N, N), float("-inf"))
    return torch.triu(mask, diagonal=1)

That’s the most consequential single function in modern AI. Every chatbot, every code completion, every reasoning model on Earth is, at the bottom, this mask plus the block from Chapter 13.

Vol 1 refresher. Cross-entropy loss for a single position is −logP(targeti∣inputs<i)-\log P(\text{target}_i \mid \text{inputs}_{<i})−logP(target​i​​∣inputs​<i​​). For a whole sequence, sum across positions and divide by length. The Vol 1 Ch 7 derivation comes out of maximum likelihood minimizing cross-entropy is equivalent to maximizing the probability the model assigns to the actual data.

Parallel training, serial inference

The asymmetry between how a GPT trains and how it generates is worth pausing on, because it shapes everything about deployment.

Training. You feed the whole sequence in one forward pass. The causal mask makes each position predict its successor without leaking the answer. NNN tokens, one matmul per layer. Embarrassingly parallel. GPUs love it. This is why pretraining a 70-billion-parameter LLM is feasible at all.

Inference. You generate one token at a time. Feed [t1, t2, t3], get t4. Append. Feed [t1, t2, t3, t4], get t5. Append. Feed five tokens, get six. The serial depth at inference equals the output length. This is the autoregressive tax, and it’s why latency is the dominant constraint on every production LLM. Output a 500-token response and you’ve done 500 sequential forward passes through every layer of the model.

KV caching (which we cover later in this chapter) saves you from recomputing the prefix on every step. Speculative decoding lets a small draft model propose multiple tokens at once. Continuous batching keeps the GPU busy across requests. None of these tricks change the fundamental shape generation is sequential, training is parallel, and the production challenge of LLM inference is mostly about working around that.

Sampling: the choices that change the model’s personality

After training, sampling decisions matter more than people expect. The model produces a probability distribution over the next token. How you turn that distribution into a single token determines whether the output is deterministic, creative, repetitive, or nonsense.

Method What it does When to use
Greedy Pick argmax every step Deterministic tasks, code completion, factual QA
Temperature TTT Divide logits by TTT before softmax; sample Creative tasks, higher TTT = more diversity
Top-kkk Restrict sampling to top-kkk tokens Kills low-probability tails, prevents nonsense
Top-ppp (nucleus) Sample from smallest set with cumulative probability ≥p\geq p≥p Adapts to distribution shape; pre-2024 default
Min-ppp Keep tokens where p≥pmin⋅pmaxp \geq p_{\min} \cdot p_{\max}p≥p​min​​⋅p​max​​ 2024+; adapts cutoff to distribution sharpness
Speculative Draft model proposes; verifier checks 2-3× latency reduction at no quality loss

A reasonable default in 2026 for open-weights models is min-ppp at 0.05 with temperature 0.7. For deterministic tasks (code, structured output), greedy or temperature 0. For creative writing, temperature 0.9-1.1 with min-ppp. The combinations matter: greedy + top-kkk is contradictory (greedy picks one token, top-kkk assumes you’re sampling). Most APIs accept all of these as parameters, and the defaults are usually wrong for your specific task. Think about it.

What made GPT eat the world

The GPT architecture has barely changed since GPT-2 in 2019. The block is the same one from Chapter 13. Decoder-only, causal masking, autoregressive next-token prediction. Everything that’s happened since has been about scale, data, and what you do after pretraining:

  1. Decoder-only is cheap to scale. No encoder overhead, one stream of attention and FFN per layer, weights tied between input embedding and output head. The architecture is as simple as a transformer can be while still being useful.
  2. In-context learning emerged. Around 6-13B parameters, the model started being able to learn new tasks from few-shot examples in the prompt, with no fine-tuning. Brown et al. (2020) made this the centerpiece of the GPT-3 paper. We still don’t fully understand why induction heads (Anthropic’s interpretability team, 2022) are part of the answer.
  3. Post-training got serious. Pretraining gives you a model that completes arbitrary text. RLHF, DPO, and constitutional methods (Chapters 18, 33) turn that into a chat assistant. The recipe matters more than the architecture.

Encoder-only models won classification. Decoder-only models won everything else.

Encoder-decoder: when input and output are different shapes

Many tasks are naturally input-to-output. Translation: English to French. Summarization: long article to short summary. Speech recognition: audio to text. Structured extraction: prose to JSON. For these, encoder-decoder is the cleanest fit: bidirectional encoder produces a dense representation of the source, causal decoder generates the output while cross-attending to that representation at every step.

The decoder block has three sub-layers instead of two: masked self-attention (so the decoder can’t look at its own future), then cross-attention (queries from the decoder, keys and values from the encoder output), then FFN. Cross-attention is the only place encoder information enters the decoder. The encoder runs once per input. The decoder runs autoregressively but cross-attends to the same encoder output at every step.

Two papers from 2019 defined the modern playbook. T5 (Raffel et al.) introduced span corruption: pick random spans of the input, replace each with a unique sentinel token, train the decoder to output only the corrupted spans with their sentinel prefixes. Cheaper than reconstructing the whole sequence and competitive with BERT-style MLM. BART (Lewis et al.) went the other way with a multi-noise denoising objective: corrupt the input five different ways (mask, delete, infill, permute sentences, rotate documents), train the decoder to reconstruct the original. BART outputs the whole sequence, not just the corrupted spans, so it costs more to train but gets correspondingly better at long-form generation.

from transformers import T5ForConditionalGeneration, T5Tokenizer
tok = T5Tokenizer.from_pretrained("google/flan-t5-base")
model = T5ForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained("google/flan-t5-base")
inputs = tok("translate English to French: Attention is all you need.", return_tensors="pt")
out = model.generate(**inputs, max_new_tokens=32)
print(tok.decode(out[0], skip_special_tokens=True))

The T5 trick: the task name goes into the input text. One model handles dozens of tasks because each task is just text in, text out. This pattern got generalized later by instruction-tuned decoder-only LLMs, but T5 codified it first.

Why decoder-only mostly won

Since around 2022, decoder-only models have eaten most of the territory that used to belong to encoder-decoder. Translation? Decoder-only LLMs match or beat T5 in most language pairs. Summarization? Same. Reasoning, code, structured extraction? Decoder-only with prompting. The reasons:

  1. One architecture scales easier than two. Distributed training, KV caching, RLHF all simpler when there’s just a decoder.
  2. In-context learning generalizes for free. A decoder-only LLM can do “translate this” via prompting; an encoder-decoder needs to be trained on the task.
  3. RLHF assumes a decoder. The post-training stack we cover in Chapter 18 fits decoder-only naturally.

Where encoder-decoder still wins is when the input modality is genuinely different from the output. Speech recognition (Whisper). Some translation systems where beam search quality matters and the input has a clear source-sequence identity. Some image-to-text and structured extraction tasks where the encoder can specialize on input format. We’ll see Whisper next.

ViT: the same block, reading images as patches

The Vision Transformer paper has the best title in the field: An Image is Worth 16×16 Words. The proposal was almost embarrassingly simple. Take an image. Slice it into a grid of fixed-size patches. Flatten each patch into a vector, project it through a linear layer, prepend a [CLS] token, add positional embeddings, feed the resulting sequence to a vanilla transformer encoder. No convolutions. No vision-specific architectural decisions. Just a transformer eating image tiles in raster order.

For a 224×224 image with 16×16 patches, you get 14×14=19614 \times 14 = 19614×14=196 patches, each a flat vector of 16×16×3=76816 \times 16 \times 3 = 76816×16×3=768 pixel values. Project each to dimension 768 with a single learned matrix and you have a 196-token sequence ready for the standard transformer block. Done. The “linear projection of flat patches” is equivalent to a 2D convolution with kernel size 16 and stride 16, which is how it’s typically implemented:

class PatchEmbed(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, patch_size=16, in_chans=3, embed_dim=768):
        super().__init__()
        self.proj = nn.Conv2d(in_chans, embed_dim, kernel_size=patch_size, stride=patch_size)
    def forward(self, x):
        return self.proj(x).flatten(2).transpose(1, 2)

That’s the entire vision-specific component. Everything after it is the transformer encoder we built in Chapter 13.

For classification, the [CLS] token’s final hidden state goes through a linear head and softmax. For dense prediction (segmentation, depth estimation), you discard [CLS] and use the patch embeddings directly each patch token corresponds to a 16×16 region of the image. For self-supervised learning (DINOv2), neither head exists at training time; the model learns to predict patch features under augmentation.

Why it took data

ViT needs a lot of data to match CNNs on equal compute, because it has none of the convolutional inductive biases. CNNs assume translation invariance (a cat is a cat regardless of where it appears) and locality (nearby pixels are more related than distant ones). ViT assumes nothing it has to learn both from data. Without ImageNet-21k pretraining (14 million labeled images) or strong self-supervision, CNNs still win on small datasets.

DeiT (2021) fixed this with distillation from a CNN teacher, making ViT trainable on ImageNet-1k alone. DINOv2 (2023) fixed it permanently with self-supervised pretraining: train the encoder to produce consistent features under image augmentation, no labels required. DINOv2 features beat CLIP on every non-text vision task and are the 2026 default for image embeddings.

Model Year What changed
ViT 2020 The original; full global attention
DeiT 2021 Distillation from CNN teacher; trainable on small datasets
Swin 2021 Hierarchical with shifted windows; sub-quadratic
DINOv2 2023 Self-supervised; best general image features in 2026
SigLIP 2023 ViT + text encoder, sigmoid contrastive loss; CLIP’s successor
SAM 3 2025 Segment-anything with promptable mask decoder

The reason ViT matters for the rest of this book is not vision per se. It’s that the same transformer block we built for language eats image tokens just as well, which means one architecture handles every modality you care about. Multimodal models (Chapter 24) compose a ViT encoder with a language decoder and call it a day. Audio transformers (next section) do the same trick with spectrograms instead of patches.

Whisper: an encoder-decoder for audio

Audio is just an image of frequency over time. That sentence is the entire conceptual basis for Whisper.

OpenAI’s Whisper (Radford et al., 2022) takes an encoder-decoder transformer architecture, feeds the encoder log-mel spectrograms 80 mel-frequency bins by 3000 time frames for a 30-second audio clip and trains the decoder to autoregressively produce text tokens. The trick that made Whisper dominate speech recognition was not architectural. It was data: 680,000 hours of weakly-labeled audio from the internet across 97 languages, with no clean academic corpus and no phoneme-level labels. Whisper learned to be robust to accents, noise, and obscure languages because it was trained on actual messy audio rather than studio-quality benchmarks.

The pipeline:

  1. Resample to 16 kHz, clip or pad to 30 seconds. Whisper always processes 30-second windows.
  2. Compute log-mel spectrogram. 80 mel bins, 10 ms hop. 3000 frames.
  3. Convolutional stem. Two Conv1D layers with stride 2 reduce 3000 frames to 1500.
  4. Transformer encoder. 24 layers (large), self-attention over 1500 timesteps. Outputs 1500 × 1280 hidden states.
  5. Transformer decoder. Autoregressive over a BPE token vocabulary; cross-attends to the encoder output.

What’s interesting about Whisper is the task token convention. Instead of training separate models for transcription, translation, and language ID, Whisper handles all of them in one decoder by prefixing the output with control tokens:

<|startoftranscript|> <|en|> <|transcribe|> <|0.00|>
<|startoftranscript|> <|fr|> <|translate|>  <|0.00|>

The first prefix tells the model “this is English audio, transcribe it.” The second tells it “this is French audio, translate to English.” Same model. Different prefix. This is the audio-domain equivalent of the prompt engineering we’ll cover in Chapter 21 task control via input format, not architectural change.

from faster_whisper import WhisperModel
model = WhisperModel("large-v3-turbo", compute_type="int8_float16")
segments, info = model.transcribe("meeting.wav", vad_filter=True)
for s in segments:
    print(f"{s.start:.2f} - {s.end:.2f}: {s.text}")

The 2024 release of Whisper-large-v3-turbo cut the decoder from 32 layers to 4, achieving 8× faster decoding with under 1 word-error-rate point of regression. That latency unlock is why turbo is the default for real-time voice agents in 2026; we’ll see voice pipelines in Chapter 26 when we cover production agents.

What Whisper doesn’t do: speaker diarization (who’s talking), real-time streaming (the 30-second window is fixed), or anything beyond 30 seconds of audio context. Production wrappers like faster-whisper and WhisperX bolt on streaming via voice-activity detection plus overlap. For diarization, pair with pyannote.audio. The point is that Whisper is a transcription model, not a complete audio understanding system, and you build the system around it.

Mixture of Experts: more parameters, fewer active FLOPs

Every dense transformer pays the same FLOP cost per token: roughly 2N2N2N floating-point operations for NNN parameters in a forward pass. Scale up a dense model and every token pays the full bill. By 2024 the frontier was hitting a compute wall to be meaningfully smarter, you needed exponentially more FLOPs per token, and inference cost was eating all the gains.

Mixture of Experts (MoE) breaks this link. Replace each FFN with a bank of EEE independent experts plus a router that picks the top kkk experts per token. Total parameters scale with EEE. Active parameters per token scale with kkk. Storage and capacity grow; per-token compute stays flat.

The numbers are striking:

Model Total params Active per token
Llama 3 70B (dense) 70B 70B
Mixtral 8×22B 141B ~39B
DeepSeek-V3 671B 37B
Kimi K2 1T ~32B

DeepSeek-V3 has nearly ten times the parameters of Llama 3 70B but does roughly half the active FLOPs per token, and it beats Llama 3 70B on most benchmarks. More parameters means more knowledge. More active FLOPs means more compute. MoE decouples them.

How a MoE layer actually works

A dense block:

h=x+Attn(Norm(x)),h=h+FFN(Norm(h))h = x + \text{Attn}(\text{Norm}(x)), \quad h = h + \text{FFN}(\text{Norm}(h)) h=x+Attn(Norm(x)),h=h+FFN(Norm(h))

A MoE block replaces the FFN with a routed mixture:

scores=router(Norm(h)),topk=argmaxk(scores)\text{scores} = \text{router}(\text{Norm}(h)), \quad \text{top}_k = \text{argmax}_k(\text{scores}) scores=router(Norm(h)),top​k​​=argmax​k​​(scores)

h=h+∑e∈topkgate(scorese)⋅Experte(Norm(h))h = h + \sum_{e \in \text{top}_k} \text{gate}(\text{scores}_e) \cdot \text{Expert}_e(\text{Norm}(h)) h=h+​e∈top​k​​​∑​​gate(scores​e​​)⋅Expert​e​​(Norm(h))

Every expert is an independent FFN, typically SwiGLU. The router is a single linear layer producing a score per expert. Each token picks its own top-kkk experts and gets a softmax-weighted mixture of their outputs.

In code:

def moe_forward(h, experts, router, top_k=2):
    scores = router(h)
    top_vals, top_idx = scores.topk(top_k, dim=-1)
    gates = top_vals.softmax(dim=-1)
    out = torch.zeros_like(h)
    for k in range(top_k):
        for e in range(len(experts)):
            mask = (top_idx[..., k] == e)
            if mask.any():
                out[mask] += gates[..., k:k+1][mask] * experts[e](h[mask])
    return out

That’s a pedagogical implementation. Real MoE inference uses fused kernels and expert-parallelism across GPUs. The for loop is for understanding only.

The load-balancing problem and how DeepSeek solved it

If the router puts 90% of tokens through expert 3, the other experts starve. They get fewer gradient updates, learn less, drift further from being useful, and the imbalance compounds. Three eras of fixes:

  1. Auxiliary load-balancing loss. Add a penalty term proportional to the variance in expert usage (Switch Transformer, Mixtral). Works, but adds a hyperparameter and a competing gradient signal that can hurt the main objective.
  2. Expert capacity with token dropping. Each expert processes at most some quota of tokens; overflow tokens skip the layer entirely. Hurts quality on hard tokens.
  3. Auxiliary-loss-free balancing. DeepSeek-V3’s 2024 trick: add a learned per-expert bias that shifts the router’s top-kkk selection. The bias is updated outside the training loss after each step, nudge it positive for under-used experts and negative for over-used ones. Selection uses scores + bias. The gates used for weighting expert outputs use the raw scores, unchanged. This decouples routing balance from expression: the bias gets the load right without distorting the model’s predictions.

This is one of those engineering ideas that reads as obvious in hindsight and was apparently very non-obvious until DeepSeek shipped it. Every frontier MoE in 2026 has either adopted it or has a paper explaining why their alternative is better.

Fine-grained experts and shared experts

Classic MoE designs (GShard, Switch, early Mixtral) had small numbers of large experts: EEE around 8-64, kkk around 1-2, each expert as wide as a full FFN. Modern fine-grained MoE (DeepSeek-V3, Qwen-MoE) goes the other way: EEE around 256, kkk around 8, each expert about 1/8 the width of a full FFN. Same total parameters, but the number of possible “expert combinations” per token explodes (2568)≈4×1014\binom{256}{8} \approx 4 \times 10^{14}(​8​256​​)≈4×10​14​​. Each token can effectively get a custom FFN drawn from a vast space.

DeepSeek also splits experts into shared (every token goes through them) and routed (top-kkk selection). Shared experts capture common knowledge that every prediction needs; routed experts specialize. V3 runs 1 shared plus top-8-of-256 routed. Empirically, this split improves quality for free the shared expert handles the “always-on” computation, and the routed experts can afford to be more specialized because they don’t have to redundantly carry common knowledge.

When to pick MoE, and when not to

Pick MoE if Pick dense if
You want frontier quality at lower per-token inference cost You’re deploying to edge / consumer hardware
You have expert-parallel infrastructure (or are using a hosted API) You can’t afford to keep all experts in VRAM
Your workload is token-heavy chat or code Your model is small (<7B) MoE’s advantage shows up above ~6B active params
Total parameter budget is dominated by FFNs Latency-critical single-user serving

The catch is memory. All experts live on GPU regardless of which fire. A 671B-parameter model needs about 1.3 TB of VRAM at fp16 just for the weights. Frontier MoE deployment requires expert parallelism sharding experts across GPUs and routing tokens across the network. Latency in this regime is dominated by all-to-all communication, not matmul. Production stacks like vLLM and SGLang handle this for you, but you should know it’s the bottleneck before you blame the model.

We come back to MoE serving in Chapter 19 (the inference deep dive) and Chapter 20 (modern LLM architectures, which spends real time on DeepSeek-V3’s full bag of tricks).

KV cache and Flash Attention: the two production-essential add-ons

The transformer block we built in Chapter 13 trains efficiently and runs slowly. Two add-ons turn it from a research artifact into something you can deploy. Both deserve full chapters of their own we get to inference deeply in Chapter 19 but you need the names and shapes here because every other chapter in this volume assumes them.

KV caching

The autoregressive tax. To generate NNN tokens with a naive decoder, you do roughly N2N^2N​2​​ work at every step you recompute attention over the full prefix. For a 4K-token response that’s 16 million attention ops, almost all redundant. Once you’ve computed the keys and values for a token, they don’t change.

The fix is to cache the key and value tensors at every layer for every prefix token. At each generation step, the new token’s query attends to the cached keys and values plus its own. Total work drops from O(N2)O(N^2)O(N​2​​) to O(N)O(N)O(N).

class KVCache:
    def __init__(self):
        self.K = []
        self.V = []
    def append(self, k, v):
        self.K.append(k)
        self.V.append(v)

That’s the cache. Real implementations are more elaborate preallocated tensors instead of growing lists, per-layer per-head sharding, contiguous memory layout but the idea is one paragraph.

The cost of KV caching is memory. Per token, per layer, per head, you store two dheadd_{\text{head}}d​head​​-dimensional vectors. For Llama 3 70B with 80 layers, 8 KV heads, dhead=128d_{\text{head}} = 128d​head​​=128 in fp16: about 4 KB per token per layer, 320 KB per token across the model, which means 10 GB of KV cache for a single 32K-context conversation. That’s at batch size 1.

This is why GQA (Chapter 13) won over MHA. Cutting KV heads from 64 to 8 cuts the cache by 8×, which translates directly into 8× more concurrent users on the same GPU. MLA (DeepSeek-V3) goes further by compressing KKK and VVV into a low-rank latent. KV cache memory is the dominant constraint on production LLM throughput, and architectural decisions about attention are mostly decisions about KV cache.

Flash Attention

Standard attention writes the full N×NN \times NN×N score matrix to GPU main memory (HBM), then reads it back, applies softmax, writes it again, reads it again, multiplies by VVV, writes the result. Three round trips through HBM. On an H100, HBM bandwidth is roughly 3 TB/s while on-chip SRAM is around 30 TB/s every HBM round trip is a 10× slowdown vs keeping data on-chip.

Flash Attention (Dao et al., 2022) is a tiled implementation that never materializes the N×NN \times NN×N matrix in HBM. The attention computation is reorganized so that small tiles of QQQ, KKK, VVV are loaded into SRAM, the tile’s contribution to the softmax-weighted output is computed there, and only the final output is written back. A running-max trick keeps the softmax exact Flash Attention computes bit-identical output to standard attention, modulo fp16 non-associativity. Total memory drops from O(N2)O(N^2)O(N​2​​) to O(N)O(N)O(N). Wall-clock speed up by 2-4× on A100s, more on Hopper.

The version history matters because production stacks pin specific versions:

Version Year Key change
Flash 1 2022 Tiled SRAM kernel
Flash 2 2023 Better parallelism, causal-first ordering
Flash 3 2024 Hopper asynchrony, FP8 support
Flash 4 2026 Blackwell five-stage pipeline; forward-pass-only at launch

You almost never invoke Flash Attention directly. PyTorch’s torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention auto-dispatches to it on supported hardware. Production inference engines (vLLM, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM) pull in pinned versions. The detail you need to know: when someone says “we’re getting 700 TFLOPs of FP16 attention on H100,” they mean Flash Attention 3 with the asynchrony pipeline; the same kernel without those tricks gets a third of that.

What else lives in the inference stack

The two we just covered are the foundation. Production LLM serving in 2026 also relies on:

  • Continuous batching (Orca, vLLM): swap finished sequences out and new ones in without draining the batch. 5-10× throughput vs static batching.
  • PagedAttention (vLLM): KV cache allocated in 16-token blocks with a page table; eliminates fragmentation, enables prefix sharing.
  • Prefix caching: KV cache for shared prompts (system prompts, few-shot examples, RAG context) reused across requests. For agent workloads with repeated tool prompts, routinely 5× throughput.
  • Speculative decoding (EAGLE, Medusa, draft-verify): cheap draft model proposes kkk tokens; big model verifies in one forward pass. 2-3× latency reduction at no quality loss.

We pull all of this apart in Chapter 19 and again in Chapter 29 when we get to production serving.

Scaling laws: how to spend a billion dollars of compute

Every model on the previous pages was trained with a specific budget some number of FLOPs distributed across some number of parameters and some number of training tokens. Scaling laws answer: given CCC FLOPs of compute, what split between parameters NNN and tokens DDD produces the lowest loss?

This question matters for two reasons. If you’re training your own foundation model (Chapter 17), getting it wrong means leaving billions of dollars on the table. If you’re using someone else’s model, scaling laws tell you whether your fine-tune has enough data, whether to pick a bigger or smaller base, and where the returns from “just train more” go to die.
