The Kaplan era and the Chinchilla correction

OpenAI’s Kaplan et al. (2020) was the first paper to systematically map loss as a function of NNN and DDD. The conclusion was: “make the model bigger.” GPT-3 (2020) was trained at 175B parameters on roughly 300 billion tokens about 1.7 tokens per parameter. The Kaplan curves backed this up.

Hoffmann et al. at DeepMind (2022) replicated the experiment more carefully and found something different. The data was wrong because the prior runs hadn’t covered enough of the (N,D)(N, D)(N,D) space. The corrected scaling law is:

L(N,D)=ANα+BDβ+EL(N, D) = \frac{A}{N^\alpha} + \frac{B}{D^\beta} + E L(N,D)=​N​α​​​​A​​+​D​β​​​​B​​+E

with α≈0.34\alpha \approx 0.34α≈0.34, β≈0.28\beta \approx 0.28β≈0.28, E≈1.69E \approx 1.69E≈1.69 (the irreducible loss the entropy of the training data, the floor below which no model can go regardless of size).

Take the derivative at fixed compute C≈6NDC \approx 6NDC≈6ND and you get the Chinchilla-optimal point:

Nopt≈0.6⋅C/6,Dopt≈0.6⋅C/6,Dopt/Nopt≈20N_{\text{opt}} \approx 0.6 \cdot \sqrt{C/6}, \quad D_{\text{opt}} \approx 0.6 \cdot \sqrt{C/6}, \quad D_{\text{opt}} / N_{\text{opt}} \approx 20 N​opt​​≈0.6⋅√​C/6​​​,D​opt​​≈0.6⋅√​C/6​​​,D​opt​​/N​opt​​≈20

Twenty tokens per parameter, give or take. The DeepMind team trained a model called Chinchilla (70B parameters, 1.4T tokens) to validate this and beat GPT-3 (175B, 300B) on every benchmark at 2.5× lower inference cost. GPT-3 was, by this analysis, ten times undertrained.

Why everyone over-trains anyway

Chinchilla-optimal minimizes loss per training FLOP. For a frontier lab burning a hundred million dollars to pretrain one model, that’s the right metric. For a chatbot serving a trillion tokens per month, it isn’t inference cost dominates total cost, and inference scales with NNN, not DDD. Llama 3 8B was trained on 15 trillion tokens, 1,875 tokens per parameter, ninety-four times past Chinchilla-optimal.

Why? Because at deployment scale, the math goes the other way. You pay training cost once. You pay inference cost forever. Training a smaller model for longer raises training cost a bit but cuts every future inference call by a factor proportional to NNN. For models served at scale, the optimal compute split is biased heavily toward over-training, with ratios typically in the 100-500 tokens per parameter range. Sardana and Frankle (2024) formalized this in Beyond Chinchilla-Optimal: Accounting for Inference in Language Model Scaling Laws.

So the 2026 picture has two regimes:

Regime Optimal D/N Examples
Train once, evaluate, publish ~20 Chinchilla, research checkpoints
Train once, serve forever 100-500 Llama 3, Phi-3, Qwen 2.5

If you’re starting a project that involves “we train a model” your first decision is which regime you’re in. The answer determines how you spend your compute.

What scales beyond the law

Scaling laws give you the asymptote, not the absolute constants. Several factors shift the constants in your favor:

  • Data quality. Curating “good” tokens (the Phi-3 approach: textbooks, code, synthetic explanatory material) shifts effective compute by more than 2×. A well-curated trillion tokens beats five trillion of mostly-garbage Common Crawl.
  • Optimizer. The Muon optimizer (Jordan et al., 2024) shows about 2× effective compute over AdamW at matched data on small models. Some 2026 training runs default to it.
  • Architecture tricks. MoE decouples total parameters from active FLOPs, breaking the basic scaling law shape. The relevant question for MoE becomes scaling per active parameter.
  • Post-training. Some capabilities (instruction following, code, math) shift more from SFT and RLHF than from additional pretraining. Pretraining gets you a base; post-training gets you the assistant.

The data ceiling is the biggest open problem. The web has roughly 5-10 trillion high-quality English tokens after filtering. Frontier pretraining is approaching that ceiling, which is why synthetic data, multilingual coverage, multimodal training, and longer post-training stages are all becoming more important than just-keep-pretraining. Whether this constitutes “the end of scaling” or “a shift to scaling other axes” depends on who you ask, and the answer probably won’t be clear until 2027 at the earliest.

Emergence is mostly a measurement artifact

You’ll hear claims that certain abilities “emerge suddenly” at some parameter scale arithmetic at 13B, multi-step reasoning at 70B, chain-of-thought following somewhere in between. Schaeffer et al. (2023) showed in Are Emergent Abilities of Large Language Models a Mirage? that most of these jumps are scoring artifacts. Discontinuous metrics (exact match, accuracy at threshold) hide smooth improvement in the underlying logits. Continuous metrics (cross-entropy) show the smooth curves that scaling laws predict.

Plan budgets against continuous loss. Be skeptical of any claim that requires the y-axis to be a step function.

Sharp edges across all the variants

A few cross-cutting issues that show up regardless of which variant you’re running.

Distribution shift between pretraining and use. BERT trained with [MASK] in 12% of positions; that token never appears in real text. GPT trained on the entire internet; your application probably wants a much narrower distribution. Whisper trained on weakly-labeled internet audio; the conference call you want to transcribe is acoustically different. Every fine-tune is a fight against the gap between what the model was trained on and what you actually want it to do.

Tokenizer is not invisible. Every transformer has a tokenizer attached, and the tokenizer changes the math. A model with a vocabulary of 50,000 tokens has different scaling-law constants than a model with 200,000 tokens. Code performance depends heavily on whether your tokenizer treats indentation efficiently. Multilingual performance depends on whether non-English languages got reasonable subword merges. We dedicate Chapter 17 to this; for now, know that “the model” is shorthand for “the model and its tokenizer,” and they cannot be swapped independently.

Variant boundaries are squishier than the table suggests. In 2026, the lines between encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder are blurrier than in 2020. Decoder-only LLMs do classification by being asked. Encoder models like ModernBERT have caught up enough that you’d pick them for inference cost, not because decoder-only can’t. Multimodal models like LLaVA stitch a ViT encoder onto a Llama decoder. The taxonomy is still useful for understanding, but the “which variant should I use?” question is increasingly answered by “whichever has the best off-the-shelf checkpoint for your task,” which is usually a decoder-only LLM.

The architecture has barely changed since GPT-2. This is true and important. The GPT-2 block from 2019, with RoPE, RMSNorm, SwiGLU, GQA, and MoE swapped in, is the 2026 frontier model. Every gain since has come from data, scale, and post-training. If you’re reading a paper that claims a new architectural insight will give you 50% more performance for free, the prior is that they’re wrong. The improvements that have stuck (the ones in the 2026 modernization table from Chapter 13) are unglamorous, individually small, and add up.

What you’d actually use

For each variant, the production-default choice in 2026:

Variant Default checkpoint Library
Encoder (text) ModernBERT or nomic-embed-text-v1.5 for embeddings HuggingFace transformers
Decoder-only (open) Llama 3.x, Qwen 2.5, Mistral, DeepSeek-V3 vLLM for serving, HF for prototyping
Decoder-only (hosted) Claude, GPT, Gemini Native SDKs
Encoder-decoder (text) Flan-T5 family (rarely needed) HuggingFace transformers
Vision encoder DINOv2 (general features), SigLIP (text-aligned) HuggingFace + timm
Speech Whisper-large-v3-turbo via faster-whisper faster-whisper for production
MoE (open) Mixtral, Qwen-MoE, DeepSeek-V3, Llama 4 vLLM or SGLang for serving

For attention specifically, you should always be calling torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention or letting your framework do it for you. The custom-kernel landscape (Flash Attention 3, FlexAttention, FlashInfer) changes every six months. Pin a version, test against it, and let library maintainers handle the version churn.

The reference reading you’ll come back to: HuggingFace’s modeling_*.py files for whichever architecture you’re working with. These are 300-700 lines each, written by people who know what they’re doing, and reading them is the fastest way to understand any new model that ships.

Where this goes next

Chapter 15 takes a detour into generative models that aren’t transformers VAEs, GANs, diffusion, flow matching. You need them because half the modern multimodal models we cover in Chapter 24 are diffusion-based, because Stable Diffusion lives there, and because flow matching is quietly becoming the default for image and video generation. Chapter 16 covers reinforcement learning, which we’ll need for RLHF and alignment in Chapter 18.

Chapter 17 is where we do what this chapter described in the abstract: pre-train a real GPT-style model, end-to-end. Distributed training with FSDP and DeepSpeed, the data pipeline, scaling laws applied to a real budget, the actual amount of compute you need.

Chapter 19 picks up the inference thread we sketched here and turns it into production-grade serving: PagedAttention internals, speculative decoding implementations, quantization (INT8, FP8, NVFP4), continuous batching, and the comparative shape of vLLM, SGLang, and TensorRT-LLM.

Chapter 20 returns to architectural variants but only the modern, frontier-relevant ones. DeepSeek-V3’s full bag of tricks (MLA, fine-grained MoE, MTP, FP8 training), Jamba’s hybrid SSM-transformer block, native sparse attention, multi-token prediction. The cutting edge as of late 2025.

You now have the architectural vocabulary to read the rest of this volume. From here forward, when a chapter says “the model” without qualification, it means a decoder-only causal transformer with RoPE, GQA, RMSNorm, SwiGLU, possibly MoE, served with KV caching and Flash Attention. Every word in that sentence has been earned.

Chapter 15 Generative Models

In 2014 a generative model was a network that could produce a 64×64 face that, if you squinted, looked roughly human. By 2024 the same conceptual problem had been solved at every scale and modality: 1024×1024 photorealistic images on demand, minute-long video clips conditioned on natural-language prompts, voice clones from three seconds of audio, 3D scenes reconstructed from a phone walking through a room. The progress was not driven by one architecture. It was driven by four five if you count the modern token-based autoregressive variant each of which makes a different bet about how to learn a probability distribution you cannot write down.

This chapter is about those bets. The reason a chapter on generative models lives in the Modern AI & LLMs volume rather than off in its own track is that almost every modern multimodal model is a generative model wearing a transformer’s clothes. The image encoder in your favorite vision-language model is a ViT (Chapter 14). The image generator in DALL-E 3, Midjourney, and your autonomous-agent’s screenshot tool is a diffusion or flow-matching model. Audio assistants generate speech with diffusion. 3D reconstruction borrows diffusion priors. If you only know transformers, half the AI you ship is opaque to you.

We’re going to fix that. By the end of this chapter you’ll be able to read a paper or a model card from any modality and know which family it belongs to, what the training loss is, what the inference cost shape looks like, and where it breaks. We’ll spend the most time on diffusion and flow matching because they are the dominant backbones in 2026, and we’ll cover the conditioning toolkit (ControlNet, LoRA, IP-Adapter) because it’s what turns a generative model from a research demo into an agency-grade pipeline. We’ll close with evaluation, which is the hardest unsolved problem in the field every metric is gameable, every benchmark has a rot date, and the best you can do is triangulate.

Five families, one job

A generative model has one job: given training samples drawn from some distribution pdatap_\text{data}p​data​​, output new samples that look like they came from the same distribution. Faces, sentences, MIDI files, protein structures, point clouds the same problem whenever you squint.

The catch is that pdatap_\text{data}p​data​​ lives in a space with millions of dimensions (a 512×512 RGB image is about 786,000 dimensions), the actual data sits on a thin manifold inside that space, and you typically have around ten million examples. Brute-forcing the density is hopeless. Every generative model in production is a compromise that trades one hard problem for a slightly less hard one.

Five families have survived the last decade.

Family Bet Examples Where it shines
Explicit, tractable Write logp(x)\log p(x)logp(x) as a sum you can evaluate Autoregressive (PixelCNN, GPT), normalizing flows Exact likelihoods, density estimation
Explicit, approximate Bound logp(x)\log p(x)logp(x) from below, optimize the bound VAE, diffusion (DDPM) The dominant image / video / audio backbone in 2026
Implicit Skip density entirely; learn a generator + a discriminator GAN family (StyleGAN, WGAN) Narrow-domain photorealism, fast inference, distillation
Score-based / continuous-time Learn ∇xlogp(x)\nabla_x \log p(x)∇​x​​logp(x) or its time-derivative Score matching, flow matching, rectified flow Faster sampling, simulation-free training
Token-based autoregressive Quantize data to discrete codes, model with a transformer VQ-VAE + GPT, AudioLM, Parti Speech, music, “everything but image” with cross-modal mixing

The first family autoregressive over text is the one we already covered in Chapter 14 (it’s what GPT does). The fifth is a special case of the first that adds a learned tokenizer. The interesting story for this chapter is in the middle three: VAE, GAN, diffusion, and the score-based family that diffusion turns into when you stop and look closely. They all dominate different niches and they all leak into the others as components.

When you read a new generative-model paper, run this five-question triage before the method section:

  1. What is being modeled? Pixels, latents, discrete tokens, 3D Gaussians, waveforms?
  2. Is the density explicit or implicit? Do they write down logp(x)\log p(x)logp(x)?
  3. Is sampling one-shot or iterative? Iterative usually means slower inference; one-shot usually means adversarial or distilled.
  4. Conditioning? Unconditional, class label, text, image, pose, depth?
  5. Evaluation? FID, CLIP score, human preference, task accuracy? Each has known failure modes.

You’ll re-answer these for every model in this chapter. By the end they’ll be reflex.

VAEs: the encoder you use without knowing it

The variational autoencoder (Kingma and Welling, 2013) is the model that quietly became the input layer of every modern image and video pipeline. You almost never train a VAE for its own sake in 2026 it produces blurry samples compared to diffusion. But the moment you load Stable Diffusion or Flux or SD3, the first thing your image hits is a VAE. The diffusion model inside operates not on raw pixels but on the VAE’s compressed latent representation. Understand the VAE and you understand the invisible first layer of every image pipeline you use.

The reparameterization trick

A plain autoencoder maps x→z→x^x \to z \to \hat{x}x→z→​x​^​​ with mean-squared reconstruction loss. It compresses well. It does not generate. Sample a random point in its code space and the decoder produces noise the code space is not a distribution, just a bag of training-set memories.

The VAE’s fix is one trick: train the encoder to output a distribution over codes, not a point.

q(z∣x)=N(μ(x),diag(σ2(x)))q(z \mid x) = \mathcal{N}(\mu(x), \text{diag}(\sigma^2(x))) q(z∣x)=N(μ(x),diag(σ​2​​(x)))

then sample from q(z∣x)q(z \mid x)q(z∣x) before decoding, and add a KL penalty that pulls q(z∣x)q(z \mid x)q(z∣x) toward a fixed prior p(z)=N(0,I)p(z) = \mathcal{N}(0, I)p(z)=N(0,I). The KL penalty forces the code space to be globally Gaussian, which means at inference time you can drop the encoder, sample z∼N(0,I)z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)z∼N(0,I), decode, and get a plausible new sample.

The “reparameterization trick” is what makes this trainable. Sampling from q(z∣x)q(z \mid x)q(z∣x) is a stochastic operation and gradients can’t flow through stochasticity. Rewrite the sample as

z=μ(x)+σ(x)⋅ε,ε∼N(0,I)z = \mu(x) + \sigma(x) \cdot \varepsilon, \quad \varepsilon \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I) z=μ(x)+σ(x)⋅ε,ε∼N(0,I)

Now zzz is a deterministic function of μ\muμ and σ\sigmaσ, plus a non-parameter noise term. Gradients flow through μ\muμ and σ\sigmaσ. Stochastic optimization works.

Vol 1 refresher. The KL divergence between two Gaussians has a closed form: KL[N(μ,σ2)∥N(0,I)]=12∑i(σi2+μi2−logσi2−1)\text{KL}[\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2) \| \mathcal{N}(0, I)] = \frac{1}{2} \sum_i (\sigma_i^2 + \mu_i^2 - \log \sigma_i^2 - 1)KL[N(μ,σ​2​​)∥N(0,I)]=​2​​1​​∑​i​​(σ​i​2​​+μ​i​2​​−logσ​i​2​​−1). Compute it directly. People still ship monte-carlo KL estimators in 2026 three times slower, no benefit.

The full loss the evidence lower bound (ELBO) is two terms:

L=\underbrace∥x−x^∥2reconstruction+β⋅\underbraceKL[q(z∣x)∥N(0,I)]regularizer\mathcal{L} = \underbrace{\| x - \hat{x} \|^2}_\text{reconstruction} + \beta \cdot \underbrace{\text{KL}[q(z \mid x) \| \mathcal{N}(0, I)]}_\text{regularizer} L=\underbrace∥x−​x​^​​∥​2​​​reconstruction​​+β⋅\underbraceKL[q(z∣x)∥N(0,I)]​regularizer​​

The β\betaβ knob trades reconstruction quality against code-space Gaussianness. β<1\beta < 1β<1 gives sharper reconstructions and a less-Gaussian code space; β>1\beta > 1β>1 gives a cleaner code space and blurrier reconstructions. The 2017 β\betaβ-VAE paper made this knob famous and kicked off the disentanglement literature, but for production VAE encoders, the standard recipe is β\betaβ small (around 0.01-0.1) since you don’t need the code space to be perfectly Gaussian a diffusion model is going to learn the rest of the distribution shape on top of the latents anyway.

Why VAEs don’t ship standalone

The Gaussian decoder likelihood implies MSE reconstruction loss, which is Bayes-optimal for the mean of a posterior over plausible images. The mean of many plausible MNIST 7s is a fuzzy 7. VAE samples look like averages, because that’s what the loss rewards.

Two fixes: VQ-VAE replaces the continuous latent with a nearest-neighbor lookup in a learned codebook (the “discrete decoder”), giving sharp samples but moving the modeling problem to “how do you generate sequences of code indices,” which a transformer is good at. Or: use the VAE only as an encoder, stack a diffusion model on the latents, and let diffusion handle the sampling. The second option is what won.

Where VAEs live in 2026

Every latent diffusion model has a VAE at its input and output:

Pipeline VAE role
Stable Diffusion 1.x / 2.x / XL Encode 512²×3 → 64²×4 latents; decode after diffusion
Stable Diffusion 3 / Flux Encode → 128²×16 latents (more channels = more headroom)
Sora, Veo (video) Spatiotemporal VAE; compress video to a 4D latent grid
Stable Audio, MusicGen Encodec / SoundStream / DAC = audio VAEs
Whisper-style ASR (Chapter 14) No VAE; spectrogram is the encoding

The VAE is a separately trained, frozen component. You almost never retrain it. The image-scale VAEs from Stable Diffusion (the sd-vae-ft-ema and sdxl-vae-fp16-fix checkpoints) are reused across many downstream models, because retraining a VAE is a year of careful work and the existing ones are good enough.

Production gotcha. The original SD 1.x VAE silently produces NaNs at 1024² when cast to fp16. SDXL ships a madebyollin/sdxl-vae-fp16-fix checkpoint that solved this. Always prefer the fp16-fix variant or use bf16 throughout. This is the kind of bug that costs you a weekend of debugging if you don’t know it exists.

GANs: sharp, narrow, and immortal as a component

Goodfellow’s 2014 paper proposed something that, in retrospect, shouldn’t have worked. Skip density entirely. Train two networks: a generator G(z)G(z)G(z) that maps random noise to fake samples, and a discriminator D(x)D(x)D(x) that classifies real vs fake. They play a minimax game:

minGmaxDEx∼real[logD(x)]+Ez∼N(0,I)[log(1−D(G(z)))]\min_G \max_D \mathbb{E}_{x \sim \text{real}}[\log D(x)] + \mathbb{E}_{z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)}[\log(1 - D(G(z)))] ​G​min​​​D​max​​E​x∼real​​[logD(x)]+E​z∼N(0,I)​​[log(1−D(G(z)))]

If both networks converge, GGG has learned the data distribution without ever explicitly modeling logp(x)\log p(x)logp(x). The discriminator’s gradient becomes the generator’s training signal, and that signal updates as the generator improves a moving target the generator chases until the discriminator can’t tell real from fake any better than chance.

In practice it almost always doesn’t converge. The literature on “things that can go wrong with a GAN” is its own subfield. Mode collapse: the generator finds one mode the discriminator can’t classify and mints it forever. Vanishing gradient: the discriminator gets too good too fast and the generator’s loss saturates. Training instability: any combination of learning rates, batch sizes, weight initializations, and noise levels can make a GAN that worked yesterday completely diverge today.

The history of GAN papers from 2014 to 2021 is a history of architectural and loss-function tricks that progressively made them stable enough to use:

Year What it added
2015 DCGAN: convolutions, batch norm, LeakyReLU first stable architecture
2017 WGAN-GP: replace cross-entropy with Wasserstein distance + gradient penalty
2017 Spectral norm: bound the discriminator’s Lipschitz constant
2018 Progressive GAN: train low-resolution first, add layers first megapixel results
2019 StyleGAN / StyleGAN2: mapping network + AdaIN best faces in the field
2021 StyleGAN3: alias-free, translation-equivariant still the face gold standard

By 2021, GANs could produce 1024×1024 faces good enough to fool most people. Then diffusion arrived, and within two years GANs lost most of the ground they’d gained. Open-domain text-to-image, video, audio, 3D diffusion or flow matching everywhere.

But GANs didn’t die. They have one property neither diffusion nor flow matching has: one-shot inference. A trained GAN’s generator is a single forward pass. No iterative denoising, no schedule, no KV cache. For narrow domains where you don’t need open-ended prompting (the canonical example is faces), a StyleGAN3 generates a 1024×1024 photoreal portrait in tens of milliseconds on a consumer GPU. Diffusion at the same resolution and quality takes seconds.

This speed advantage is why GANs survive as a component in the 2026 stack, not as a standalone generator. Adversarial distillation is the dominant fast-text-to-image technique: train a one-step or few-step student to match a slow diffusion teacher’s output distribution, using a GAN-style discriminator as part of the training loss. SDXL-Turbo, SD3-Turbo, LCM, ADD all of them use adversarial losses to compress 50-step diffusion models into 1-4 steps. The minimax game lives on as a training-time knob for turning slow generators into fast ones.

GAN discriminators also show up as perceptual losses inside diffusion training, where they push the generator toward outputs that are not just close to the target in pixel space but plausibly real-looking in feature space. The original 2014 idea let an adversary tell you what real looks like turns out to be useful even after the adversarial-as-primary-loss approach has been outclassed.

Diffusion: how denoising became the universal generative substrate

In 2015 Sohl-Dickstein and colleagues proposed an idea that didn’t take off for five years. Define a Markov chain that gradually adds Gaussian noise to data over many small steps, until the data is indistinguishable from pure noise. Train a neural network to reverse the chain to denoise step by step. At inference, start from pure noise and run the reverse chain to produce a sample. The math worked but the samples were mediocre.

Ho, Jain, and Abbeel’s 2020 paper Denoising Diffusion Probabilistic Models (DDPM) changed two things. They simplified the loss to a clean MSE on predicted noise, and they trained the resulting model for long enough to actually compete with GANs. Two years later, Stable Diffusion was the most-downloaded generative model in history and “diffusion” had become the substrate beneath nearly every image, video, and audio system.

The forward process

Define a fixed Markov chain that adds Gaussian noise in TTT small steps:

q(xt∣xt−1)=N(1−βt⋅xt−1,βt⋅I)q(x_t \mid x_{t-1}) = \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{1 - \beta_t} \cdot x_{t-1}, \beta_t \cdot I) q(x​t​​∣x​t−1​​)=N(√​1−β​t​​​​​⋅x​t−1​​,β​t​​⋅I)

with a small noise schedule βt\beta_tβ​t​​ (the original paper uses linear from 10−410^{-4}10​−4​​ to 0.020.020.02 over T=1000T = 1000T=1000 steps). The brilliant property is that the cumulative version has a closed form:

q(xt∣x0)=N(α¯t⋅x0,(1−α¯t)⋅I),α¯t=∏s=1t(1−βs)q(x_t \mid x_0) = \mathcal{N}(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} \cdot x_0, (1 - \bar{\alpha}_t) \cdot I), \quad \bar{\alpha}_t = \prod_{s=1}^{t} (1 - \beta_s) q(x​t​​∣x​0​​)=N(√​​α​¯​​​t​​​​​⋅x​0​​,(1−​α​¯​​​t​​)⋅I),​α​¯​​​t​​=​s=1​∏​t​​(1−β​s​​)

You can sample xtx_tx​t​​ from x0x_0x​0​​ in one shot by drawing a Gaussian noise vector ε\varepsilonε and computing

xt=α¯t⋅x0+1−α¯t⋅εx_t = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} \cdot x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \cdot \varepsilon x​t​​=√​​α​¯​​​t​​​​​⋅x​0​​+√​1−​α​¯​​​t​​​​​⋅ε

This is the most consequential equation in modern image generation. It says: noise schedules don’t have to be simulated. You can jump to any noise level instantly during training.

The reverse process

Train a neural network εθ(xt,t)\varepsilon_\theta(x_t, t)ε​θ​​(x​t​​,t) to predict the noise that was added at step ttt. Given that, denoise:

xt−1=1αt(xt−βt1−α¯tεθ(xt,t))+σt⋅zx_{t-1} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\alpha_t}}\left(x_t - \frac{\beta_t}{\sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t}} \varepsilon_\theta(x_t, t)\right) + \sigma_t \cdot z x​t−1​​=​√​α​t​​​​​​​1​​(x​t​​−​√​1−​α​¯​​​t​​​​​​​β​t​​​​ε​θ​​(x​t​​,t))+σ​t​​⋅z

where z∼N(0,I)z \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)z∼N(0,I) and σt\sigma_tσ​t​​ is either βt\sqrt{\beta_t}√​β​t​​​​​ or a learned per-step variance. The expression looks ugly but it’s just algebra solving for xt−1x_{t-1}x​t−1​​ given the posterior q(xt−1∣xt,x0)q(x_{t-1} \mid x_t, x_0)q(x​t−1​​∣x​t​​,x​0​​) and substituting the noise-predicted estimate of x0x_0x​0​​.

The training loss is one line

The full ELBO derivation has a KL term per timestep. With DDPM’s parameterization, all of those KL terms simplify to MSE on noise prediction with specific coefficients. Ho et al. dropped the coefficients (calling it the “simple” loss) and quality improved:

Lsimple=Ex0,t,ε[∥ε−εθ(α¯t⋅x0+1−α¯t⋅ε,t)∥2]\mathcal{L}_\text{simple} = \mathbb{E}_{x_0, t, \varepsilon} \left[ \left\| \varepsilon - \varepsilon_\theta\left(\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t} \cdot x_0 + \sqrt{1 - \bar{\alpha}_t} \cdot \varepsilon, t\right) \right\|^2 \right] L​simple​​=E​x​0​​,t,ε​​[​∥​∥​​ε−ε​θ​​(√​​α​¯​​​t​​​​​⋅x​0​​+√​1−​α​¯​​​t​​​​​⋅ε,t)​∥​∥​​​2​​]

In code:

def train_step(x0, model, alpha_bars, T):
    t = torch.randint(0, T, (x0.size(0),))
    eps = torch.randn_like(x0)
    a_bar = alpha_bars[t].view(-1, 1, 1, 1)
    x_t = a_bar.sqrt() * x0 + (1 - a_bar).sqrt() * eps
    eps_hat = model(x_t, t)
    return F.mse_loss(eps_hat, eps)

Sample data, sample a timestep, sample noise, jump to the noisy version, predict the noise, MSE. One stable loss, no minimax, no KL, no reparameterization. This is why diffusion won the training loop is dramatically more reliable than GANs’ minimax game and the samples are dramatically better than VAEs’ blurry mean.

Three intuitions for why this works

  1. Denoising is easy at every scale. At t=Tt = Tt=T, the input is pure noise; the optimal output is the data mean. At t=0t = 0t=0, the input is barely noisy; the optimal output is nearly the input. At intermediate ttt, the problem is hard but the network gets gradients from every noise level into the same weights.
  2. It’s score matching in disguise. Vincent (2011) proved that predicting the noise is mathematically equivalent to estimating ∇xlogq(xt∣x0)\nabla_x \log q(x_t \mid x_0)∇​x​​logq(x​t​​∣x​0​​) the score of the noised distribution. The reverse process is a guided random walk that climbs the density gradient toward high-probability regions.
  3. The implicit ELBO is loose enough to train cleanly. DDPM optimizes a re-weighted version of the variational lower bound. The bound isn’t tight, but it’s a single loss function with no adversarial dynamics, and gradients are well-conditioned.

Pitfalls that bite in production

Schedule choice matters. The original linear β\betaβ schedule is fine for low-resolution data but undertrains the high-noise regime at higher resolutions. Cosine schedule (Nichol and Dhariwal, 2021) is the modern default. The Karras et al. (2022) EDM framework provides a unified formulation that subsumes most schedules.

ε\varepsilonε-prediction vs vvv-prediction. ε\varepsilonε-prediction has poor signal-to-noise at very small or very large ttt. vvv-prediction (where v=αt⋅ε−σt⋅xv = \alpha_t \cdot \varepsilon - \sigma_t \cdot xv=α​t​​⋅ε−σ​t​​⋅x) is more stable across the schedule. SDXL, SD3, and Flux all use vvv-prediction. If a recipe says “switch to v-prediction and your training stabilizes,” it’s probably right.

1000 steps is too many in production. Nobody serves 1000-step DDPM. The whole DDIM/DPM-Solver/distillation literature exists because you need to get from 1000 steps to 4-50, and we cover that in the inference section below.

Latent diffusion: the recipe that powers Stable Diffusion

Pixel-space diffusion at 512×512 means the U-Net runs on tensors of shape (B,3,512,512)(B, 3, 512, 512)(B,3,512,512). Each sampling step is around 100 GFLOPs for a 500M-parameter network. Fifty steps is 5 TFLOPs per image. Train on a billion images and the compute bill is absurd.

Most of those FLOPs go into pushing perceptually unimportant details the high-frequency texture that a lossy VAE can compress away. Rombach et al. (2022) proposed: train a VAE once (the first stage), freeze it, and run diffusion entirely in the VAE’s latent space (the second stage). For Stable Diffusion 1.5, the VAE compresses 512×512×3512 \times 512 \times 3512×512×3 pixels into 64×64×464 \times 64 \times 464×64×4 latents a 16× reduction in spatial area, an 8× reduction in channels, and an effective 64× reduction in compute per diffusion step. The U-Net is exactly the same size; it’s just operating on a much smaller tensor.

This is the Stable Diffusion recipe. The diffusion loss doesn’t change. The only change is the data domain.

def train_step(x_pixel, vae, unet, alpha_bars, T):
    with torch.no_grad():
        z0 = vae.encode(x_pixel).sample() * 0.18215
    t = torch.randint(0, T, (z0.size(0),))
    eps = torch.randn_like(z0)
    a_bar = alpha_bars[t].view(-1, 1, 1, 1)
    z_t = a_bar.sqrt() * z0 + (1 - a_bar).sqrt() * eps
    eps_hat = unet(z_t, t, text_embeddings)
    return F.mse_loss(eps_hat, eps)

The 0.18215 scaling factor is a reminder that the VAE’s latent variance is not naturally one. Forgetting to scale produces a U-Net trained on latents with wildly wrong variance, and your model never learns anything. Every checkpoint ships its own scaling factor. Always check the model card.

Text conditioning via cross-attention

Stable Diffusion adds two components on top of the latent diffusion recipe: a frozen text encoder (CLIP for SD 1.x and 2.x, plus T5-XXL for SD3 and Flux) and cross-attention layers in the U-Net. Each U-Net block, after self-attention, runs a cross-attention where the queries come from the image-latent features and the keys and values come from the text-encoder output. The text tokens are the only path through which prompts influence the image.

class CrossAttention(nn.Module):
    def forward(self, x, text_tokens):
        q = self.to_q(x)
        k = self.to_k(text_tokens)
        v = self.to_v(text_tokens)
        out = F.scaled_dot_product_attention(q, k, v)
        return self.to_out(out)

That’s the text-conditioning pipe. The reason prompt fidelity got dramatically better between SD 1.x (CLIP-only) and SD3 (T5-XXL with 128+ tokens) is that T5 produces much richer text embeddings, and cross-attention can use the additional information. SD3’s MMDiT goes further: instead of cross-attention as a side-channel, it runs joint attention with both image latent tokens and text tokens in the same sequence, letting them mix freely at every layer.

Classifier-free guidance

A trained conditional diffusion model produces a noise estimate εθ(xt,t,c)\varepsilon_\theta(x_t, t, c)ε​θ​​(x​t​​,t,c) where ccc is the conditioning (text, class label, image). At inference, you can amplify the conditioning’s influence by computing both a conditional and an unconditional estimate, then extrapolating:

εcfg=(1+w)⋅εθ(xt,t,c)−w⋅εθ(xt,t,∅)\varepsilon_\text{cfg} = (1 + w) \cdot \varepsilon_\theta(x_t, t, c) - w \cdot \varepsilon_\theta(x_t, t, \emptyset) ε​cfg​​=(1+w)⋅ε​θ​​(x​t​​,t,c)−w⋅ε​θ​​(x​t​​,t,∅)

with w∈[3,7]w \in [3, 7]w∈[3,7] a typical range. The technique is called classifier-free guidance (CFG) and it’s how every modern diffusion sampler trades sample diversity for prompt adherence. Higher www means stronger prompt fidelity but lower diversity and, past about w=10w = 10w=10, oversaturated and unnatural-looking outputs. The sweet spot for most pipelines is w=5w = 5w=5 to 777.

To make this work, the model must be trained with the conditioning randomly dropped out about 10% of the time, replaced with a null embedding. That’s the only training-time change. CFG is almost free to add and is the single biggest sample-quality lever in production diffusion stacks.

The lineage from SD 1.5 to Flux

Model Year Backbone Latent shape Text encoder Params
SD 1.5 2022 U-Net 64×64×4 CLIP-L (77 tokens) 860M
SDXL 2023 U-Net + refiner 128×128×4 CLIP-L + OpenCLIP-G 2.6B + 6.6B
SD3 2024 MMDiT 128×128×16 T5-XXL + CLIP-L + CLIP-G 2-8B
Flux.1-dev 2024 MMDiT 128×128×16 T5-XXL + CLIP-L 12B
Flux.1-schnell 2024 MMDiT distilled 128×128×16 T5-XXL + CLIP-L 12B, 1-4 step

The trend is clear. Replace U-Net with DiT (a Diffusion Transformer over latent patches same patch trick as ViT in Chapter 14). Scale the text encoder. Increase latent channels. Distill for fast inference. The architectural details vary by lab but the recipe is the same: VAE encoder, transformer over latents, cross-attention or joint attention to text, VAE decoder.

Flow matching: the straight-line answer

DDPM’s reverse process traces a curved path through high-dimensional space from noise to data. You can solve the underlying ODE in 20-50 steps with a good sampler (DDIM, DPM-Solver, UniPC), and you can distill it down to 1-4 steps with consistency models or adversarial distillation. But what if the path were straight to begin with? Then a single Euler step would work.

That’s the flow matching idea, introduced by Lipman et al. (2023) and developed in parallel by Liu et al. (2022) under the name rectified flow.

Straight-line interpolation

Define a straight-line interpolation from data to noise:

xt=(1−t)⋅x0+t⋅x1,t∈[0,1]x_t = (1 - t) \cdot x_0 + t \cdot x_1, \quad t \in [0, 1] x​t​​=(1−t)⋅x​0​​+t⋅x​1​​,t∈[0,1]

where x0∼pdatax_0 \sim p_\text{data}x​0​​∼p​data​​ and x1∼N(0,I)x_1 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, I)x​1​​∼N(0,I). The time derivative along this line is constant:

dxtdt=x1−x0\frac{dx_t}{dt} = x_1 - x_0 ​dt​​dx​t​​​​=x​1​​−x​0​​

Train a neural vector field vθ(xt,t)v_\theta(x_t, t)v​θ​​(x​t​​,t) to match this derivative:

L=Ex0,x1,t∥vθ(xt,t)−(x1−x0)∥2\mathcal{L} = \mathbb{E}_{x_0, x_1, t} \left\| v_\theta(x_t, t) - (x_1 - x_0) \right\|^2 L=E​x​0​​,x​1​​,t​​∥v​θ​​(x​t​​,t)−(x​1​​−x​0​​)∥​2​​

That’s the conditional flow matching loss. Sample (x0,x1,t)(x_0, x_1, t)(x​0​​,x​1​​,t), compute xtx_tx​t​​ in closed form, regress the network output against x1−x0x_1 - x_0x​1​​−x​0​​. No ODE simulation during training.

At inference, integrate the vector field backwards in time:

def sample(net, num_steps):
    x = torch.randn(batch_size, *shape)
    for i in range(num_steps):
        t = 1.0 - i / num_steps
        x = x - (1.0 / num_steps) * net(x, t)
    return x

Start from noise, Euler-step toward data, done.

Why everyone switched

Three reasons flow matching became the 2024-2026 default for image, video, and audio generation:

  1. Simulation-free training. No ODE unrolling, no expensive simulation step. Identical training compute per step as DDPM.
  2. Better loss geometry. Straight paths have consistent signal-to-noise across the full ttt schedule, whereas DDPM’s ε\varepsilonε-loss has bad SNR at the edges. The model learns more uniformly.
  3. Faster inference. With straight paths, fewer sampling steps suffice to produce the same quality. SDXL-Turbo and SD3-Turbo and Flux-schnell all distill flow-matched bases to 1-4 step inference at minimal quality loss.

The relationship to DDPM is not as different as the names suggest flow matching with a Gaussian-conditional path is algebraically equivalent to diffusion under a specific noise schedule. What flow matching added was conceptual clarity (the target is just a velocity), a cleaner loss, and the license to experiment with non-Gaussian interpolants. Stable Diffusion 3, Flux, AudioCraft 2, and Stable Audio 2.5 all use flow matching variants.

Rectified flow and the reflow procedure

Liu’s rectified flow goes one step further. The straight-line training target produces paths that are not actually straight, because many x0x_0x​0​​s map to the same x1x_1x​1​​ under the random pairing during training. Rectified flow’s reflow procedure:

  1. Train the first flow v1v_1v​1​​ on random pairings.
  2. Sample new pairs (x1,x0)(x_1, x_0)(x​1​​,x​0​​) by integrating v1v_1v​1​​ from x1x_1x​1​​ to its landing x0x_0x​0​​.
  3. Train v2v_2v​2​​ on these “ODE-matched” pairs. Because the pairs are now genuinely straightenable, the learned paths are flatter.
  4. Repeat once more if you want.

Two reflow iterations get you to near-linear paths and enable 2-4 step inference at SDXL-Turbo quality. The reflow-distilled Flux.1-schnell runs in 1.2 seconds at 1024² on an L4 GPU; the un-distilled Flux.1-dev runs in 15 seconds on the same hardware for similar quality. That’s the production case for flow matching: it’s not just a cleaner training objective, it’s the substrate that makes one-step inference feasible.

Conditioning: ControlNet, LoRA, IP-Adapter

Text alone is a clumsy control signal. A prompt like “a woman in a red dress walking a dog on a busy street” pins down maybe ten percent of the actual image pose, perspective, composition, the dog’s breed, the dress’s silhouette, whether it’s day or night, what culture’s busy street, all left to the model’s priors. To ship production image pipelines you need finer-grained control. Three tools dominate the 2026 toolkit.

ControlNet: spatial structure

Zhang et al. (2023). Take a frozen pretrained diffusion model. Clone the encoder half of its U-Net. Attach the clone to accept an extra conditioning input a depth map, edge map, pose skeleton, scribble, segmentation mask. Connect the clone back to the original decoder via zero-convolution skip connections (1×1 convolutions initialized to zero, so the clone starts as a no-op). Train on triples of (prompt, condition, image) with the standard diffusion loss.

SD U-Net decoder: ... ← orig_enc_features + zero_conv(controlnet_enc(condition))

The zero-conv initialization is the trick that makes ControlNet trainable without breaking the base model. At step zero the conditioning input has no effect the base model is unchanged. As training proceeds, the zero-convs learn small deltas that nudge the base toward the conditioning. The base never has to be retrained; one ControlNet ships per modality, around 70-360 MB for SD 1.5 / SDXL.

You can compose ControlNets at inference: load the depth ControlNet at weight 0.7, the pose ControlNet at weight 0.5, and the model honors both signals. Sum of weights around 1.0 is a safe default; pushing past 1.5 produces over-controlled, broken outputs.

LoRA: parameter-efficient fine-tuning

Hu et al. (2021). For any linear layer W∈Rd×dW \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}W∈R​d×d​​ in the model, freeze it and add a low-rank delta:

W′=W+α⋅BA,A∈Rr×d,B∈Rd×rW' = W + \alpha \cdot B A, \quad A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d}, B \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times r} W​′​​=W+α⋅BA,A∈R​r×d​​,B∈R​d×r​​

with r≪dr \ll dr≪d. Rank 4-16 is standard for attention layers, 64-128 for heavy personalization runs. The new parameter count is 2dr2 d r2dr instead of d2d^2d​2​​. For SDXL’s 640-dimensional attention with r=16r = 16r=16, that’s 20K trainable parameters per layer instead of 410K roughly a 20× reduction. Across the whole model: a LoRA is typically 20-200 MB versus the base’s 5 GB.

LoRA was originally designed for LLMs (we’ll see it again in Chapter 22 for fine-tuning) but ports cleanly to diffusion. At inference you can scale the delta with α\alphaα, swap LoRAs in and out, and stack multiple LoRAs additively. A typical agency pipeline runs a base SDXL or Flux checkpoint with two to five LoRAs loaded one for style, one for a specific subject, one for art-direction tweaks plus one or two ControlNets and an IP-Adapter.

IP-Adapter: reference images

Ye et al. (2023). A small adapter that accepts an image as conditioning alongside text. It uses a frozen CLIP image encoder to produce image tokens, then injects them into the model’s cross-attention alongside the text tokens. About 20 MB per base model.

The use case is “generate an image in the style of this reference” or “include this person’s face” without training a full LoRA. You point IP-Adapter at a reference image, type a prompt that uses the reference’s style, and the model blends both signals. For e-commerce, advertising, and creative workflows where you need to honor a brand’s visual identity, IP-Adapter is faster and cheaper than the LoRA route.

How to think about the toolkit

Tool What it controls Size When to use
ControlNet Spatial structure (pose, depth, edges) 70-360 MB Exact layout, composition
LoRA Style, subject, concept 20-200 MB Personalization, art direction
IP-Adapter Style/subject from reference image 20 MB No text can describe the look
DreamBooth Full subject fine-tune 2-5 GB Strong identity, high compute budget

Rule of thumb: ControlNet for spatial control, LoRA for semantic control, IP-Adapter for visual reference that can’t be described in words. Combine them. The 2026 production image pipeline is a base model + a stack of these adapters, hot-swapped per request.

Across modalities: what changes, what doesn’t

The diffusion-and-flow-matching backbone we just covered for images generalizes to every modality with surprisingly few changes. The neural network changes (you need an architecture that fits the data shape), the data domain changes (pixels vs spectrograms vs point clouds), and the conditioning changes. The training loss is essentially identical.

Video. Treat video as a 4D tensor (T,H,W,C)(T, H, W, C)(T,H,W,C) instead of a 3D image (H,W,C)(H, W, C)(H,W,C). Add temporal attention to the network so it can model motion. Sora, Veo, Kling, Stable Video Diffusion all use a Diffusion Transformer over spatiotemporal patches the same DiT architecture as SD3, just operating on patches that span both space and time. Latent video diffusion (Sora-style) compresses with a spatiotemporal VAE first, then runs flow matching in the compressed latent.

Audio. Either model the waveform directly with a 1D U-Net (DiffWave, WaveGrad), or compress with a neural audio codec (Encodec, SoundStream, DAC) into discrete tokens or continuous latents and run diffusion in that space (Stable Audio 2.5, AudioCraft 2, MusicGen). The codec-then-diffusion pattern dominates because direct waveform diffusion is computationally brutal 24 kHz audio is nearly a million samples per minute.

3D. Two flavors. Reconstruction: 3D Gaussian Splatting fits an explicit scene representation from a few images, no diffusion involved. Generation: text-to-3D models like DreamFusion or stable-3D use a diffusion model trained on 2D images as a prior to optimize a NeRF or Gaussian splat representation, with a technique called Score Distillation Sampling. This is one of the few places in 2026 where diffusion is used as a guide rather than a sampler.

Molecules and proteins. Equivariant diffusion. The same training loss, but the network is structured to respect rotational and translational symmetries of 3D molecular structures. RFdiffusion, EDM, AlphaFold 3’s diffusion module same idea, just hard-wired symmetry.

The pattern across modalities is: pick or train an autoencoder for the modality, run diffusion or flow matching in the latent space, condition on text or class or whatever cross-modal input you have. The modality-specific work is in the autoencoder and the conditioning. The generative core is identical to what we built for images.

Inference economics: getting from 1000 steps to 1

Production diffusion serving is mostly about reducing the number of sampling steps. The original DDPM ran 1000 steps. Nobody ships that. Three strategies, often combined:

  1. Faster samplers, same model. DDIM (Song, Meng, Ermon, 2021) reformulates the reverse process as a deterministic ODE you can solve in 20-50 steps with no quality loss. DPM-Solver and DPM-Solver++ (Lu et al., 2022) push that down to 10-20 steps using higher-order ODE solvers. UniPC (Zhao et al., 2023) does 8-16 steps. These are drop-in replacements for the reverse loop the trained εθ\varepsilon_\thetaε​θ​​ weights are untouched.
  2. Distillation. Train a student to match the teacher in fewer steps. Progressive Distillation (Salimans and Ho, 2022) repeatedly halves the step count. Consistency Models (Song et al., 2023) train the student to map any noisy intermediate directly to the final sample. Latent Consistency Models (LCM), SDXL-Turbo, SD3-Turbo, and Flux-schnell all use distillation variants. End state: 1-4 step sampling.
  3. Caching and compilation. torch.compile(unet, mode="reduce-overhead"), FlashAttention via SDPA, bf16 weights, TensorRT-LLM diffusion backends. Cuts per-step latency by another 1.5-3×. Stacks with the first two.

A latency budget for a production diffusion server has two terms:

latency=num\_steps×step\_cost+VAE\_decode\text{latency} = \text{num\_steps} \times \text{step\_cost} + \text{VAE\_decode} latency=num\_steps×step\_cost+VAE\_decode

Throughput is the inverse, scaled by batch size. TTFT is small (one step). The full response time is what users feel. Whenever you read “X is faster than diffusion” in a paper abstract in 2026, it’s almost always either fewer steps at the same step cost (a sampler win or a distillation win) or the same steps at lower step cost (a quantization or compilation win). The math is unforgiving.

Variant Steps Latency at 1024² on L4
Flux.1-dev 50 ~15 s
SDXL-base 30 ~4 s
Flux.1-schnell 4 ~1.2 s
SDXL-Lightning 2-step 2 ~0.3 s

The 2026 production rule for fast text-to-image: flow-matched base + distillation. SD3-Turbo (SD3 + flow + distillation), Flux-schnell (Flux-dev + rectified-flow straightening), CogView-4-Flash, the various “Lightning” and “LCM” derivatives. Pure diffusion bases live on as legacy checkpoints.

Evaluation: every metric is gameable

A generative model’s quality is judged on sample quality and conditioning adherence, neither of which has a closed-form measure. You generate ten thousand samples, something assigns them numbers, you trust the numbers across model families and resolutions and architectures. Three metrics survived the 2014-2026 gauntlet, and each has known failure modes that adversarial researchers exploit.

FID sample quality via Inception features

Heusel et al. (2017). Compute Inception-v3 features for NNN real images and NNN generated. Fit a Gaussian to each pool. Compute the Fréchet distance between the two Gaussians:

FID=∥μr−μg∥2+Tr(Σr+Σg−2(ΣrΣg)1/2)\text{FID} = \|\mu_r - \mu_g\|^2 + \text{Tr}(\Sigma_r + \Sigma_g - 2(\Sigma_r \Sigma_g)^{1/2}) FID=∥μ​r​​−μ​g​​∥​2​​+Tr(Σ​r​​+Σ​g​​−2(Σ​r​​Σ​g​​)​1/2​​)

Lower means more similar feature distributions. The metric is widely cited and widely gamed.

Failure modes. FID is biased on small N covariance estimates are unstable below 10,000 samples, leading to falsely low scores. It depends on Inception-v3 a network trained on ImageNet, which means FID is meaningless on domains far from ImageNet (faces, art, scientific imagery). Overfitting to the Inception prior lowers FID without improving visual quality. CMMD (Jayasumana et al., 2024) replaces Inception features with CLIP features and Fréchet distance with maximum mean discrepancy, fixing several of these issues.

If you see a paper reporting FID on N = 1000, the result is meaningless. Always demand N≥10,000N \geq 10{,}000N≥10,000.

CLIP score prompt adherence

Cosine similarity between a generated image’s CLIP image embedding and the prompt’s CLIP text embedding. Average across thousands of generated images, get a scalar comparable between models.

Failure modes. CLIP itself has compositional weaknesses “a red cube on a blue sphere” often confuses it, so models can score well on CLIP without actually following structured prompts. Short prompts inflate the score mechanically because they have more high-similarity matches in the wild. Including filler like “high quality, 4k, masterpiece” boosts CLIP score without improving image-text binding. The metric is best as one of several, not on its own.

Human preference the ground truth

Generate with model A and model B on the same prompt set. Show pairs to humans (or to a strong LLM judge), have them pick the better image, aggregate to an Elo-style rating. Benchmarks like PartiPrompts (1,600 diverse prompts), HPSv2 (107K human annotations), and ImageReward (137K preference pairs) standardize the prompt sets. Public arenas like the Hugging Face image arena run this continuously across hundreds of models.

Failure modes. Crowdworker preferences differ from expert preferences Prolific or MTurk annotators skew younger and more tech-friendly, which biases preferences toward certain aesthetics. LLM-judge reward hacking GPT-4-class judges can be fooled by aesthetically pleasing but factually wrong outputs. Prompt distribution choice picking your own benchmark prompts can favor the model you’re trying to promote.

The right answer is to triangulate. A production eval report includes FID on 10-30K samples, CLIP score or CMMD on the same samples versus their prompts, win rate in a blinded arena versus the previous model, and a qualitative failure-mode analysis on 50-500 hand-flagged outputs covering known weak points (hand anatomy, text rendering, consistent object count). Any single metric is a lie. Three corroborating metrics plus qualitative review is a claim.

Sharp edges across all the families

Training distribution shapes everything. A diffusion model trained on LAION-5B (mostly photographic, mostly English-captioned) does well on photorealistic prompts and badly on hand-drawn art. A model trained on stock photography produces an unmistakably “stock-photography” aesthetic. This is not a bug you fix with prompt engineering it’s a property of the training distribution and shifting it requires retraining or fine-tuning.

The conditioning is where the safety problems live. Generative models inherit every bias and risk in their training data. CSAM filtering, copyrighted-character generation, deepfakes, NSFW outputs none of these are model-internal failures, they’re consequences of the training data plus the prompt the user sent. Production deployments add safety classifiers on inputs and outputs (Chapters 18 and 35 dig into this), watermarking on outputs, and rate-limited prompt categories. We cover these systems in Volume 3.

Distillation collapses diversity. A 50-step diffusion model produces a wider distribution of plausible outputs for the same prompt than its 4-step distilled student. Distillation trades sample diversity for inference speed. For applications where you want many varied outputs from one prompt (concept art, ideation), use the un-distilled base. For applications where you want a single canonical output fast (product photography, real-time editing), use the distilled student.

Reward hacking on metrics is universal. Every benchmark in the literature has been hacked by subsequent papers. FID hacked by overfitting Inception priors. CLIP score hacked by quality-prompt stuffing. HPSv2 hacked by training on its training set. Be skeptical of monotonic improvements on a single metric. Look at samples. Look at failure modes. Read the paper’s limitations section if it has one and don’t trust it if it doesn’t.

The architecture changes faster than the principles. Between 2020 and 2026 we’ve gone from U-Nets to DiTs, from ε\varepsilonε-prediction to vvv-prediction to flow matching, from CLIP text encoders to T5-XXL, from 50-step samplers to 1-step distillation. Anything you write down today about which architecture is best will be at least partially wrong by 2027. The principles VAE for compression, denoising as the training signal, classifier-free guidance for conditioning are durable. The implementations rotate.

What you’d actually use

For most of what you’ll build, the following stack is the boring default:

Need Default
Text-to-image, hosted OpenAI Image API, Anthropic Claude image generation, Google Imagen
Text-to-image, open weights Flux.1-dev for quality, Flux.1-schnell for speed
Local inference, consumer hardware Quantized Flux via diffusers + bitsandbytes
Production stack HuggingFace Diffusers + ComfyUI for workflow orchestration
Fine-tuning LoRA via PEFT or DreamBooth via diffusers
Spatial control ControlNet + IP-Adapter via diffusers
Video generation, hosted Veo 2, Runway Gen-4, Kling, Sora
Speech generation XTTS-v2, Kokoro for open weights; ElevenLabs hosted
Music Stable Audio, MusicGen
3D from images 3D Gaussian Splatting via gsplat
Evaluation FID via clean-fid, CLIP score via OpenCLIP, ImageReward for HPSv2-style scoring

For workflow orchestration specifically, ComfyUI has become the de facto standard for serious image-generation pipelines. It’s a node-based graph editor over diffusers internals, and it’s the tool of choice for agencies and serious hobbyists alike. If you’re building anything beyond a single API call, you’ll end up reading or writing ComfyUI workflows.

Don’t roll your own diffusion infrastructure. Don’t write your own samplers. Don’t reimplement classifier-free guidance. The diffusers library handles all of it, the maintainers know the failure modes you don’t, and the ecosystem of pretrained components VAEs, ControlNets, LoRAs, IP-Adapters assumes you’re using it.

Where this goes next

Chapter 16 is reinforcement learning. We need it for two things: RLHF, which is how you align an LLM after pretraining (Chapter 18); and the broader policy-optimization toolkit that underlies how agents learn to act in environments (Chapter 27 on autonomous systems).

Chapter 17 picks up the pretraining thread for LLMs specifically distributed training across thousands of GPUs, the data pipeline, the infrastructure to actually train a model the size of those we keep talking about. Chapter 18 covers post-training: SFT, RLHF, DPO, the alignment toolkit. Together they’re how you produce a Claude or a Llama from raw transformer architecture.

Chapter 24 is about multimodal AI, where most of this chapter’s machinery shows up bolted onto language models. CLIP, ViT-as-encoder, BLIP, LLaVA, Flamingo, the early-fusion-vs-late-fusion architectural debate, omni-modal models, vision-language-action models for robotics. The diffusion and flow-matching backbones we built here are the generators in those systems. The transformer is the connective tissue.

You now know what every modern image, video, and audio model is doing under the hood. The substrate is diffusion or flow matching, conditioned via cross-attention or joint attention, accelerated by distillation, served behind a stack of ControlNets and LoRAs. Every paper you read for the next two years will fit somewhere in that frame. Onward.

Chapter 16 Reinforcement Learning

For most of its history, reinforcement learning was the field that produced agents which played Atari, walked simulated robots across uneven terrain, and beat humans at Go. Cool demos. Mostly irrelevant to the AI engineer shipping products. RL was where you went if you wanted to publish a paper about gridworld convergence proofs, not where you went to fix anything in production.

Then the alignment people noticed something. A pretrained language model is a beautiful next-token predictor and a terrible assistant. It hallucinates facts because that’s what the data taught it. It refuses to refuse, helps with things it shouldn’t, and rambles when it should be terse. None of these are bugs you can fix with more pretraining the loss function rewards completing internet text, not being helpful. To make the model helpful, you need to change what you’re optimizing. Specifically, you need to optimize the model’s behavior over a sequence of tokens against a scalar score that captures “is this response actually good.” That’s a reinforcement-learning problem, not a supervised-learning problem, and the field had two decades of math sitting around waiting to be applied.

In 2022, OpenAI’s InstructGPT paper described a three-stage pipeline supervised fine-tuning, then a learned reward model, then PPO that turned GPT-3 into ChatGPT. Within a year, every frontier lab had implemented some version of it. By 2024, the pure RLHF-with-PPO recipe was being replaced by Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a closed-form variant that skips the explicit RL loop, and by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), a critic-free variant that powers reasoning models like DeepSeek-V3. Today, every frontier LLM has been through some flavor of preference-based RL, and you cannot understand modern alignment without understanding the underlying RL machinery.

This chapter is about that machinery. We’ll start with the framework that makes RL formal the Markov decision process and walk through the core algorithms in the order they were discovered, building up to PPO. Then we’ll bridge to RLHF and the LLM-specific story: how preferences become rewards, why the KL penalty is the load-bearing detail, what reward hacking looks like, and where DPO and GRPO fit. By the end of the chapter you’ll be able to read any RL paper or any alignment recipe and know exactly what shape it is.

The Markov decision process

You’re building a chess bot, an inventory planner, a trading agent, or the PPO loop that fine-tunes a reasoning model. Four different domains, one mathematical object: all four collapse to a Markov decision process.

Supervised learning gives you input-output pairs (x,y)(x, y)(x,y) and asks you to fit a function. Reinforcement learning gives you no labels only a stream of states, the actions you took, and a scalar reward. Did the move win the game? Did the restock decision save money? Did the trade make a profit? Did the token the model just emitted lead to a higher reward at the end of the response? You cannot learn from this stream until you formalize it. What I saw, what I did, what happened next, how good that was each of those has to become an object you can reason about. That formalization is the MDP.

An MDP is five things:

  • A set of states S\mathcal{S}S. Everything the agent needs to make a decision. In gridworld, the cell coordinates. In chess, the board position. In an LLM, the prompt plus all tokens generated so far.
  • A set of actions A\mathcal{A}A. The choices available. Move up/down/left/right. Play a chess move. Emit the next token.
  • A transition distribution P(s′∣s,a)P(s' \mid s, a)P(s​′​​∣s,a). Given current state and action, the probability distribution over the next state. Deterministic in chess (the rules are fixed), stochastic in inventory (demand is random), almost-deterministic in LLM decoding.
  • A reward function R(s,a,s′)R(s, a, s')R(s,a,s​′​​). The scalar signal. Win = +1, loss = -1. Revenue minus cost. The reward model’s score at the end of an LLM response.
  • A discount factor γ∈[0,1)\gamma \in [0, 1)γ∈[0,1). How much future reward matters compared to present reward. γ=0.99\gamma = 0.99γ=0.99 buys a horizon of about 100 steps; γ=0.9\gamma = 0.9γ=0.9 buys about 10. The effective horizon is 1/(1−γ)1 / (1 - \gamma)1/(1−γ).

The defining property is the Markov assumption: the future depends only on the present state. P(st+1∣st,at)=P(st+1∣s0,a0,…,st,at)P(s_{t+1} \mid s_t, a_t) = P(s_{t+1} \mid s_0, a_0, \ldots, s_t, a_t)P(s​t+1​​∣s​t​​,a​t​​)=P(s​t+1​​∣s​0​​,a​0​​,…,s​t​​,a​t​​). If you need the last three observations to make a decision, your “state” is not just the current observation it’s the last three. If your state representation isn’t Markovian, you’re not solving a different problem; you’re solving the same problem with a broken state, and the algorithms in this chapter will fail in subtle ways.

Policies, returns, values

An agent acts according to a policy π(a∣s)\pi(a \mid s)π(a∣s) a conditional distribution over actions given the state. Run the policy in the environment for one episode and you get a trajectory of states, actions, and rewards. The return from time ttt is the discounted sum of future rewards:

Gt=rt+γrt+1+γ2rt+2+⋯G_t = r_t + \gamma r_{t+1} + \gamma^2 r_{t+2} + \cdots G​t​​=r​t​​+γr​t+1​​+γ​2​​r​t+2​​+⋯

Two functions summarize how good a state or action is under a given policy. The value function Vπ(s)V^\pi(s)V​π​​(s) is the expected return starting from sss under π\piπ. The action-value function Qπ(s,a)Q^\pi(s, a)Q​π​​(s,a) is the expected return starting from sss with first action aaa, then following π\piπ. Every RL algorithm in this chapter either estimates one of these, or it estimates the gradient of expected return with respect to policy parameters. Two paths.

The Bellman equations

Both VπV^\piV​π​​ and QπQ^\piQ​π​​ satisfy fixed-point recursions called Bellman equations:

Vπ(s)=Ea∼π(⋅∣s)[r+γVπ(s′)]V^\pi(s) = \mathbb{E}_{a \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s)}\left[r + \gamma V^\pi(s')\right] V​π​​(s)=E​a∼π(⋅∣s)​​[r+γV​π​​(s​′​​)]

Qπ(s,a)=Es′∼P(⋅∣s,a)[r+γEa′∼π(⋅∣s′)Qπ(s′,a′)]Q^\pi(s, a) = \mathbb{E}_{s' \sim P(\cdot \mid s, a)}\left[r + \gamma \mathbb{E}_{a' \sim \pi(\cdot \mid s')} Q^\pi(s', a')\right] Q​π​​(s,a)=E​s​′​​∼P(⋅∣s,a)​​[r+γE​a​′​​∼π(⋅∣s​′​​)​​Q​π​​(s​′​​,a​′​​)]

Both decompose the return into “this step’s reward” plus “discounted value of where you land.” The optimal V∗V^*V​∗​​ and Q∗Q^*Q​∗​​ satisfy the same recursion with the policy replaced by a maximization:

V∗(s)=maxaE[r+γV∗(s′)],Q∗(s,a)=E[r+γmaxa′Q∗(s′,a′)]V^*(s) = \max_a \mathbb{E}\left[r + \gamma V^*(s')\right], \quad Q^*(s, a) = \mathbb{E}\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q^*(s', a')\right] V​∗​​(s)=​a​max​​E[r+γV​∗​​(s​′​​)],Q​∗​​(s,a)=E[r+γ​a​′​​​max​​Q​∗​​(s​′​​,a​′​​)]

This is the most important equation in RL. Every algorithm in this chapter value iteration, Q-learning, DQN, actor-critic, PPO is some way of estimating, sampling, or approximating one of these recursions.

Writing down the MDP before writing code

Before training anything, write down the five tuples explicitly. Most “RL doesn’t work” debugging traces back to an MDP formulation that was broken on paper. Some examples:

Domain State Action Reward γ\gammaγ
Robot locomotion Joint angles + velocities Continuous torques per joint Forward velocity – energy cost 0.99
Game (chess, Go) Board + history Legal move Win = +1, loss = -1 1.0 (finite horizon)
Inventory Stock level + recent demand Order quantity Revenue – holding cost – stockout cost 0.95
RLHF for LLMs Prompt + tokens generated so far Next token Reward model’s score at end of response 1.0 (episode ~hundreds of tokens)
GRPO for reasoning Prompt + partial response Next token Verifier (0/1: did the math/code work?) at end 1.0

You cannot write the training loop until you can write this table. If you find yourself unable to fill in any column, the issue is conceptual, not algorithmic.

Value-based methods: estimating Q∗Q^*Q​∗​​ from samples

The earliest algorithms in RL estimate the Bellman optimal Q-function Q∗Q^*Q​∗​​ directly. If you can find Q∗Q^*Q​∗​​, you can act optimally by always picking argmaxaQ∗(s,a)\arg\max_a Q^*(s, a)argmax​a​​Q​∗​​(s,a). Two questions: how do you estimate Q∗Q^*Q​∗​​ when you don’t know the transition dynamics, and how do you act while you’re estimating?

Temporal difference learning

The pivot on which all of modern value-based RL turns is temporal difference (TD) learning. Monte Carlo methods (the predecessor) wait until an episode ends, compute the actual return GtG_tG​t​​, and use it to update VVV or QQQ. That’s high variance and you have to wait for episodes to terminate. TD learning splits the difference: from a single transition (s,a,r,s′)(s, a, r, s')(s,a,r,s​′​​), form a one-step bootstrapped target r+γV(s′)r + \gamma V(s')r+γV(s​′​​) and nudge V(s)V(s)V(s) toward it.

V(s)←V(s)+α[r+γV(s′)−V(s)]V(s) \leftarrow V(s) + \alpha[r + \gamma V(s') - V(s)] V(s)←V(s)+α[r+γV(s​′​​)−V(s)]

The bracketed quantity is the TD error δ=r+γV(s′)−V(s)\delta = r + \gamma V(s') - V(s)δ=r+γV(s​′​​)−V(s). It’s the bias-variance trade-off in one symbol bootstrapping introduces some bias (because you’re using your own estimate on the right-hand side) but dramatically reduces variance and lets you update online from every transition.

For control (learning to act, not just evaluate a fixed policy), there are two flavors that differ in one symbol:

Q-learning, off-policy:

Q(s,a)←Q(s,a)+α[r+γmaxa′Q(s′,a′)−Q(s,a)]Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha\left[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)\right] Q(s,a)←Q(s,a)+α[r+γ​a​′​​​max​​Q(s​′​​,a​′​​)−Q(s,a)]

SARSA, on-policy:

Q(s,a)←Q(s,a)+α[r+γQ(s′,a′)−Q(s,a)]Q(s, a) \leftarrow Q(s, a) + \alpha\left[r + \gamma Q(s', a') - Q(s, a)\right] Q(s,a)←Q(s,a)+α[r+γQ(s​′​​,a​′​​)−Q(s,a)]

The difference is the next-action term. Q-learning uses maxa′Q(s′,a′)\max_{a'} Q(s', a')max​a​′​​​​Q(s​′​​,a​′​​) the greedy action, regardless of what the agent actually does. SARSA uses Q(s′,a′)Q(s', a')Q(s​′​​,a​′​​) where a′a'a​′​​ is whatever action the agent actually took next. Q-learning learns Q∗Q^*Q​∗​​; SARSA learns QπQ^\piQ​π​​ for the (typically ε\varepsilonε-greedy) policy that’s actually running.

def q_learning(env, episodes, alpha=0.1, gamma=0.99, epsilon=0.1):
    Q = defaultdict(lambda: {a: 0.0 for a in env.actions})
    for _ in range(episodes):
        s = env.reset()
        while True:
            a = epsilon_greedy(Q, s, epsilon)
            s_next, r, done = env.step(s, a)
            target = r + (0 if done else gamma * max(Q[s_next].values()))
            Q[s][a] += alpha * (target - Q[s][a])
            if done:
                break
            s = s_next
    return Q

Twelve lines. That’s all of Q-learning. The max over next-state actions is what makes it off-policy it learns the value of acting greedily even while exploring randomly.

The classic motivating example for the on-policy/off-policy distinction is cliff-walking. Picture a 4×12 grid where the bottom row (except start and goal) is a cliff with reward −100-100−100. Q-learning learns the optimal path along the cliff edge. SARSA learns a safer path one row away from the cliff, because it incorporates the probability of the agent slipping off during ε\varepsilonε-exploration. With ε→0\varepsilon \to 0ε→0, both reach optimal. In practice, when you’ll be exploring at deployment, SARSA’s caution is what you actually want.

From tables to neural networks: DQN

Tabular Q-learning works when the state space is small enough to enumerate. It does not work for Atari, where the state is a 210×160210 \times 160210×160 pixel grid and you’d need 256100,800256^{100{,}800}256​100,800​​ table entries. The fix is to replace the table with a neural network: Qθ(s,a)Q_\theta(s, a)Q​θ​​(s,a) approximated by a CNN.

This breaks Q-learning in two ways. First, the targets r+γmaxa′Qθ(s′,a′)r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q_\theta(s', a')r+γmax​a​′​​​​Q​θ​​(s​′​​,a​′​​) depend on the same θ\thetaθ being trained, which makes the optimization unstable the target moves as the network learns. Second, sequential transitions are highly correlated, which violates the i.i.d. assumption of stochastic gradient descent.

Mnih et al.'s 2015 Deep Q-Network (DQN) paper fixed both:

  1. Target network. Keep a frozen copy θ−\theta^-θ​−​​ of the network, used only for computing targets. Update θ−←θ\theta^- \leftarrow \thetaθ​−​​←θ every few thousand steps.
  2. Replay buffer. Store transitions in a circular buffer; sample minibatches for training. Decorrelates the gradient updates.

With those two tricks, DQN learned to play Atari games from raw pixels at superhuman level. It was the result that made deep reinforcement learning a serious research area. For our purposes, the takeaway is the architectural pattern: a neural network is a function approximator for QQQ, but the optimization needs scaffolding (target networks, replay buffers) that doesn’t appear in the tabular version.

DQN itself isn’t the dominant algorithm in 2026 outside of niche applications policy-gradient methods are easier to scale to continuous actions and to language models but the target-network and replay-buffer patterns show up everywhere. Soft Actor-Critic (SAC), the dominant continuous-control algorithm, uses both. So do twin-Q variants like TD3.

Policy gradient: parameterize the policy directly

Q-learning and DQN parameterize the value function and act by argmaxaQ\arg\max_a Qargmax​a​​Q. That’s fine for discrete action spaces but breaks when actions are continuous (what’s argmax\arg\maxargmax over a 10-dimensional torque vector?) or when you want a stochastic policy by design (an LLM generating tokens isn’t supposed to be deterministic).

Policy gradient methods take the other path. Parameterize the policy πθ(a∣s)\pi_\theta(a \mid s)π​θ​​(a∣s) directly as a neural network. Compute the gradient of expected return with respect to θ\thetaθ. Step uphill. No argmax\arg\maxargmax. No Bellman recursion. Just gradient ascent on J(θ)=Eτ∼πθ[G(τ)]J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}[G(\tau)]J(θ)=E​τ∼π​θ​​​​[G(τ)].

The policy gradient theorem

Williams (1992) showed that this gradient is computable from sampled trajectories alone. The policy gradient theorem states:

∇J(θ)=Eτ∼πθ[∑tGt⋅∇θlogπθ(at∣st)]\nabla J(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{\tau \sim \pi_\theta}\left[\sum_t G_t \cdot \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)\right] ∇J(θ)=E​τ∼π​θ​​​​[​t​∑​​G​t​​⋅∇​θ​​logπ​θ​​(a​t​​∣s​t​​)]

where GtG_tG​t​​ is the return from step ttt onwards. The proof is two lines: differentiate J(θ)=∑τP(τ;θ)G(τ)J(\theta) = \sum_\tau P(\tau; \theta) G(\tau)J(θ)=∑​τ​​P(τ;θ)G(τ), use the log-derivative trick ∇P(τ;θ)=P(τ;θ)⋅∇logP(τ;θ)\nabla P(\tau; \theta) = P(\tau; \theta) \cdot \nabla \log P(\tau; \theta)∇P(τ;θ)=P(τ;θ)⋅∇logP(τ;θ), factor logP(τ;θ)=∑tlogπθ(at∣st)\log P(\tau; \theta) = \sum_t \log \pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)logP(τ;θ)=∑​t​​logπ​θ​​(a​t​​∣s​t​​) plus environment terms that don’t depend on θ\thetaθ and so vanish under the gradient.

In practice this gives the REINFORCE algorithm:

for episode in range(num_episodes):
    states, actions, rewards = rollout(policy)
    returns = compute_returns(rewards, gamma)
    for s, a, G in zip(states, actions, returns):
        loss = -G * log_pi(s, a)
        loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()

Sample trajectories, compute returns, multiply by the gradient of log-policy, average, ascend. That’s it. Vanilla REINFORCE is a sledgehammer high variance, poor sample efficiency, often unstable but it’s the foundation of every policy-gradient method that came after, including PPO and (with one more derivation) DPO.

Variance reduction: baselines and reward-to-go

Two standard tricks turn vanilla REINFORCE into something usable.

Baseline subtraction. Replace GtG_tG​t​​ with Gt−b(st)G_t - b(s_t)G​t​​−b(s​t​​) for any function b(s)b(s)b(s) that depends only on the state, not the action. The expected gradient is unchanged because E[b(st)⋅∇logπ(at∣st)]=0\mathbb{E}[b(s_t) \cdot \nabla \log \pi(a_t \mid s_t)] = 0E[b(s​t​​)⋅∇logπ(a​t​​∣s​t​​)]=0 for any state-only baseline. The variance, however, drops dramatically. The standard choice is b(st)=V^(st)b(s_t) = \hat{V}(s_t)b(s​t​​)=​V​^​​(s​t​​), an estimate of the state value. Subtracting this from GtG_tG​t​​ gives the advantage A(st,at)=Gt−V(st)A(s_t, a_t) = G_t - V(s_t)A(s​t​​,a​t​​)=G​t​​−V(s​t​​), which measures how much better the action you took was than the state’s average value.

Reward-to-go. Replace GtG_tG​t​​ (the full return from step 0) with Gtfrom tG_t^{\text{from } t}G​t​from t​​ (only future rewards from step ttt onwards). Past rewards don’t depend on action ata_ta​t​​ in expectation, so they contribute zero-mean noise to the gradient. Always use reward-to-go.

Combined:

∇J(θ)≈1N∑i∑t[Gt(i)−V^(st(i))]⋅∇θlogπθ(at(i)∣st(i))\nabla J(\theta) \approx \frac{1}{N} \sum_i \sum_t \left[G_t^{(i)} - \hat{V}(s_t^{(i)})\right] \cdot \nabla_\theta \log \pi_\theta(a_t^{(i)} \mid s_t^{(i)}) ∇J(θ)≈​N​​1​​​i​∑​​​t​∑​​[G​t​(i)​​−​V​^​​(s​t​(i)​​)]⋅∇​θ​​logπ​θ​​(a​t​(i)​​∣s​t​(i)​​)

This is REINFORCE with a baseline, which is one critic-network step away from being actor-critic. Train two networks: an actor πθ\pi_\thetaπ​θ​​ that emits actions, and a critic VϕV_\phiV​ϕ​​ that estimates state values. The critic provides the baseline for the actor’s gradient and is itself trained on TD targets. A2C (Advantage Actor-Critic) is the simplest version. A3C is the asynchronous variant that runs many actors in parallel. Together they were the dominant deep-RL approach circa 2017-2018, and they’re the immediate ancestor of PPO.

PPO: the algorithm everything bows to

A2C is on-policy: the gradient Eπθ[A⋅∇logπθ]\mathbb{E}_{\pi_\theta}[A \cdot \nabla \log \pi_\theta]E​π​θ​​​​[A⋅∇logπ​θ​​] requires data sampled from the current πθ\pi_\thetaπ​θ​​. After one gradient step, πθ\pi_\thetaπ​θ​​ has changed, and the rollout you collected is now slightly off-policy. Re-using it gives a biased gradient. So A2C does one update per rollout and discards the data, which is wasteful rollouts are expensive (especially in real environments, and especially in LLM RLHF where each rollout is generating hundreds of tokens through a large model).

You’d like to do many gradient updates per rollout without having the policy drift so far that the on-policy assumption breaks badly. Trust Region Policy Optimization (TRPO, Schulman et al. 2015) was the first principled fix: solve a constrained optimization that limits the KL divergence between old and new policy. Theoretically clean, computationally hairy TRPO needs a conjugate-gradient solve per update.

Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO, Schulman et al. 2017) replaced the hard trust-region constraint with a much simpler clipped surrogate objective. Define the importance ratio:

rt(θ)=πθ(at∣st)πθold(at∣st)r_t(\theta) = \frac{\pi_\theta(a_t \mid s_t)}{\pi_{\theta_\text{old}}(a_t \mid s_t)} r​t​​(θ)=​π​θ​old​​​​(a​t​​∣s​t​​)​​π​θ​​(a​t​​∣s​t​​)​​

This is the likelihood ratio of the new policy versus the policy that collected the data. rt=1r_t = 1r​t​​=1 means no change. The clipped objective:

LCLIP(θ)=Et[min(rt(θ)At,clip(rt(θ),1−ε,1+ε)At)]L^{\text{CLIP}}(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_t\left[\min\left(r_t(\theta) A_t, \text{clip}(r_t(\theta), 1-\varepsilon, 1+\varepsilon) A_t\right)\right] L​CLIP​​(θ)=E​t​​[min(r​t​​(θ)A​t​​,clip(r​t​​(θ),1−ε,1+ε)A​t​​)]

Two cases. If At>0A_t > 0A​t​​>0 (good action) and the ratio tries to grow past 1+ε1 + \varepsilon1+ε, the clip caps the gradient don’t push a good action past ε\varepsilonε above its old probability. If At<0A_t < 0A​t​​<0 (bad action) and the ratio tries to grow past 1−ε1 - \varepsilon1−ε in the bad direction, the clip caps it the other way. The min\minmin ensures that on the beneficial side of the clip, the gradient still flows; we don’t punish ourselves for an update that already moved correctly.

Typical ε=0.2\varepsilon = 0.2ε=0.2. The picture is a piecewise-linear function of the ratio with a flat roof on the good side and a flat floor on the bad side. The clipping prevents the policy from drifting too far per update, which lets you safely run many epochs of gradient descent on the same rollout typically 4-10 without the on-policy assumption breaking.

The full PPO loss adds a value-function loss (training the critic) and an entropy bonus (encouraging exploration):

L(θ,ϕ)=LCLIP(θ)−cv⋅(Vϕ(st)−Vttarget)2+ce⋅H(πθ(⋅∣st))L(\theta, \phi) = L^{\text{CLIP}}(\theta) - c_v \cdot (V_\phi(s_t) - V_t^{\text{target}})^2 + c_e \cdot H(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid s_t)) L(θ,ϕ)=L​CLIP​​(θ)−c​v​​⋅(V​ϕ​​(s​t​​)−V​t​target​​)​2​​+c​e​​⋅H(π​θ​​(⋅∣s​t​​))

with typical cv=0.5c_v = 0.5c​v​​=0.5, ce=0.01c_e = 0.01c​e​​=0.01, ε=0.2\varepsilon = 0.2ε=0.2.

for epoch in range(K_EPOCHS):
    for batch in minibatches(rollout):
        log_probs = policy.log_prob(batch.actions, batch.states)
        ratio = (log_probs - batch.log_probs_old).exp()
        surr1 = ratio * batch.advantages
        surr2 = ratio.clamp(1 - eps, 1 + eps) * batch.advantages
        policy_loss = -torch.min(surr1, surr2).mean()
        value_loss = (value_net(batch.states) - batch.returns).pow(2).mean()
        entropy = policy.entropy(batch.states).mean()
        loss = policy_loss + 0.5 * value_loss - 0.01 * entropy
        loss.backward()
        optimizer.step()

This is a slightly polished version of every PPO implementation in 2026. CleanRL’s reference implementation, HuggingFace’s TRL PPOTrainer, OpenAI’s original code all the same shape. PPO is robust: the exact hyperparameters rarely matter within plus or minus fifty percent. The clip coefficient ε=0.2\varepsilon = 0.2ε=0.2 has been standard for nine years and approximately nobody has found a reason to change it.

Three diagnostics to watch

Mean KL between old and new policy. Should stay in roughly [0,0.02][0, 0.02][0,0.02]. If it blows past 0.10.10.1, lower the learning rate or reduce the number of update epochs.

Clip fraction the fraction of samples whose ratio falls outside [1−ε,1+ε][1-\varepsilon, 1+\varepsilon][1−ε,1+ε]. Should sit around 0.1-0.3. Near zero means you could update more aggressively (raise LR or epochs); above 0.4 means you’re over-fitting the rollout and need to lower them.

Explained variance of the critic 1−Var(Vtarget−Vpred)/Var(Vtarget)1 - \text{Var}(V_\text{target} - V_\text{pred}) / \text{Var}(V_\text{target})1−Var(V​target​​−V​pred​​)/Var(V​target​​). Climbs toward 1 as the critic learns. If it stays near zero, your critic isn’t training.

These three numbers are your dashboard. If any one of them is misbehaving, your run is in trouble even if the reward curve still looks like it’s climbing.

PPO is the de facto default in 2026. Robotics, simulated environments, game-playing agents, and most importantly, large-language-model alignment all use PPO or one of its derivatives.

RLHF: turning preferences into a reward

This is the chapter’s payoff section. Everything we’ve built so far MDPs, policy gradients, PPO is what you need to understand RLHF. The pipeline is conceptually simple and load-bearing for nearly every aligned LLM in production.
