You have a pretrained language model. It writes grammatical English and confidently makes things up. You’d like it to be helpful, harmless, and honest (the three H’s of the InstructGPT paper). Writing a reward function for “good assistant response” by hand is impossible. Helpfulness isn’t a closed-form expression over tokens. But humans can compare two responses to the same prompt and pick the better one. That comparison signal is cheap to collect at scale.

RLHF (Christiano et al., 2017; Ouyang et al., 2022) converts pairwise preferences into a numeric reward, then RL-optimizes the language model against that reward. Three stages:

Stage 1 Supervised fine-tuning (SFT)

Start from the pretrained base model. Fine-tune on human-written demonstrations of the target behavior instruction-response pairs, helpful explanations, polite refusals. The result is πSFT\pi_\text{SFT}π​SFT​​, a model that’s already biased toward the target behavior but still has all the failure modes of being too literal, too verbose, or insufficiently picky about safety.

This is plain next-token prediction with the same cross-entropy loss as pretraining. The data is just curated. Without SFT, the next stages won’t work you need a reasonable starting point.

Stage 2 Train a reward model

Collect pairs of responses to the same prompt. A human labeler picks which response is better call them (y+,y−)(y_+, y_-)(y​+​​,y​−​​). Train a reward model Rϕ(x,y)R_\phi(x, y)R​ϕ​​(x,y) to assign higher scores to preferred responses, using the Bradley-Terry pairwise loss:

L(ϕ)=−E(x,y+,y−)[logσ(Rϕ(x,y+)−Rϕ(x,y−))]\mathcal{L}(\phi) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_+, y_-)}\left[\log \sigma(R_\phi(x, y_+) - R_\phi(x, y_-))\right] L(ϕ)=−E​(x,y​+​​,y​−​​)​​[logσ(R​ϕ​​(x,y​+​​)−R​ϕ​​(x,y​−​​))]

The Bradley-Terry model assumes the probability of preferring y+y_+y​+​​ over y−y_-y​−​​ is the sigmoid of the difference in their underlying scores. This loss has been the default for pairwise learning since 1952 and remains the standard for reward models in 2026.

The reward model is typically initialized from the SFT model (same backbone), with the language-modeling head replaced by a single scalar output. You don’t need a separate architecture you need a transformer that can process prompt+response and output one number.

from trl import RewardTrainer, RewardConfig
from transformers import AutoModelForSequenceClassification, AutoTokenizer

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("./sft-checkpoint")
rm = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    "./sft-checkpoint", num_labels=1
)
trainer = RewardTrainer(
    model=rm, tokenizer=tok, train_dataset=preference_data,
    args=RewardConfig(output_dir="./rm", num_train_epochs=1, learning_rate=1e-5),
)
trainer.train()

preference_data contains rows of (prompt, chosen, rejected). The Bradley-Terry loss is built into HuggingFace’s TRL library; you don’t write it by hand.

Stage 3 PPO against the reward model with a KL penalty

Initialize a trainable policy πθ\pi_\thetaπ​θ​​ from πSFT\pi_\text{SFT}π​SFT​​. Keep a frozen reference πref=πSFT\pi_\text{ref} = \pi_\text{SFT}π​ref​​=π​SFT​​. The reward at the end of a response yyy is

rtotal(x,y)=Rϕ(x,y)−β⋅KL(πθ(⋅∣x)∥πref(⋅∣x))r_\text{total}(x, y) = R_\phi(x, y) - \beta \cdot \text{KL}(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x) \| \pi_\text{ref}(\cdot \mid x)) r​total​​(x,y)=R​ϕ​​(x,y)−β⋅KL(π​θ​​(⋅∣x)∥π​ref​​(⋅∣x))

The first term is the reward model’s score. The second term is a KL penalty that prevents πθ\pi_\thetaπ​θ​​ from drifting arbitrarily from the SFT model. Run PPO with this combined reward.

The KL penalty is the single most important detail in RLHF. Without it, PPO will happily find responses that score very high under the reward model but are nonsense adversarial examples to the reward model that exploit its biases. The reward model was only trained on in-distribution completions; it doesn’t know how to score weird off-distribution outputs, and PPO is very good at finding those. The KL term keeps πθ\pi_\thetaπ​θ​​ near the manifold where the reward model was trained, which is the manifold where the reward model is reliable.

β\betaβ is typically 0.01-0.05, often adapted dynamically based on observed KL if the actual KL exceeds a target, β\betaβ doubles; if below half, β\betaβ halves. HuggingFace TRL’s PPOTrainer does this automatically with adap_kl_ctrl=True.

from trl import PPOTrainer, PPOConfig, AutoModelForCausalLMWithValueHead

policy = AutoModelForCausalLMWithValueHead.from_pretrained("./sft-checkpoint")
ref = AutoModelForCausalLMWithValueHead.from_pretrained("./sft-checkpoint")

ppo = PPOTrainer(
    config=PPOConfig(
        learning_rate=1.41e-5, batch_size=64,
        init_kl_coef=0.05, target_kl=6.0, adap_kl_ctrl=True,
    ),
    model=policy, ref_model=ref, tokenizer=tok,
)

for batch in dataloader:
    responses = ppo.generate(batch["query_ids"], max_new_tokens=128)
    rewards = rm(torch.cat([batch["query_ids"], responses], dim=-1)).logits[:, 0]
    stats = ppo.step(batch["query_ids"], responses, rewards)

The reward model scores the full response. The advantage is computed at the token level using GAE, exactly as in standard PPO. The value head lives on the policy backbone AutoModelForCausalLMWithValueHead attaches a small MLP head that outputs a state value for each token position. The reference model is frozen by convention; double-check you haven’t accidentally shared parameters.

Reward hacking: the dominant failure mode

Stage 3 can go wrong in a specific, recognizable way called reward hacking. The reward model is imperfect. It was trained on a finite preference dataset, has biases (often implicit length preferences, formality preferences, certain phrasing tics), and crucially, doesn’t know how to score adversarial off-distribution responses. PPO’s job is to find policies that maximize reward. Given an imperfect reward, PPO will often find policies that exploit the imperfection rather than improving along the dimension humans actually care about.

Symptoms: reward score climbs steadily during training; human evaluation score plateaus or decreases; KL divergence to reference grows past target; outputs start exhibiting weird patterns (excessive length, repeated phrases, oddly formatted responses, sycophancy).

Mitigations: stop training early when reward climbs but eval scores stop improving; raise β\betaβ to keep the policy closer to reference; broaden the reward model’s training data; train multiple reward models and use their mean or minimum; hold out a blind human eval set you check periodically. The pattern is that you can’t trust the reward score alone you need an external check that the policy is actually improving along the dimension you care about.

Length hacking is particularly common: reward models trained on helpful responses often implicitly reward length, since longer answers tend to be more thorough. Trained against this, the policy learns to pad. Fixes include length-normalizing the reward or adding an explicit length penalty.

DPO: skipping the explicit RL loop

In late 2023, Rafailov et al. published Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), a paper that quietly broke the RLHF pipeline as it was practiced. The argument: the entire stage-2-plus-stage-3 of RLHF can be collapsed into a single supervised loss over preference data. No separate reward model. No PPO. No KL penalty as a runtime concern.

The derivation hinges on a closed-form solution to the KL-constrained reward maximization. Given a reward r(x,y)r(x, y)r(x,y) and a reference policy πref\pi_\text{ref}π​ref​​, the optimal KL-regularized policy is

π∗(y∣x)=1Z(x)πref(y∣x)exp(1βr(x,y))\pi^*(y \mid x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)} \pi_\text{ref}(y \mid x) \exp\left(\frac{1}{\beta} r(x, y)\right) π​∗​​(y∣x)=​Z(x)​​1​​π​ref​​(y∣x)exp(​β​​1​​r(x,y))

Solve this for r(x,y)r(x, y)r(x,y) in terms of π∗\pi^*π​∗​​ and πref\pi_\text{ref}π​ref​​:

r(x,y)=βlogπ∗(y∣x)πref(y∣x)+βlogZ(x)r(x, y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y \mid x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y \mid x)} + \beta \log Z(x) r(x,y)=βlog​π​ref​​(y∣x)​​π​∗​​(y∣x)​​+βlogZ(x)

Substitute into the Bradley-Terry preference likelihood. The partition function Z(x)Z(x)Z(x) cancels because it depends only on the prompt. You’re left with a loss in terms of the policy’s log-likelihoods alone:

LDPO(θ)=−E[logσ(βlogπθ(y+∣x)πref(y+∣x)−βlogπθ(y−∣x)πref(y−∣x))]\mathcal{L}_\text{DPO}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}\left[\log \sigma\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_+ \mid x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_+ \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_- \mid x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_- \mid x)}\right)\right] L​DPO​​(θ)=−E[logσ(βlog​π​ref​​(y​+​​∣x)​​π​θ​​(y​+​​∣x)​​−βlog​π​ref​​(y​−​​∣x)​​π​θ​​(y​−​​∣x)​​)]

This is a standard supervised loss. Train the policy directly on preference pairs by maximizing the likelihood that it prefers chosen over rejected responses, weighted by the log-ratio against the reference. No reward model. No PPO. No rollouts. Just two forward passes per example (policy and frozen reference) and a backward pass.

The benefits are substantial. Cheaper training (no rollout loop, no separate reward-model training run). More stable optimization (no PPO drift, no reward hacking against an imperfect reward model). Comparable or better alignment quality on most benchmarks.

The drawbacks: DPO assumes the Bradley-Terry preference model is correct, which it approximately is for human preferences but not perfectly. DPO doesn’t easily extend to settings where you have a verifier instead of preferences (math correctness, code execution). And DPO has its own failure modes over-optimization can still happen, and the implicit reward implied by DPO can drift toward unintended attributes.

In 2026, DPO is the default for alignment in most production pipelines. PPO-based RLHF is reserved for cases where you specifically need an explicit reward model (multi-stage training, safety RM separate from helpfulness RM, online learning from new preferences). We’ll come back to DPO in Chapter 18 when we cover alignment more thoroughly.

GRPO: critic-free policy optimization for reasoning

The other 2024-2025 development worth covering is Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), introduced by DeepSeek for their reasoning models. GRPO is essentially PPO with two changes that make it much cheaper for LLM training:

  1. No critic. PPO trains a value network alongside the policy to compute advantages. For LLMs, the value network is often as large as the policy itself doubling the model memory. GRPO replaces the critic with a group-relative baseline: sample GGG responses to the same prompt, compute their rewards, and use the mean reward of the group as the baseline. The advantage of response iii is Ai=(ri−r¯)/std(r)A_i = (r_i - \bar{r}) / \text{std}(r)A​i​​=(r​i​​−​r​¯​​)/std(r).

  2. Verifier-based rewards. For reasoning tasks like math and code, you don’t need a learned reward model you can check the answer programmatically. Did the math problem’s final answer match? Did the code pass the unit tests? Reward is binary or ternary, computed by running a verifier rather than scoring with a trained network.

The combination is powerful. No critic means half the GPU memory for the same model size. Verifier rewards mean no separate reward-model training run, and the reward is exactly what you care about (correctness) rather than a learned proxy. DeepSeek-V3 and the R1 reasoning model use GRPO; the recipe has been reproduced by other labs and is now the dominant approach for capability-focused RL on LLMs (as opposed to alignment-focused RL, where DPO dominates).

We’ll see GRPO again in Chapter 18 alongside reasoning model training.

Sharp edges across the RL stack

Sparse rewards are nearly impossible to learn from. A win-only reward signal in a complex environment doesn’t propagate gradient information until the agent stumbles into a win, which on hard problems may be never. Reward shaping (intermediate signals), demonstrations (imitation learning warm-start), and curriculum learning (easy versions first) are the mitigations. For LLMs, this is one reason process reward models (scoring partial reasoning steps) have become important pure outcome rewards on hard problems are too sparse for efficient learning.

Reward hacking is a fundamental issue, not a tuning issue. Goodhart’s Law: when a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure. Every reward function you optimize against is a proxy for what you actually want. The proxy will be exploited. The question isn’t whether this will happen but how soon and how badly. Best practices: hold out blind eval sets, cap training time, use multiple reward signals, use KL regularization to a known-good reference.

On-policy vs off-policy is a real distinction. Policy gradient methods (REINFORCE, A2C, PPO) are on-policy: their gradient estimator assumes data was sampled from the current policy. PPO’s clipped surrogate makes “slightly off-policy” tolerable (for KKK epochs of updates) but doesn’t extend to fully off-policy data. Q-learning and DQN are off-policy and can reuse old data freely. DPO is fully off-policy because it’s a supervised loss. Pick the right method for your data regime trying to use PPO on a static dataset of preferences without rollouts is a category error.

Discount factor matters. γ=1\gamma = 1γ=1 on an infinite-horizon problem produces infinite values and broken training. γ\gammaγ too low and the agent acts myopically; γ\gammaγ too high and credit assignment becomes noisy. For LLM RLHF, γ=1\gamma = 1γ=1 is usually fine because episodes are short (hundreds of tokens) and bounded.

Reward scale matters more than people expect. Rewards of {+100,−100}\{+100, -100\}{+100,−100} versus {+1,−1}\{+1, -1\}{+1,−1} produce identical optimal policies but vastly different gradient magnitudes. Always normalize rewards (running standardization) before plugging them into PPO. The same applies to advantages normalize per-batch to zero mean, unit std.

The KL penalty is doing more work than you think. In RLHF specifically, the KL-to-reference is often the difference between a successful run and a complete failure. If you find yourself wanting to lower β\betaβ to “let the policy learn more,” you’re probably about to enable reward hacking. The right answer is usually to raise β\betaβ slightly and improve the reward model, not to loosen the regularizer.

What you’d actually use

For every common RL situation, the 2026 default:

Need Default
Tabular RL, gridworld-scale Custom Q-learning or SARSA, ~100 LOC
Continuous control, simulated Stable-Baselines3 PPO, SAC, or TD3
Atari / discrete games CleanRL PPO (single-file reference implementation)
LLM alignment from preferences TRL DPO for most cases; PPO for safety-critical multi-RM setups
LLM reasoning with verifiable rewards TRL GRPO or veRL
Multi-agent PettingZoo + MAPPO or MADDPG
Robotics sim-to-real Isaac Sim + PPO with domain randomization
Game-playing at scale IMPALA, AlphaZero family

HuggingFace TRL is the canonical library for LLM-side RL RewardTrainer, DPOTrainer, PPOTrainer, GRPOTrainer, all maintained and tested. CleanRL is the canonical reference for classical-RL implementations single-file, runnable, well-commented. Don’t write your own PPO from scratch for production. The number of fiddly correctness details (advantage normalization, ratio in log-space, gradient clipping, KL diagnostics) makes hand-rolled PPO a guaranteed pit of subtle bugs.

The opinion that’s worth being clear about: for most LLM alignment work in 2026, start with DPO. The PPO-based RLHF stack is heavier, more failure-prone, and rarely produces meaningfully better results than DPO on alignment-style preferences. Reach for PPO when you have a specific reason you need an online learning loop, you have separate reward signals you want to balance dynamically, you’re doing capability training with verifier rewards (in which case you actually want GRPO). Otherwise, DPO is the boring default.

Where this goes next

Chapter 17 covers pretraining LLMs end to end the distributed training, the data pipeline, the actual cost of building a model. That’s the first of two chapters that build a real, deployable LLM. Chapter 18 is the second, and that’s where this chapter pays off: alignment via SFT, RLHF, DPO, and the broader post-training toolkit. Chapter 18 also covers RL-for-reasoning (GRPO, process reward models) and constitutional AI in detail. By the end of those two chapters you’ll have walked the full path from “transformer architecture” to “production assistant.”

Chapter 27 covers autonomous systems agents that act in environments over long horizons. Most of the underlying machinery is what we built here: Q-functions for value estimation, policy gradients for action selection, PPO and GRPO when training is happening. The big additions are tool use, memory, and the failure modes specific to long-horizon agentic behavior. The RL we just covered is the ground level. The agent layer sits on top.

In the meantime: read CleanRL’s PPO implementation. Read TRL’s DPO trainer. Run them on a tiny task. The intuition for why a clipped surrogate works and why the KL penalty is load-bearing comes from running the code, not from reading more equations. The math is honest about what it claims; the algorithms are honest about their failure modes. Reinforcement learning has become unreasonably useful in the last three years, and you need it from here on out.

Chapter 17 Building an LLM

There is a moment, somewhere around the second week of staring at a training loss curve, when the abstraction layer between you and a large language model dissolves. Up until that moment the model has been a thing you call. After that moment, it is a thing you assemble. Tokenizers, data shards, attention layers, optimizer states, gradient communication patterns, eval gates, manifest files you can name every piece. None of them are mysterious. All of them are mechanical. The miracle is not that any single piece is hard; it is that getting all of them to work together at scale, for weeks, on tens of thousands of GPUs, without any one of them breaking, produces a system that can hold a conversation.

This chapter is the longest one in the book and there’s a reason. Building an LLM end-to-end is the most ambitious software engineering project of our era. A frontier training run is roughly 10^25 floating-point operations executed across about a hundred million dollars of compute, on a dataset that took months to assemble and probably contains a non-trivial fraction of every English sentence ever written down. The architecture is not what makes this hard. We covered the architecture in Chapter 13. Every transformer block in Llama 3 is the same as every transformer block in your laptop GPT from Chapter 13. What makes building an LLM hard is the system around the model: how you turn text into integers, how you assemble a multi-trillion-token training corpus, how you move data through tens of thousands of GPUs, how you avoid losing two weeks of progress to a corrupted checkpoint, and how you decide whether what you trained is actually good enough to ship.

That’s what we’re going to walk through. By the end of this chapter, you’ll be able to read the Llama 3 technical report or the DeepSeek-V3 paper and understand every line of every table. You’ll be able to estimate the compute budget for a hypothetical training run from first principles. You’ll know why every detail of the modern training stack byte-level BPE, FSDP, BF16 mixed precision, content-addressed artifacts, KL budget gates exists, and what would break if you removed it.

We’ll go in the order you’d actually do the work: tokenizer first, then data pipeline, then pre-training architecture and training loop, then distributed training and scaling, then the orchestration layer that ties it all together into a runnable pipeline. The companion chapter (Chapter 18) covers what comes after pre-training alignment, RLHF, DPO, the post-training stack that turns a base model into an assistant. Read both and you’ve walked the full path from raw text to a deployable model.

The big picture

Before we dive in, let’s name the stages and what each one produces. A frontier LLM training pipeline has roughly twelve stages. Some run sequentially, a few can run in parallel, and the dependency graph between them is the shape of the work.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ 01. Tokenizer training        → vocab + merges              │
│ 02. Tokenizer assembly        → production tokenizer        │
│ 03. Data pipeline             → sharded tokenized dataset   │
│ 04. Pre-training              → base model checkpoint       │
│ 05. Distributed scaling       → trained at production size  │
│ 06. SFT                       → instruction-following model │
│ 07. Reward modeling           → RM checkpoint               │
│ 08. DPO / RLHF                → aligned policy              │
│ 09. Constitutional / refinement → final policy              │
│ 10. Evaluation                → eval report                 │
│ 11. Quantization              → 4-bit/8-bit deployable      │
│ 12. Inference serving         → production endpoint         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Stages 1 through 5 are this chapter. Stages 6 through 9 are Chapter 18 (alignment). Stage 10 (evaluation) gets covered in pieces throughout we touch on it here for the gating role it plays in deciding what ships. Stages 11 and 12 are Chapter 19 (LLM inference) and Chapter 29 (production serving).

The reason it’s worth seeing the whole pipeline before zooming in is that decisions made in stage 1 propagate forward and constrain everything downstream. The tokenizer’s vocabulary cannot be changed after pre-training without rebuilding from scratch. The data mix in stage 3 determines what the model can be aligned for in stages 6-9. The architectural choices in stage 4 (number of layers, dimension, attention type) determine the memory math in stage 5 and the inference economics in stage 11. There are no isolated decisions. Every stage is downstream of every choice that came before.

So let’s start at the top.

Tokenizers: how text becomes integers

A language model does not read English. It does not read any natural language at all. It reads sequences of integers. The mapping between text and those integers is the tokenizer, and despite living off to the side of the architecture, it shapes more about model behavior than most engineers realize.

To see why, look at one specific gap. The string "Hello, world!" is, to your eyes, thirteen characters of greeting. To Llama 3’s tokenizer, it is the integer sequence [9906, 11, 1917, 0] four tokens. The same string in GPT-2’s tokenizer is [15496, 11, 995, 0] also four, by coincidence. But take a Korean greeting like "안녕하세요": GPT-2 sees nine tokens, GPT-4 sees five, Llama 3 sees three. Same text, different number of tokens, different speed of inference, different cost per API call, different effective context window.

The tokenizer is not preprocessing. It is architecture. It is fixed at pre-training time and propagates through every downstream decision the model ever makes.

The three ways to slice text, and why two of them lost

There are three obvious approaches to converting text into integers, and you can derive each one in about ten seconds. Two of them are bad. One is what every modern LLM uses.

Word-level tokenization splits text on whitespace and punctuation. "The cat sat" becomes ["The", "cat", "sat"]. Three words, three integers. Simple. Until you encounter "tokenization", or a German compound noun like "Geschwindigkeitsbegrenzung" (speed limit), or a chemistry paper with "4-(hydroxymethyl)benzaldehyde". Word-level tokenization needs a vocabulary entry for every word it might ever see. English alone has over a million word forms. Add code, URLs, scientific notation, and a hundred languages, and you need an effectively infinite vocabulary. Miss any word and you get the dreaded [UNK] (unknown) token, which is the model’s way of saying “I have no idea what this is, please don’t blame me for the next word being wrong.” Word-level tokenizers are why pre-2018 NLP systems were so brittle on out-of-distribution text.

Character-level tokenization goes the other way. "hello" becomes ["h", "e", "l", "l", "o"]. Five characters, five integers. The vocabulary is tiny a few hundred entries cover all of Unicode’s basic plane. There are no unknown tokens. But sequences become very long. A sentence that would be ten word-tokens becomes maybe fifty character-tokens. Worse, the model has to learn that t, h, e together mean “the” relearning, from data, something a child knows by age four. You’re burning attention capacity on a problem that has a deterministic answer. Character-level models work but they’re inefficient.

Subword tokenization is the compromise that won. Common words stay whole "the" is one token. Rare words decompose into meaningful pieces "unhappiness" becomes ["un", "happi", "ness"]. Vocabulary stays manageable, somewhere in the 30,000-200,000 range. Sequences stay reasonably short. Unknown tokens essentially disappear because any word can be built from subword pieces.

Every modern LLM uses some flavor of subword tokenization. The interesting question is which flavor, and the three main answers are BPE, WordPiece, and SentencePiece.

BPE: byte-pair encoding, in plain English

Byte-Pair Encoding (BPE) is a 1994 compression algorithm that someone repurposed for tokenization in 2016 and it ate the field. The intuition is so simple it’s hard to believe at first.

Start by treating every individual character as a token. Count how often each adjacent pair of tokens appears in your training data. The most frequent pair is the one most worth giving its own dedicated entry in the vocabulary. Merge it into a new token. Now repeat: count pairs again on the updated text, find the new most-frequent pair, merge it. Each merge is a vocabulary entry. Stop when you’ve made as many merges as your target vocabulary size demands.

Here’s BPE running on a tiny corpus with three words: "lower" (5 occurrences), "lowest" (2 occurrences), and "newest" (6 occurrences).

Step 0: characters
  l o w e r       (×5)
  l o w e s t     (×2)
  n e w e s t     (×6)

Step 1: count pairs
  (w,e): 13   (e,s): 8   (s,t): 8   (l,o): 7   (o,w): 7   ...

Step 2: merge (w,e) → "we"
  l o we r        (×5)
  l o we s t      (×2)
  n e we s t      (×6)

Step 3: count pairs again
  (we,s): 8   (s,t): 8   (l,o): 7   (o,we): 7   (n,e): 6   ...

Step 4: merge (we,s) → "wes" (ties broken by first encounter)
  l o we r        (×5)
  l o wes t       (×2)
  n e wes t       (×6)

...continue until vocabulary size reached.

The merge table the ordered list of (pair → new token) pairs is the tokenizer. To encode a new piece of text, apply the merges in the same order they were learned. The order matters. If merge 1 created "th" and merge 5 created "the", you have to apply merge 1 first so that "the" can form from "th"+"e" in merge 5.

In code, the heart of BPE training is forty lines:

from collections import Counter

class BPE:
    def __init__(self):
        self.merges = {}
        self.vocab = {i: bytes([i]) for i in range(256)}

    def _pairs(self, tokens):
        return Counter(zip(tokens, tokens[1:]))

    def _merge(self, tokens, pair, new_id):
        out, i = [], 0
        while i < len(tokens):
            if i < len(tokens) - 1 and (tokens[i], tokens[i+1]) == pair:
                out.append(new_id)
                i += 2
            else:
                out.append(tokens[i])
                i += 1
        return out

    def train(self, text, num_merges):
        tokens = list(text.encode("utf-8"))
        for i in range(num_merges):
            pairs = self._pairs(tokens)
            if not pairs:
                break
            best = max(pairs, key=pairs.get)
            new_id = 256 + i
            tokens = self._merge(tokens, best, new_id)
            self.merges[best] = new_id
            self.vocab[new_id] = self.vocab[best[0]] + self.vocab[best[1]]

That’s the algorithm. Production tokenizers wrap this with regex pre-tokenization (more on that in a moment), special-token handling, normalization, and a Rust implementation that runs a hundred times faster than this Python but the core merging loop is what’s printed above.

Byte-level BPE, and why it was the unlock

The version of BPE every modern LLM uses is byte-level BPE, introduced by GPT-2 in 2019. The standard BPE we just walked through operates on Unicode characters. Byte-level BPE operates on raw bytes (values 0-255).

Why does that matter? Because it makes the base vocabulary exactly 256 entries, and any input text in any language, binary data, corrupted UTF-8, emojis, mathematical notation can be encoded without ever producing an unknown token. Chinese characters are typically 3 UTF-8 bytes each. Emojis are 4 bytes. Arabic, Devanagari, Cyrillic, Korean, Japanese all are sequences of bytes, and BPE finds patterns in byte sequences exactly the way it finds patterns in ASCII bytes. You can throw any input at a byte-level BPE tokenizer and it will produce some sequence of integers, possibly inefficient but never undefined. There is no [UNK] token.

This single property is why every frontier LLM since GPT-2 uses byte-level BPE: GPT-3, GPT-4, GPT-4o, Claude, Llama 3 (which switched from SentencePiece in Llama 2 to a tiktoken-style byte-level BPE in Llama 3), DeepSeek-V3, Qwen, Mistral. The 1994 algorithm plus a bytes-not-characters trick became the universal foundation.

WordPiece and SentencePiece, in one paragraph each

WordPiece (Devlin et al., 2018) looks like BPE but picks merges using a different criterion. BPE merges the most frequent pair. WordPiece merges the pair whose joint frequency exceeds the product of its parts’ frequencies that is, pairs that appear together more often than they “should” by chance. Mathematically: BPE maximizes count(A,B); WordPiece maximizes count(A,B) / (count(A) × count(B)). The difference is subtle but produces measurably different vocabularies. WordPiece favors statistically surprising mergers over merely common ones. BERT uses WordPiece. Most other modern models don’t, and the reason is mostly historical inertia rather than empirical superiority.

SentencePiece (Kudo and Richardson, 2018) is a tokenization library, not an algorithm. It supports both BPE and a different algorithm called Unigram (which we’ll spare you the math for it starts with a too-large vocabulary and prunes back instead of merging up). The key feature of SentencePiece is that it treats the input as one long stream of Unicode characters including whitespace, with no language-specific pre-tokenization. This makes it genuinely language-agnostic Chinese, Japanese, Thai, and other languages where words don’t have spaces between them get tokenized correctly out of the box. Llama 2 used SentencePiece BPE with 32,000 tokens. T5 uses SentencePiece Unigram. Llama 3 left SentencePiece for tiktoken-style byte-level BPE.

In 2026 the boring default is byte-level BPE with 100,000-200,000 vocabulary entries. Most of the variation between major LLMs is in the training data and the vocabulary size, not the algorithm. Once you’ve understood byte-level BPE deeply, you’ve understood 90% of what every production tokenizer is doing.

Vocabulary size: the trade-off you actually have to make

Pick your tokenizer’s vocabulary size and you’re committing to a real engineering trade-off whose cost you’ll feel forever.

A larger vocabulary compresses text more aggressively. The same English paragraph that takes 100 tokens with a 32K vocabulary might take 70 tokens with a 128K vocabulary. That’s 30% fewer tokens during inference, which translates directly to 30% lower latency and 30% lower cost per response.

But the embedding matrix grows linearly with vocabulary size. With a 4,096-dimensional embedding, a 128K vocabulary needs 128,000×4,096=524128{,}000 \times 4{,}096 = 524128,000×4,096=524 million parameters in the embedding alone. A 32K vocabulary needs 131 million. The 393-million-parameter difference is a real cost those parameters live on the GPU during training, take up KV-cache space during inference, and contribute to overall model size on disk.

Model Vocab Size Tokenizer Avg tokens / English word
BERT 30,522 WordPiece ~1.4
GPT-2 50,257 Byte-level BPE ~1.3
Llama 2 32,000 SentencePiece BPE ~1.4
GPT-4 ~100,256 Byte-level BPE (cl100k_base) ~1.2
Llama 3 128,256 Byte-level BPE (tiktoken-style) ~1.1
GPT-4o 200,019 Byte-level BPE (o200k_base) ~1.0

The trend is clear: vocabularies are growing. GPT-2 had 50K. GPT-4 doubled it. Llama 3 quadrupled Llama 2’s vocabulary. GPT-4o pushed past 200K. The reasoning is straightforward at frontier scale, the embedding parameters are a small fraction of total model size (a few percent), but the compression efficiency win shows up on every single token of every single inference call, forever. Bigger vocab is an inference-economics trade.

The multilingual tax

There’s one more thing about tokenizer vocabulary size that every team building a multilingual product needs to know. Tokenizers trained primarily on English are brutal to other languages.

Korean text in GPT-2’s tokenizer averages 2-3 tokens per syllable. Some Chinese characters take 3 tokens. A Korean user effectively has half the context window of an English user, paying the same per-token price for roughly half the information density. We call this the multilingual tax, and it’s been the dominant complaint about commercial tokenizers since 2020.

This is exactly why Llama 3 quadrupled its vocabulary from 32K to 128K to dedicate more vocabulary entries to non-English scripts and reduce the multilingual tax. GPT-4o went further with 200K specifically because OpenAI wanted to even out token-per-word cost across languages. If you’re building for a non-English market, this is a real consideration.

The full pipeline: it’s not just BPE

A production tokenizer is not one algorithm but a five-stage pipeline:

Raw Text ─▶ Normalize ─▶ Pre-tokenize ─▶ BPE ─▶ Special tokens ─▶ IDs

Normalization handles Unicode quirks. The “ﬁ” ligature (Unicode U+FB01) and “fi” (two characters) look identical but are different code points. Without normalization, the same word gets different tokens depending on which form your input uses. The standard fix is NFKC (Normalization Form KC) a Unicode standard that collapses compatibility variants to canonical forms.

Pre-tokenization splits text into chunks before BPE runs. Without this, BPE could happily merge across word boundaries for example, learning a token for "e c" if "the cat" appears often enough. GPT-2 uses a regex pattern that splits on contractions, words with optional leading spaces, numbers, punctuation, and whitespace. The leading space convention is important: " the" (with a leading space) and "the" (without) are different tokens, which is how the tokenizer encodes word boundaries without an explicit separator.

GPT2_PATTERN = r"""'(?:[sdmt]|ll|ve|re)| ?\p{L}+| ?\p{N}+| ?[^\s\p{L}\p{N}]+|\s+(?!\S)|\s+"""

That regex looks intimidating; what it actually does is split text the way a slightly-pedantic human would when manually tokenizing.

Special tokens are vocabulary entries reserved for structural markers: <|begin_of_text|>, <|end_of_text|>, <|user|>, <|assistant|>, padding tokens, and so on. Special tokens bypass BPE entirely they’re matched exactly in the input and replaced with their fixed integer ID before BPE merges run on the surrounding text. The reason is that you don’t want the merge algorithm to break apart a token like <|user|> into pieces just because some weird BPE merge produced a partial match.

Chat templates: where most production bugs live

Here’s where most teams get burned. When you call a chat API, you send a structured list of messages [{"role": "system", "content": "..."}, {"role": "user", "content": "..."}]. The model does not see this JSON. It sees a flat sequence of tokens. The chat template is the formula for converting messages into that flat sequence using special tokens. And every model formats it differently.

Llama 3:

<|begin_of_text|><|start_header_id|>system<|end_header_id|>

You are helpful.<|eot_id|><|start_header_id|>user<|end_header_id|>

Hello<|eot_id|><|start_header_id|>assistant<|end_header_id|>

GPT-style:

<|im_start|>system
You are helpful.<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>user
Hello<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>assistant

The model was trained on one specific template. Send anything that deviates by even a missing newline let alone a wrong special-token name and the input falls outside the training distribution. The model was not trained to handle that, and its outputs become unreliable in subtle ways. We have personally lost weekends to chat-template bugs that produced “almost working” agents.

The fix is to always use the model’s official tokenizer (AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(...) in HuggingFace) and its apply_chat_template method, never to format the conversation by hand. The template is part of the model’s contract; treat it accordingly.

What you actually use in production

Three libraries:

tiktoken (OpenAI): Rust BPE implementation with Python bindings. Used for OpenAI models (GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4o). Encodes around 100 million tokens per second on a single CPU thread. The Python wrapper is just enc = tiktoken.get_encoding("cl100k_base"); ids = enc.encode(text).

HuggingFace Tokenizers: Rust implementation supporting BPE, WordPiece, and Unigram with full pre-tokenization, normalization, and post-processing pipelines. The Python wrapper is AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(...). This is the boring default for any open-weights model.

SentencePiece (Google): C++ implementation, originally for Google’s models. Now mostly used through HuggingFace as a backend.

You almost never write your own tokenizer in production. You train a custom tokenizer at most once, on a representative sample of your training corpus, using HuggingFace’s tokenizers library. The training itself is fast gigabytes of text in minutes. After that, you treat the tokenizer as immutable infrastructure.

Data pipelines: the real bottleneck

You have a tokenizer. Now you need data and not the kind of “data” that fits in a CSV file. We’re talking about terabytes of text, cleaned, deduplicated, filtered for quality, tokenized into fixed-length sequences, and served to a multi-GPU training cluster fast enough that the GPUs never wait for the next batch.

Most people think training an LLM is about the model architecture. It is not. Llama 3 used 15.6 trillion tokens. GPT-3 used 300 billion. DeepSeek-V3 used 14.8 trillion. The architecture across all three is roughly the same: stacked transformer blocks with multi-head attention and FFNs. The difference in capability comes overwhelmingly from data quality and quantity.

Where the data comes from

There is no public dataset called “all the text on the internet that we can train on.” Frontier models assemble their training corpora from a mix of sources, and the exact mix is one of the most carefully guarded secrets at every lab. We know enough of the public side to understand the categories.

Source Approximate size Quality Models that use it
Common Crawl ~250 TB raw, ~5-10 TB after filtering Low (needs heavy filtering) GPT-3, Llama, every open model
Wikipedia ~20 GB High Every major LLM
GitHub code ~1 TB+ raw Medium (lots of duplicates) StarCoder, CodeLlama, DeepSeek-Coder
Books (BookCorpus, Project Gutenberg) ~100 GB High GPT-2, GPT-3, early models
Academic papers (arXiv, S2ORC) ~100 GB High for STEM Llama, Galactica
StackOverflow, Reddit ~100 GB Medium Llama, Falcon
C4, RefinedWeb, FineWeb ~5-15 TB Medium-High (pre-filtered web) T5, Falcon, Llama 3
Synthetic data Variable High when curated Phi, recent Qwen, Llama 3

The Llama 3 paper is unusually transparent about the data mix: roughly 50% general web data, 25% code, 13% books and academic papers, 8% math data, 4% multilingual web data. Total: 15.6 trillion tokens from sources exceeding 5 TB of raw text after filtering.

The ratios matter as much as the totals. Too much web data and the model becomes a Reddit parrot. Too little code and it can’t program. Too little math and it fails at reasoning. Too much synthetic data and it inherits the biases of whatever model produced the synthetic data. There is no formula for the right mix every team gets there through ablation experiments.

Cleaning: the difference between coherent paragraphs and HTML soup

Raw web data is filthy. A typical Common Crawl dump contains HTML markup, JavaScript, navigation menus, cookie banners, advertisements, machine-translated spam, near-duplicate pages, listing pages with no actual content, and a depressing amount of low-quality SEO text designed to game search rankings rather than communicate meaning.

Cleaning is the single most important stage in the data pipeline because it determines what the model is going to imitate. Train on raw Common Crawl and your model will fluently produce HTML tags interleaved with product listings. Train on cleaned Common Crawl and it produces coherent paragraphs.

The cleaning pipeline is a stack of filters, each removing one category of noise:

HTML stripping removes markup and keeps only visible text. Libraries like trafilatura and readability-lxml do this well they’re designed to extract article content while discarding navigation, ads, and boilerplate.

Language detection classifies each document by language. The standard tool is Meta’s fastText lid.176.bin model, which classifies into 176 languages with a confidence score. Documents with low confidence (under about 0.8) are usually mixed-language or garbage. You filter to your target languages.

Quality filtering is the interesting part. The RefinedWeb dataset (which trained Falcon) introduced a perplexity-based filter: train a small bigram or KenLM language model on Wikipedia, then score each candidate document under that model. High perplexity means the document is unlike clean reference text likely keyword spam, machine-generated content, or low-quality boilerplate. Filter out documents above a perplexity threshold and you’ve removed a substantial chunk of garbage.

Other quality heuristics catch common patterns:

  • Documents with too few words (under ~50) are probably stubs.
  • Documents where more than 30% of words are ALL CAPS are probably spam.
  • Documents with high ratios of special characters or numbers are probably structured listings rather than prose.
  • Documents with low sentence-length variance are often machine-generated.

Each individual filter rule is crude. Stacking ten of them produces a pipeline that removes most of the truly bad content.

def quality_filter(text, min_words=50, max_caps=0.3, max_special=0.1):
    words = text.split()
    if len(words) < min_words:
        return False
    caps_ratio = sum(1 for w in words if w.isupper()) / len(words)
    if caps_ratio > max_caps:
        return False
    special = sum(1 for c in text if not c.isalnum() and not c.isspace())
    if special / max(len(text), 1) > max_special:
        return False
    return True

Deduplication: the most underrated cleaning step

The most impactful single cleaning operation, by a wide margin, is deduplication. Common Crawl contains enormous numbers of duplicated and near-duplicated pages copies of the same news article syndicated across hundreds of sites, legal disclaimers and terms-of-service pages, cookie banners, header and footer text. The Llama team reported that deduplication removed approximately 38% of their candidate web data. More than a third of Common Crawl is redundant.

Why does deduplication matter so much? Two reasons. First, training compute spent on duplicates is wasted the model already saw that text once and updates its weights toward the same gradient. Second, and more dangerously, training on duplicates causes the model to memorize specific passages and regurgitate them at generation time. This is both a quality issue (your model now produces verbatim copies of training text) and a legal issue (those copies might be copyrighted material).

Exact deduplication is easy. Hash each document with SHA-256, drop duplicates. Done in O(n) time.

Near-duplicate detection is the hard problem. Two copies of the same article with slightly different ad headers around them are 95% identical, but byte-for-byte they differ so SHA-256 won’t catch them. You need a similarity-aware deduplication scheme.

The standard solution is MinHash + LSH (Locality-Sensitive Hashing), and the algorithm is genuinely beautiful. Here’s how it works.

Step 1: Shingling. Convert each document into a set of n-grams (typically 5-grams of words). The sentence “the quick brown fox jumps” with 3-word shingles becomes the set {"the quick brown", "quick brown fox", "brown fox jumps"}. Two documents that share a lot of content will share a lot of shingles. Two documents that don’t share content will share almost no shingles.

Step 2: Jaccard similarity. The natural similarity metric on sets is the Jaccard index: size of intersection divided by size of union. Two identical documents have Jaccard 1.0; two completely different documents have Jaccard 0.0; two near-duplicates have Jaccard around 0.8-0.95.

Step 3: MinHash signature. Computing exact Jaccard between every pair of documents is O(n²) in the number of documents infeasible for billions of pages. MinHash is a clever trick: for each document, compute K hash values, where each hash is the minimum hash over the document’s shingle set under a different hash function. The fixed-length K-dimensional vector this produces is the document’s MinHash signature.

The magic property: the probability that two documents agree on any single position of their MinHash signatures equals their Jaccard similarity. So if you have signatures of length 128, the fraction of positions on which two documents agree is an unbiased estimator of their Jaccard similarity. You’ve replaced exact set comparison with fixed-size signature comparison.

Step 4: LSH bucketing. Even with MinHash, comparing every pair is still O(n²). LSH adds the final trick: split the K-dimensional signatures into B bands of R rows each. Hash each band to a bucket. Documents that land in the same bucket for any band are candidates. Compute exact Jaccard only on the candidate pairs.

import hashlib
from collections import defaultdict

def shingles(text, k=5):
    words = text.lower().split()
    return {" ".join(words[i:i+k]) for i in range(len(words) - k + 1)}

def minhash_signature(shingle_set, num_hashes=128):
    signature = []
    for i in range(num_hashes):
        min_hash = float("inf")
        for shingle in shingle_set:
            h = int(hashlib.sha256(f"{i}:{shingle}".encode()).hexdigest(), 16)
            min_hash = min(min_hash, h)
        signature.append(min_hash)
    return signature

def lsh_buckets(signature, bands=16):
    rows = len(signature) // bands
    return [
        (b, hashlib.md5(str(signature[b*rows:(b+1)*rows]).encode()).hexdigest())
        for b in range(bands)
    ]

The math behind the band-and-row tuning is elegant. With B bands and R rows per band, the probability that two documents with Jaccard similarity sss collide in at least one band is 1−(1−sR)B1 - (1 - s^R)^B1−(1−s​R​​)​B​​. This curve has a sharp S-shape around s≈(1/B)1/Rs \approx (1/B)^{1/R}s≈(1/B)​1/R​​, so you can tune BBB and RRR to set the threshold above which documents are likely to be flagged as candidates. Standard values are B=16B = 16B=16, R=8R = 8R=8 (so K=128K = 128K=128), which gives a sharp transition around Jaccard 0.7-0.8.

The whole pipeline is efficient: MinHash signatures are O(n) in document count, LSH bucketing is O(n) with hashing, and only candidate pairs incur the exact Jaccard computation. This scales to billions of documents on a single big-memory machine, or trillions with distributed processing.

Sequence packing and document boundaries

Your transformer expects fixed-length input sequences (typically 2048, 4096, or 8192 tokens during pre-training). Your documents are wildly variable: some are 50 tokens, some are 50,000.

The naive approach is to pad each document to the maximum sequence length, but that wastes enormous compute on padding tokens that contribute nothing to learning.

The efficient approach is sequence packing: concatenate multiple documents into a single fixed-length sequence, separated by end-of-document tokens (<|eot_id|> in Llama 3, <|endoftext|> in GPT). A 2048-token sequence might contain three short documents back-to-back: [doc_A_tokens, EOT, doc_B_tokens, EOT, doc_C_tokens, EOT].

This raises an architectural question: should attention cross document boundaries within a packed sequence? Naively, yes every position attends to every previous position. But that means tokens from document B can attend to document A, learning spurious correlations. Modern implementations use a block-diagonal attention mask within packed sequences, where attention only flows within each document. Llama 3 uses this; HuggingFace’s attention_mask parameter supports it.

For long documents that exceed the sequence length, you split them into multiple sequences. The split point matters: splitting mid-sentence is bad; splitting at paragraph or sentence boundaries is better. Some pipelines align splits with these boundaries when possible.

Chinchilla and the over-training era

The most important quantitative result on data is Chinchilla scaling. We covered it briefly in Chapter 14, but it bears repeating in detail because it shapes the data budget for every modern model.

The 2020 Kaplan et al. paper from OpenAI established that for a fixed compute budget, you should split it between model parameters NNN and training tokens DDD at a roughly fixed ratio. They concluded the optimum was about D/N≈1.7D/N \approx 1.7D/N≈1.7 push parameters hard, train for relatively few tokens. GPT-3 followed this prescription: 175B parameters trained on 300B tokens.

Hoffmann et al. at DeepMind redid the experiment more carefully in 2022 and got a different answer. The corrected Chinchilla scaling law is

L(N,D)=ANα+BDβ+EL(N, D) = \frac{A}{N^\alpha} + \frac{B}{D^\beta} + E L(N,D)=​N​α​​​​A​​+​D​β​​​​B​​+E

with α≈0.34\alpha \approx 0.34α≈0.34, β≈0.28\beta \approx 0.28β≈0.28, and EEE being the irreducible loss (the entropy of the data a floor below which no model can go).

Take the derivative at fixed compute C≈6NDC \approx 6NDC≈6ND and the compute-optimal point is D/N≈20D/N \approx 20D/N≈20 twenty tokens per parameter, not 1.7. GPT-3 was about ten times under-trained. The DeepMind team validated this by training Chinchilla (70B, 1.4T tokens) and beating GPT-3 (175B, 300B tokens) on every benchmark, at 2.5× lower inference cost.

But here’s the modern twist. Llama 3 was trained on ratios far above Chinchilla-optimal. Llama 3 8B saw 15 trillion tokens 1,875 tokens per parameter, ninety-four times Chinchilla-optimal. This isn’t a mistake. It’s an explicit choice.

Chinchilla-optimal minimizes loss per training FLOP. For a frontier lab burning a hundred million dollars to pretrain one model, that’s the right metric. For a chatbot serving a trillion tokens per month, inference cost dominates total cost, and inference cost scales with model size NNN. Training a smaller model for longer raises training cost a bit but cuts every future inference call by a factor proportional to NNN. Sardana and Frankle (2024) formalized this in Beyond Chinchilla-Optimal: for inference-dominated workloads, the optimal D/ND/ND/N is much higher, typically 100-500.

So the 2026 picture has two regimes:

Regime Optimal D/N Examples
Train once, evaluate, publish ~20 Chinchilla, research checkpoints
Train once, serve forever 100-500 Llama 3, Phi-3, Qwen 2.5

Pick a regime before you pick anything else. The answer to “how much data should I train on?” is a function of how much you’ll serve the model, not just how much training compute you have.

Pre-training: putting the model in motion

You have a tokenizer. You have a tokenized dataset. Now you train the model.

The architecture is what we built in Chapter 13: a stack of transformer blocks with self-attention, feed-forward networks, residual connections, layer normalization (or RMSNorm in modern variants), pre-norm placement, and learned token and position embeddings. The base recipe is GPT-style decoder-only, and we showed in Chapter 13 how to assemble all of it in about 100 lines of PyTorch. Here we’re focused on what happens during training the loss, the optimizer, the schedule, the loss curve, the diagnostics that tell you whether training is healthy.

What the training loop actually does

At its core, pre-training a language model is one operation repeated billions of times: sample a batch of tokenized sequences, compute the loss, backpropagate gradients, update weights.

for step in range(num_steps):
    batch = next_batch_from_dataloader()  # shape: (B, N) of token IDs
    inputs = batch[:, :-1]                # all but last token
    targets = batch[:, 1:]                # shifted by one
    logits = model(inputs)                # shape: (B, N-1, vocab_size)
    loss = F.cross_entropy(
        logits.view(-1, vocab_size),
        targets.view(-1)
    )
    optimizer.zero_grad()
    loss.backward()
    nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
    optimizer.step()
    scheduler.step()

That’s the entire pre-training loop, conceptually. Every LLM in production was trained by some elaboration of these ten lines, just at scale.

The loss is cross-entropy between the model’s predicted distribution and the true next token, averaged over every position in the batch. At step zero, before any training, the model is essentially random its predictions are uniform over the vocabulary, so the cross-entropy loss is approximately logV\log VlogV where VVV is vocabulary size. For a 50K vocabulary that’s about 10.8. Within a few hundred steps the loss drops sharply as the model learns the most basic statistical patterns of the data capitalization, common bigrams, whitespace conventions. Within a few thousand steps it’s learning higher-order patterns: word frequencies, simple grammar. Tens of thousands of steps in, it’s learning multi-token patterns, syntactic structure, simple factual associations. Hundreds of thousands of steps in, complex reasoning patterns start emerging.

The shape of the loss curve over a healthy pre-training run is one of the most beautiful artifacts in modern engineering. It looks like a smooth, almost-noiseless monotonic decrease, with a steep drop in the first 1% of steps and a long, gentle slope from there. If your loss curve has spikes, plateaus, or oscillations, something is wrong and you should stop and debug before continuing every step of bad training is wasted compute that you can’t get back.

Learning rate schedule: warmup and decay

The learning rate matters more than people new to deep learning expect. Too high and the loss diverges in the first hundred steps the gradients push weights into pathological regions. Too low and training takes forever to converge and ends up at a worse final loss.

Modern LLM training uses a two-phase schedule:

  1. Warmup: linearly ramp the learning rate from zero to the peak value over the first 1-5% of training steps (typically 2,000-10,000 steps). This stabilizes early training when the model’s gradients are noisy and large.
  2. Decay: smoothly decrease the learning rate over the rest of training, typically following a cosine schedule that ends at about 10% of the peak. This lets the model take small, refined steps in the late stages of training as it homes in on a good solution.

Peak learning rates are usually around 3×10−43 \times 10^{-4}3×10​−4​​ for small models (under 1B parameters) and 6×10−46 \times 10^{-4}6×10​−4​​ down to 1×10−41 \times 10^{-4}1×10​−4​​ for larger models. The exact value depends on batch size, model architecture, and optimizer.

def get_lr(step, max_lr, warmup_steps, total_steps, min_lr_ratio=0.1):
    if step < warmup_steps:
        return max_lr * step / warmup_steps
    progress = (step - warmup_steps) / (total_steps - warmup_steps)
    return max_lr * (min_lr_ratio + (1 - min_lr_ratio) * 0.5 * (1 + math.cos(math.pi * progress)))

The optimizer of choice is AdamW (Adam with decoupled weight decay) and has been since 2017. Recent work (Muon, 2024) has shown about 2× compute efficiency over AdamW on transformer training, and some 2026 runs are switching, but AdamW is still the safe default.

Gradient clipping: the boring step that saves runs

Real-world pre-training datasets are messy. Even after extensive cleaning, you’ll occasionally encounter a batch that produces unusually large gradients perhaps because the batch happened to include unusual tokens, repetitive content, or out-of-distribution text. If you let those gradients flow into the optimizer unchanged, they’ll push weights to extreme values, and the model may never recover. We call these loss spikes, and they are the dominant cause of training run instability.

The fix is gradient clipping. Compute the global L2 norm of all gradients across all parameters; if it exceeds a threshold (typically 1.0), scale all gradients down proportionally. This is one line of code (torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)) and it prevents the vast majority of training failures.

loss.backward()
torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), 1.0)
optimizer.step()

If your training run is spiking despite gradient clipping, the next things to look at are: batch composition (are you sampling unusual data?), learning rate (try lower), and weight initialization (very rare to mess up but check anyway).

Building GPT-2 Small: the pedagogical scale

A useful concrete exercise is to assemble GPT-2 Small (124M parameters) and train it on a small corpus to confirm the loss curve looks right. The architecture has 12 transformer layers, 12 attention heads, 768-dimensional embeddings, and 50,257-vocabulary tokenization (using GPT-2’s tokenizer). Pulling the parameter count apart:

Component Shape Parameters
Token embeddings (50257, 768) 38.6M
Position embeddings (1024, 768) 0.8M
Per block: attention (Q, K, V, O) 4 × (768, 768) 2.4M
Per block: FFN (up, down) (768, 3072) + (3072, 768) 4.7M
Per block: 2× LayerNorm 2 × 768 × 2 tiny
Total per block ~7.1M
Total (12 blocks) 85M
Grand total embeddings + blocks + final LN 124.4M

The output projection the linear layer that produces logits over the vocabulary is tied to the input embedding matrix (transposed). This is weight tying, and it has two benefits: it saves 38.6M parameters, and it improves model quality slightly because it forces the model to use the same vector space for understanding tokens (input) and predicting them (output). Every modern LLM uses weight tying.

In about 100 lines of PyTorch you can assemble this exact architecture, point it at a tokenized corpus (TinyShakespeare works for sanity-checking), and watch the loss go from 10.8 down to about 3.5 over a few thousand steps on a single GPU. It won’t write coherent prose at that scale, but the loss curve will show you exactly the shape every frontier model’s loss curve has during early training. The numbers are smaller, the dynamics are identical.

This is the moment we mentioned at the start of the chapter the abstraction layer dissolves. After you’ve watched a 124M model’s loss curve in real time, every press release about a new model’s “training run” becomes legible. They’re doing exactly this, just bigger.

Distributed training: when one GPU is not enough

Now we hit the wall that defines modern LLM engineering. A 7-billion-parameter model, in BF16 (2 bytes per parameter), needs 14 GB just for the weights. Adam optimizer stores two additional copies of every parameter as full FP32 floats (4 bytes each, twice for first and second moment estimates). That’s another 56 GB. Backward-pass gradients add 14 GB more. We’re at 84 GB before a single activation is allocated.

An NVIDIA A100 has 80 GB. An H100 has 80 GB. We’ve already exceeded a single GPU’s memory and we haven’t started the forward pass.

Now scale up: a 70B model in BF16 needs 140 GB for weights alone. 405B needs 810 GB. Llama 3 405B was trained on 16,384 H100 GPUs, and this wasn’t a flex it was the minimum to fit the work into memory and finish in a reasonable wall-clock time. Distributed training isn’t a performance optimization; it’s the only path to training models at modern scale.

There are three fundamental parallelism strategies, plus a fourth that combines them. We’ll walk through each.

Memory math: where every byte goes

Before parallelism, let’s name the consumers of GPU memory during training. For a model with NNN parameters in BF16 with Adam:

Component Per-parameter cost Total
Weights (BF16) 2 bytes 2N2N2N
Adam first moment mmm (FP32) 4 bytes 4N4N4N
Adam second moment vvv (FP32) 4 bytes 4N4N4N
Master weights (FP32) 4 bytes 4N4N4N
Gradients (BF16) 2 bytes 2N2N2N
Subtotal 16 bytes 16N16N16N
Activations (per layer) varies $\sim$2-8 GB per layer

The breakdown is striking. Weights are only 2N2N2N. The optimizer states (mmm, vvv, master weights) are 12N12N12N six times more memory than the weights themselves. Gradients add another 2N2N2N. The total is 16N16N16N before activations even enter the picture.

For 7B parameters, that’s 16×7×109=11216 \times 7 \times 10^9 = 11216×7×10​9​​=112 GB. Plus tens of GB for activations. Doesn’t fit.

The optimizer is the biggest memory consumer. If you reduce only the model size, you barely move the needle. The clever approaches reduce optimizer-state memory either by sharding it across GPUs (FSDP, ZeRO) or by using an optimizer with smaller per-parameter state (8-bit Adam, Lion, Sophia).

Mixed precision: FP32, FP16, BF16

Modern training uses multiple floating-point formats simultaneously. The trick: do most of the math in 16-bit (faster and half the memory) but maintain a master copy of the weights in 32-bit so accumulated updates don’t lose precision.

Format Total bits Exponent bits Mantissa bits Range
FP32 32 8 23 ±1038\pm 10^{38}±10​38​​
FP16 16 5 10 ±65,504\pm 65{,}504±65,504
BF16 16 8 7 ±1038\pm 10^{38}±10​38​​ (same as FP32)

FP16 has narrow dynamic range. Gradients with very small magnitudes (which happen all the time in deep networks) underflow to zero in FP16, killing learning signal. The fix is loss scaling: multiply the loss by a large constant before backward, divide gradients by the same constant before the optimizer step. This shifts the gradient distribution into FP16’s representable range. It works but is fiddly.

BF16 has the same exponent range as FP32 (8 bits) but cuts mantissa precision (7 bits vs FP32’s 23). It can represent the same range of magnitudes as FP32, so loss scaling is rarely needed. The trade-off is rougher precision in each individual number, but this turns out to barely matter for training because gradient noise dwarfs precision error.

Google’s TPUs use BF16 natively. NVIDIA’s A100, H100, and Blackwell support both. Since around 2022, the industry has converged on BF16 as the default for LLM training. FP16 still appears in some inference contexts (faster on specific hardware) but BF16 is the boring default for training.

Data parallelism: the simplest scaling strategy

The most basic distributed strategy: copy the entire model to N GPUs, split each training batch into N equal parts, each GPU runs forward and backward on its shard, then average the gradients across all GPUs (a collective operation called all-reduce), and every GPU applies the same averaged gradient to its weight copy.

┌──── GPU 1 ────┐  ┌──── GPU 2 ────┐ ... ┌──── GPU N ────┐
│ Full model    │  │ Full model    │     │ Full model    │
│ Batch shard 1 │  │ Batch shard 2 │     │ Batch shard N │
│ Compute grads │  │ Compute grads │     │ Compute grads │
└───────┬───────┘  └───────┬───────┘     └───────┬───────┘
        │                  │                     │
        └──────────────► All-reduce ◄─────────────┘
                  (average gradients)
                              │
        ┌──────────────────── │ ────────────────────┐
        ▼                     ▼                     ▼
    Update              Update                  Update
    (identical update on every GPU)

The win: linear throughput scaling in the number of GPUs. With N GPUs you process N times more data per step. The all-reduce overlaps with computation, so communication overhead is small.

The catch: every GPU still holds a complete copy of the model, optimizer states, and gradients. For a 70B model, every GPU needs 840 GB. Data parallelism does nothing to reduce per-GPU memory. It only reduces wall-clock training time.

For models that fit on a single GPU (under ~5B parameters in BF16 with Adam), pure data parallelism is the right strategy. For larger models, you need to also shard the model itself.

Tensor parallelism: splitting individual layers

Instead of replicating the full model on each GPU, tensor parallelism splits individual layers across GPUs. Consider a weight matrix of shape (8192,8192)(8192, 8192)(8192,8192) in a feedforward layer. With 4-way tensor parallelism, each GPU holds a (8192,2048)(8192, 2048)(8192,2048) shard. Each GPU multiplies the input by its shard, producing a partial result. The partial results are gathered (via all-reduce or all-gather) to produce the full output.

The math is simple: for column-parallel linear layers (splitting the output dimension), each GPU produces a different chunk of columns, and concatenation reconstructs the full result. For row-parallel (splitting the input dimension), each GPU produces a partial sum that needs to be all-reduced. In a transformer FFN, the first linear (expand from ddd to 4d4d4d) is column-parallel, the second linear (contract back to ddd) is row-parallel this avoids an all-reduce between the two layers.

def column_parallel_linear(x, weight_shard, gpu_id, num_gpus):
    # weight_shard has shape (d_in, d_out / num_gpus)
    partial = x @ weight_shard
    # All-gather to assemble the full output
    full_output = all_gather(partial, dim=-1)
    return full_output

def row_parallel_linear(x_shard, weight_shard, gpu_id, num_gpus):
    # x_shard has shape (..., d_in / num_gpus)
    # weight_shard has shape (d_in / num_gpus, d_out)
    partial = x_shard @ weight_shard
    # All-reduce to sum partial outputs
    full_output = all_reduce(partial)
    return full_output

The win: per-GPU memory drops linearly in the parallelism degree. A 70B model with 8-way tensor parallelism uses 8.75B parameters per GPU.

The catch: tensor parallelism requires fast inter-GPU communication. Each layer needs an all-reduce, which means a round-trip across the network for every linear layer. This is fine within a single node where GPUs are connected by NVLink (900 GB/s on H100). It is awful across nodes connected by InfiniBand (typically 400 Gb/s, about 50 GB/s, twenty times slower than NVLink).

In practice, tensor parallelism is almost always confined to within a single node typically 8-way TP across the 8 GPUs in one DGX-H100 box. Going wider than 8-way means crossing nodes, and the network becomes the bottleneck.

Pipeline parallelism: splitting layers across nodes

The third strategy splits the model by layers. GPU 1 runs layers 1-8. GPU 2 runs layers 9-16. GPU 3 runs layers 17-24. GPU 4 runs layers 25-32. Data flows through the pipeline: GPU 1 computes its layers, sends the activations to GPU 2, which computes its layers, sends to GPU 3, and so on.

The win: communication between GPUs is small only the activations at layer boundaries are sent, which is tiny compared to gradient or weight communication. This works well across nodes connected by ordinary InfiniBand.

The catch: pipeline bubbles. With four pipeline stages and one batch, while GPU 4 is computing the forward pass on the batch, GPUs 1, 2, and 3 are idle (they’ve already done their work). With naive pipelining, GPU utilization is only 1/N1/N1/N for NNN stages. For 16-stage pipeline, you’d waste 94% of compute.

The fix is micro-batching. Split each batch into M smaller micro-batches. GPU 1 starts processing micro-batch 2 as soon as it finishes forwarding micro-batch 1. This overlaps work across stages. With M micro-batches and N stages, the bubble drops to roughly (N−1)/M(N-1)/M(N−1)/M. With 16 micro-batches and 4 stages, the bubble is 3/16=19%3/16 = 19\%3/16=19% much better. Modern pipeline schedules (1F1B “one forward one backward”, interleaved 1F1B) push this even lower.

Pipeline parallelism is essential for very large models. Llama 3 405B uses 16-way pipeline parallelism across nodes.

FSDP and ZeRO: sharding everything

The most aggressive memory strategy is to shard every component weights, gradients, optimizer states across GPUs, with each GPU only holding 1/N1/N1/N of each.

This is what FSDP (Fully Sharded Data Parallel) in PyTorch and ZeRO (Zero Redundancy Optimizer) in DeepSpeed do. They’re the same idea developed independently by two teams and given different names.

The trick: before each layer’s forward pass, FSDP does an all-gather to collect the full parameters from all GPUs into local memory. After the forward pass, it discards the non-local parameters. During backward, it all-gathers again to reconstruct parameters for gradient computation. After backward, it does a reduce-scatter to distribute gradient shards so each GPU only stores 1/N1/N1/N of the gradients.

     ┌──────────────────────────────┐
     │ Parameter shards distributed │
     │ across N GPUs (1/N each)     │
     └──────────────┬───────────────┘
                    │
         ┌──── all-gather ────┐
         │                    │
         ▼                    ▼
     Layer forward       Layer forward      ...
     (full params)       (full params)
         │                    │
    discard non-local    discard non-local  ...
         │                    │
         ▼                    ▼
     Compute loss        Compute loss
         │                    │
         ▼                    ▼
     Layer backward      Layer backward
     (re-all-gather)     (re-all-gather)
         │                    │
         └─── reduce-scatter ──┘
              (gradient shards)
                    │
                    ▼
             Optimizer update
            (each GPU updates
             its local shard)

The math for a 70B model on 8 GPUs:

Component Without FSDP With FSDP (8 GPUs)
Weights (BF16) 140 GB / GPU 17.5 GB / GPU
Adam states (FP32) 560 GB / GPU 70 GB / GPU
Gradients (BF16) 140 GB / GPU 17.5 GB / GPU
Total 840 GB 105 GB

Without FSDP, a 70B model can’t fit on a single 80GB GPU. With FSDP on 8 GPUs, each GPU uses 105 GB wait, that still doesn’t fit. You’d need 16 GPUs to drop below 80 GB per GPU, or you combine FSDP with activation checkpointing (recompute activations during backward instead of storing them trades 30% extra compute for substantial memory savings).

ZeRO has three sharding stages, each progressively more aggressive:

  • ZeRO-1: Shard optimizer states only.
  • ZeRO-2: Shard optimizer states + gradients.
  • ZeRO-3: Shard optimizer states + gradients + parameters. Equivalent to FSDP.

DeepSpeed also offers ZeRO-Offload (move optimizer states to CPU RAM, slower but cheaper and larger) and ZeRO-Infinity (offload to NVMe SSDs). These trade compute speed for memory capacity useful when you’re training a model that just barely doesn’t fit.

3D parallelism: combining all three

Real frontier-scale training combines all three parallelisms. Llama 3 405B on 16,384 H100s used:

  • 8-way tensor parallelism within each node (across 8 GPUs connected by NVLink).
  • 16-way pipeline parallelism across nodes (16 pipeline stages along the model’s depth).
  • 128-way data parallelism across the remaining dimension.

The math: 8×16×128=16,3848 \times 16 \times 128 = 16{,}3848×16×128=16,384 GPUs. Each GPU sees a different data shard (data parallel), holds one slice of each layer (tensor parallel), and computes a different group of layers (pipeline parallel). The three dimensions are orthogonal, and the framework (typically Megatron-LM at this scale, or PyTorch’s native distributed primitives) handles the combinatorics.

Coordinating 16,384 GPUs to all stay in sync, recover from individual GPU failures (which happen multiple times per day at this scale), and finish a 54-day training run is one of the largest-scale distributed-systems engineering challenges in the world. Meta and the other frontier labs have dedicated teams just for the training infrastructure.

DeepSeek-V3: the efficiency play

DeepSeek-V3 (December 2024) trained a model competitive with Llama 3 405B for an estimated $5.6M a tenth of Meta’s reported $61M. The architectural innovations that enabled this are worth naming because they’re influencing every 2026 training run.

Mixture of Experts: only 37B of the 671B total parameters activate per token. Active parameters are what determine FLOPs per token, so DeepSeek’s per-token training compute is comparable to a 37B dense model even though the total parameter count is much higher.

Auxiliary-loss-free load balancing (we covered this in Chapter 14): better expert routing without an extra training loss term.

Multi-token prediction: train the model to predict multiple tokens ahead instead of just the next one. Improves data efficiency.

FP8 training: most of the training is in FP8 instead of BF16. Cuts memory and compute roughly in half on Hopper-generation GPUs.

Multi-head Latent Attention: compresses K and V to a low-rank latent, dramatically reducing KV-cache memory during inference (we covered this in Chapter 14).

The combination delivered competitive quality at roughly 1/10th the cost. This isn’t a hardware advantage DeepSeek was using H800s, which are H100s with reduced memory bandwidth. It’s pure architectural efficiency. We covered the architectural details in Chapter 14, and we’ll see them again in Chapter 20 when we visit modern LLM architectures end-to-end.

The complete pipeline: stages, manifests, and gates

Everything we’ve covered so far tokenizer, data, pre-training, distributed scaling is one stage of one pipeline. Each stage has its own conventions, its own outputs, its own seed, its own logs. A frontier training run is not a notebook. It’s a multi-week orchestration of dozens of stages, on tens of thousands of GPUs, where one corrupted checkpoint or one data contamination event can cost you weeks of wall-clock and millions of dollars.

The way teams survive this is through pipeline hygiene. Every stage has a deterministic input, a deterministic output, a manifest, a content-addressed hash, and an evaluation gate. Let’s walk through the pieces.

Twelve stages, one DAG

Every Phase 10 lesson is one stage of the pipeline, and the dependencies form a DAG (directed acyclic graph):

01 Tokenizer    →    02 Production tokenizer
                          │
                          ▼
                     03 Sharded dataset
                          │
                          ▼
                     04 Base checkpoint
                          │
                          ▼
                     05 Scaled training
                          │
                          ▼
                     06 SFT checkpoint
                       │     │
                ┌──────┘     └──────┐
                ▼                   ▼
          07 RM + PPO          08 DPO
                └──────┐     ┌──────┘
                       ▼     ▼
                   09 CAI / refinement
                          │
              ┌───────────┼───────────┐
              ▼           ▼           ▼
        10 Eval    11 Quantization   12 Serving
              │           │           │
              └───────────┼───────────┘
                          ▼
                      Ship gate

A change in stage 02 (tokenizer) invalidates every downstream artifact you have to redo everything. A change in stage 10 (eval) invalidates only the ship decision; nothing has to be retrained. Knowing the dependency graph tells you what a partial failure costs.

The manifest: a single file that describes the run

A manifest is one file (usually YAML) that describes a training run completely enough to replay it. Nothing the pipeline produces depends on state outside the manifest. Without this discipline, training a model becomes a nightmare of “wait, which version of the data did this run use?” three weeks later when you’re trying to debug a regression.

pipeline_version: 1.2.3
seed: 42
git_commit: a1b2c3d4
budget_usd: 100000
gates:
  mmlu: ">= baseline + 0.5"
  humaneval: ">= baseline + 1.0"
  truthfulqa: ">= baseline"
  safety_refusal_rate: "<= 0.05"
  kl_from_reference: "<= 25.0"
  cost_total_usd: "<= 100000"
stages:
  01_tokenizer:
    recipe: bpe_128k_byte_level
    input_hash: sha256:abc123...
    output_hash: sha256:def456...
    wall_clock_sec: 3600
    cost_usd: 12
  02_dataset:
    sources: [common_crawl_2024_05, wikipedia_20240601, github_2024]
    mix_ratios: {web: 0.50, code: 0.25, books: 0.13, math: 0.08, multilingual: 0.04}
    total_tokens: 15600000000000
    input_hash: sha256:def456...
    output_hash: sha256:ghi789...
    ...

Every stage has an input hash (matching the previous stage’s output) and an output hash. The manifest’s most important property: the output hash of stage N is the input hash of stage N+1. Any deviation halts the pipeline. This is how you catch data corruption early. It’s also how a teammate on a different continent verifies that their replay produced the same artifact as yours.

The output isn’t necessarily bit-identical between runs distributed training has non-deterministic floating-point reduce orders that produce 1e-5 numerical drift. What matters is equivalence in downstream metrics: same loss curve shape, same eval scores. This is the crucial distinction between deterministic (bit-identical) and reproducible (equivalent metrics). Modern LLM training is reproducible but not deterministic, and the manifest is what makes reproducibility verifiable.

Artifact stores: content-addressed, immutable

Pipeline outputs go into an artifact store, typically S3, R2, or GCS. Two rules:

  1. Content-addressed. Artifacts are stored by SHA-256 of their contents, not by name. A filename like latest.pt is a foot-gun what does “latest” mean three weeks from now? A name like ckpt-7b-step-20000-sha256:abc123.safetensors is a contract.

  2. Immutable. Once an artifact is written, never overwrite it. If you produce a new version, give it a new hash. If a corrupt artifact gets uploaded, leave it; the manifest will reject anything that doesn’t match expected hashes anyway.

The artifact store is the single source of truth for everything. The orchestrator looks up inputs by hash. The eval gate references artifact hashes. The serving infrastructure pulls weights by hash. There’s never any ambiguity about which version of which thing you’re operating on.

Eval gates: the difference between training and shipping

“Training finished” is not the same as “ready to ship.” The eval gate is the difference a set of numeric thresholds that the trained artifact must clear before it can be promoted to production.

gates:
  mmlu: ">= baseline + 0.5"           # No regression on knowledge
  humaneval: ">= baseline + 1.0"      # Code capability must improve
  truthfulqa: ">= baseline"           # Don't drop on factuality
  safety_refusal_rate: "<= 0.05"      # Refuse <5% of safe queries
  kl_from_reference: "<= 25.0"        # Alignment hasn't drifted
  cost_total_usd: "<= 100000"         # Budget compliance
