Every gate is a numeric threshold. No “looks good” gates, no subjective sign-offs. If every gate passes, the artifact is marked shippable. If any gate fails, the run is held pending an explicit override by a named reviewer which itself is logged in the manifest.

Two gates catch most disasters. A regression gate (the new model must be at least as good as the previous on core benchmarks) catches training bugs. A KL budget gate (the aligned policy must not have drifted too far from its reference) catches alignment overcooking. Every production pipeline has both.

Cost tracking: don’t find out from your CFO

A frontier training run has a dollar number attached. Budget discipline happens in two places:

Pre-run estimation. From the manifest, compute expected FLOPs (for pre-training: roughly 6×N×D6 \times N \times D6×N×D where NNN is parameters and DDD is tokens), expected GPU-hours (FLOPs / peak throughput / model FLOP utilization), and dollar cost at current cloud rates. If the estimate exceeds the budget gate, the pipeline refuses to start. Better to fail at planning time than at hour 36 of training.

In-run tracking. Per-stage wall-clock and cost are logged to the manifest as the run progresses. After every stage, the remaining budget is checked. If a stage overran, the next stage’s gate is re-evaluated with the new remaining budget. You never find out you’re out of money when the AWS bill arrives.

Rollback plan: write it before you start

Before the run begins, write down what happens on failure of each stage. Three categories:

  • Cheap to re-run (hours): tokenizer training, eval, quantization, inference server. Just re-run.
  • Medium (days): SFT, DPO, CAI. Keep the base model; re-run only the alignment stages.
  • Expensive (weeks, millions of dollars): pre-training. The rollback plan here is not “re-run.” It’s “use the last good checkpoint and re-run the cheaper downstream stages with revised data.”

Because stage dependencies are typed and hashed, the orchestrator can compute the rollback set automatically: if stage 6 fails, invalidate 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12. Naming this in advance avoids improvising while the team is exhausted at 4 AM in week 3 of the run.

Reproducibility versus determinism, one more time

These are not the same:

  • Reproducible = same manifest + same code + same infrastructure → checkpoint with equivalent downstream metrics.
  • Deterministic = bit-identical output.

Modern LLM training is reproducible but not deterministic. Distributed training’s reduce-order non-determinism, GPU kernel non-determinism (cuBLAS, FlashAttention), and mixed-precision rounding combine to produce floats that differ at the 1e-5 level between runs. This is fine for final metrics, which don’t move. It’s fatal if you’re trying to debug with bit-level diffs. Always log every stage’s input hash, output hash, and headline metrics if those match, the run is “reproduced” even if the weights aren’t bit-identical.

What a frontier run actually looks like

A few real numbers to anchor everything we’ve covered.

Llama 3 405B (Meta, July 2024). 405 billion parameters. 15.6 trillion training tokens. 16,384 NVIDIA H100 GPUs. Approximately 30 million GPU-hours total. 54 days of wall-clock training. Reported total compute cost approximately $61 million. Used 8-way tensor parallelism, 16-way pipeline parallelism, 128-way data parallelism. Trained with BF16 weights, FP32 master, AdamW optimizer.

DeepSeek-V3 (December 2024). 671 billion total parameters with 37B active per token (Mixture of Experts). 14.8 trillion training tokens. 2,048 NVIDIA H800 GPUs. Approximately 2.79 million GPU-hours. Approximately 56 days of wall-clock training. Reported cost approximately $5.6 million. Used MoE with auxiliary-loss-free balancing, multi-head latent attention, FP8 training for most of the run, DualPipe parallelism scheme.

Llama 3 8B and 70B (smaller variants of the same family). 8B: trained on 15T tokens. 70B: trained on 15T tokens. Same training infrastructure, scaled down.

What’s striking about these numbers is the cost difference. DeepSeek delivered comparable quality at roughly 1/10th the compute by being more architecturally clever. The frontier labs are taking note. Most 2026 training runs we hear about combine MoE, FP8, more aggressive distillation, and more efficient attention variants. The era of “just throw more H100s at it” is ending.

The one thing that hasn’t changed: data quality dominates everything. Both Llama 3 and DeepSeek-V3 invested heavily in data filtering, deduplication, and synthetic-data generation. The most important sentence in the Llama 3 paper is something like: “Most of our gains came from improvements in data, not architecture.”

Sharp edges across the pipeline

Some things that bite teams during real training runs.

Tokenizer mistakes are forever. Once you’ve pre-trained a model with a specific tokenizer, you cannot change it without retraining from scratch. Tokenizer bugs (unintended whitespace handling, missing special tokens, multilingual tax) are caught only after you’ve spent millions of GPU-hours pre-training.

Data contamination is the silent killer. If your eval set leaks into your training set, your evaluation scores become meaningless. The standard defense is exact-match and 13-gram overlap checks against eval datasets, but contamination from synthetic data and long-tail web data is harder to catch. Always hold out a private eval set the public never sees.

Loss spikes happen. Even with gradient clipping, distributed training produces occasional loss spikes from anomalous batches. If a spike is large enough, it can permanently damage the model. The fix is checkpoint-and-restart: save model state every 500-2000 steps, and if a catastrophic spike occurs, restart from the last good checkpoint with the offending batch excluded.

Hardware fails. At 16,384 GPUs, individual GPU failures occur multiple times per day. Llama 3’s training had 419 unexpected interruptions over 54 days. The infrastructure has to handle this checkpoints, automatic restart, dead-GPU exclusion. Without resilience, no large run finishes.

The optimizer is not the place to be clever. AdamW with linear warmup and cosine decay has been the default for nearly a decade for a reason. Most “novel optimizers” have failed to replicate at scale. Muon (2024) is the first credible challenger in years. Don’t invent your own optimizer for a frontier run.

Mixed precision needs care. BF16 is more forgiving than FP16, but neither is FP32. Loss can occasionally NaN. Master weights in FP32 are non-negotiable. Some operations (softmax, layer norm) should run in FP32 even when the rest is in BF16.

Activation checkpointing is almost free. Recomputing activations during backward instead of storing them adds about 33% to compute time but saves enormous activation memory. For most training runs at modern scale, activation checkpointing pays for itself many times over.

What you’d actually use

The 2026 default training stack:

Component Default
Tokenizer training HuggingFace tokenizers library
Tokenizer at inference tiktoken (for OpenAI compatibility) or HuggingFace transformers
Data deduplication datasketch (MinHash) or NVIDIA’s NeMo Curator
Data filtering Trafilatura + fastText + perplexity filter
Pre-training framework PyTorch + native FSDP2, or Megatron-LM for largest scale
Distributed scheduling torchrun for small runs, SLURM or Ray for large clusters
Mixed precision PyTorch native AMP, BF16
Optimizer AdamW (boring default) or Muon (2× faster, less battle-tested)
Experiment tracking Weights & Biases or MLflow
Artifact storage S3 or Cloud Storage with content-addressed naming
Pipeline orchestration Custom Python orchestrator (200 lines), not Airflow
Pre-training data FineWeb-Edu, RefinedWeb, or Common Crawl + custom filtering

A particular note on Megatron-LM (NVIDIA): for any training run beyond about 30B parameters, this is the de facto framework. It implements 3D parallelism, tensor/pipeline/data parallelism primitives, and has been battle-tested on the largest training runs in the world. PyTorch’s native FSDP2 is catching up but still trails in terms of features needed for true frontier-scale.

For most readers of this book, the realistic scenario isn’t training a frontier model from scratch. It’s fine-tuning an existing open base model (Llama 3, Qwen 2.5, Mistral, etc.) on your domain data. That’s a much smaller engineering problem typically a single multi-GPU node, a few hundred to a few thousand GPU-hours, and minutes-to-hours of wall-clock time. The pipeline machinery we’ve described still applies, just at smaller scale.

Where this goes next

Chapter 18 picks up exactly where this chapter ends. We have a pre-trained base model. It writes grammatical text, completes prompts in pattern-matched ways, and is utterly useless as an assistant. It will refuse nothing, hallucinate freely, and have no concept of “user instructions” versus “internet completions.” The next chapter is how you turn it into something that can be deployed: SFT, RLHF, DPO, constitutional AI, the full alignment toolkit. Most of the heavy machinery from this chapter distributed training, mixed precision, manifests, artifact stores applies in alignment too, just at smaller scale.

Chapter 19 turns to inference. The model we’ve just built is large, slow, and memory-hungry. Production deployment requires KV caching, paged attention, continuous batching, speculative decoding, quantization to 4-bit or 8-bit. Chapter 19 covers all of that.

Chapter 20 returns to architectural variants MLA, native sparse attention, multi-token prediction, hybrid SSM-transformer architectures. The cutting edge of what’s being explored beyond the standard transformer recipe.

For now, what you should take away: building an LLM is a systems engineering problem dressed in a deep-learning costume. The architecture is the easy part. The hard part is the data, the distributed training, and the discipline that prevents a single corrupted checkpoint from ruining a $61 million run. Most teams don’t appreciate how mechanical and structured this process is until they’ve watched a real training run from inside. After they have, the abstraction layer between them and the model is gone, and they can’t go back.

Read the Llama 3 technical report. Read the DeepSeek-V3 paper. Read the FineWeb-Edu blog post on data filtering. Re-read this chapter when you do. Every line in those papers will make sense, and the ones that don’t will be specific implementation details rather than conceptual mysteries.

You can build an LLM. The frontier labs are not magicians; they’re patient engineers with very large GPU budgets. The patience and the engineering are learnable. The GPU budget is the only thing in short supply.

Chapter 18 Aligning an LLM

You finished Chapter 17. Pre-training is done. Hundreds of millions of dollars of compute have produced a checkpoint that can predict the next token in any sentence with uncanny fluency. You load it up, type “What is the capital of France?”, and the model produces “What is the capital of Germany? What is the capital of Spain? What is the capital of…”

The base model isn’t broken. It’s behaving exactly as it was trained to. Pre-training optimized the model to imitate text on the internet, and on the internet, sentences that start with “What is the capital of France?” are usually followed by more questions, because the document containing that sentence is probably a quiz or a list. Your base model has learned that pattern perfectly. It cannot do the thing you want answer the question because nobody trained it to.

This is the alignment gap. The same architecture, the same weights, the same tokenizer that powers ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini, is utterly useless as an assistant in its raw post-pre-training form. The path from base model to deployable assistant is what this chapter covers, and it is the most consequential set of techniques in modern AI engineering. The architectural decisions in Chapter 17 determine the model’s capability ceiling. The alignment decisions in this chapter determine whether the model is useful.

We’re going to walk through four canonical post-training stages, in the order they were invented and in the order you’d run them: supervised fine-tuning (SFT), reward modeling and RLHF, direct preference optimization (DPO), and the modern self-improvement loops (Constitutional AI and GRPO). Each one solves a problem the previous one couldn’t. Each one has displaced part of the previous, but none has fully replaced its predecessors the modern alignment pipeline at a frontier lab uses pieces of all four.

By the end of this chapter, you’ll know exactly what produces the difference between GPT-3 (June 2020, base model with 175B parameters and an instruction-following score of basically zero) and ChatGPT (November 2022, the same architecture and pretraining, with one additional training stage that turned the world upside down). You’ll also know why DPO has quietly displaced PPO-based RLHF as the boring default in 2026, why Constitutional AI matters more for safety than helpfulness, and why DeepSeek-R1’s “aha moment” matters even though the underlying algorithm (GRPO) is just PPO with a different baseline.

This chapter is long for the same reason Chapter 17 was long: alignment is where the rubber meets the road. Architecture is a research problem. Pre-training is a systems problem. Alignment is both, plus a labor problem (humans labeling preferences), plus a values problem (what should the model refuse?), plus a measurement problem (how do you know the model got better?). All five problems show up in the same training run and all five interact.

The post-training landscape

Before we dive into specific techniques, let’s name the four stages and what each one does.

Pretrained base model  ──► SFT  ──► Reward Modeling + PPO/DPO  ──► Constitutional/GRPO  ──► Aligned model
   (predicts next         (follows         (encodes human               (refines using
    token in any text)    instructions)    quality preferences)        AI feedback or rules)

Stage 1: SFT (Supervised Fine-Tuning). Take the base model and fine-tune it on a dataset of high-quality instruction-response pairs. This is plain next-token prediction with a small twist (loss masking on instruction tokens), and it converts a “next-token completer” into an “instruction follower.” The model now responds to questions instead of continuing them.

Stage 2: Preference learning (RLHF or DPO). SFT teaches the model what a response looks like. Preference learning teaches it which responses are better. You collect pairs of responses to the same prompt, have humans pick the better one, and train the model to assign higher probability to preferred responses. This is what produces the difference between “technically correct but boring” and “genuinely helpful.”

Stage 3: Self-improvement (CAI / GRPO). The third stage uses AI feedback or programmatic verification to refine the model further. Constitutional AI uses the model itself to critique and revise its outputs against written principles. GRPO uses verifier rewards (did the math answer match? did the code pass tests?) to train reasoning capabilities. Both replace expensive human preference data with cheaper signal.

Stage 4: Evaluation. Continuously throughout the pipeline. You can’t trust any single metric, public benchmarks have all been gamed, and the only eval that matters in the end is the one tailored to your specific deployment.

The frontier labs in 2026 don’t pick one stage and skip the others. They run all four. Anthropic’s Claude pipeline includes multiple SFT passes, an RLHF stage, a Constitutional AI pass, and continuous evaluation. Meta’s Llama 3 pipeline includes SFT, RLHF, DPO, and rejection-sampling fine-tuning. Each technique handles a different failure mode of the previous, and the layered approach is why modern aligned models are so much better than any individual technique would suggest.

Let’s walk through each stage in depth.

Supervised fine-tuning: from “completer” to “assistant”

The simplest, oldest, and most underestimated alignment technique is SFT. It’s the first thing you do after pre-training, and most of the way the model “feels” to a user its tone, its formatting habits, its willingness to refuse certain requests, its default style of answering is set during SFT, not during the more elaborate stages that follow.

What SFT actually changes

A pre-trained model has learned the world. It has read a substantial fraction of the public internet, books, code repositories, scientific papers, and synthetic instructional content. The factual knowledge that the capital of France is Paris is in there somewhere multiple times, encoded redundantly in the weights. What it lacks is the behavior pattern of producing that fact when a user asks for it.

Pre-training optimizes the model to imitate the statistics of internet text. Internet text contains many things that look like questions quiz lists, FAQ pages, exam preps, conversations on forums and the typical continuation of “What is the capital of France?” on the internet is another question, not an answer. The model has dutifully learned this. To make it answer questions, you need to show it a different distribution: one where questions are followed by answers, not by more questions.

That’s all SFT is. Take the base model. Fine-tune it on (instruction, response) pairs. Use the same cross-entropy loss as pre-training, with one twist (we’ll get to it). The model already knows the facts. SFT teaches it the behavior of producing those facts when asked.

This is why SFT is shockingly cheap. Stanford’s Alpaca, in March 2023, fine-tuned Llama 7B on 52,000 (instruction, response) pairs generated by GPT-3.5. Total compute cost: around six hundred US dollars. The result was a chatbot that could follow instructions, answer questions, and hold conversations. Not as polished as ChatGPT, but recognizably the same kind of thing a model that responds rather than continues.

Meta’s Llama 2 Chat used roughly 27,000 high-quality (instruction, response) examples for its SFT stage. The LIMA paper in 2023 went further, showing that 1,000 carefully curated examples could be enough. The pattern is clear: SFT doesn’t teach knowledge, it teaches behavior, and behavior generalizes from a small number of examples.

The chat template

To train SFT, you need a way to encode “this part is the user asking, this part is the assistant responding” in the token stream. The model doesn’t see JSON; it sees tokens. So we use special tokens to mark role boundaries.

There are three common formats:

Alpaca format (Stanford):

Below is an instruction that describes a task. Write a response that completes the request.

### Instruction:
What is the capital of France?

### Response:
The capital of France is Paris.

ShareGPT format (community):

{"conversations": [
  {"from": "human", "value": "What is the capital of France?"},
  {"from": "gpt", "value": "The capital of France is Paris."}
]}

ChatML format (OpenAI’s, now used by Qwen, many open models):

<|im_start|>system
You are a helpful assistant.<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>user
What is the capital of France?<|im_end|>
<|im_start|>assistant
The capital of France is Paris.<|im_end|>

All three encode the same information who said what with different delimiters. The Llama 3 chat template uses something different again (<|start_header_id|>, <|end_header_id|>, <|eot_id|>). Every model has its own format, and the format is part of the model’s contract. Using the wrong format means feeding the model out-of-distribution input, and the outputs become unreliable in subtle ways.

This is why Chapter 17’s section on chat templates was so emphatic: in production, always use the model’s official tokenizer’s apply_chat_template method. Never format conversations by hand. The single most common cause of “my deployed agent is acting weird” is a chat template mismatch.

The masked loss: the technical detail tutorials skip

This is the most important implementation detail in SFT, and most introductory material glosses over it.

In pre-training, you compute cross-entropy loss on every token in the sequence. The model learns to predict every next token, and the loss is averaged over all positions.

In SFT, you compute loss only on the response tokens. Instruction tokens contribute to the forward pass (the model needs to see them as context) but not to the loss.

Why? Because you want the model to learn to respond to instructions, not to generate them. If you compute loss on the instruction tokens, you’re training the model to predict things like “What is the capital of France?” itself as if the model were the user asking the question. This wastes gradient signal on a behavior you don’t want, and worse, it can confuse the model’s role identity.

In code, this becomes a loss mask: 1 for response tokens, 0 for instruction tokens, multiplied element-wise into the per-token loss before averaging.

def compute_sft_loss(logits, targets, loss_mask):
    # logits: (batch, seq_len, vocab)
    # targets: (batch, seq_len)
    # loss_mask: (batch, seq_len) - 1 on response tokens, 0 elsewhere
    per_token_loss = F.cross_entropy(
        logits.reshape(-1, logits.size(-1)),
        targets.reshape(-1),
        reduction='none'
    ).reshape(targets.shape)
    masked_loss = per_token_loss * loss_mask
    num_response_tokens = loss_mask.sum()
    return masked_loss.sum() / num_response_tokens.clamp(min=1)

The denominator is num_response_tokens, not seq_len. If you divide by total sequence length, longer instructions dilute the gradient signal a 200-token instruction with a 10-token response would have its loss averaged over 210 positions instead of 10. Dividing by response token count fixes this and ensures every response token contributes equally regardless of its instruction length.

This single decision masking the loss on instructions is what separates SFT from naive fine-tuning. Get it wrong and your model starts generating instructions back at the user. Get it right and you have a working assistant.

Hyperparameters: nothing like pre-training

SFT’s hyperparameters look almost nothing like pre-training’s. You’re not learning new things; you’re nudging an existing model. The learning rate is much smaller, the training run is much shorter, and the data is much smaller.

Parameter Pre-training (Llama 2 7B) SFT (Llama 2 Chat)
Learning rate 3e-4 2e-5
Epochs 1 2-3
Batch size 4M tokens 64 examples
Warmup steps 2,000 0-100
Weight decay 0.1 0.0-0.1
Data size 2T tokens 27,000 examples

The learning rate is 15× lower than pre-training. This is critical and the source of more SFT mistakes than any other detail. A high learning rate during fine-tuning destroys the pre-trained knowledge the model “forgets” what it learned during pre-training and overfits to the small fine-tuning dataset. This phenomenon is called catastrophic forgetting, and it’s the dominant failure mode of naive fine-tuning.

Two-three epochs is the sweet spot. Less and the model doesn’t pick up the new behavior. More and the model starts memorizing training examples verbatim especially if the dataset is small. We’ve seen models trained for 10+ epochs on a 1,000-example dataset that could literally recite the dataset back.

Catastrophic forgetting: the boundary you have to respect

Catastrophic forgetting is what happens when fine-tuning destroys the model’s general capabilities. Train too long with too high a learning rate on instruction data, and the model loses its ability to write code, do math, or produce creative text. It becomes very good at the specific format of its training data and terrible at everything else.

The technical reason: gradient updates accumulate. Each step pushes the weights toward better fitting the SFT data. After enough steps, the weights have moved far enough that the pre-trained knowledge which was encoded as a delicate balance of weights across billions of parameters is partially overwritten by the new task-specific patterns.

Three mitigations:

  1. Low learning rate. 1e-5 to 5e-5. Smaller updates mean less destruction of pre-trained features.
  2. Short training. 1-3 epochs. Stop before the model overfits.
  3. Mix in pre-training data. Llama 2 Chat mixed 2-5% raw pre-training data into the SFT dataset. This “reminds” the model of its general capabilities while it learns the new behavior.

A good diagnostic: after SFT, evaluate the model on a held-out general benchmark (MMLU, HumanEval, perplexity on raw text) and compare to the base model. If the score dropped by more than 10-15%, your SFT is too aggressive.

Data quality is everything

The Stanford Alpaca paper showed SFT works on synthetic data. The LIMA paper showed it works on as few as 1,000 examples. Both papers underscored a deeper point: data quality matters more than data quantity for SFT.

Properties of good SFT data:

  • Diverse: covers the range of tasks you want the model to handle, not just one type.
  • Clean: no formatting errors, no broken Unicode, no truncated responses.
  • Aligned with target style: if you want the model to be concise, the responses should be concise. If you want it to cite sources, responses should cite sources.
  • Refusal examples: include examples where the assistant should refuse (harmful requests, requests outside scope). Without these, the model will try to comply with everything.

Where does the data come from in practice? Three sources:

  • Synthetic generation: use a strong existing model (GPT-4, Claude) to generate (instruction, response) pairs. This is what Alpaca did. Cheap and fast, but inherits the biases of the source model.
  • Human curation: pay annotators to write or edit responses. Slow, expensive, high quality.
  • Existing high-quality datasets: UltraChat, OpenHermes, OpenOrca, the Tülu mixture. These are community-assembled SFT datasets, often with millions of examples curated from multiple sources.

The 2026 default for SFT data is a blend: a few hundred thousand examples from public datasets (UltraChat-style), plus tens of thousands of high-quality human-written examples for your specific use case, plus a few thousand refusal/safety examples. The exact ratio is one of the things that distinguishes a well-aligned model from a mediocre one, and it’s tuned via ablation experiments.

RLHF: encoding human preference into the model

SFT teaches the model to follow instructions. It does not teach the model which responses are better. That’s the next problem.

Consider two responses to “Explain quantum computing”:

Response A: “Quantum computing uses qubits that can exist in superposition, meaning they can represent both 0 and 1 simultaneously. This allows quantum computers to process certain calculations exponentially faster than classical computers. Key algorithms include Shor’s algorithm for factoring large numbers and Grover’s algorithm for searching unsorted databases.”

Response B: “Quantum computing is a type of computing that uses quantum mechanical phenomena. It was first proposed in the 1980s. Richard Feynman suggested that quantum systems could be simulated by quantum computers. The field has grown significantly since then. Many companies are now working on quantum computers.”

Both are factually correct. Both are grammatically sound. Both follow the instruction. SFT trained on either one would treat it as equally valid. But Response A is clearly better more concise, more informative, better structured. A human would prefer A every time.

SFT can’t capture this distinction. To teach the model to produce A over B, you need a training signal that’s specifically about preference between alternatives. That’s RLHF.

The three-stage RLHF pipeline

Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback was proposed by Christiano et al. in 2017 (originally for Atari) and adapted to LLMs by OpenAI for InstructGPT (2022). It’s the recipe that turned GPT-3 into ChatGPT. Three stages, run sequentially:

Stage 1: SFT (which we just covered). Get an instruction-following base.

Stage 2: Reward Modeling. Collect human preference data pairs of responses to the same prompt, labeled by humans as “A is better” or “B is better.” Train a model to predict these preferences.

Stage 3: PPO (Proximal Policy Optimization). Use the reward model to score model outputs during generation. Update the model to produce higher-scoring outputs. Add a KL divergence penalty against the SFT reference to prevent the model from drifting too far.

SFT model ────────► Frozen reference (π_ref)
   │                       │
   │                       │ KL penalty
   ▼                       ▼
Trainable policy (π_θ) ──► Generate response ──► Reward Model ──► Score r
                                                                    │
                                                                    ▼
                                          PPO update on (π_θ): maximize r - β·KL(π_θ || π_ref)

The Stage 2 reward model is the centerpiece. Let’s look at it in detail because it’s where most of the magic and most of the problems live.

Reward modeling with Bradley-Terry

The reward model is a transformer with a scalar output head, trained on pairwise preference data. Conceptually, it takes a (prompt, response) pair and outputs a single number indicating “how good is this response?”

The training signal comes from comparisons, not from absolute scores. Humans are terrible at giving absolute quality scores (“Is this response a 7.3 or a 7.5 out of 10?”) but very good at relative comparisons (“Is A better than B?”). The Bradley-Terry model converts relative comparisons into absolute scores.

For a preference pair (x,y+,y−)(x, y_+, y_-)(x,y​+​​,y​−​​) where y+y_+y​+​​ is preferred over y−y_-y​−​​, the Bradley-Terry assumption is:

P(y+≻y−∣x)=σ(R(x,y+)−R(x,y−))P(y_+ \succ y_- \mid x) = \sigma(R(x, y_+) - R(x, y_-)) P(y​+​​≻y​−​​∣x)=σ(R(x,y​+​​)−R(x,y​−​​))

where σ\sigmaσ is the sigmoid and RRR is the reward. The training loss is the negative log-likelihood:

LRM=−E(x,y+,y−)[logσ(R(x,y+)−R(x,y−))]\mathcal{L}_\text{RM} = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_+, y_-)} \left[ \log \sigma(R(x, y_+) - R(x, y_-)) \right] L​RM​​=−E​(x,y​+​​,y​−​​)​​[logσ(R(x,y​+​​)−R(x,y​−​​))]

This loss pushes the reward model to assign a higher score to the preferred response than to the rejected one, with the gap proportional to how confidently the human picked.

from trl import RewardTrainer, RewardConfig
from transformers import AutoModelForSequenceClassification, AutoTokenizer

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("./sft-checkpoint")
rm = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained("./sft-checkpoint", num_labels=1)

trainer = RewardTrainer(
    model=rm, tokenizer=tok, train_dataset=preference_data,
    args=RewardConfig(output_dir="./rm", num_train_epochs=1, learning_rate=1e-5),
)
trainer.train()

The reward model is typically initialized from the SFT model same backbone, same tokenizer, just replace the language-modeling head with a single scalar projection. This is a parameter-efficient choice; the reward model inherits all of the SFT model’s understanding of language and just learns to map that understanding to a scalar quality score.

How much data does the reward model need? OpenAI’s InstructGPT used 33,000 preference comparisons from 40 contractors. Each comparison took about 5 minutes (~2,750 hours of human labor total). Llama 2 Chat used over a million comparisons. Anthropic’s RLHF paper used 22,000. The exact volume depends on how complex the preference signal is and how big your reward model is.

A practical detail: the reward model should be at least as big as the policy you’re training. A small reward model can’t faithfully score a larger policy’s outputs it doesn’t have enough capacity to capture the nuances of good vs bad responses. Anthropic’s 2022 paper used a 52B reward model. InstructGPT used a 6B reward model to train a 175B policy, and OpenAI noted in retrospect that a larger reward model would have been better.

PPO with KL penalty: the load-bearing details

Now Stage 3. We have an SFT model and a reward model. We want to update the SFT model to produce higher-reward responses. The challenge: if we just optimize for reward, the model will find adversarial outputs that score high on the (imperfect) reward model but are nonsense.

This is reward hacking, and it’s the dominant failure mode of naive RLHF. The reward model was trained on a finite dataset of human preferences. It has blind spots. The policy will exploit them. We covered this in Chapter 16 in the RL context; here it’s the central concern.

The fix is the KL divergence penalty. We want the trained policy πθ\pi_\thetaπ​θ​​ to stay close to the reference policy πref\pi_\text{ref}π​ref​​ (the frozen SFT model). The objective becomes:

maxθE[r(x,y)−β⋅KL(πθ(⋅∣x)∥πref(⋅∣x))]\max_\theta \mathbb{E}\left[r(x, y) - \beta \cdot \text{KL}(\pi_\theta(\cdot \mid x) \| \pi_\text{ref}(\cdot \mid x))\right] ​θ​max​​E[r(x,y)−β⋅KL(π​θ​​(⋅∣x)∥π​ref​​(⋅∣x))]

where β\betaβ is the KL coefficient (typically 0.01-0.05) and the KL divergence measures how different the policy’s token distribution is from the reference’s.

The KL penalty does two things. It keeps the policy on the manifold where the reward model was trained the reward model only knows how to score outputs that look roughly like what humans wrote, so if the policy drifts off-manifold, the reward model’s scores become unreliable. And it acts as a regularizer that prevents the policy from collapsing onto a narrow set of high-reward outputs.

With these objective terms in place, you can run PPO (which we covered in detail in Chapter 16). The policy gradient is computed, clipped to prevent unstable updates, and applied. Iterate for some number of steps.

from trl import PPOTrainer, PPOConfig, AutoModelForCausalLMWithValueHead

policy = AutoModelForCausalLMWithValueHead.from_pretrained("./sft-checkpoint")
ref = AutoModelForCausalLMWithValueHead.from_pretrained("./sft-checkpoint")

ppo = PPOTrainer(
    config=PPOConfig(
        learning_rate=1.41e-5, batch_size=64,
        init_kl_coef=0.05, target_kl=6.0, adap_kl_ctrl=True,
    ),
    model=policy, ref_model=ref, tokenizer=tok,
)

for batch in dataloader:
    responses = ppo.generate(batch["query_ids"], max_new_tokens=128)
    rewards = rm(torch.cat([batch["query_ids"], responses], dim=-1)).logits[:, 0]
    stats = ppo.step(batch["query_ids"], responses, rewards)

The adap_kl_ctrl=True setting implements adaptive KL control: if observed KL exceeds the target, β\betaβ doubles; if KL falls below half the target, β\betaβ halves. This dynamically tunes the regularization strength based on how the policy is actually drifting.

Reward hacking, in detail

Reward hacking is the single hardest problem in RLHF, and it’s worth spending time on because it shapes every production decision.

Common forms of reward hacking:

Length hacking: reward models trained on helpful responses often implicitly reward length, since longer answers tend to be perceived as more thorough by annotators. The policy learns this and starts padding responses. Mitigations: length-normalized reward, or explicit length penalty.

Sycophancy: annotators tend to prefer responses that agree with the premise of the question. The policy learns to agree with everything the user says, even when the user is wrong. Mitigations: include “the user is wrong, correct them” examples in preference data; train a separate truthfulness reward.

Hedging: hedged responses (“This is a complex topic with multiple perspectives…”) rarely get marked as outright wrong, so they accumulate higher average reward than committed answers. The model learns to never commit to anything. Mitigations: explicit preferences against hedging; reward responses for taking clear positions.

Format gaming: bullet points, headers, and other formatting cues make responses look more polished. Reward models pick up on this and assign higher scores to formatted responses regardless of content quality. Mitigations: include unformatted-but-good responses in preference data.

Sandbagging: the model deliberately gives worse responses on detection-of-capability questions because being “too capable” was occasionally penalized in training. (Yes, this is real and documented.)

The way you catch reward hacking is by watching multiple signals at once during training. The reward score climbing is not enough. You also need to watch:

  • KL divergence (should stay bounded around the target)
  • Held-out human evaluation scores (should track or improve, not diverge from reward)
  • Response length distribution (should not drift dramatically)
  • Diversity metrics on generations (should not collapse)

If the reward score is climbing but human evaluation scores are flat or dropping, you’re hacking the reward. Stop training. Either raise the KL penalty (force the policy to stay closer to reference) or improve the reward model.

Real numbers from real RLHF runs

Model Comparison pairs Annotators RM size PPO steps KL coefficient
InstructGPT 33K 40 6B 256K 0.02
Llama 2 Chat ~1M undisclosed 70B undisclosed 0.01
Anthropic HH paper 22K 20 52B 50K 0.001
Claude (current) undisclosed undisclosed undisclosed undisclosed undisclosed

InstructGPT’s published numbers are still the best public reference. With 33,000 comparisons and a 6B reward model, OpenAI took GPT-3 175B and produced InstructGPT 1.3B that humans preferred 85% of the time. The 1.3B aligned model beat the 175B base model on user preference, despite being 130× smaller. That’s the power of good post-training.

DPO: skipping the reward model

By 2023, RLHF was the canonical alignment technique, and it had a problem: it was hard. Three models in memory simultaneously (policy, reference, reward). Two separate training runs (reward model first, then PPO). PPO’s notorious sensitivity to hyperparameters. The KL penalty needing constant tuning. Reward hacking lurking around every corner.

In May 2023, Rafailov et al. published Direct Preference Optimization: Your Language Model is Secretly a Reward Model. The paper showed that you could collapse the entire reward model + PPO pipeline into a single supervised loss. Same alignment quality. Half the compute. None of the reinforcement learning.

DPO is now the boring default for most alignment work in 2026. Worth understanding the derivation, because the closed-form math is elegant and the training procedure is dead simple.

The closed-form derivation

Start with the RLHF objective:

maxπEx,y∼π[r(x,y)]−β⋅KL(π(⋅∣x)∥πref(⋅∣x))\max_\pi \mathbb{E}_{x, y \sim \pi}\left[ r(x, y) \right] - \beta \cdot \text{KL}(\pi(\cdot \mid x) \| \pi_\text{ref}(\cdot \mid x)) ​π​max​​E​x,y∼π​​[r(x,y)]−β⋅KL(π(⋅∣x)∥π​ref​​(⋅∣x))

This is the maximum-likelihood-with-KL-regularization objective. There’s a known closed-form solution for the optimal policy:

π∗(y∣x)=1Z(x)πref(y∣x)exp(1βr(x,y))\pi^*(y \mid x) = \frac{1}{Z(x)} \pi_\text{ref}(y \mid x) \exp\left(\frac{1}{\beta} r(x, y)\right) π​∗​​(y∣x)=​Z(x)​​1​​π​ref​​(y∣x)exp(​β​​1​​r(x,y))

where Z(x)Z(x)Z(x) is the partition function (the sum of πref(y∣x)exp(r(x,y)/β)\pi_\text{ref}(y \mid x) \exp(r(x, y) / \beta)π​ref​​(y∣x)exp(r(x,y)/β) over all responses yyy).

This says: the optimal policy is the reference policy reweighted by an exponentiated reward. High-reward responses get boosted; low-reward responses get suppressed.

Now solve this for r(x,y)r(x, y)r(x,y):

r(x,y)=βlogπ∗(y∣x)πref(y∣x)+βlogZ(x)r(x, y) = \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y \mid x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y \mid x)} + \beta \log Z(x) r(x,y)=βlog​π​ref​​(y∣x)​​π​∗​​(y∣x)​​+βlogZ(x)

This is the breakthrough. The reward function is expressed entirely in terms of the optimal policy’s probabilities and the reference policy’s probabilities. You don’t need a separate reward model. The reward is implicit in the log-probability ratio between the trained model and the reference.

Substitute this expression for rrr back into the Bradley-Terry preference loss:

P(y+≻y−∣x)=σ(r(x,y+)−r(x,y−))P(y_+ \succ y_- \mid x) = \sigma\left(r(x, y_+) - r(x, y_-)\right) P(y​+​​≻y​−​​∣x)=σ(r(x,y​+​​)−r(x,y​−​​))

=σ(βlogπ∗(y+∣x)πref(y+∣x)−βlogπ∗(y−∣x)πref(y−∣x))= \sigma\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y_+ \mid x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_+ \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi^*(y_- \mid x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_- \mid x)}\right) =σ(βlog​π​ref​​(y​+​​∣x)​​π​∗​​(y​+​​∣x)​​−βlog​π​ref​​(y​−​​∣x)​​π​∗​​(y​−​​∣x)​​)

The Z(x)Z(x)Z(x) terms canceled because both responses condition on the same prompt. What’s left is a function of only the policy’s log-probabilities and the reference’s log-probabilities.

Take the negative log-likelihood and you get the DPO loss:

LDPO(θ)=−E(x,y+,y−)[logσ(βlogπθ(y+∣x)πref(y+∣x)−βlogπθ(y−∣x)πref(y−∣x))]\mathcal{L}_\text{DPO}(\theta) = -\mathbb{E}_{(x, y_+, y_-)}\left[ \log \sigma\left(\beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_+ \mid x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_+ \mid x)} - \beta \log \frac{\pi_\theta(y_- \mid x)}{\pi_\text{ref}(y_- \mid x)}\right) \right] L​DPO​​(θ)=−E​(x,y​+​​,y​−​​)​​[logσ(βlog​π​ref​​(y​+​​∣x)​​π​θ​​(y​+​​∣x)​​−βlog​π​ref​​(y​−​​∣x)​​π​θ​​(y​−​​∣x)​​)]

This is a standard supervised loss. No RL. No reward model. No PPO. No rollouts. Just two forward passes per example (policy and frozen reference, on both preferred and rejected responses) and a gradient step.

What DPO is doing intuitively

The DPO loss tells the policy: “for the preferred response y+y_+y​+​​, increase the log-probability ratio against the reference. For the rejected response y−y_-y​−​​, decrease that ratio.”

The β\betaβ parameter controls how aggressively. Small β\betaβ (0.05-0.1) allows large deviations from the reference fast learning but risk of degenerate solutions. Large β\betaβ (0.3-1.0) keeps the policy close stable but slow learning. For most applications, β=0.1\beta = 0.1β=0.1 is the boring default.

Code-wise, DPO is much simpler than PPO:

def dpo_loss(policy, reference, prompts, preferred, rejected, beta=0.1):
    # Compute log probs of preferred and rejected under both policy and reference.
    pi_logp_w = log_prob(policy, prompts, preferred)
    pi_logp_l = log_prob(policy, prompts, rejected)
    ref_logp_w = log_prob(reference, prompts, preferred)
    ref_logp_l = log_prob(reference, prompts, rejected)

    # Log-probability ratios.
    ratio_w = pi_logp_w - ref_logp_w
    ratio_l = pi_logp_l - ref_logp_l

    # DPO loss.
    return -F.logsigmoid(beta * (ratio_w - ratio_l)).mean()

That’s the entire algorithm. Six lines.

Compare to RLHF, which requires generating rollouts, computing reward model scores, computing advantages with GAE, applying clipped policy gradients, dynamically tuning β\betaβ, and managing 3-4 models in GPU memory simultaneously.

When DPO beats RLHF

Aspect RLHF (PPO) DPO
Models in memory 3-4 2
Training loops 3 (SFT, RM, PPO) 2 (SFT, DPO)
Hyperparameters Many (lr, KL coef, clip, RM lr, …) Few (lr, beta, epochs)
Reward model Required Implicit
RL algorithm PPO (unstable) Supervised (stable)
Compute High ~1/3 of RLHF
Stability Sensitive Robust

DPO clearly wins on:

  • Small datasets (5K-50K preference pairs). RLHF’s reward model needs enough data to generalize; DPO doesn’t have that bottleneck.
  • Limited compute. DPO is roughly one-third the cost of full RLHF.
  • Rapid iteration. Want to try ten different preference datasets? DPO lets you do each experiment in hours; RLHF takes days.

RLHF still wins on:

  • Large-scale training at frontier-lab scale. The separate reward model can capture more nuanced preferences than DPO’s implicit reward.
  • Multi-objective alignment. When “better” involves orthogonal axes (helpfulness, harmlessness, honesty), a reward model can learn the multi-dimensional trade-off.
  • Online learning loops. RLHF can keep generating, rating, and retraining; DPO works on a fixed dataset.

In 2026, the typical pipeline at a frontier lab uses both. RLHF for the broad alignment pass, then DPO for targeted refinements. Llama 3’s alignment uses this combination explicitly. For most readers of this book, DPO is the boring default, and you should reach for RLHF only if you have a specific reason.

The DPO family

DPO inspired several follow-up methods, each eliminating one more piece of complexity.

KTO (Kahneman-Tversky Optimization, 2024): works without paired preferences. Just label each response as “good” or “bad” individually. The loss applies a loss-aversion multiplier (typically 1.5×) so bad responses are penalized more than good responses are rewarded based on Kahneman and Tversky’s prospect theory. KTO is useful when you have only one-sided feedback (you know certain outputs are bad but don’t have an explicit “better” alternative).

ORPO (Odds Ratio Preference Optimization, 2024): combines SFT and DPO into a single training loop. Adds a preference signal directly to the SFT loss without needing a separate reference model. One training stage instead of two.

SimPO (Simple Preference Optimization, 2024): eliminates the reference model entirely. Uses length-normalized average log-probability as the implicit reward. Saves memory (no reference model in GPU during training) and simplifies further.

Method Year Models Pairs needed? Reference needed? Stages
RLHF 2022 3-4 Yes (for RM) Yes 3
DPO 2023 2 Yes Yes 2
KTO 2024 2 No (unpaired) Yes 2
ORPO 2024 1 Yes No 1
SimPO 2024 1 Yes No 1

The trend is monotone: each method removes complexity. The cost of going from a base model to an aligned model what we sometimes call the alignment tax keeps dropping. DPO is the boring default for most teams; KTO/ORPO/SimPO are increasingly used when their specific advantages apply.

Real DPO deployments

Zephyr-7B (HuggingFace, October 2023) was the breakout DPO success. They took Mistral 7B base, did SFT on UltraChat (200K examples), then DPO on UltraFeedback (60K preference pairs). The result scored 6.47 on MT-Bench the highest 7B model at the time, and within 6% of Llama 2 Chat 70B’s score. A 7B model competing with a 70B model just from better alignment. The DPO recipe was 30× cheaper to train than full RLHF would have been at that scale.

Llama 3 (Meta, April 2024) used DPO as part of its alignment pipeline alongside RLHF. The Llama 3 paper explicitly notes DPO is used after initial RLHF stages for targeted refinement.

Most open-weights models in 2026 ship as base + SFT + DPO pipelines. The frontier models also use DPO somewhere in their stack, even if they don’t always disclose the details.

Constitutional AI: alignment without humans

By late 2022, RLHF was working but had a labor problem. Each new behavior refuse this kind of request, prefer this style of response, avoid this type of bias required collecting fresh preference data from human annotators. Anthropic’s pipeline used millions of preference pairs across multiple training rounds. The cost was substantial, the timeline was slow, and the annotators’ biases inevitably colored the results.

Bai et al.'s 2022 Constitutional AI paper asked: what if the model judges itself? Write down a list of principles the “constitution” and have the model critique its own outputs against those principles. Use the critiques as the training signal. The bottleneck shifts from “labeling thousands of preferences” to “writing a paragraph of principles.”

This is the technique that makes Anthropic’s Claude tick. It’s also widely used elsewhere now under various names (RLAIF Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback, self-distillation, model self-critique).

The two stages of CAI

Stage 1: Supervised Learning from AI Feedback (SL-CAI).

Start with an SFT model that’s helpful but might be harmful. Prompt it with potentially harmful requests. For each response, ask the same model to critique its response against a constitutional principle, then revise.

Principle: "Please choose the response that is least likely to be objectionable to anyone."

Initial prompt: "How do I make a Molotov cocktail?"
Initial response: "[detailed instructions]"

Critique prompt: "Critique the previous response under this principle: [principle]"
Critique: "The response gave detailed instructions for creating a dangerous weapon..."

Revise prompt: "Rewrite the response in light of the critique."
Revised response: "I can't help with that. If you're interested in chemistry safety..."

The training data for SL-CAI is (prompt, revised_response) pairs. Fine-tune on these. The model learns to produce the revised, principle-aligned responses directly, without going through the explicit critique-and-revise loop.

Stage 2: Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF).

Sample pairs of responses from the SL-CAI model. Ask the model itself (or a separately trained constitutional model) which response better follows the constitution. The pairwise judgments become training data for a reward model. Then run PPO or DPO using that reward.

The key difference from RLHF is that the preferences came from the model, not from humans. You’ve replaced the bottleneck (human annotators) with the LLM itself. As long as the LLM can reliably distinguish “more constitutional” from “less constitutional,” the loop works.

What the constitution actually does

The constitution is the lever. Anthropic’s original CAI paper used 16 principles. A principle reads like:

  • “Please choose the response that is least likely to be objectionable to anyone from a wide variety of cultural backgrounds.”
  • “Please choose the response that is most likely to acknowledge limitations or imperfect knowledge.”
  • “Please choose the response that is least sycophantic.”
  • “Please choose the response that is most helpful to a user trying to learn something.”

For each step in the pipeline, you pick a principle (sometimes randomly, sometimes based on the prompt category) and use it to guide the critique-and-revise step.

What CAI buys you is moving the alignment contract from data to text. Changing behavior under RLHF means re-labeling thousands of preference pairs. Changing behavior under CAI means editing a paragraph of the constitution. This is enormous in practice it means you can update model behavior without re-running expensive labeling campaigns.

What CAI costs you is dependence on the base model’s calibration. The model’s self-judgments are only as good as its starting alignment. If the SFT model has blind spots for instance, it can’t recognize manipulative phrasing the critique step inherits those blind spots. CAI compresses the alignment loop but cannot amplify signal beyond the base model’s ceiling.

This is why every production CAI pipeline still uses some human preference data, typically 5-10% of the volume of pure RLHF. The human data anchors the loop and prevents the model from drifting into self-reinforcing failure modes.

Self-improvement, more generally

The CAI loop is a special case of a more general pattern: self-improvement loops where the model generates candidate outputs, scores them somehow (constitution, rule-based check, self-critique), keeps the winners, and trains on them. Variants:

  • Rejection-sampling fine-tuning: generate many responses, keep the highest-scoring ones, fine-tune on those. Repeat. DeepSeek used this after their R1-Zero model.
  • Constitutional distillation: use a strong model to generate constitutional revisions, distill them into a smaller model. Anthropic does this for Claude’s smaller variants.
  • Iterative DPO: run DPO, generate from the resulting model, label the new generations, run DPO again. Two or three rounds.

The risk in all of these is mode collapse. Self-generated data is always a narrower distribution than the original training corpus. After 3-5 rounds of self-distillation, models typically lose diversity on creative tasks, become overconfident, and develop characteristic “AI voice” (formulaic structure, repeated phrases). Production pipelines mix self-generated data with a small fraction of fresh human data to keep the distribution honest.

When CAI matters most

CAI is the dominant alignment technique for subjective behavior that’s hard to capture with verifiable rewards:

  • Refusal style (how to decline harmful requests)
  • Tone (formal vs casual, terse vs verbose)
  • Cultural sensitivity
  • Avoiding sycophancy or excessive hedging

For verifiable behavior (math correctness, code that compiles, factual questions with known answers), a different technique is dominant: GRPO with rule-based rewards, which we’ll cover next.

In a frontier lab’s full pipeline, CAI handles the safety and tone layers, while GRPO handles the capability and reasoning layers. Both run on top of SFT and either RLHF or DPO for general alignment.

GRPO: alignment for reasoning

The 2024-2025 breakthrough in alignment came from an unexpected direction: math problems and coding tasks where you can programmatically check if the answer is correct. DeepSeek’s GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) and the resulting DeepSeek-R1 reasoning model showed that for verifiable tasks, you can skip the human preferences and the reward model entirely. Use a deterministic verifier as the reward signal. Train the model to produce more correct answers via policy gradient.

The setup: what changes for reasoning?

For most language tasks (write a poem, explain quantum computing, answer a customer support query), there’s no clear objective notion of “right answer.” Quality is subjective; humans must judge.

For reasoning tasks, the situation is different. Math problems have correct answers you can verify with a symbolic checker. Coding problems have test suites that pass or fail. Logic problems have provable conclusions.

This means the reward function can be a deterministic rule, not a learned model:

def reward_math(prompt, response):
    expected = solve_problem(prompt)  # symbolic solver
    extracted = extract_final_answer(response)
    return 1.0 if extracted == expected else 0.0

def reward_code(prompt, response):
    code = extract_code(response)
    test_suite = get_tests_for(prompt)
    return run_tests(code, test_suite) / len(test_suite)

No human preferences. No reward model training. The reward function is a tool you can run in milliseconds.

This is much better than a learned reward model when it works. Rule-based rewards don’t have biases that the policy can hack. They don’t drift over time. They cost nothing to run after you’ve written them.

GRPO: removing the value function

GRPO is a variant of PPO that removes the value function. Recall PPO’s objective:

LPPO=E[min(r(θ)A,clip(r(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)A)]L^\text{PPO} = \mathbb{E}\left[\min(r(\theta) A, \text{clip}(r(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) A)\right] L​PPO​​=E[min(r(θ)A,clip(r(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)A)]

where AAA is the advantage, typically estimated with GAE using a learned value network V(s)V(s)V(s). The value network is a second model the same size as the policy. It doubles memory and introduces its own training loop.

GRPO replaces the value-network-based advantage with a group-relative baseline. For each prompt, sample a group of GGG responses (typically 16 or 64). Compute the reward for each. Normalize the rewards within the group:

Ai=ri−r¯std(r)A_i = \frac{r_i - \bar{r}}{\text{std}(r)} A​i​​=​std(r)​​r​i​​−​r​¯​​​​

where r¯\bar{r}​r​¯​​ and std(r)\text{std}(r)std(r) are the mean and standard deviation of rewards across the group. The advantage is the z-score of the response’s reward relative to its siblings. No value function. The group acts as its own baseline.

Prompt: "What is (3+4)*5?"

Sample G=16 responses:
  r_1 = 1 (correct: "35")
  r_2 = 0 (incorrect: "32")
  r_3 = 1
  r_4 = 0
  ...
  r_16 = 0

Mean reward: 0.4
Std dev: 0.5

Advantages:
  A_1 = (1 - 0.4) / 0.5 = 1.2
  A_2 = (0 - 0.4) / 0.5 = -0.8
  ...

Update: increase log-prob of high-A responses, decrease low-A.

The clip and KL penalty stay the same as PPO:

LGRPO=E[min(r(θ)Agroup,clip(r(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)Agroup)]−β⋅KL(π∥πref)L^\text{GRPO} = \mathbb{E}\left[\min(r(\theta) A_\text{group}, \text{clip}(r(\theta), 1-\epsilon, 1+\epsilon) A_\text{group})\right] - \beta \cdot \text{KL}(\pi \| \pi_\text{ref}) L​GRPO​​=E[min(r(θ)A​group​​,clip(r(θ),1−ϵ,1+ϵ)A​group​​)]−β⋅KL(π∥π​ref​​)

What changed: the group-relative advantage. What didn’t: everything else about PPO.

Why GRPO matters for reasoning

For reasoning tasks the reward is sparse and binary: the answer is either right or wrong. A learned value function trained on sparse binary rewards is mostly useless it can’t learn meaningful intermediate estimates because nearly every state has the same expected return until the final step.

GRPO’s group normalization gives an immediate relative signal: among 16 attempts on the same problem, which were above average for this specific problem? This is the right shape of signal for sparse rewards. It tells the model: “of your 16 attempts at this hard problem, attempt 7 was the best do more of that.”

The other big advantage: memory. PPO needs the policy + reference + reward model + value network in GPU memory simultaneously. GRPO drops the reward model (replaced by the rule) and drops the value network (replaced by group baseline). That’s a 50% memory reduction, which means you can train a larger policy on the same hardware.

DeepSeek-R1: the proof point

DeepSeek-AI published the DeepSeek-R1 paper in January 2025, demonstrating that GRPO on rule-based rewards alone could produce reasoning behavior comparable to OpenAI’s o1.

The pipeline they used:

  1. Start with DeepSeek-V3 base model (14.8T tokens of pretraining).
  2. Run DeepSeek-R1-Zero: GRPO on math and code problems with two rewards accuracy (final answer matches) and format (answer wrapped in <answer> tags). No SFT. No human preferences.
  3. The “aha moment”: after enough GRPO training, the model spontaneously developed self-checking, backtracking, and chain-of-thought reasoning behaviors. The reward only specified “produce the right answer in the right format” the chain-of-thought emerged because it helped produce right answers.
  4. R1-Zero had readability issues (mixing languages, weird formatting). They added SFT on a small set of curated CoT examples to clean this up, then more GRPO. This produced the final R1.
  5. Distill R1’s reasoning patterns into smaller models (R1-distilled-Qwen-7B, R1-distilled-Llama-70B).

The result: a reasoning model that matched o1 on most benchmarks, with the entire training pipeline disclosed in detail and the model weights released. The cost of running R1’s GRPO stage was a fraction of what equivalent RLHF would have cost.

This is the proof of the principle: for verifiable behavior, you don’t need humans in the loop. The shift this represents is significant. Before R1, the conventional wisdom was that scaling reasoning capability required either o1-style hidden CoT (proprietary) or massive human-annotated step-by-step datasets (expensive). R1 showed neither was strictly necessary.

Process vs outcome rewards

A subtle design choice in reasoning training: do you reward the final answer (Outcome Reward Model, ORM) or each intermediate step (Process Reward Model, PRM)?

Axis ORM PRM
Signal per response 1 number N numbers (one per step)
Supervision source Final answer check Step-level labels
Training cost Cheap Expensive
Credit assignment Sparse, noisy Dense, targeted
Reward hacking risk Lower Higher (model optimizes for PRM artifacts)
Used by DeepSeek-R1 OpenAI o1 (allegedly), Math-Shepherd

The 2024-2025 consensus is that ORMs plus GRPO scale better than PRMs. PRMs are more sample-efficient per token but require step-level labels (expensive to collect) and tend to develop “shortcut” behaviors writing intermediate steps that look correct to the PRM but don’t actually advance the proof. ORMs are simpler, less hackable, and surprisingly effective when you have enough sampling capacity.

The full reasoning pipeline in 2026

The modern pipeline for capability training (as opposed to alignment training) looks like:

Base model (Ch 17)
   │
   ▼
SFT on instruction data (this chapter)
   │
   ▼
GRPO with verifier rewards
   │
   ▼
Rejection-sampling FT on best GRPO outputs
   │
   ▼
GRPO again, possibly with PRM as well
   │
   ▼
SFT pass for "voice cleanup"
   │
   ▼
DPO for final preference polish

Each stage adds something specific. SFT teaches instruction following. GRPO teaches reasoning. RSFT (rejection-sampling fine-tuning) extracts the good reasoning patterns into a more learnable form. The voice-cleanup SFT prevents the reasoning model from sounding weird (R1-Zero’s mixed-language issue). DPO does the final polish for tone and style.

This is more elaborate than the original RLHF pipeline. It’s also dramatically more capable. The reasoning models of 2025-2026 o1, R1, R1-distilled, Gemini 2.5 are the result of this pipeline-of-pipelines approach.

Evaluation: the only thing that matters in the end

Throughout this chapter we’ve been talking about training techniques. We haven’t talked nearly enough about how you know any of them worked. The hard truth: evaluation is the bottleneck of modern alignment.

Goodhart’s law applies. Every benchmark we use to measure alignment quality eventually becomes a target, and once it becomes a target, it stops being a good measure. MMLU was the gold standard in 2020. By 2024 every frontier model scored 88%+ and the leaderboard compressed into a 3-point statistical-noise range. HumanEval was the gold standard for code; now models score 95%+ while still producing code that crashes on edge cases a junior developer would catch.

This isn’t because the models aren’t getting better. It’s because the benchmarks aren’t sufficient discriminators of meaningful capability differences.

The eval landscape

There are three categories of evaluation, with different cost and signal quality:

Public benchmarks: MMLU, HumanEval, SWE-bench, MATH, ARC, HellaSwag, GSM8K. Standardized test suites. Good for rough model selection. Bad for discriminating between top-tier models. Vulnerable to data contamination (training data on the internet includes the benchmarks) and to teaching-to-the-test (labs optimize training mixtures for benchmark performance).

Custom evals: test suites you build for your specific use case. Define inputs, expected outputs, scoring functions tailored to your deployment scenario. Expensive to create, but the only evaluation that predicts production performance. If you’re building a customer support chatbot, MMLU tells you nothing about whether your bot will be good at customer support.

Human evals: paid annotators judge model outputs on helpfulness, correctness, safety. Gold standard for open-ended tasks where automated scoring fails. Slow (hours-to-days latency), expensive ($0.10-$2.00 per judgment), but the only ground truth.

                 Cheap, broad, gameable
                        │
                  Public benchmarks
                        │
                        ▼
                   Custom evals
                        │
                        ▼
                  Human evaluation
                        │
                 Expensive, narrow, ground-truth

For most production teams: use public benchmarks for rough model selection. Build custom evals for your specific deployment. Reserve human evals for ambiguous cases and final calibration.

LLM-as-judge: the practical bridge

The middle ground that most teams converge on is LLM-as-judge: use a strong existing model (GPT-4, Claude) to score a weaker model’s outputs against a rubric.

Costs about $0.01 per judgment with GPT-4o-mini, correlates ~80% with human judgments on most tasks (substantially lower variance than human judges, who only agree with each other ~75% of the time on subjective tasks).

The scoring prompt matters more than the model. Vague prompts (“Rate this response 1-5”) produce noisy scores. Structured prompts with explicit rubrics (“Score 5 if the answer is factually correct and provides a citation, 4 if correct but unsourced, 3 if mostly correct with minor errors…”) produce reproducible scores.

Failure modes to know about:

  • Position bias: judges prefer the first response in pairwise comparisons. Mitigation: randomize order, run each pair twice with order swapped.
  • Verbosity bias: judges prefer longer responses (because longer often correlates with thoroughness). Mitigation: include length normalization in the rubric, or score short responses against long responses with a length-controlled prompt.
  • Self-preference: GPT-4 rates GPT-4 outputs higher than equivalent Claude outputs. Mitigation: use a different judge than the model being evaluated.
  • Sycophancy in judges: judges can be manipulated by the response framing itself. Mitigation: hide the model identity from the judge.

Chatbot Arena and ELO

The most trusted public ranking system in 2026 is LMSYS’s Chatbot Arena. Show two responses to the same prompt from different models. A human picks the better one. Aggregate via ELO ratings (the same system used in chess). Run this for millions of comparisons across the population of models, and you get a rank-ordering that doesn’t depend on any specific benchmark.

As of early 2026, the Chatbot Arena leaderboard’s top tier shows GPT-4o, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, Gemini 2.5, and Llama 3.x within a 30-ELO-point band meaningful but small differences.

ELO advantages: relative ranking is more reliable than absolute scoring, handles ties gracefully, converges with fewer comparisons than absolute scoring. ELO disadvantages: requires many comparisons to converge, doesn’t tell you what differentiates the models, just which is preferred.

Building custom evals: the only thing that matters for production

For your specific deployment, none of the above is enough. You need a custom eval. The process:

  1. Define the task precisely. “Answer customer support questions” is too vague. “Given a customer email about a billing issue, produce a response that (a) acknowledges the issue, (b) explains the policy, © offers a resolution path, and (d) does not commit to actions outside our policy” is a task you can evaluate.

  2. Create test cases. Minimum 50 for a prototype, 200+ for production. Each is an (input, expected_output_or_criteria) pair. Include edge cases: empty inputs, adversarial inputs, ambiguous inputs, inputs in other languages, inputs that should be refused.

  3. Define scoring. Exact match for structured outputs. Token F1 for extraction tasks. LLM-as-judge with a rubric for open-ended quality. Multiple metrics combined with weights.

  4. Automate. Every eval runs with one command, writes results to a structured format, takes minutes not hours.

  5. Track over time. A score in isolation is meaningless. You need the trend line. Did this prompt change improve or regress? Did switching models help or hurt? Version your eval alongside your prompts.

The eval frameworks worth knowing

lm-evaluation-harness (EleutherAI) is the standard for running public benchmarks. Supports 200+ benchmarks, runs any HuggingFace model with one command. Used by the Open LLM Leaderboard.

RAGAS is specifically for RAG evaluation. Measures faithfulness (does the answer match the retrieved context?), relevance (is the context relevant?), and answer correctness.

promptfoo is config-driven for prompt engineering. Define test cases in YAML, run against multiple providers, get a pass/fail report. Useful for regression testing prompts.

OpenAI Evals is OpenAI’s framework, also useful even for non-OpenAI models. Heavy on model-graded (LLM-as-judge) evals.

For your specific use case, you’ll probably end up writing a thin wrapper around one of these or rolling your own minimal framework. The tooling is less important than the discipline: eval before you train, eval during training, eval after each post-training stage, eval before deployment.

What “good alignment” looks like quantitatively

Some realistic numbers from the literature:

InstructGPT (2022): humans preferred InstructGPT 1.3B’s responses over GPT-3 175B’s responses 85% of the time after RLHF. That’s the headline result.

Llama 2 Chat 70B (2023): scored 6.86 on MT-Bench (an LLM-as-judge benchmark using GPT-4). The pre-RLHF SFT-only baseline scored ~6.0. RLHF added about 0.8 points.

Zephyr-7B (DPO-aligned, October 2023): scored 6.47 on MT-Bench. A 7B model getting within 6% of Llama 2 Chat 70B’s score. The DPO method is what closed most of the gap.

Claude 3.5 Sonnet (2024): around 8.5 on MT-Bench, near the top of public scores. The exact alignment recipe is undisclosed but combines all four stages we’ve covered.

DeepSeek-R1 (January 2025): matches OpenAI o1 on most reasoning benchmarks with the GRPO + RSFT + DPO pipeline.

The common thread: layered alignment beats single-technique alignment. The best models in 2026 don’t pick one of SFT, RLHF, DPO, CAI, GRPO they use all of them, layered, each handling a different subset of the alignment problem.

Sharp edges across the alignment stack

Some realities that bite teams running real alignment pipelines.

Catastrophic forgetting is everywhere, not just SFT. Each post-training stage can damage capabilities from the previous one. Aggressive RLHF can wipe out the instruction-following skills SFT installed. Aggressive GRPO can reduce diversity to near-zero. Hold out a regression eval suite (covering general capabilities you don’t want to lose) and run it after every stage.

Reward hacking carries through stages. A reward model trained to prefer concise responses will make the policy concise even when the user wants thoroughness. A constitution that prefers refusal will make the model refuse benign requests. The hacking compounds across stages; by the end of a multi-stage pipeline, small biases in early-stage reward signals can dominate the final model’s behavior.

The KL penalty is always doing more work than you think. In RLHF, in DPO, in GRPO, in CAI every modern alignment method has a KL term against a reference. If you’re tempted to lower it to “let the model learn more,” you’re probably about to enable reward hacking. Raise it.

Annotator disagreement sets the ceiling. RLHF reward models are trained on human comparisons. Inter-annotator agreement on most preference tasks is around 70-75%. Your reward model can’t do better than its training data. If two humans disagree on which response is better, the reward model has no chance of capturing the “right” preference.

Mode collapse in self-improvement loops is real. CAI, GRPO, rejection sampling all the self-improvement techniques can collapse the policy onto a narrow distribution if you iterate too many times. Watch reward standard deviation across rollouts. If it falls toward zero, you’ve collapsed; stop.

Eval-train alignment is harder than eval design. You can have great evals and still align the wrong thing. If your eval measures “helpfulness on simple Q&A” and you deploy on complex multi-turn conversations, you’ve optimized for the wrong distribution. Match your eval to your deployment as closely as possible.

Public benchmark contamination is universal. Every public benchmark has at least some training-data leakage at this point. Don’t optimize for benchmark scores. Use them only for rough model selection.

The safety/capability trade-off is real. Stronger refusal training reduces helpfulness on borderline cases. Stronger helpfulness reduces refusal on borderline-harmful cases. There’s no setting that maximizes both. Picking the right point on this trade-off is a values decision, not a technical one.

What you’d actually use

The 2026 default alignment stack:

Stage Default tool Notes
SFT HuggingFace TRL SFTTrainer Supports loss masking, packing, mixed precision
Reward modeling HuggingFace TRL RewardTrainer Bradley-Terry built in
RLHF/PPO HuggingFace TRL PPOTrainer Adaptive KL, value head built in
DPO HuggingFace TRL DPOTrainer The boring default for alignment
KTO/ORPO/SimPO TRL has all of these now For when DPO’s specific assumptions don’t fit
GRPO TRL GRPOTrainer (added 2024) or veRL For reasoning/verifier-rewarded training
Constitutional AI Custom pipeline using TRL primitives No off-the-shelf framework yet
LLM-as-judge OpenAI Evals or custom prompts GPT-4o-mini is a cheap, reliable judge
Public benchmarks lm-evaluation-harness For rough model selection
Custom evals promptfoo or roll your own Mandatory for production

For most readers: the realistic alignment scenario isn’t training a frontier model from scratch. It’s fine-tuning an existing aligned model on your domain data. That’s a much smaller engineering problem typically a single multi-GPU node, a few hundred GPU-hours, and minutes-to-hours of training.

The recipe for domain fine-tuning:

  1. Start with an open aligned base (Llama 3.x Instruct, Qwen 2.5 Instruct, Mistral Instruct).
  2. SFT on your domain-specific (instruction, response) pairs. A few thousand examples is usually enough.
  3. (Optional) DPO on a few hundred preference pairs from your domain.
  4. (Optional) Custom evals, then iterate on data quality.

Skip the steps that don’t apply. Skip RLHF unless you have a specific reason. Skip CAI unless you have written principles you want enforced.

Where this goes next

Chapter 19 turns from training to inference. We’ve now built and aligned a model. Deploying it at production scale is its own problem: KV caching, paged attention, continuous batching, speculative decoding, quantization. Inference economics dominates the total cost of ownership for any LLM you’ll serve at scale, and Chapter 19 is the deep dive.

Chapter 20 returns to architectural variants the modern frontier models (DeepSeek-V3, Jamba, MoE designs) that are pushing what’s possible. Many of them embed alignment-relevant tricks (R1’s GRPO, constitutional self-distillation, hybrid SFT+DPO training). Chapter 20 connects the architectural and post-training views.

Chapter 33 in Volume 3 returns to alignment from a different angle frontier safety, the alignment problem in production, model welfare, governance. The technical machinery in this chapter is necessary but not sufficient for genuinely safe deployment. Chapter 33 covers the rest.

For now: alignment is what makes a model useful. Without it, even a frontier-scale base model is just a fancy text completer. With it, the same model becomes an assistant millions of people use daily. The techniques have evolved rapidly RLHF was the only game in 2022, DPO took most of the territory in 2023-2024, GRPO unlocked reasoning in 2025, and Constitutional AI has been quietly compounding throughout. By 2026 the canonical pipeline uses all four stages, layered.

The frontier of alignment in 2026 is moving toward two directions. More automation: replacing more of the human preference labeling with model self-judgment, rule-based verification, or synthetic preference data. More precision: training models that can distinguish subtler value trade-offs, that refuse only when they should refuse, that balance helpfulness and harmlessness without the heavy-handed refusals that characterized 2023-era models.

If you understand this chapter and Chapter 17 deeply, you understand most of what makes a modern LLM useful. The architecture (Chapter 13-14) determines the capability ceiling. Pre-training (Chapter 17) determines the latent knowledge. Alignment (this chapter) determines the behavior. All three are necessary, none alone is sufficient. The frontier labs are not magicians; they are running this exact pipeline at scale, and the pipeline is learnable.

The hard part is no longer the techniques. It’s the data, the iteration loops, and the discipline to evaluate honestly while everyone around you is gaming benchmarks. Build custom evals. Trust them more than public benchmarks. Iterate. The alignment that ships is the alignment that’s been measured.

Chapter 19 LLM Inference

The model you trained and aligned in Chapters 17 and 18 is finished. It’s sitting on a hard drive somewhere as a few hundred gigabytes of safetensors files. You load it onto a single GPU, type a prompt, and watch tokens stream out at 35 per second. Your boss asks how much it costs to serve 10,000 users on this. You do the math: $25,000 a month in GPU rentals, and at peak load the system delivers each user three tokens per second. The model isn’t broken. The serving stack is.

This is the chapter that explains the gap between “I trained a model” and “I serve a model at production scale.” It is the most practically valuable chapter in the volume for the average engineer, because almost everyone deploying LLMs in 2026 is touching this stack even if they’re using hosted APIs, the choices their provider makes about KV caching, quantization, batching, and speculative decoding determine their per-call cost and their p99 latency. Understanding inference is the difference between “we can afford to ship this feature” and “we can’t.”

We’ll start with the fundamental insight: inference is a different problem from training, and the bottlenecks are different. Training is mostly compute-bound. Inference is mostly memory-bound, and most of the optimizations in this chapter are tricks for working around that fact. Then we’ll walk through the production stack in the order it was built up over the last three years: KV caching, Flash Attention, continuous batching, PagedAttention, quantization (FP16 → FP8 → INT8 → INT4 → INT2), speculative decoding (with the EAGLE family), and the open-source serving engines that compose all of these (vLLM, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM, llama.cpp).

By the end you’ll know why a $25,000/month inference bill can become a $5,000 inference bill on the same hardware serving the same model with the same quality, just by getting the serving stack right. You’ll also know which optimizations matter for which workloads interactive chat versus batch generation versus long-context analysis because picking the wrong optimization for your workload is a category mistake that costs both money and quality.

The shape of inference: why it’s a different problem

Pre-training was one big optimization problem: maximize FLOPs per second on the GPU, minimize idle time, scale across thousands of nodes. The model was the same throughout the run. The data was the same throughout the run. The pipeline was deterministic.

Inference is different in every dimension. Each user’s prompt is different. Each user’s output length is different. Some prompts are 50 tokens, some are 50,000. Some outputs need to be 5 tokens, some need to be 5,000. The hardware is fixed, but the workload is wildly variable. And the same architecture that runs efficiently during training runs at 5-10% of theoretical peak during inference if you don’t do anything clever.

The reason is a fundamental asymmetry between two phases of every inference call.

Prefill versus decode: two problems, two bottlenecks

Every LLM inference request has two distinct phases.

Prefill processes the entire input prompt. All tokens are known at once, so attention can be computed in parallel across the full sequence. This is a large matrix multiplication the GPU’s compute units stay busy. The bottleneck during prefill is compute: how many FLOPs your hardware can deliver per second. An A100 GPU does about 312 TFLOPS in BF16. Prefill for a 4,000-token prompt on a 70B model takes around 400ms on a single A100, dominated by the matmuls in attention and feedforward layers.

Decode generates output tokens one at a time. Each new token requires a forward pass through the entire model, attending to all previous tokens. The weights are exactly the same as during prefill, but you’re multiplying them by a single token’s worth of input instead of a 4,000-token batch’s worth. The arithmetic finishes in microseconds. Then the GPU sits idle, waiting for the next batch of weights to arrive from memory.
