The bottleneck during decode is memory bandwidth: how fast you can stream model weights from HBM (high-bandwidth memory) to the compute units. An A100 has 2 TB/s of HBM bandwidth. A 70B model in BF16 occupies 140 GB. Reading the entire model from HBM once takes 70 milliseconds, and you have to do that for every single output token.

This is the autoregressive tax: generation is inherently sequential, you can’t generate token N+1 until you’ve generated token N, and at batch size 1 you read the entire model from memory for each generated token. At 70 ms per token of forced memory traffic, you cannot exceed about 14 tokens per second on a single A100 for a 70B model, no matter how much compute you throw at it. The compute would happily do more it’s the memory bandwidth that gates you.

                 ┌─── Prefill ───┐    ┌────── Decode ──────┐
                 │ All tokens    │    │ One token at       │
                 │ known         │    │ a time             │
                 │ Parallel      │    │ Sequential         │
                 │ matmul        │    │ matmul x N         │
                 │ ─────────     │    │ ─────────          │
                 │ Compute-bound │    │ Memory-bound       │
                 └───────────────┘    └────────────────────┘
                       │                     │
                       ▼                     ▼
                  Saturated GPU         Idle GPU waiting
                  (good utilization)    on memory reads
                                        (poor utilization)

The ops:byte ratio: a language for talking about this

There’s a clean conceptual framework for this called arithmetic intensity or the ops:byte ratio. It measures how many compute operations you perform per byte loaded from memory:

ops:byte=FLOPs per forward passbytes read from memory\text{ops:byte} = \frac{\text{FLOPs per forward pass}}{\text{bytes read from memory}} ops:byte=​bytes read from memory​​FLOPs per forward pass​​

Each GPU has a crossover point where compute and memory bandwidth balance. For an A100: 312 TFLOPS / 2 TB/s = 156 ops/byte. Below 156, you’re memory-bound and adding compute doesn’t help. Above 156, you’re compute-bound and adding memory bandwidth doesn’t help.

Quick estimates of where typical workloads land:

Scenario ops:byte Bound by
Prefill (batch=1, 4K tokens) ~4,000 Compute
Decode (batch=1) ~1 Memory
Decode (batch=32) ~32 Memory
Decode (batch=256) ~256 Right at crossover
Decode (batch=1024) ~1,024 Compute

The story this table tells: decode is memory-bound at low batch sizes, and the only path to high throughput is increasing the effective batch size. This is why every production inference stack is built around batching the entire engineering problem of LLM serving is “how do I keep the batch size large enough that decode becomes compute-bound, given that I can’t control when users send requests.”

The optimizations that follow in this chapter mostly attack this problem from two directions: reducing how much memory you have to read per token (KV cache, Flash Attention, quantization), or increasing how many useful tokens you produce per memory read (continuous batching, speculative decoding, prefix caching).

KV caching: the optimization that makes decode feasible at all

A naive autoregressive decoder is pathologically wasteful. To generate token N, it computes attention over all N tokens. To generate token N+1, it computes attention over all N+1 tokens which means it recomputes the keys and values for tokens 1 through N from scratch, even though those keys and values haven’t changed. By the time you’re at token 1,000, you’ve recomputed the key for token 1 nine hundred and ninety-nine times.

The fix is the KV cache: store the keys and values from each token’s forward pass, and reuse them on subsequent steps.

The math

Per layer, per attention head, the keys KKK and values VVV are projections of the hidden state through learned weight matrices WKW_KW​K​​ and WVW_VW​V​​. Once a token has been processed once, its KKK and VVV are fixed (assuming you don’t change the position embedding scheme mid-generation, which you don’t). You can store them.

When generating token N+1N+1N+1:

  • Compute the query QN+1Q_{N+1}Q​N+1​​ for the new token.
  • Compute the new key and value KN+1K_{N+1}K​N+1​​, VN+1V_{N+1}V​N+1​​.
  • Append them to the cache.
  • Run attention with QN+1Q_{N+1}Q​N+1​​ against the entire cached K1:N+1K_{1:N+1}K​1:N+1​​, V1:N+1V_{1:N+1}V​1:N+1​​.

The compute drops from O(N2)O(N^2)O(N​2​​) per generated token (recomputing all previous keys/values) to O(N)O(N)O(N) (just doing attention with the new query against the cache).

class KVCache:
    def __init__(self, num_layers, num_heads, head_dim, max_seq_len, dtype=torch.float16):
        self.k_cache = torch.zeros(num_layers, num_heads, max_seq_len, head_dim, dtype=dtype)
        self.v_cache = torch.zeros(num_layers, num_heads, max_seq_len, head_dim, dtype=dtype)
        self.seq_len = 0

    def update(self, layer_idx, new_keys, new_values):
        end = self.seq_len + new_keys.size(-2)
        self.k_cache[layer_idx, :, self.seq_len:end, :] = new_keys
        self.v_cache[layer_idx, :, self.seq_len:end, :] = new_values
        return self.k_cache[layer_idx, :, :end, :], self.v_cache[layer_idx, :, :end, :]

This is essentially what every production inference stack does, just with much more elaborate memory management on top.

The memory cost

KV caching trades compute for memory. The size of the KV cache per token is:

bytes per token=2×L×Hkv×dhead×dtype size\text{bytes per token} = 2 \times L \times H_{kv} \times d_{head} \times \text{dtype size} bytes per token=2×L×H​kv​​×d​head​​×dtype size

where LLL is the number of layers, HkvH_{kv}H​kv​​ is the number of KV heads (smaller than total query heads if using GQA), and dheadd_{head}d​head​​ is the per-head dimension.

For Llama 3 70B (80 layers, 8 KV heads with GQA, 128 head dim, BF16):

2×80×8×128×2=327,680 bytes=320 KB per token2 \times 80 \times 8 \times 128 \times 2 = 327{,}680 \text{ bytes} = 320 \text{ KB per token} 2×80×8×128×2=327,680 bytes=320 KB per token

Multiply by sequence length:

  • 4,096 tokens: 1.28 GB
  • 32,768 tokens: 10 GB
  • 131,072 tokens: 40 GB

That last number is the killer. A single 128K-context conversation for Llama 3 70B consumes 40 GB of KV cache half of an A100’s memory. With 100 concurrent users at 4K tokens each, KV cache alone requires 128 GB. KV cache memory is the dominant constraint on production LLM throughput, and architectural decisions about attention (GQA, MLA, MoE) are mostly decisions about KV cache size.

Why GQA wins

We covered Grouped-Query Attention briefly in Chapter 14. Here’s where it pays off most concretely. Going from full multi-head attention (MHA) with 64 KV heads to GQA with 8 KV heads cuts the KV cache by 8×. On an A100 serving Llama 3 70B, this translates directly into 8× more concurrent conversations on the same hardware.

DeepSeek-V3’s Multi-Head Latent Attention goes further: compress KKK and VVV to a low-rank latent (typically rank 512), store the compressed latent, decompress at attention time. The cache becomes one-tenth the size of GQA’s, at the cost of a small additional matmul per attention call. For long-context workloads, this is the clearest architectural win of 2024.

KV cache quantization

Since KV cache dominates memory and is read on every decode step, quantizing the KV cache itself is a major lever. FP8 KV cache halves memory at near-zero quality loss. INT8 KV cache halves memory with measurable but small quality loss. INT4 KV cache cuts memory to a quarter at noticeable quality cost (this gets worse with longer contexts because errors compound across attention computations).

The 2026 default in vLLM, SGLang, and TensorRT-LLM is FP8 KV cache via kv_cache_dtype="fp8". It’s nearly free quality-wise and substantial throughput-wise.

Flash Attention: tiling the attention computation

The other foundational inference optimization is Flash Attention. We covered it briefly in Chapter 14; here we’ll go deeper because it’s load-bearing for everything else.

What Flash Attention solves

Standard attention has three round-trips through GPU main memory (HBM):

  1. Compute S=QKTS = QK^TS=QK​T​​, write the N×NN \times NN×N score matrix to HBM.
  2. Read it back, apply softmax, write the N×NN \times NN×N probability matrix back to HBM.
  3. Read that back, multiply by VVV, write the N×dN \times dN×d output to HBM.

For sequence length 4,096 and head dimension 128, the score matrix is 4,0962×2=324{,}096^2 \times 2 = 324,096​2​​×2=32 MB per head per layer. With 32 layers and 32 heads, that’s 32 GB of HBM traffic just for the score matrices, before counting the probability matrices and the value reads. On an H100 with 3 TB/s HBM bandwidth, that’s 11 ms of pure memory traffic per attention computation, and it scales as O(N2)O(N^2)O(N​2​​).

The compute in attention is dominated by two matmuls (QKTQK^TQK​T​​ and probability times VVV), totaling about 2N2d2N^2 d2N​2​​d FLOPs. At 1 PFLOP/s peak compute on H100, that’s negligible compared to the memory traffic for typical sequence lengths. Standard attention is memory-bandwidth-bound.

The tiling trick

Flash Attention (Dao et al., 2022) reorganizes the computation so the N×NN \times NN×N score matrix never gets written to HBM. Instead:

  1. Tile QQQ, KKK, VVV into small blocks (typically 128×128 each).
  2. For each output tile of QQQ, iterate through the corresponding tiles of KKK and VVV, accumulating the output in on-chip SRAM (much faster than HBM).
  3. Use a clever running-softmax algorithm (the “online softmax”) that computes the correct softmax-normalized output without ever materializing the full score matrix.
  4. After all tiles for a given QQQ block, write the output back to HBM.

The result: total HBM traffic drops from O(N2)O(N^2)O(N​2​​) to O(Nd)O(Nd)O(Nd). For N=4,096N = 4{,}096N=4,096 and d=128d = 128d=128, that’s a 32× reduction in memory traffic. Flash Attention also enables very long contexts (32K+ tokens) without running out of memory without it, attention’s quadratic memory cost makes long contexts infeasible.

Wall-clock speedup on real workloads is typically 2-4× for prefill and 1.5-3× for decode at long contexts.

The version history

Flash Attention has gone through four major versions:

Version Year Key change
Flash 1 2022 Tiled SRAM kernel, basic forward+backward
Flash 2 2023 Better parallelism, causal-first ordering, 2-3× over Flash 1
Flash 3 2024 Hopper async pipeline, FP8 support, exp2 instructions
Flash 4 2026 Blackwell five-stage pipeline, forward-only at launch

You almost never invoke Flash Attention directly. PyTorch’s torch.nn.functional.scaled_dot_product_attention auto-dispatches to it when it’s installed and the inputs match. Production inference engines (vLLM, SGLang, TensorRT-LLM) pin specific versions. The detail to know: when you read about a model achieving “700 TFLOPs of attention on H100,” the kernel making that possible is Flash Attention 3 with its asynchrony pipeline.

Continuous batching: the scheduling trick that pays for itself

KV cache and Flash Attention are foundational, but they don’t solve the scheduling problem. Even with both, naive serving leaves the GPU mostly idle. Here’s why.

The static batching problem

Static batching processes a fixed-size batch of requests together. Wait for N requests to arrive, run them all in parallel, return all the responses when the slowest request finishes.

This sounds reasonable. It’s a disaster.

If your batch is 64 requests and one request needs 50 output tokens while another needs 500, the first request finishes after 50 decode steps and then occupies a batch slot doing nothing for the next 450 steps. Meanwhile, new requests arrive and queue up, waiting for the entire batch to drain.

Realistic chat workloads have output lengths that follow a heavy-tailed distribution 80% of requests need 100-300 tokens, 15% need 1,000-3,000, 5% need over 5,000. With static batching, the long-tail requests dominate batch-level latency and force every other slot to idle.

What continuous batching does

Continuous batching (also called iteration-level batching, or in-flight batching) reevaluates the batch composition every decode step. As soon as any request finishes, immediately replace it with a waiting request. The batch size stays constant, but membership churns.

Step 1: [R1 ← step 1, R2 ← step 1, R3 ← step 1, R4 ← step 1]
Step 10: [R1 ← step 10, R2 done!, R3 ← step 10, R4 ← step 10]
Step 11: [R1 ← step 11, R5 ← step 1, R3 ← step 11, R4 ← step 11]
                          ^ R2's slot taken by waiting R5

Each step is now a batched forward pass over whichever requests are currently active, regardless of where each is in its generation. The implementation is fiddly you need different KV cache pointers per request, different position offsets, careful batching of variable-length sequences but the throughput win is enormous.

For typical chat workloads with mixed output lengths, continuous batching delivers 2-5× higher throughput than static batching on the same hardware. It was first introduced in the Orca paper (2022) and is now table stakes every production LLM serving stack implements it.

PagedAttention: virtual memory for KV cache

Continuous batching solves the scheduling problem but introduces a memory problem. If different requests are at different positions in their KV caches, and requests come and go dynamically, memory becomes fragmented. Allocating contiguous KV cache buffers for new requests becomes harder over time.

The fragmentation problem

Naive KV cache allocation reserves a contiguous buffer per request, sized for the maximum sequence length. If max length is 32K and a request needs 200 tokens, you’ve reserved 32K worth of memory and used 200 wasting 99% of the allocation.

Some systems solve this with smaller per-request buffers and resizing on demand, but that creates fragmentation: as requests arrive, run, and depart, free memory becomes scattered into many small chunks. You might have 2 GB free total but no contiguous 1 GB block, and a new long request can’t allocate.

The vLLM solution: pages and a page table

vLLM (Kwon et al., 2023) borrowed the solution from operating systems: virtual memory. Instead of contiguous allocation, KV cache is allocated in fixed-size pages (typically 16 tokens each). Pages can be anywhere in physical GPU memory. A page table maps each request’s logical token positions to physical pages.

Without PagedAttention:
  GPU memory: [Req A: 2GB block][free 0.5GB][Req B: 1GB block][free 1.5GB fragmented]
  New 1.5 GB request? Cannot fit. Reject.

With PagedAttention:
  Page pool: 256 pages of 16 tokens each
  Request A: pages [3, 7, 12, 45, 88, ...]
  Request B: pages [1, 4, 9, 22, 67, ...]
  No fragmentation, no waste.

A token’s location in physical memory is found via the page table lookup (one extra memory access per attention call, which is cheap relative to the attention compute itself). Pages are returned to the free list when their request finishes.

vLLM reports near-zero memory waste (~4%) under PagedAttention, compared to ~60-80% waste under naive allocation. That’s directly more concurrent users on the same hardware.

Copy-on-write for shared prefixes

PagedAttention also enables a beautiful optimization: copy-on-write for shared prefixes. If 50 chat requests share the same system prompt (typical for production chatbots), the KV cache pages for that system prompt can be stored once and referenced by all 50 requests’ page tables. Only when a request starts generating its unique completion does it allocate its own pages.

For a 2,000-token system prompt shared across all requests, this can save 90%+ of the KV cache memory that would otherwise duplicate the same content 50 times.

Prefix caching across requests

Closely related is prefix caching: storing the KV cache of common prefixes between requests, so a new request that shares a prefix with a previous one can skip the prefill computation entirely.

For a 2,000-token system prompt, prefill takes maybe 200 ms on an H100. If your server serves 100 requests per second with that shared prefix, prefix caching saves 20 seconds of prefill work per second equivalent to having an extra 20 GPUs of prefill compute.

SGLang’s RadixAttention indexes prefixes in a radix tree (trie) by their token content. Any new request matching a stored prefix gets the cached KV for free. Partial matches are supported: if you share 1,500 of 2,000 prefix tokens with a cached entry, you reuse those 1,500 and recompute only 500.

For agent workloads with repeated tool prompts, prefix caching is routinely a 5× throughput improvement.

Quantization: trading bits for bandwidth

Inference is memory-bound. The most direct way to reduce memory pressure is to make the data smaller. This is quantization: replacing high-precision number formats with lower-precision ones.

The number format zoo

Every floating-point number has three parts: a sign bit, exponent bits (determining range), and mantissa bits (determining precision). Different formats trade these off:

FP32:  [1 sign][8 exponent][23 mantissa]   = 32 bits
FP16:  [1 sign][5 exponent][10 mantissa]   = 16 bits
BF16:  [1 sign][8 exponent][ 7 mantissa]   = 16 bits
FP8:   [1 sign][4 exponent][ 3 mantissa]   = 8 bits (E4M3)
FP8:   [1 sign][5 exponent][ 2 mantissa]   = 8 bits (E5M2)
INT8:  [1 sign][   7 value bits        ]   = 8 bits
INT4:  [1 sign][   3 value bits        ]   = 4 bits

FP32 is the gold standard for training accumulation. Range: ~10−3810^{-38}10​−38​​ to 103810^{38}10​38​​, precision: ~7 decimal digits.

FP16 halves the bits with reduced range (max ~65,504) and precision. Standard inference format pre-2023. Vulnerable to overflow on large activations.

BF16 trades precision for range same exponent bits as FP32, fewer mantissa bits. The dominant training format since 2022. We covered this in Chapter 17.

FP8 in two flavors: E4M3 (better precision, used for weights and activations) and E5M2 (better range, used for gradients during training). Native on H100 GPUs. 30-50% inference speedup over FP16 with negligible quality loss.

INT8: 256 evenly-spaced integer values. No exponent. Needs a scale factor to map weights into the integer range. Integer arithmetic is faster than floating-point on most hardware.

INT4: 16 levels. The scale factor does heavy lifting. Quality depends entirely on quantization technique; naive INT4 fails badly, sophisticated methods (GPTQ, AWQ) retain 95%+ of original quality.

How quantization actually works

The core operation is simple. Given a tensor WWW in FP16, find a scale factor sss such that the values fit in the integer range. Round each value:

Wq=round(W/s),W~=Wq⋅sW_q = \text{round}(W / s), \quad \tilde{W} = W_q \cdot s W​q​​=round(W/s),​W​~​​=W​q​​⋅s

The quantization error is bounded by s/2s/2s/2 per element. For per-tensor quantization (one scale for the whole matrix), s=max(∣W∣)/2n−1s = \max(|W|) / 2^{n-1}s=max(∣W∣)/2​n−1​​. For per-channel quantization (one scale per row or column), each row gets its own scale. Per-channel is dramatically better quality because outlier values don’t ruin the scale for everything else.

def quantize_per_channel(tensor, num_bits=8, axis=0):
    qmax = 2 ** (num_bits - 1) - 1
    abs_max = tensor.abs().amax(dim=1 - axis, keepdim=True)
    scale = abs_max / qmax
    quantized = (tensor / scale).round().clamp(-qmax - 1, qmax).to(torch.int8)
    return quantized, scale

That’s the kernel of every quantization method. The sophistication is in how you choose the scale, which parts of the model you quantize, and how you handle outliers.

The sensitivity hierarchy

Not everything in a model tolerates quantization equally. There’s a clear ordering:

Component Sensitivity Typical quantization
Weights Low (Gaussian, near zero) INT4 works with sophisticated methods
Activations Medium (wider range, outliers) INT8 with care, FP8 native
KV cache High (errors compound across attention) FP8 or INT8
Attention logits Highest (softmax amplifies error) Keep in BF16/FP16

Weights quantize the easiest. Their values cluster near zero in a roughly Gaussian distribution, with 95%+ falling within ∣w∣<0.1|w| < 0.1∣w∣<0.1. The full FP16 range is hugely overkill. INT4 works well for weights.

Activations are harder. They flow through the network during inference and their magnitudes vary by orders of magnitude depending on the input. A single attention head might produce activations 100× larger than the mean outliers that are critical for model quality. Quantizing them naively destroys information. The fix is either to use higher precision for outlier channels (LLM.int8()), use per-token activation scales, or smooth the outliers via SmoothQuant.

KV cache is in the middle but errors compound: each attention call uses the cache, so a small error in the cache contributes to errors in every subsequent computation. Long contexts magnify the problem.

Attention logits (the values inside the softmax) are the most sensitive. The softmax exponential amplifies small differences a quantization error of 0.01 in a pre-softmax logit can shift the attention distribution noticeably. Most quantization schemes keep attention computation in higher precision even when everything else is quantized.

GPTQ: Hessian-guided weight quantization

The first method that made INT4 quantization practical for LLMs was GPTQ (Frantar et al., 2022). The insight: not all weights are equally important. Weights that have high second-order importance (high diagonal of the Hessian of the loss with respect to the weight) need to be quantized more carefully than weights that don’t.

GPTQ uses a small calibration dataset (typically 128 examples) to estimate the Hessian, then quantizes weights one column at a time. After each column is quantized, the rounding error is propagated to the remaining columns to compensate. This iterative compensation is why GPTQ produces dramatically better INT4 quality than naive quantization.

AWQ: protect the salient weights

AWQ (Lin et al., 2023) takes a different angle. It observes that a small fraction of weights (about 1%) are salient because they multiply against large activation values. Quantization errors on those weights have outsized impact on outputs.

AWQ identifies salient weights using calibration data, then scales them up before quantization (and scales the corresponding activations down to compensate). This pulls the salient weights into a range where INT4 quantization is more accurate. Same final precision, much better quality.

AWQ typically matches or slightly beats GPTQ at INT4, runs 1.5-2× faster to apply (no Hessian computation), and is now the most-used quantization method for served LLMs.

GGUF: the format for laptops

GGUF (GPT-Generated Unified Format, used by llama.cpp) is the file format for running LLMs on CPUs and Apple Silicon. It supports mixed-precision quantization: different layers can use different bit widths. The first and last layers (embedding and output projection) typically stay at higher precision, middle layers go to INT4 or INT3.

GGUF files are self-contained weights, tokenizer, and metadata in one file. The format is designed for the “load entire model into memory and run on CPU/Metal” deployment pattern. The most popular variant, Q4_K_M, balances quality and size a 70B model at Q4_K_M is about 40 GB and runs at 10-15 tokens/second on an M3 Max with 64 GB unified memory.

Real numbers from real deployments

Model Format Size Perplexity (WikiText) MMLU Tokens/sec
Llama 3 70B FP16 140 GB 3.12 79.5% 38 (A100)
Llama 3 70B FP8 70 GB 3.14 79.3% 55 (H100)
Llama 3 70B GPTQ INT4 35 GB 4.32 77.8% 72 (A100)
Llama 3 70B AWQ INT4 35 GB 4.18 78.1% 75 (A100)
Llama 3 70B GGUF Q4_K_M 40 GB 4.25 77.9% 28 (M3 Max CPU)

The pattern is consistent. FP8 is nearly free: 30-50% speedup, ~0.1% quality loss. Use it on every H100 deployment. INT4 (with AWQ or GPTQ) costs 1-2 MMLU points but doubles throughput and quarters memory: enables 70B models on a single 48GB GPU. GGUF Q4_K_M opens up CPU deployment for models that previously needed a GPU.

For most production deployments in 2026, the boring default is: FP8 KV cache, INT4 AWQ weights, BF16 attention logits.

Speculative decoding: parallelizing the sequential

KV caching, Flash Attention, batching, and quantization all reduce the cost per decode step. Speculative decoding attacks a different bottleneck: the sequential nature of decoding itself.

The Leviathan trick

Recall: decode is slow because it’s sequential generate one token, append it, generate the next, append. You can’t parallelize across tokens within a single request.

Or can you?

What if you could guess the next 5 tokens cheaply with a small model, then verify them all in a single forward pass through the big model? If the verification passes, you’ve gotten 5 tokens for the cost of one big-model forward. If it fails partway through, you accept the verified prefix and discard the rest.

This is speculative decoding, introduced in Leviathan, Kalman, and Matias (2023). The trick has a beautiful guarantee: the output distribution is exactly identical to what you’d get from sampling from the verifier directly. Not approximately. Identically. This is the entire reason it’s acceptable in production pure latency optimization with zero quality tradeoff.

Here’s how it works mathematically. Let ppp be the draft model’s distribution over the next token, qqq be the verifier’s distribution. Sample a draft token d∼pd \sim pd∼p. Accept it with probability min(1,q(d)/p(d))\min(1, q(d)/p(d))min(1,q(d)/p(d)). On rejection, sample from the residual distribution (q−p)+∥(q−p)+∥1\frac{(q - p)_+}{\|(q - p)_+\|_1}​∥(q−p)​+​​∥​1​​​​(q−p)​+​​​​. The resulting samples are distributed exactly as qqq.

Stack NNN of these calls together using one verifier forward pass on prefix + d_1 + ... + d_N. The verifier returns q1,q2,…,qN+1q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_{N+1}q​1​​,q​2​​,…,q​N+1​​ in parallel (this is just a normal forward pass with N+1N+1N+1 output positions). Walk left to right applying the rejection rule. On the first rejection, sample the correction from the residual and stop. On full acceptance of all NNN drafts, sample one bonus token from qN+1q_{N+1}q​N+1​​.

def speculative_decode(prefix, draft, verifier, num_speculative=5):
    drafts = []
    for _ in range(num_speculative):
        p = draft.next_token_dist(prefix + drafts)
        d = sample(p)
        drafts.append(d)

    q_dists = verifier.next_token_dists(prefix + drafts)

    accepted = []
    for i, d in enumerate(drafts):
        u = random()
        ratio = q_dists[i][d] / draft.prob(prefix + drafts[:i], d)
        if u < min(1, ratio):
            accepted.append(d)
        else:
            correction = sample_residual(q_dists[i], draft.next_token_dist(prefix + drafts[:i]))
            accepted.append(correction)
            return accepted

    bonus = sample(q_dists[-1])
    accepted.append(bonus)
    return accepted

What determines the speedup

Two parameters drive the speedup. Acceptance rate α\alphaα is the per-token Bernoulli-success probability how often the draft is right. Cost ratio c=cost(draft)/cost(verifier)c = \text{cost}(\text{draft}) / \text{cost}(\text{verifier})c=cost(draft)/cost(verifier) is the relative compute cost.

Expected accepted tokens per verifier forward:

E[accepted]=1−αN+11−αE[\text{accepted}] = \frac{1 - \alpha^{N+1}}{1 - \alpha} E[accepted]=​1−α​​1−α​N+1​​​​

For α=0.8,N=5\alpha = 0.8, N = 5α=0.8,N=5: E[accepted]≈3.4E[\text{accepted}] \approx 3.4E[accepted]≈3.4 tokens per verifier call. That’s 3.4× the throughput at the same verifier cost.

For α=0.9,N=5\alpha = 0.9, N = 5α=0.9,N=5: E[accepted]≈4.1E[\text{accepted}] \approx 4.1E[accepted]≈4.1. Substantially better.

For α=0.95,N=7\alpha = 0.95, N = 7α=0.95,N=7: E[accepted]≈6.0E[\text{accepted}] \approx 6.0E[accepted]≈6.0. Approaching 6× speedup.

The single biggest lever is α\alphaα. Going from α=0.6\alpha = 0.6α=0.6 (cheap draft) to α=0.9\alpha = 0.9α=0.9 (well-trained draft) is a near-doubling of throughput. This is the engineering target for the EAGLE family.

EAGLE: training a better draft

The original Leviathan paper used an off-the-shelf small model from the same family (e.g., Llama 3 8B drafting for Llama 3 70B). This works, but α\alphaα tops out around 0.6-0.7 because the draft is independently trained it has its own distribution, only loosely correlated with the verifier’s.

The EAGLE family (Li et al., 2024-2025) makes the draft purpose-built for matching the verifier:

EAGLE-1 (2024): the draft is a tiny transformer (1-2 layers) that takes the verifier’s last-layer hidden state as input and predicts the next token directly. Because the draft sees the verifier’s own representation, its distribution is much closer to the verifier’s. α\alphaα climbs to 0.7-0.8.

EAGLE-2 (2024): adds a dynamic draft tree. Instead of proposing a single chain of NNN tokens, the draft proposes a small tree of candidates. The verifier scores all candidates in one forward pass with tree attention (a non-causal mask that respects tree topology). Walk the highest-probability path. α\alphaα per accepted-path token climbs above 0.85.

EAGLE-3 (2025): two more refinements. First, drop the feature-prediction loss that EAGLE-1/2 used (caps how much data helps) and train directly on token prediction. Second, training-time test (TTT): during draft training, feed the draft’s own previous predictions back as inputs over multiple steps, the same way it operates at inference. This aligns the train and test distributions and prevents error accumulation. Measured speedup: up to 6.5× on chat workloads, 38% throughput improvement at batch 64 on H100.

Method Draft type α\alphaα Speedup Training cost
Vanilla Separate small LLM 0.55-0.70 1.8-2.3× None
Medusa Extra LM heads on verifier 0.65-0.75 2-3× ~1B SFT tokens
EAGLE-1 1-layer transformer on hidden states 0.70-0.80 2.5-3× ~60B tokens
EAGLE-2 EAGLE-1 + dynamic draft tree 0.80-0.88 3-4× ~60B tokens
EAGLE-3 Multi-layer + TTT 0.88-0.92 3.5-6.5× ~60-200B tokens

The 2026 default in vLLM and SGLang is EAGLE-3. TensorRT-LLM has the fastest Medusa path for NVIDIA-distributed models.

When speculative decoding fires

Workload Acceptance rate Speedup
Chat (natural language) 0.85-0.92 3-4×
Code generation 0.90-0.95 4-6× (highly predictable)
Structured output (JSON, SQL) 0.92-0.97 5-7×
Creative writing (high temperature) 0.50-0.65 1.5-2×
Long-form generation Same as chat Same as chat (amortized)

Code and structured output benefit the most because the target distribution is highly predictable once you’ve seen function add(a, b) {, the next tokens are almost surely return a + b. High-temperature creative sampling benefits the least, because the verifier’s distribution is genuinely flat and any draft will frequently be “wrong.”

In production, speculative decoding is a no-brainer for chat and code. It’s table stakes every modern inference server defaults to it.

The serving engines: putting it all together

Production LLM inference in 2026 is dominated by four open-source serving engines. They all implement the optimizations above with different strengths.

vLLM: the boring default

vLLM (developed by Berkeley) was the first major open-source engine to ship PagedAttention and continuous batching, and it has the widest model support. If you’re starting a new deployment in 2026, vLLM is the boring default.

vllm serve meta-llama/Llama-3-70B-Instruct \
    --tensor-parallel-size 4 \
    --max-model-len 32768 \
    --enable-prefix-caching \
    --kv-cache-dtype fp8 \
    --quantization awq

That command gives you a production-ready OpenAI-compatible API server with 4-way tensor parallelism, FP8 KV cache, prefix caching, and AWQ INT4 weights. It will saturate your GPUs for typical chat workloads.

vLLM’s main strengths: widest model compatibility, runs on NVIDIA/AMD/Intel GPUs, OpenAI-compatible API (drop-in replacement for OpenAI calls), constant feature additions tracking the research frontier.

SGLang: prefix caching and structured output

SGLang (Berkeley) builds on the same foundations as vLLM but adds two major advantages: RadixAttention for prefix caching and a domain-specific language for structured LLM programs.

For workloads with shared system prompts, RAG context, or multi-turn conversations, SGLang’s prefix caching produces 2-5× higher throughput than vLLM through aggressive prefix reuse. For structured output (JSON, SQL, regex-constrained generation), SGLang’s compiler optimizes the generation graph in ways vLLM can’t.

SGLang is the right choice when your workload has lots of shared context across requests, or when you need structured output with high throughput.

TensorRT-LLM: peak NVIDIA performance

TensorRT-LLM (NVIDIA) compiles models into hand-tuned NVIDIA GPU kernels. It fuses operations (attention + linear + activation in one kernel), uses FP8 natively on H100, and integrates with NVIDIA Triton Inference Server. It achieves the highest single-GPU throughput on NVIDIA hardware.

The cost: more setup, NVIDIA-only, slower to add support for new models (a model has to be officially packaged for TensorRT-LLM, and lag behind by weeks or months). For shops standardized on NVIDIA H100/H200/Blackwell, TensorRT-LLM is the throughput champion.

llama.cpp: CPU and Apple Silicon

llama.cpp is the standard for CPU inference and Apple Silicon (M-series). It supports GGUF format, quantization down to 2-bit, and metal acceleration on Apple GPUs. A 70B model at Q4_K_M runs at 10-15 tokens/second on an M3 Max with 64 GB of unified memory.

For laptop, edge, or no-GPU deployments, this is the only realistic option. For server deployments, it’s slower than the GPU options but cheaper if your throughput needs are modest.

Real numbers comparison

For Llama 3 70B on 4×A100-80GB in BF16:

Metric vLLM SGLang TensorRT-LLM
Throughput (1 user) ~50 TPS ~55 TPS ~65 TPS
Throughput (100 users) ~2,500 total ~3,200 total ~3,000 total
TTFT (no cache) ~400 ms ~300 ms (prefix hit) ~350 ms
Max context 128K 128K 128K
Setup complexity Low Low Medium-high

For workloads with shared prefixes, SGLang wins. For raw single-stream throughput on NVIDIA, TensorRT-LLM wins. For everything else and best ergonomics, vLLM wins. The right answer depends on your workload.

Sharp edges across the inference stack

Some realities that bite teams running real inference deployments.

The TTFT-vs-throughput trade-off is real. Time to first token (TTFT) is what users feel as “the model is thinking.” Throughput is what your bill cares about. Increasing batch size improves throughput but hurts TTFT (new requests wait for the next batch step). Disaggregated prefill/decode (NVIDIA Dynamo, llm-d) splits the work across separate pools to optimize both, but adds operational complexity.

Quantization can quietly degrade specific capabilities. A model that’s INT4-quantized to 99% MMLU score might lose 5-10% on math and code benchmarks. The aggregate metric hides domain-specific damage. Always run your specific eval suite (Chapter 18) on the quantized model, not just public benchmarks.

KV cache eviction is a real production concern. When KV cache fills up, you have to evict somebody. Different policies (LRU, oldest-prefill-first, longest-idle) trade latency variance against throughput. Most engines default to LRU but expose tuning knobs.

Speculative decoding has variance. The expected speedup is large, but per-request the variance is significant some requests get 6× speedup, some get 1×. p50 latency improves but p99 might not. Watch both percentiles.

Long context is paid for in KV cache memory. A 1M-token context request occupies 320 GB of KV cache for Llama 3 70B. You either dedicate substantial hardware to it or you sliding-window the attention. There’s no free lunch.

Batch size is your most important knob. If you remember nothing else from this chapter, remember: to maximize throughput, run the largest batch size you can fit in memory. Most other optimizations are upstream of this they exist to enable larger batches.

The serving stack changes every six months. Speculative decoding wasn’t production-ready in 2023. EAGLE-3 didn’t exist before 2025. Flash Attention 4 launched in 2026. Pin versions, expect frequent rebuilds, and don’t be surprised when your production stack needs upgrading after every major release.

What you’d actually use

The 2026 default inference stack:

Need Default
Open-weights model serving (general) vLLM
Workloads with shared prefixes / multi-turn SGLang
Maximum NVIDIA single-GPU throughput TensorRT-LLM
CPU / Apple Silicon llama.cpp + GGUF
Quantized weights AWQ for GPU, GGUF Q4_K_M for CPU
KV cache FP8 if hardware supports, otherwise FP16
Speculative decoding EAGLE-3 if available, EAGLE-2 otherwise
Hosted API (don’t roll your own) OpenAI, Anthropic, AWS Bedrock, Together, Fireworks

For most readers: don’t roll your own inference infrastructure. The hosted providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, AWS Bedrock, Together AI, Fireworks AI, Anyscale) have spent millions of engineering hours optimizing their stacks. Unless you have specific reasons to self-host (data sovereignty, custom models, cost at very high scale), use a hosted API and move on.

If you do self-host, start with vLLM. Add SGLang if your workload has shared prefixes. Move to TensorRT-LLM only if you’ve measured that vLLM is leaving substantial throughput on the table on your specific hardware.

Where this goes next

Chapter 20 returns to architectural variants. Modern frontier architectures (DeepSeek-V3, Jamba, MoE designs) include several inference-relevant tricks we’ve referenced Multi-Head Latent Attention’s ultra-compact KV cache, hybrid SSM-transformer designs that reduce per-token compute, native sparse attention. Chapter 20 connects the architectural and inference views.

Chapter 21 begins the “applied” half of Volume 2, covering how you actually use LLMs from an application perspective: prompt engineering, structured outputs, embeddings, context management. The inference performance from this chapter is what makes those applications economically viable.

Chapter 29 in Volume 3 returns to inference from the production operations angle: SRE for LLMs, multi-region deployment, KV cache locality across regions, A/B testing, FinOps, the operational layer above the serving engines we covered here.

For now: inference is the unglamorous half of the LLM stack. Pretraining gets the headlines. Alignment gets the academic papers. But every production deployment lives or dies on inference economics, and the optimizations in this chapter KV caching, Flash Attention, continuous batching, PagedAttention, quantization, speculative decoding are what make modern LLM products economically possible.

The opinion that’s worth being clear about: understand the ops:byte ratio, and the rest follows. Every optimization in this chapter is either reducing memory traffic, increasing batch size, or parallelizing the sequential decode. Once you can place any new optimization on that map, you can predict what it will do for your workload before you measure. That’s the mental model that distinguishes an engineer who understands inference from one who just configures it.

Read the vLLM source. Read the SGLang RadixAttention paper. Read the EAGLE-3 paper. The implementations are far more elaborate than any chapter could capture, and reading the actual production code is what turns “I understand the concepts” into “I can debug a stalled inference server at 2 AM.” Most readers won’t write a serving engine from scratch. All of you will deploy on top of one, and knowing how it works is the difference between “we have a $25K bill we can’t explain” and “we ship features that need an LLM to work.”

The frontier of inference in 2026 is moving toward two directions. Disaggregation: separating prefill and decode onto different hardware pools, each optimized for its specific bottleneck. NVIDIA’s Dynamo and the open-source llm-d project both bet on this. Adaptive computation: routing simpler queries to smaller models, harder queries to larger models, sometimes mid-conversation. Both reduce the average per-request cost without sacrificing capability. By 2027, the inference stack will look meaningfully different from what’s described here. The fundamentals KV cache memory dominates, batching is the central scheduling problem, decode is memory-bound will not.

Chapter 20 - Modern LLM Architectures

You open a model card and see this:

{
  "hidden_size": 7168,
  "num_hidden_layers": 61,
  "num_attention_heads": 128,
  "kv_lora_rank": 512,
  "num_experts": 256,
  "num_experts_per_tok": 8,
  "first_k_dense_layers": 3,
  "max_position_embeddings": 163840
}

If those fields feel like vendor trivia, you are still reading model cards like a user. Read them like an engineer. Every field is a budget decision. hidden_size is representation width. num_hidden_layers is depth. kv_lora_rank is a bet that KV cache bandwidth matters more than extra projection compute. num_experts_per_tok is the number of expert MLPs that run for each token. first_k_dense_layers is a stability trick. max_position_embeddings is an operational promise that will become a memory bill the moment somebody sends a long prompt.

The architecture of a modern large language model is not a mystical object. It is a decoder transformer with a small set of knobs turned. Some knobs are now boring defaults: RMSNorm, RoPE, SwiGLU, pre-norm, grouped-query attention. Some knobs are frontier choices: Multi-Head Latent Attention, Mixture-of-Experts, native sparse attention, differential attention, hybrid state-space layers. The art is knowing which knob buys what, which knob costs what, and which knob is just a lab’s way of spending more compute without admitting it.

Chapters 17, 18, and 19 took you through the lifecycle: build the model, align it, serve it. This chapter turns back to the model object itself. You will learn to read a modern architecture from its config, compute its parameter count and KV cache pressure, understand why DeepSeek-V3 is not just “a big Llama,” and decide when a hybrid Transformer-Mamba model is a serious option instead of a curiosity.

The goal is not to memorize model families. The goal is to make new model releases boring. When a lab ships a new checkpoint, you should be able to look at the config and say: dense or MoE, GQA or MLA, full attention or sparse, pure transformer or hybrid, active parameters, KV cache per sequence, likely inference bottleneck. That is the level at which architecture becomes engineering.

The invariant core

Start with the thing that has barely changed since GPT-2:

  1. Token embeddings map token IDs to vectors.
  2. A stack of decoder blocks transforms those vectors.
  3. Each block has self-attention, an MLP, residual connections, and normalization.
  4. The final hidden states project back to vocabulary logits.
  5. Training predicts the next token with cross-entropy.

The modern decoder block looks like this:

x
|
|--> RMSNorm --> attention --> residual add
|
|--> RMSNorm --> SwiGLU MLP or MoE MLP --> residual add
|
v
next block

That skeleton covers Llama, Mistral, Qwen, Gemma, Mixtral, DeepSeek-V3, and most of the open model zoo. The differences live inside the attention and MLP boxes.

The useful mental model is a diff from GPT-2.

Component GPT-2-era baseline 2026 default
Norm LayerNorm RMSNorm
Norm placement pre-norm in GPT-2, post-norm in original Transformer pre-norm
Position learned absolute embeddings RoPE plus scaling
MLP activation GELU SwiGLU or GeGLU
Attention KV heads full multi-head attention GQA, MQA, or MLA
MLP density dense MLP dense for small models, MoE for frontier scale
Context 1k-4k 32k-1M, with architectural help
Training precision FP32/FP16 BF16/FP8 mixed training

If you understand this table, you understand 80 percent of model architecture. The remaining 20 percent is where the frontier lives.

The six knobs that became defaults

The easiest way to read architectures is to treat every model as a set of knobs. Most 2024-2026 open models turn the same six.

RMSNorm: normalize scale, not mean

LayerNorm subtracts the mean, divides by the standard deviation, then applies a learned scale and bias. RMSNorm skips the mean subtraction and bias:

RMSNorm(x)=x1d∑ixi2+ϵ⊙γ\text{RMSNorm}(x) = \frac{x}{\sqrt{\frac{1}{d}\sum_i x_i^2 + \epsilon}} \odot \gamma RMSNorm(x)=​√​​d​​1​​∑​i​​x​i​2​​+ϵ​​​​​x​​⊙γ

This says: keep the vector’s direction, control its magnitude, and avoid extra work that does not seem to buy much in large decoder-only models.

RMSNorm is not the reason a model is intelligent. It is the kind of small, stable engineering improvement that becomes universal because it is cheap and rarely hurts. Llama, Mistral, Qwen, Gemma, and DeepSeek use it. If a new decoder model does not use RMSNorm, ask why.

RoPE: position as rotation

Learned absolute position embeddings are a lookup table. If the table has 2048 rows, position 2049 is out of distribution. Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) makes position a deterministic rotation applied to query and key vectors before the dot product [Su et al., 2021].

For each pair of dimensions in a query or key vector, RoPE rotates by an angle determined by the token position:

q_rot = rotate(q, theta_position)
k_rot = rotate(k, theta_position)
score = q_rot dot k_rot

The effect is that relative position becomes visible to attention through the dot product. There is no learned table to run out of. With scaling tricks like NTK-aware scaling and YaRN, models can often stretch beyond their original training context, though not for free.

The practical rule: RoPE is the default for decoder LLMs. The interesting part is not “does it use RoPE?” The interesting part is the base frequency, the scaling recipe, and whether attention is full, sliding-window, sparse, or compressed.

SwiGLU: the MLP learned to gate itself

GPT-2 used a GELU MLP:

x -> linear -> GELU -> linear

Modern LLMs usually use a gated MLP:

x -> (linear_a(x) * SiLU(linear_gate(x))) -> linear_out

That is SwiGLU [Shazeer, 2020]. The gate lets the model control which features pass through, token by token. It costs an extra projection, so implementations reduce the intermediate width to keep parameter count in the same ballpark. Instead of the old 4 * hidden_size MLP width, Llama-style models often use about 8/3 * hidden_size, rounded to hardware-friendly multiples.

This is one of those changes that sounds small until you look at real configs. Llama 3 8B has hidden_size = 4096 and intermediate_size = 14336, which is 3.5 times hidden size, not exactly 4 and not exactly 8/3. The formula gets bent by empirical tuning and tensor-core alignment. The point is the same: the MLP is gated, and the hidden width is chosen to balance quality and cost.

GQA: the KV cache became the villain

Full multi-head attention gives every query head its own key and value head. That is clean, expressive, and expensive at inference. The KV cache stores keys and values for every previous token, every layer, every KV head. At long context, that cache can be larger than the weights.

Grouped-query attention (GQA) shares key and value heads across groups of query heads [Ainslie et al., 2023]. Example: Llama 3 70B uses 64 query heads and 8 KV heads. Eight query heads share each KV head. You shrink KV cache by 8x versus full multi-head attention while preserving most quality.

The KV cache formula from Chapter 19 is worth keeping in your head:

KV bytes=2⋅L⋅Hkv⋅dhead⋅S⋅B\text{KV bytes} = 2 \cdot L \cdot H_{kv} \cdot d_{head} \cdot S \cdot B KV bytes=2⋅L⋅H​kv​​⋅d​head​​⋅S⋅B

Where L is layers, H_kv is KV heads, d_head is head dimension, S is sequence length, and B is bytes per element.

This says: context length and KV heads multiply directly. If you double context, KV memory doubles. If you cut KV heads from 64 to 8, KV memory drops 8x.

That is why GQA is everywhere.

MoE: parameters stopped meaning compute

A dense model activates every parameter for every token. A Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) model keeps many expert MLPs but routes each token through only a few.

router(hidden) -> scores over experts
top_k(scores) -> chosen experts
output = weighted_sum(expert_i(hidden))

Mixtral 8x7B has about 47B total parameters but activates around 13B per token. DeepSeek-V3 has 671B total parameters and around 37B active parameters [DeepSeek-AI, 2024]. The total parameter count tells you capacity. The active parameter count tells you approximate per-token compute.

That distinction matters because a 671B/37B model behaves economically more like a 37B model at inference but stores and routes like a much larger object. MoE gives you more memorization and specialization capacity per active FLOP. It also gives you router instability, expert imbalance, all-to-all communication, and serving complexity.

MoE is not a free lunch. It is a way to trade dense compute for sparse routing and memory complexity.

Pre-norm: depth without collapse

The original transformer applied normalization after sublayers. Modern decoder LLMs apply normalization before attention and before the MLP. Pre-norm makes deep transformers easier to train because gradients have a cleaner path through the residual stream.

There is not much more to say. If a large decoder model uses post-norm everywhere, that is a deliberate research choice and you should expect a stability story. The boring default is pre-norm.

Read the config, not the press release

Model announcements talk about benchmark wins. Configs tell you what you have to serve.

Here is a compact calculator you can run for Llama-style dense models and many MoE variants. It is not a replacement for reading the model implementation, but it gives you the first-order numbers: embedding params, attention params, dense MLP params, MoE expert params, and KV cache size.

from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class ModelConfig:
    vocab_size: int
    hidden_size: int
    num_layers: int
    num_attention_heads: int
    num_kv_heads: int
    intermediate_size: int
    max_seq_len: int
    bytes_per_element: int = 2
    num_experts: int = 0
    num_experts_per_tok: int = 0
    moe_intermediate_size: int = 0
    first_k_dense_layers: int = 0
    kv_lora_rank: int = 0


def dense_mlp_params(hidden, intermediate):
    return 3 * hidden * intermediate


def attention_params(config):
    h = config.hidden_size
    q = h * h
    if config.kv_lora_rank:
        kv_down = h * config.kv_lora_rank
        kv_up = config.kv_lora_rank * h * 2
        out = h * h
        return q + kv_down + kv_up + out
    kv = 2 * h * (config.num_kv_heads * (h // config.num_attention_heads))
    out = h * h
    return q + kv + out


def kv_cache_bytes(config):
    if config.kv_lora_rank:
        return config.num_layers * config.kv_lora_rank * config.max_seq_len * config.bytes_per_element
    head_dim = config.hidden_size // config.num_attention_heads
    return (
        2
        * config.num_layers
        * config.num_kv_heads
        * head_dim
        * config.max_seq_len
        * config.bytes_per_element
    )


def estimate(config):
    embeddings = config.vocab_size * config.hidden_size
    attn_total = config.num_layers * attention_params(config)
    if config.num_experts:
        dense_layers = config.first_k_dense_layers
        moe_layers = config.num_layers - dense_layers
        dense_total = dense_layers * dense_mlp_params(config.hidden_size, config.intermediate_size)
        expert_one = dense_mlp_params(config.hidden_size, config.moe_intermediate_size)
        moe_total = moe_layers * config.num_experts * expert_one
        active_moe = moe_layers * config.num_experts_per_tok * expert_one
        active_mlp = dense_total + active_moe
        mlp_total = dense_total + moe_total
    else:
        mlp_total = config.num_layers * dense_mlp_params(config.hidden_size, config.intermediate_size)
        active_mlp = mlp_total
    total = embeddings + attn_total + mlp_total
    active = embeddings + attn_total + active_mlp
    return {
        "total_params_b": total / 1e9,
        "active_params_b": active / 1e9,
        "kv_cache_gb": kv_cache_bytes(config) / (1024**3),
    }


llama3_8b = ModelConfig(
    vocab_size=128256,
    hidden_size=4096,
    num_layers=32,
    num_attention_heads=32,
    num_kv_heads=8,
    intermediate_size=14336,
    max_seq_len=131072,
)

deepseek_shape = ModelConfig(
    vocab_size=129280,
    hidden_size=7168,
    num_layers=61,
    num_attention_heads=128,
    num_kv_heads=128,
    intermediate_size=18432,
    max_seq_len=131072,
    num_experts=256,
    num_experts_per_tok=8,
    moe_intermediate_size=2048,
    first_k_dense_layers=3,
    kv_lora_rank=512,
)

for name, cfg in [("llama3_8b", llama3_8b), ("deepseek_shape", deepseek_shape)]:
    print(name, estimate(cfg))

The output will not exactly match vendor parameter counts. Real models have biases, shared experts, tied or untied heads, embedding padding, special MTP modules, rope-specific projections, and implementation details that this simplified calculator ignores. That is fine. You are not doing accounting for an SEC filing. You are building the first-order engineering picture.

The number you should stare at is often not total parameters. It is kv_cache_gb.

For Llama 3 8B at 128k context, BF16 KV cache is around 16-17 GB per sequence. The BF16 weights are around 16 GB. One long-context sequence can cost about as much memory as the entire model. That single fact explains half the architecture research in long-context LLMs.

A worked reading: Llama 3 8B

Take the Llama 3 8B-style config:

{
  "hidden_size": 4096,
  "intermediate_size": 14336,
  "num_hidden_layers": 32,
  "num_attention_heads": 32,
  "num_key_value_heads": 8,
  "vocab_size": 128256,
  "max_position_embeddings": 131072,
  "rope_theta": 500000.0
}

Read it line by line.

hidden_size = 4096 says every token is represented by a 4096-dimensional vector. Wider hidden states give more representational capacity, but every projection matrix grows roughly as hidden_size^2. Doubling width is not a 2x cost. It is closer to 4x in the dense projections.

intermediate_size = 14336 says the MLP expands each token to 14336 features before projecting back down. Because this is a SwiGLU-style MLP, the MLP has three large matrices: gate, up, and down. Approximate MLP parameters per layer:

3⋅4096⋅14336≈176M3 \cdot 4096 \cdot 14336 \approx 176\text{M} 3⋅4096⋅14336≈176M

Across 32 layers, the MLP stack alone is about 5.6B parameters. That is why people say the MLP holds most transformer parameters. Attention gets the diagrams; the feedforward layers hold the bulk of the weights.

num_attention_heads = 32 and num_key_value_heads = 8 says GQA is active. There are 32 query heads but only 8 KV heads. KV cache is 4x smaller than full MHA:

full MHA: 32 KV heads
GQA:       8 KV heads
saving:   4x

max_position_embeddings = 131072 says the model was built for 128k context. Do not read that as “128k is free.” Read it as “the serving stack must be prepared to hold KV cache for up to 128k tokens.” A short-chat deployment may never hit that path. A document-analysis deployment will hit it all day.

rope_theta = 500000.0 is the long-context RoPE knob. The original RoPE base is often 10000. Increasing the base changes the frequency range so the model can represent longer positions more smoothly. This is not magic extrapolation; it is a way to make long position encodings less pathological.

After reading those fields, you know the engineering shape:

  • dense model;
  • easy to fine-tune compared with MoE;
  • GQA for KV cache control;
  • large vocabulary, so embeddings are non-trivial;
  • long-context capable, but memory-bound at max length;
  • likely straightforward in vLLM, SGLang, and llama.cpp-derived tooling depending on quantization.

No benchmark required.

A worked reading: Mixtral-style MoE

Now read a Mixtral-style config:

{
  "hidden_size": 4096,
  "intermediate_size": 14336,
  "num_hidden_layers": 32,
  "num_attention_heads": 32,
  "num_key_value_heads": 8,
  "num_local_experts": 8,
  "num_experts_per_tok": 2
}

Most fields look like a dense 7B model. The difference is the expert block. num_local_experts = 8 and num_experts_per_tok = 2 means each MoE layer stores eight MLPs but runs two per token.

The active MLP parameter count per token is roughly:

2⋅3⋅4096⋅143362 \cdot 3 \cdot 4096 \cdot 14336 2⋅3⋅4096⋅14336

The stored MLP parameter count is:

8⋅3⋅4096⋅143368 \cdot 3 \cdot 4096 \cdot 14336 8⋅3⋅4096⋅14336

So the model stores 4x more MLP capacity than it activates per token. The attention stack still runs densely. This is why a Mixtral 8x7B-like model lands around 47B total parameters but only around 13B active parameters.

The operational read:

  • capability may exceed a dense model with similar active compute;
  • weight memory is much larger than active parameter count suggests;
  • routing can create latency variance;
  • fine-tuning is more delicate because router behavior can shift;
  • serving wants expert-aware batching and enough VRAM to place experts efficiently.

That is what the config tells you before you run a single request.

A worked reading: DeepSeek-style MLA + MoE

The DeepSeek-style config adds fields dense and Mixtral-style models do not have:

{
  "hidden_size": 7168,
  "num_hidden_layers": 61,
  "num_attention_heads": 128,
  "kv_lora_rank": 512,
  "num_experts": 256,
  "num_experts_per_tok": 8,
  "first_k_dense_layers": 3,
  "moe_intermediate_size": 2048
}

kv_lora_rank tells you attention is no longer normal GQA. K and V are compressed into a latent. That means the KV cache formula changes. If your inference engine assumes standard GQA cache layout, support is not automatic.

num_experts = 256 and num_experts_per_tok = 8 means the model has a wide expert pool and denser sparse activation than Mixtral. Top-8 of 256 is still sparse, but every token touches more experts than top-2 routing. This improves composition and smooths specialization, at the cost of more expert compute and communication.

first_k_dense_layers = 3 says the model intentionally delays sparse routing. That is a training stability clue.

moe_intermediate_size = 2048 says each expert is much narrower than a dense Llama-style MLP. The model gets capacity by having many experts, not by making each expert huge.

The operational read:

  • this is a frontier MoE, not a drop-in 37B;
  • attention is optimized for long-context cache pressure;
  • serving requires MLA-aware kernels and MoE routing support;
  • total weight placement matters as much as active compute;
  • architecture and training system are tightly coupled.

Once you can read these three configs, most model cards become variations on a theme.

Dense models: the clean baseline

Dense models are still the boring default under roughly 100B parameters. Every token runs every layer and every MLP. Serving is simpler, fine-tuning is simpler, debugging is simpler, and the inference stack is better tested.

The important dense families:

Family What to notice
Llama 3 / 3.1 / 3.3 RMSNorm, SwiGLU, RoPE, GQA, long context, strong dense baseline [Dubey et al., 2024]
Qwen 2.5 Strong multilingual/code models, RoPE scaling, dense and MoE variants [Qwen Team, 2024]
Gemma 2 GQA, GeGLU, local sliding-window attention in parts of the stack [Gemma Team, 2024]
Mistral 7B Efficient 7B dense baseline, sliding-window attention lineage
Phi family Small dense models tuned for data quality and reasoning-style curricula

Dense models win when you care about predictable latency, broad inference support, and ease of fine-tuning. If you are deploying on one or a few GPUs, a dense 7B, 8B, 14B, 32B, or 70B model is usually where you start.

The dense model’s weakness is capacity per active FLOP. If every token activates all parameters, then making the model much more capable means making every token more expensive. That is why frontier open models started moving toward MoE.

MoE models: capacity without dense compute

An MoE transformer usually replaces the MLP in some or all blocks with a set of experts. The attention layer remains dense. The router is a small linear projection from hidden state to expert logits. For each token, it picks the top-k experts and combines their outputs.

The basic equations:

r=xWrr = xW_r r=xW​r​​

E=top-k(r)E = \text{top-k}(r) E=top-k(r)

y=∑e∈Esoftmax(r)e⋅experte(x)y = \sum_{e \in E} \text{softmax}(r)_e \cdot \text{expert}_e(x) y=​e∈E​∑​​softmax(r)​e​​⋅expert​e​​(x)

This says: score every expert, choose a few, run only those, then mix their outputs.

The model now has two parameter counts:

Count Meaning
Total parameters Everything stored on disk and in memory
Active parameters Parameters used for one token’s forward pass

For dense models, these are the same. For MoE models, they can differ by 10x or more.

Expert parallelism in one page

At small scale, you can put all experts on one device or one node. At frontier scale, you cannot. Expert parallelism shards experts across devices. GPU 0 holds some experts, GPU 1 holds others, and so on.

For each MoE layer:

  1. Each token’s router chooses experts.
  2. Tokens are grouped by destination expert.
  3. An all-to-all communication sends token hidden states to the GPUs holding those experts.
  4. Experts run their MLPs.
  5. Another all-to-all sends outputs back to the original token order.

In pseudocode:

hidden_states on every rank
router chooses expert IDs
all_to_all_dispatch(hidden_states, expert_ids)
run_local_experts()
all_to_all_combine(expert_outputs)
continue transformer block

The compute saving comes from running only a few experts. The systems cost comes from moving token activations across the cluster. The more uneven the routing, the worse the communication and straggler problem.

This is why MoE architecture papers talk so much about load balancing. A bad router is not just a modeling issue. It becomes a distributed systems issue.

Capacity factor and dropped tokens

Training MoE models usually defines a capacity per expert: the maximum number of tokens an expert can process in a batch. If too many tokens route to one expert, the overflow tokens are dropped, rerouted, or handled by a fallback path. Dropping tokens sounds insane until you remember this happens inside a residual block across huge batches; early Switch Transformer-style systems did it because it kept hardware utilization predictable.

Modern frontier MoE systems try hard to avoid dropping. They use better routing, auxiliary losses or bias balancing, shared experts, and larger top-k. But the core tension remains:

  • strict expert capacity gives predictable compute and communication;
  • loose capacity preserves quality but can create stragglers;
  • higher top-k improves quality but increases active compute;
  • more experts increases capacity but increases placement and routing complexity.

When you see num_experts_per_tok, read it as both a modeling choice and a communication multiplier.

Routing is the hard part

The router sounds like a tiny implementation detail. It is not. Bad routing breaks MoE.

If the router sends too many tokens to one expert, that expert becomes overloaded while others sit idle. You either drop tokens, delay tokens, or run uneven all-to-all communication. None of those are good. Early MoE models used auxiliary load-balancing losses to push the router toward equal expert usage. That works, but it adds another objective that competes with language modeling.

DeepSeek-V3’s auxiliary-loss-free load balancing is cleaner. It adds a bias term to each expert’s routing score. During training, overloaded experts get their bias decreased; underloaded experts get their bias increased. The main language modeling loss stays untouched. The router still balances because the logits are nudged directly [DeepSeek-AI, 2024].

This is a good example of architecture as systems engineering. The problem is not “the model lacks intelligence.” The problem is “one expert is receiving too many tokens, causing communication hotspots and unstable training.” The solution is a feedback controller hidden inside the router.

Shared experts: a dense floor under sparse routing

Some MoE models add shared experts: expert MLPs that run for every token regardless of routing. Think of them as a dense floor. Routed experts specialize; shared experts preserve general computation that every token needs.

DeepSeek-V3 uses shared experts. The reason is practical: sparse routing can over-specialize. Shared experts give the model a stable always-on path and reduce the burden on the router to select a perfect expert set for every token.

MoE serving is different from dense serving

MoE inference is not just “run fewer parameters.” You still need the experts available somewhere. On one GPU, the total weights may not fit. Across GPUs, tokens must be dispatched to the devices holding their selected experts and then gathered back. That is all-to-all communication. At large scale, all-to-all can dominate.

This is why MoE pairs naturally with:

  • expert parallelism, where different GPUs hold different experts;
  • fast interconnects, because token dispatch is communication-heavy;
  • careful batching, because expert load variance hurts throughput;
  • specialized serving engines that understand MoE routing.

The model may activate 37B parameters per token, but it is not as easy to serve as a dense 37B model. If someone says “37B active, so it serves like 37B,” ask where the other 634B parameters live and how tokens reach them.

MLA: DeepSeek’s KV-cache bet

Grouped-query attention reduces KV cache by sharing K and V heads. Multi-Head Latent Attention (MLA) goes further. Instead of storing full keys and values in the cache, MLA compresses them into a low-rank latent and reconstructs the per-head representation when needed [DeepSeek-AI, 2024].

Standard GQA cache stores K and V:

token -> K heads, V heads -> KV cache

MLA stores a compressed latent:

token -> c_kv -> cache
             -> decompressed K/V during attention

If kv_lora_rank = 512, each token per layer stores 512 BF16 numbers for the latent rather than separate K and V vectors across KV heads. The saving is large at long context.

For a DeepSeek-V3-like shape:

MLA cache=L⋅rkv⋅S⋅B\text{MLA cache} = L \cdot r_{kv} \cdot S \cdot B MLA cache=L⋅r​kv​​⋅S⋅B

With L = 61, r_kv = 512, S = 131072, and BF16 B = 2, that is about 7.6 GB per sequence.

A GQA baseline with 8 KV heads and head dim 128:

GQA cache=2⋅61⋅8⋅128⋅131072⋅2\text{GQA cache} = 2 \cdot 61 \cdot 8 \cdot 128 \cdot 131072 \cdot 2 GQA cache=2⋅61⋅8⋅128⋅131072⋅2

That is about 30.5 GB per sequence.

MLA buys roughly a 4x KV cache reduction in this comparison. The cost is extra projection/decompression compute and implementation complexity. At long context, memory bandwidth is the bottleneck, so this is usually a good trade.

The key intuition: MLA spends compute to save memory. Chapter 19 gave you the rule. Decode is often memory-bound. Spending extra arithmetic to reduce HBM traffic is exactly the kind of trade modern inference likes.

DeepSeek-V3 as the full case study

DeepSeek-V3 is the architecture worth reading carefully because it combines nearly every important 2026 idea in one model: MoE, MLA, auxiliary-loss-free routing, FP8 training, MTP, and DualPipe [DeepSeek-AI, 2024].

The simplified config:

hidden_size: 7168
num_hidden_layers: 61
num_attention_heads: 128
kv_lora_rank: 512
first_k_dense_layers: 3
num_experts: 256
num_experts_per_tok: 8
shared_experts: 1
moe_intermediate_size: 2048
max_position_embeddings: 163840
vocab_size: 129280

Read it as a set of decisions:

  • hidden_size = 7168: wide enough for frontier representation capacity without going to the enormous width of dense 400B-class models.
  • num_hidden_layers = 61: deep stack, but not absurdly deep.
  • kv_lora_rank = 512: MLA is doing serious KV compression.
  • first_k_dense_layers = 3: early layers stay dense for stability before sparse routing begins.
  • num_experts = 256: each MoE layer has a large expert pool.
  • num_experts_per_tok = 8: each token activates 8 routed experts, plus shared experts.
  • moe_intermediate_size = 2048: each expert is narrower than a dense MLP because there are many experts.
  • max_position_embeddings = 163840: long context is a first-class design target, not an afterthought.

The published headline is 671B total parameters and 37B active parameters. The ratio is the point. DeepSeek-V3 stores a frontier-scale amount of knowledge and specialization but spends dense-37B-ish compute per token, plus routing and attention overhead.

Why the first layers are dense

Early transformer layers build basic lexical and local syntactic features. Sparse routing too early can destabilize training because the router has not yet seen a useful representation. Keeping the first few layers dense gives every token the same basic processing before it enters the expert system.

This pattern shows up across MoE designs. It is not mandatory, but it is common enough to treat as a stability knob.

Why top-8 instead of top-2

Mixtral-style MoE uses top-2 routing. DeepSeek-V3 uses top-8. That sounds less sparse, and it is. But with 256 experts, top-8 still activates only 3.125 percent of routed experts per token.

Top-8 buys smoother composition. A token can draw from more specialized experts at once. The cost is more expert compute and more communication. DeepSeek’s bet is that at frontier scale, the extra active experts improve quality enough to justify the systems cost.

MTP: architecture that becomes inference acceleration

Multi-token prediction (MTP) adds modules that predict future tokens beyond the immediate next token. Instead of only predicting t+1, the model also learns to predict t+2, t+3, and so on depending on depth. DeepSeek-V3 uses a sequential MTP design: each future prediction is conditioned on the previous depth’s hidden state and the next token embedding.

Training loss:

L=Lnext-token+λD∑k=1DLkL = L_{\text{next-token}} + \frac{\lambda}{D}\sum_{k=1}^{D} L_k L=L​next-token​​+​D​​λ​​​k=1​∑​D​​L​k​​

This says: keep the normal next-token loss, then add averaged future-token losses weighted by lambda.

The important detail is that MTP is not just a training regularizer. At inference, the trained MTP module can act as a draft model for speculative decoding. Chapter 19 covered why this matters: if the draft is close to the verifier, one expensive verifier forward can accept multiple tokens. DeepSeek reports the MTP path as a practical throughput win.

This is architecture folding back into serving. The model was trained with a structure that later becomes a latency optimization.

DualPipe: not model architecture, but architecture-scale training

DualPipe is a training schedule, not a layer. It belongs in this chapter because some architectures only make sense if you can train them efficiently.

DeepSeek-V3 is an MoE model trained across thousands of GPUs. MoE expert parallelism creates all-to-all communication: tokens go out to experts, expert outputs come back. Pipeline parallelism creates bubbles: devices wait for inputs or gradients. DualPipe overlaps forward and backward chunks with the all-to-all communication windows and injects work bidirectionally through the pipeline.

The headline is high GPU utilization at scale. The deeper point is that architecture and training systems co-evolve. You cannot design a 671B MoE model in isolation from the schedule that keeps the cluster busy.

For most readers, the practical lesson is simple: if you are not training at thousands-of-GPUs MoE scale, you do not need DualPipe. But you do need to recognize when an architecture’s published efficiency depends on training infrastructure you do not have.

Differential attention: subtracting the noise floor

Standard softmax attention never assigns exact zero weight to irrelevant tokens. At short context, that is fine. At 128k tokens, the tiny weights on irrelevant tokens accumulate into a meaningful noise floor.

Differential attention computes two attention maps and subtracts one from the other [Ye et al., 2025]:

A1=softmax(Q1K1T/d)A_1 = \text{softmax}(Q_1K_1^T / \sqrt{d}) A​1​​=softmax(Q​1​​K​1​T​​/√​d​​​)

A2=softmax(Q2K2T/d)A_2 = \text{softmax}(Q_2K_2^T / \sqrt{d}) A​2​​=softmax(Q​2​​K​2​T​​/√​d​​​)

DiffAttn=(A1−λA2)V\text{DiffAttn} = (A_1 - \lambda A_2)V DiffAttn=(A​1​​−λA​2​​)V

This says: compute two views of attention, subtract the shared background, and keep the signal that differs between them.

The intuition is noise cancellation. If both maps put a small diffuse mass over irrelevant tokens, subtraction cancels much of that shared noise. If a real signal appears differently across the two branches, it survives.

The original Differential Transformer showed stronger long-context behavior, but the V1 design had deployment pain: custom kernels, slower decode, and stabilizing norms that complicated training. DIFF V2 reframed the implementation so it is FlashAttention-compatible and closer to baseline decode latency [Microsoft, 2026].

When should you care?

Workload Differential attention value
2k chat Not much
8k code completion Maybe marginal
64k RAG Serious
128k multi-document QA Serious
Needle-in-haystack evals Strong candidate

Do not retrofit this into an existing model and expect magic. Attention architecture is a pre-training decision. The model has to learn to use it.

Why not just sharpen softmax?

The obvious objection is: why not lower the softmax temperature or add top-k attention? Because those are blunt instruments.

Lowering temperature makes the whole attention distribution sharper. That can help when the model should focus on one token, but it hurts when the model genuinely needs to distribute attention across multiple pieces of evidence. Hard top-k attention creates discontinuities and can throw away weak signals that matter after composition. Long-context QA often needs a few exact anchors and a lot of supporting context.

Differential attention is subtler. It does not say “always be sparse.” It says “subtract the common-mode background that both attention branches agree is uninteresting.” The output weights can even be negative, which gives the value projection more expressive control than a normal probability distribution.

That also explains the implementation difficulty. Standard attention weights are probabilities. Differential attention weights are signed contributions. You get a more expressive operator, but you have to manage stability carefully.

Native sparse attention: long context without full attention

Full attention at 128k context is brutal. Sparse attention is the obvious answer, but most sparse attention patterns either lose recall or fail to deliver real kernel speedups.

Native Sparse Attention (NSA) is DeepSeek’s answer: three attention branches combined by a learned gate [Yuan et al., 2025].

query
  |
  |--> compressed global branch
  |--> selected fine-grained branch
  |--> sliding-window local branch
  |
gate and combine

The branches:

Branch What it sees Why it exists
Compressed block summaries across the full sequence cheap global awareness
Selected full tokens from top-k relevant blocks precise long-range recall
Sliding window recent local tokens syntax, local coherence, short-range dependencies

The compute shape is the win. Let sequence length be N, compression block size l, selected block count k, selected block size b, and sliding window w.

Full attention reads N keys per query.

NSA reads roughly:

N/l+k⋅b+wN/l + k \cdot b + w N/l+k⋅b+w

At N = 65536, l = 64, k = 16, b = 64, and w = 512, that is:

1024 compressed keys + 1024 selected keys + 512 local keys = 2560 keys

Full attention would read 65536 keys. The reduction is around 25x at the attention-pattern level.

The word “native” matters. NSA is designed to be trained with the sparse pattern from the start and to run on hardware-aligned kernels. Inference-time KV pruning applied to a dense-attention model is a patch. Native sparse attention is an architectural commitment.

The caveat is the same as with differential attention: you cannot bolt it onto arbitrary weights. The model must be trained or at least continued-trained with the sparse mechanism.

The long-context toolbox

Long context is not one problem. It is several problems wearing the same coat.

Problem Symptom Architecture/tool
KV cache memory cannot fit many long requests GQA, MLA, KV quantization
Attention compute decode slows badly at long context sparse attention, sliding windows
Attention noise relevant token drowned by distractors differential attention, better retrieval formatting
Positional extrapolation model degrades beyond train length RoPE scaling, long-context continued training
Retrieval behavior model ignores middle documents training data, evals, sparse/differential attention
Cost per request long prompts destroy margins prompt caching, prefix caching, routing

Do not say “we need a 1M context model” until you know which row you are fighting. If the problem is cost, prompt caching may beat architecture. If the problem is exact recall under distractors, a longer context window alone will not fix it. If the problem is KV memory, MLA or GQA helps. If the problem is attention compute at 256k, sparse attention or a hybrid model becomes relevant.

This is why architecture chapters matter for application engineers. You may never train NSA, but you will decide whether to trust a model that advertises it.

Hybrid SSM-transformers: when attention is too expensive

State space models (SSMs) process sequences with a fixed-size recurrent state:

ht=Aht−1+Bxth_t = Ah_{t-1} + Bx_t h​t​​=Ah​t−1​​+Bx​t​​

yt=Chty_t = Ch_t y​t​​=Ch​t​​

This says: update a hidden state from the previous hidden state and current token, then emit an output. Memory does not grow with sequence length. That is the appeal.

Transformers remember previous tokens explicitly through attention. Great quality, expensive context. SSMs compress history into state. Great memory behavior, weaker exact recall. Hybrid models use both.

Jamba is the production archetype [Lieber et al., 2024]. Its recipe is roughly:

7 Mamba layers + 1 Transformer attention layer
MoE on alternating layers

The attention layers provide exact recall checkpoints. The Mamba layers do most of the cheap sequence processing. At 256k context, this can fit where a pure transformer KV cache would dominate memory.

The memory comparison is the whole story.

For a pure transformer with 32 layers, 32 KV heads, head dim 128, 256k context, BF16:

2⋅32⋅32⋅128⋅262144⋅2≈128 GB2 \cdot 32 \cdot 32 \cdot 128 \cdot 262144 \cdot 2 \approx 128\text{ GB} 2⋅32⋅32⋅128⋅262144⋅2≈128 GB

For a Jamba-style hybrid with only 4 attention layers:

2⋅4⋅32⋅128⋅262144⋅2≈16 GB2 \cdot 4 \cdot 32 \cdot 128 \cdot 262144 \cdot 2 \approx 16\text{ GB} 2⋅4⋅32⋅128⋅262144⋅2≈16 GB

The Mamba layers add fixed recurrent state, but it is tiny compared with a full transformer KV cache at that context length.

Hybrids win when context is long and exact everywhere-to-everywhere attention is too expensive. They lose when the task needs dense global reasoning across many documents, or when the model size and training budget are large enough that pure transformer plus MLA/MoE still wins.

The 2026 landscape is pragmatic:

  • Pure transformer MoE dominates frontier capability.
  • Hybrid SSM-transformers are credible for long-context enterprise workloads.
  • Pure SSM models are still research-interesting but not the default for general LLMs.
