If you are building a product, do not pick a hybrid because it sounds clever. Pick it because your context-length distribution makes the KV cache of a pure transformer economically painful.

SSMs are compression, not memory

The common mistake is to say “SSMs remember long context.” More precisely: SSMs compress history into a fixed-size state. That state may preserve what matters, or it may forget something the task later needs.

Attention keeps addressable memory. A token can directly read a previous token’s key/value representation. That is expensive but exact in a way SSM recurrence is not. SSMs trade addressability for fixed memory. The model has to learn what to keep in state as it scans the sequence.

This trade is familiar if you have worked with streaming systems. Attention is like keeping the whole event log and querying it. An SSM is like maintaining an aggregate. Aggregates are cheaper and often enough. They fail when a later query needs a detail you did not preserve.

Hybrid models are an attempt to get both:

  • SSM layers maintain cheap rolling state for most processing.
  • Attention layers periodically restore exact access to prior tokens.
  • MoE layers add capacity without making every token dense-expensive.

The question is not “are SSMs better than attention?” The question is “how much exact addressable memory does this workload require?”

Model families as architecture positions

The model zoo looks chaotic until you classify by architectural position.

Family Dense/MoE Attention Long-context strategy Practical read
Llama 3 dense GQA RoPE scaling strongest boring dense baseline
Qwen 2.5 dense and MoE variants GQA RoPE/YaRN strong multilingual/code family
Gemma 2 dense GQA + sliding-window layers local/global mix efficient practical models
Mixtral MoE GQA standard long context canonical open MoE baseline
DeepSeek-V3 MoE MLA MLA, long-context training frontier open architecture
Jamba MoE hybrid few attention layers + SSM reduce KV layers long-context memory play

This table is more useful than benchmark sorting. Benchmarks change with tuning and data. Architecture tells you what will happen when you serve, fine-tune, quantize, or stretch context.

Choosing an architecture for your job

Most teams do not train LLM architectures from scratch. They choose a model to deploy or fine-tune. The architectural choice still matters.

If you have one GPU

Pick a dense model unless you have a very specific reason not to. A dense 7B-14B model quantized to 4-bit is easy to serve and debug. MoE on one GPU often gives you the worst of both worlds: large total weight memory without enough routing parallelism to benefit.

The boring default:

  • Llama/Qwen/Gemma/Mistral-class dense model.
  • AWQ/GPTQ/GGUF depending on serving stack.
  • vLLM or SGLang on GPU, llama.cpp on CPU or Apple Silicon.

If you have one 8-GPU node

Dense 70B-class models become realistic. MoE also becomes realistic if the serving engine supports the architecture well. Your decision is now workload-shaped.

Use dense when:

  • latency predictability matters;
  • fine-tuning is part of the plan;
  • tooling support matters more than peak benchmark score.

Use MoE when:

  • throughput matters more than per-request simplicity;
  • the model’s capability gain is measurable on your evals;
  • your serving stack handles expert routing efficiently.

If you need 128k-plus context

Start by calculating KV cache. Do not start with benchmarks.

For long-context workloads, the candidates are:

  • dense GQA model with aggressive KV cache management;
  • MLA model, if available and supported;
  • sparse-attention-native model, if the checkpoint was trained for it;
  • hybrid SSM-transformer, if exact global recall is not required at every layer.

Then run long-context evals that match your task. Needle-in-haystack is not enough. Use multi-document QA, citation faithfulness, codebase navigation, and retrieval under distractors. Long context models often pass synthetic recall and still fail real synthesis.

If you are training a model

Architecture choice is downstream of budget.

Under small budgets, keep it dense. Architecture novelty will waste your debugging cycles. Use RMSNorm, RoPE, SwiGLU, GQA, pre-norm, and spend your effort on data quality and evals.

At serious scale, consider MoE when you can support expert parallelism and all-to-all communication. Consider MLA when long-context inference cost matters. Consider MTP if inference volume will be high enough to pay back training overhead through speculative decoding. Consider sparse attention only if long context is central to the product and you can train with it from the beginning.

The order matters. Data and training stability beat clever layers.

The architecture interview checklist

When evaluating a model for serious use, ask these questions in order:

  1. Is it dense, MoE, or hybrid?
  2. What is the active parameter count, not just total parameter count?
  3. What attention mechanism does it use: MHA, MQA, GQA, MLA, sparse, hybrid?
  4. What is the KV cache per sequence at your actual context length?
  5. Does your serving engine support the architecture natively?
  6. Does quantization preserve the capabilities you care about?
  7. What is the long-context training story, not just max context?
  8. Does the model require special chat templates, tool formats, or tokenizer behavior?
  9. If it is MoE, how does routing affect p99 latency?
  10. If it is hybrid or sparse, what eval proves it handles your recall pattern?

This checklist catches expensive mistakes. A model can have excellent public benchmarks and still be wrong for your deployment because the serving engine lacks MLA support, the MoE router creates latency spikes, the tokenizer breaks your domain text, or the long-context behavior collapses under distractors.

Benchmarks answer “can this model perform a task under test conditions?” Architecture answers “what will it cost and how will it fail?”

Sharp edges

Modern architectures fail in predictable ways.

MoE can make latency spiky. Two requests with the same token length can route to different expert loads. If a batch overloads a subset of experts, throughput drops. Watch p95 and p99 latency, not just average tokens per second.

Total parameters are a memory bill. A 671B/37B MoE model activates 37B parameters per token, but all experts must exist somewhere. Storage, loading time, GPU memory, and expert placement still care about total parameters.

Long context is not the same as long reasoning. A model that accepts 128k tokens may still ignore the middle, confuse documents, or fail to compose evidence across sections. Architecture extends the window. It does not guarantee useful use of the window.

Sparse attention needs native training. Inference-time pruning can help, but the real gains come when the model learns the sparse pattern during training. Do not assume you can add NSA-style sparsity to a dense checkpoint after the fact.

Hybrid models are workload-specific. SSM layers save memory, but they compress history. If your task requires exact token-level recall across many far-apart spans, sparse or full attention may beat a hybrid.

Config fields can lie by omission. Hugging Face configs do not always expose implementation details cleanly. Weight tying, sliding-window patterns, shared experts, MTP heads, rope scaling, and attention kernels may live in code rather than config. Always read the model implementation when the deployment is expensive.

Architecture does not rescue bad data. A clean dense model trained on better data often beats a clever MoE trained on a messy corpus. The model card rarely tells you enough about the data. Your evals do.

What you’d actually use

For application teams in 2026:

Need Default choice
General self-hosted chat dense Llama/Qwen/Gemma-class model
Best open frontier capability DeepSeek-family or strongest current MoE, if serving stack supports it
Predictable low-latency serving dense model with GQA
High-throughput batch generation MoE if routing is efficient, dense if not
Long-context document work MLA or hybrid model, validated on real long-context evals
Single-machine local inference dense quantized model, not giant MoE
Training your first serious LLM dense transformer with boring defaults

For training teams:

Design pressure Architectural response
KV cache too large GQA, MLA, KV quantization
Context too expensive sparse attention, hybrid SSM-transformer
Need more capacity per FLOP MoE
Router imbalance auxiliary loss or bias-based load balancing
Decode too slow MTP or separate speculative draft
Pipeline bubbles at MoE scale DualPipe-style scheduling
Long-context attention noise differential attention or sparse attention

Do not start with “what is the most advanced architecture?” Start with the bottleneck:

  • If the bottleneck is memory bandwidth, reduce KV cache or quantize.
  • If the bottleneck is capacity, consider MoE.
  • If the bottleneck is context length, consider sparse attention or hybrid layers.
  • If the bottleneck is decode latency, use speculative decoding or MTP.
  • If the bottleneck is cluster utilization, fix the parallelism schedule.

Architecture is a response to a bottleneck. Treat it that way and the field becomes much less confusing.

Where this goes next

You now have the model-side map. Chapter 21 shifts from model internals to application usage: prompting, structured outputs, embeddings, context management, and the habits that keep LLM features reliable. That chapter will feel higher-level, but it rests on the machinery you just learned. When an API exposes a 128k context window, you now know the KV cache bill behind it. When a model is “400B total, 17B active,” you know what serving trade it made. When a provider says their new model is faster, you can ask whether the win came from quantization, batching, speculative decoding, MLA, sparse attention, or just a smaller active parameter count.

The architecture lesson is not “DeepSeek is good” or “MoE is the future” or “attention is dead.” The lesson is simpler: modern LLMs are built from a finite set of engineering moves. Learn the moves, learn the costs, and every new model announcement becomes a diff you can read.

For now, keep this rule: read the config first. The config tells you what the model is. The benchmark tells you what it did under someone else’s conditions. Your job is to decide what it will cost under yours.

Chapter 21 - Working with LLMs

Calling an LLM is easy. Building with one is not.

The first version of almost every LLM feature is a prompt in a notebook. It works on three examples. The demo is impressive. Then real users arrive. They ask vague questions, paste huge documents, request JSON, change their minds halfway through a conversation, and ask the same thing in twenty different ways. The model sometimes answers perfectly, sometimes returns markdown when your code expects JSON, sometimes ignores the most relevant paragraph because it was buried in the middle of the context, and sometimes costs ten times more than the feature is worth.

The model did not suddenly get worse. You stopped working in the playground and started working in a system.

This chapter is about that system. Prompting, few-shot examples, structured outputs, embeddings, context engineering, and caching are not separate tricks. They are the application layer of LLM engineering. They decide what the model sees, what the model is allowed to emit, how your application interprets the result, and how much the whole thing costs.

The mistake beginners make is treating the model as a function:

answer = llm(prompt)

That is not the right abstraction. A production LLM call is closer to this:

answer = llm(
    system=policy,
    task=user_request,
    context=retrieved_context,
    examples=few_shot_examples,
    output_schema=schema,
    tools=available_tools,
    budget=token_budget,
    cache_policy=cache_policy,
    evals=quality_checks,
)

The model is only one part. The surrounding machinery determines whether the answer is useful.

By the end of this chapter, you should be able to design a normal LLM application from first principles: write prompts that specify behavior instead of vibes, use examples when instructions are not enough, force structured outputs when software needs typed data, use embeddings for semantic lookup, assemble context with an explicit token budget, and put cost controls around the whole pipeline.

This is the chapter where LLMs stop being a chat box and become an engineering material.

The mental model: an LLM call is a controlled experiment

Every LLM request has three broad parts:

  1. Inputs you control: system instructions, user task, context, examples, tool definitions, output format.
  2. Sampling behavior you configure: model, temperature, max output tokens, schema constraints, tool policy.
  3. Outputs you must verify: text, JSON, tool calls, citations, confidence, cost, latency.

The model is a conditional distribution. Given a context, it predicts a distribution over next tokens. Prompt engineering changes the distribution by changing the context. Structured outputs constrain which tokens are legal. Temperature changes how sharply you sample from the distribution. Embeddings and retrieval decide which facts enter the context. Caching avoids paying repeatedly for the same prefix or answer.

This is why “just improve the prompt” is not enough. Sometimes the fix is the prompt. Sometimes the fix is a schema. Sometimes it is better retrieval. Sometimes it is a smaller model. Sometimes it is a cache. Sometimes it is admitting that the task needs a tool instead of a longer instruction.

The working loop is:

Specify -> Assemble -> Generate -> Validate -> Measure -> Improve

If you skip validation, your application will eventually parse a sentence where it expected JSON. If you skip measurement, you will not know whether the new prompt improved anything. If you skip context assembly, your prompt will grow into a junk drawer.

Prompting is instruction design

Prompt engineering is not magic wording. It is instruction design under uncertainty.

The model has seen every kind of text: expert answers, bad forum replies, legal contracts, marketing copy, JSON, code, academic papers, jokes, and nonsense. Your prompt narrows the distribution. A vague prompt lets the model choose from a wide range of possible behaviors. A precise prompt makes the valid output space smaller.

Bad:

Summarize this document.

Better:

Summarize the document for a senior backend engineer.

Constraints:
- Use exactly 5 bullet points.
- Each bullet must be under 25 words.
- Focus on operational risks, not background.
- Include concrete numbers if the document contains them.
- Do not mention sections that are only marketing claims.

The second prompt is not better because it is longer. It is better because it removes ambiguity. It defines audience, length, selection criteria, and exclusions.

The prompt contract

A useful prompt has five parts:

Part Purpose
Role Bias the model toward the right expertise and tone
Task State the actual work
Context Provide facts the model should use
Constraints Define boundaries and failure behavior
Output format Make the response easy to consume

You do not need all five for every request. But if an LLM feature is unreliable, one of these is usually missing.

A clean prompt template:

You are a senior technical support engineer.

Task:
Answer the user's question using only the provided support article.

Context:
<article>
{article}
</article>

Rules:
- If the article does not contain the answer, say "I do not know from the provided article."
- Do not invent product behavior.
- Keep the answer under 120 words.
- Include the exact article section name if relevant.

User question:
{question}

Notice the key rule: using only the provided support article. Without it, the model may answer from general knowledge. Sometimes that is useful. In a support app, it is a bug.

Roles are distribution steering

“You are a senior database engineer” works because it biases the model toward text that resembles expert database answers. It does not create expertise out of nothing. A role prompt should be specific enough to narrow style and priorities, but not so theatrical that it encourages hallucination.

Weak:

You are an expert.

Better:

You are a senior PostgreSQL engineer reviewing a production migration plan.
Prioritize data safety, rollback paths, lock duration, and observability.

The second version tells the model what “expert” means for this task. It should care about locks and rollback, not generic database trivia.

Constraints beat preferences

Models treat soft wording as optional. “Try to be concise” is weaker than “Use at most 5 bullet points.” “Avoid jargon” is weaker than “Assume the reader knows Python but not Kubernetes.” “Return JSON if possible” is weaker than a structured output schema.

Good constraints are testable:

  • “Use exactly 3 bullets.”
  • “Return valid JSON with keys risk, severity, and fix.”
  • “If confidence is below 0.7, ask one clarifying question.”
  • “Use only facts from the provided documents.”
  • “Do not call tools unless the user asks for current or file-specific information.”

If you cannot test whether the model followed a constraint, the constraint is probably too vague.

Put instructions where the model can see them

Long contexts have position effects. Models usually attend best to the beginning and end of the context and worse to the middle [Liu et al., 2023]. Put stable high-priority rules near the beginning. Put the current task and the most relevant facts near the end. Do not bury the decisive instruction between ten pages of retrieved text.

For complex prompts, repeat the one load-bearing rule at the end:

Final reminder: answer only from the provided documents. If the answer is not present, say so.

This is not elegant prose. It is engineering.

Sampling controls are part of the prompt

The same prompt behaves differently under different sampling settings. If you ignore temperature, top-p, max tokens, and stop conditions, you are not fully specifying the call.

Temperature controls how sharply the model samples from the next-token distribution. Low temperature makes the model more deterministic. High temperature makes it more varied.

Use low temperature for:

  • extraction;
  • classification;
  • code transformations;
  • structured output;
  • policy decisions;
  • anything you want to test repeatably.

Use moderate temperature for:

  • explanations;
  • brainstorming with constraints;
  • writing where style matters;
  • generating multiple alternatives.

Avoid high temperature in production unless the product is explicitly creative. Randomness feels charming in a writing tool. It feels broken in a financial assistant.

The boring defaults:

Task Temperature Notes
JSON extraction 0.0 pair with schema
Classification 0.0 deterministic labels
Code edit 0.0-0.2 reduce creative rewrites
Technical explanation 0.2-0.5 allow natural wording
Brainstorming 0.7-1.0 generate diversity
Self-consistency samples 0.5-0.8 diversity is the point

Max output tokens matter too. If you give the model 8,000 output tokens for a 3-bullet answer, some models will use the space. If you give it 200 output tokens for a complex analysis, it may truncate before the conclusion. Output budget is a behavioral instruction.

Stop sequences are underused. If the model must produce one record, stop at a delimiter. If you are generating SQL, stop after the final semicolon when safe. If you are producing a tool argument, let the schema end the generation.

Your test fixture should include sampling settings. A prompt without sampling settings is not a reproducible artifact.

Prompt evals: stop arguing with vibes

Prompt work without evals becomes taste debate. One person likes the answer. Another thinks it is too verbose. A third tries a different wording and declares victory because it worked on one example.

Do not do that. Build a small eval set.

For a prompt-driven feature, collect 20-100 representative cases:

  • normal examples;
  • edge cases;
  • adversarial inputs;
  • short inputs;
  • long inputs;
  • cases where the correct answer is “I don’t know”;
  • examples from real failures.

Then score the model output with checks that match the task:

Task Checks
Classification exact label accuracy
Extraction field-level accuracy, evidence match
Summarization required facts present, forbidden claims absent
Code generation tests pass, lint passes
Support answer grounded in docs, no policy invention
JSON output schema valid, no extra prose

A tiny eval runner:

from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class PromptCase:
    name: str
    input: str
    expected_contains: list[str]
    forbidden_contains: list[str]


def score_text(output: str, case: PromptCase) -> dict:
    lower = output.lower()
    required = [x for x in case.expected_contains if x.lower() in lower]
    forbidden = [x for x in case.forbidden_contains if x.lower() in lower]
    return {
        "case": case.name,
        "required_found": len(required),
        "required_total": len(case.expected_contains),
        "forbidden_found": forbidden,
        "passed": len(required) == len(case.expected_contains) and not forbidden,
    }

This is not a perfect eval. It is better than guessing. As the feature matures, replace string checks with schema validation, unit tests, human labels, or a judge model calibrated against human review.

The rule: every prompt change should run against the eval set. If it improves one example and breaks five, it is not an improvement.

Few-shot examples teach the pattern

Instructions describe the task. Examples demonstrate it.

Few-shot prompting means putting input/output examples in the prompt before the real input. It works because examples are compressed behavior specifications. For many tasks, examples outperform elaborate instructions because the model can pattern-match the desired transformation.

Example: sentiment extraction.

Classify each review into JSON.

Example 1:
Input: "The food was excellent but the service was slow."
Output: {"sentiment": "mixed", "food": "positive", "service": "negative"}

Example 2:
Input: "Terrible experience. Never coming back."
Output: {"sentiment": "negative", "food": null, "service": "negative"}

Now classify:
Input: "{review}"
Output:

The examples teach three things at once:

  • output shape;
  • label vocabulary;
  • how to handle missing fields with null.

You could write all of that as prose. Examples are often more reliable.

Choose examples like a researcher, not like a decorator

Bad few-shot examples are worse than none. They waste tokens and teach the wrong pattern.

Good examples should be:

  • representative: close to real inputs;
  • diverse: cover different labels and edge cases;
  • minimal: no unnecessary prose;
  • consistent: same format every time;
  • hard enough: include ambiguous cases, not just easy ones.

For classification, include at least one example per label. For extraction, include missing fields, conflicting evidence, and weird formatting. For code review, include both a real bug and a non-bug so the model does not over-report.

Chain-of-thought is scratch space, not a religion

For multi-step reasoning, asking the model to reason before answering can improve accuracy [Wei et al., 2022]. Mechanically, the model writes intermediate tokens, and those tokens become context for later tokens. The model gives itself scratch paper.

But do not blindly ask every model to “think step by step.” It costs tokens, increases latency, and can produce verbose answers when the task is simple.

Use visible reasoning when:

  • the user benefits from seeing the reasoning;
  • the task is math, logic, planning, or diagnosis;
  • the output will be reviewed by a human;
  • you need an audit trail.

Avoid visible reasoning when:

  • the task is classification or extraction;
  • the final answer must be short;
  • the model already has an internal reasoning mode;
  • exposing the reasoning would leak policy, system instructions, or private intermediate analysis.

For production apps, a better pattern is often private reasoning, public answer:

Think through the problem carefully, but do not reveal hidden reasoning.
Return only:
- final_answer
- confidence
- key_evidence

If your provider supports explicit reasoning controls, use the provider feature. If not, structure the prompt so the final response contains only what the application needs.

Self-consistency: sample several answers, then vote

When accuracy matters more than latency, run the same reasoning prompt several times at nonzero temperature and vote on the final answer [Wang et al., 2023].

This works when errors are independent. If five reasoning paths produce answers 42, 42, 42, 40, 48, the majority is more trustworthy than a single sample. If all five are wrong for the same reason, voting does nothing.

The useful version is selective:

  1. Run one cheap attempt.
  2. If confidence is high and validation passes, stop.
  3. If confidence is low, sample 3-5 attempts.
  4. Vote or ask a judge model to compare.
  5. Escalate to a stronger model only when disagreement remains.

That is the pattern: spend more inference only when uncertainty justifies it.

Structured outputs: make the model speak software

Your application does not want prose when it needs data.

If the next step is a database write, API call, workflow transition, UI render, or policy decision, use structured output. Do not parse free text with regex. That path leads to late-night incidents.

Bad:

The ticket seems urgent and should go to billing.

Good:

{
  "department": "billing",
  "priority": "urgent",
  "reason": "Customer reports duplicate charge",
  "needs_human": true
}

The structured version is easy to validate, log, test, and route.

The structured output ladder

There are four levels:

Level Mechanism Reliability
Prompt only “Return JSON” weak
JSON mode valid JSON, no schema guarantee medium
Tool/function call typed arguments for a function strong
Schema-constrained decoding only schema-valid tokens are allowed strongest

Use the strongest level your provider and use case support. Prompt-only JSON is fine for experiments. It is not fine for production workflows.

Pydantic as the boundary

In Python, define the output you want as a Pydantic model. Even if your provider enforces schemas, validate again at your application boundary. Defense in depth is cheap here.

from enum import Enum
from pydantic import BaseModel, Field, ValidationError


class Priority(str, Enum):
    low = "low"
    normal = "normal"
    urgent = "urgent"


class TicketRoute(BaseModel):
    department: str = Field(description="Destination team")
    priority: Priority
    reason: str = Field(max_length=200)
    needs_human: bool

Now every model output has a contract.

def parse_ticket_route(raw_json: str) -> TicketRoute:
    try:
        return TicketRoute.model_validate_json(raw_json)
    except ValidationError as exc:
        raise ValueError(f"Invalid model output: {exc}") from exc

This catches malformed structure. It does not catch hallucinated values. If the model says the customer has a duplicate charge when the ticket does not mention one, the schema will pass. Structured output solves syntax and shape. Evidence checking is separate.

Validate against evidence, not just schema

For extraction tasks, ask for evidence spans:

class ExtractedPrice(BaseModel):
    product_name: str
    price: float
    currency: str
    evidence: str = Field(description="Exact source text supporting the price")

Then verify that evidence appears in the source document.

def verify_evidence(output: ExtractedPrice, source: str) -> bool:
    return output.evidence.strip() in source

This simple check catches a surprising number of hallucinations. It forces the model to bind each extracted value to text that exists.

Retry with errors, but cap it

When validation fails, send the validation error back and ask the model to repair the output. This is the Instructor pattern.

def repair_prompt(original_task: str, bad_output: str, error: str) -> str:
    return f"""
Your previous output did not match the required schema.

Original task:
{original_task}

Invalid output:
{bad_output}

Validation error:
{error}

Return only corrected JSON. Do not add explanations.
"""

Use at most one or two retries. Infinite retries hide bad prompts and burn money.

The production rule:

schema failure -> one repair attempt -> fail closed or human review

Do not silently accept malformed outputs because “the model probably meant it.”

Design schemas for models, not just humans

A schema can be valid JSON Schema and still be a bad LLM interface.

Bad schema:

{
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "result": {
      "type": "object"
    }
  }
}

This is too vague. The model has to invent the contents of result.

Better:

{
  "type": "object",
  "properties": {
    "route": {
      "type": "string",
      "enum": ["billing", "technical_support", "sales", "human_review"]
    },
    "priority": {
      "type": "string",
      "enum": ["low", "normal", "urgent"]
    },
    "evidence": {
      "type": "string",
      "description": "Exact phrase from the ticket that supports the route"
    }
  },
  "required": ["route", "priority", "evidence"]
}

Good LLM schemas have:

  • small enums instead of open-ended strings;
  • required fields instead of missing optional ambiguity;
  • null for unknown values when absence matters;
  • descriptions that explain semantic intent;
  • shallow nesting;
  • arrays with explicit maximum lengths;
  • evidence fields for extracted claims.

Avoid deeply nested schemas unless the domain truly needs them. The more levels the model must track, the more ways it can satisfy the syntax while failing the task.

Prefer:

{
  "items": [
    {"claim": "...", "evidence": "...", "confidence": "high"}
  ]
}

Over:

{
  "analysis": {
    "claims": {
      "primary": {
        "items": [
          {"claim": "..."}
        ]
      }
    }
  }
}

The model is not impressed by your enterprise object hierarchy.

Separate decisions from explanations

A common schema mistake is mixing a machine decision with a human explanation in one field.

Bad:

{"classification": "urgent because the user says they were double charged"}

Better:

{
  "classification": "urgent",
  "reason": "The user says they were double charged."
}

The first version forces downstream code to parse prose. The second keeps the routing decision typed and the explanation human-readable.

This pattern scales:

class Decision(BaseModel):
    label: str
    confidence: float
    reason: str
    evidence: list[str]

Every important LLM decision should have the decision, confidence, reason, and evidence separated. That gives your application something to act on and your human reviewer something to inspect.

Embeddings: meaning as geometry

Prompts control generation. Embeddings control retrieval.

An embedding is a vector representation of text. Similar meanings should land near each other. “Payment failed”, “transaction declined”, and “card charge did not go through” should be close even though they share few exact words.

This solves the vocabulary mismatch problem. Keyword search finds shared words. Embedding search finds shared meaning.

Similarity from first principles

For two vectors a and b, cosine similarity measures whether they point in the same direction:

cosine(a,b)=a⋅b∣∣a∣∣⋅∣∣b∣∣\text{cosine}(a, b) = \frac{a \cdot b}{||a|| \cdot ||b||} cosine(a,b)=​∣∣a∣∣⋅∣∣b∣∣​​a⋅b​​

This says: normalize away magnitude, compare direction. For text retrieval, direction is usually what matters.

A tiny implementation:

import math
from collections import Counter


def bag_of_words_embedding(text: str) -> dict[str, float]:
    counts = Counter(text.lower().split())
    norm = math.sqrt(sum(v * v for v in counts.values()))
    if norm == 0:
        return {}
    return {word: count / norm for word, count in counts.items()}


def cosine_sparse(a: dict[str, float], b: dict[str, float]) -> float:
    keys = set(a) | set(b)
    return sum(a.get(k, 0.0) * b.get(k, 0.0) for k in keys)

This is not a neural embedding. It is a toy that shows the interface: text in, vector out, similarity score out.

Modern embedding models replace the bag-of-words vector with a dense learned vector. The application shape stays the same.

The semantic search pipeline

The basic pipeline:

documents -> chunk -> embed -> store vectors
query -> embed -> nearest neighbors -> retrieved context

Code:

class VectorIndex:
    def __init__(self):
        self.rows = []

    def add(self, text: str, metadata: dict):
        self.rows.append({
            "text": text,
            "metadata": metadata,
            "embedding": bag_of_words_embedding(text),
        })

    def search(self, query: str, top_k: int = 5):
        query_embedding = bag_of_words_embedding(query)
        scored = []
        for row in self.rows:
            score = cosine_sparse(query_embedding, row["embedding"])
            scored.append((score, row))
        scored.sort(key=lambda item: item[0], reverse=True)
        return [
            {"score": score, "text": row["text"], "metadata": row["metadata"]}
            for score, row in scored[:top_k]
        ]

This toy index is brute force. Production systems use approximate nearest neighbor indexes like HNSW, usually through pgvector, Qdrant, Weaviate, Pinecone, FAISS, or similar systems. The principle is identical.

Chunking is part of retrieval quality

Do not embed a whole 50-page document as one vector. The vector becomes an average of unrelated topics. Split documents into chunks.

The default chunking rule:

256-512 tokens per chunk, 10-20% overlap

Small chunks retrieve precise facts but can lose context. Large chunks preserve context but retrieve less precisely. Overlap prevents boundary cuts from hiding the answer.

A simple chunker:

def chunk_words(text: str, chunk_size: int = 300, overlap: int = 50) -> list[str]:
    words = text.split()
    chunks = []
    step = chunk_size - overlap
    for start in range(0, len(words), step):
        chunk = " ".join(words[start:start + chunk_size])
        if chunk:
            chunks.append(chunk)
    return chunks

For structured documents, split by headings first, then paragraphs, then token limits. Respect document structure when you can. A markdown heading is a useful semantic boundary. Use it.

Retrieval needs evaluation

Embedding search can look good in demos and fail in production. Measure it.

Useful retrieval metrics:

Metric Meaning
recall@k Did the correct chunk appear in the top k?
precision@k How many top-k chunks were actually relevant?
MRR How high did the first relevant result rank?
answer accuracy Did the final LLM answer improve?

The last one matters most. Retrieval is not the product. Better retrieval is useful only if it improves final answers, reduces hallucination, or lowers cost.

Reranking: retrieve broad, then narrow

Embedding search uses a bi-encoder: query and documents are embedded independently. It is fast but approximate. A reranker uses a model that sees the query and document together and scores relevance more accurately.

Production pattern:

embed query -> retrieve top 50 -> rerank -> keep top 5 -> send to LLM

This often beats sending the raw top 5 from the vector database. The first stage is for recall. The reranker is for precision.

Chapter 22 goes deep on RAG. For this chapter, the point is simpler: embeddings are how your LLM application finds external memory.

Embeddings are not facts

Embedding search returns texts that are semantically close to the query. It does not prove those texts are true, current, authoritative, or sufficient.

This matters. Suppose the user asks:

Can I get a refund after 45 days?

Embedding search may retrieve:

Refunds are available within 30 days of purchase.

That is relevant. It is also a negative answer. Your prompt must let the model say “No, based on the policy.” Many bad RAG systems implicitly treat retrieved documents as support for a yes-answer because they retrieve only related chunks and then ask the model to be helpful.

Retrieval gives candidate evidence. The generation prompt still has to reason over it.

Good RAG instruction:

Use the retrieved policy excerpts to answer the question.
The excerpts may support, contradict, or fail to answer the user's request.
If the policy contradicts the request, explain the contradiction.
If the excerpts do not answer the question, say that the policy does not specify.

That one paragraph prevents a large class of false-positive answers.

Query rewriting

Users rarely ask retrieval-optimized questions. They ask:

it still hasnt shown up what now

The useful retrieval query is closer to:

delayed shipment order not delivered next steps support policy

Query rewriting uses an LLM to convert the user message into one or more search queries. This is often worth the extra call for messy domains.

def rewrite_query_prompt(user_question: str) -> str:
    return f"""
Rewrite the user's question into 3 search queries for retrieving support documentation.

Rules:
- Preserve the user's intent.
- Include likely policy terms.
- Do not answer the question.
- Return one query per line.

User question:
{user_question}
"""

Use query rewriting when:

  • user language differs from document language;
  • documents use internal terminology;
  • the user query is short or ambiguous;
  • recall matters more than one extra model call.

Skip it when queries are already well-formed, latency is tight, or you can get the same improvement with a domain-specific synonym table.

Context engineering: decide what the model sees

Prompt engineering asks, “How should I phrase the instruction?”

Context engineering asks, “What should be in the model’s window at all?”

The context window contains:

  • system instructions;
  • tool definitions;
  • retrieved documents;
  • conversation history;
  • few-shot examples;
  • user message;
  • reserved output space.

Every token competes with every other token. A 200k-token context window does not mean you should use 200k tokens. Long context costs money, increases latency, and can dilute attention.

Build a token budget

Treat the context window like a memory budget.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field


def estimate_tokens(text: str) -> int:
    return int(len(text.split()) * 1.3)


@dataclass
class ContextPart:
    name: str
    text: str
    priority: int
    max_tokens: int | None = None

    @property
    def tokens(self) -> int:
        return estimate_tokens(self.text)


@dataclass
class ContextBudget:
    max_input_tokens: int
    output_reserve: int
    parts: list[ContextPart] = field(default_factory=list)

    @property
    def available(self) -> int:
        return self.max_input_tokens - self.output_reserve

    def add(self, part: ContextPart):
        self.parts.append(part)

    def assemble(self) -> str:
        used = 0
        chosen = []
        for part in sorted(self.parts, key=lambda p: p.priority, reverse=True):
            text = part.text
            limit = part.max_tokens
            if limit and estimate_tokens(text) > limit:
                words = text.split()
                text = " ".join(words[: int(limit / 1.3)])
            tokens = estimate_tokens(text)
            if used + tokens <= self.available:
                chosen.append((part.name, text))
                used += tokens
        return "\n\n".join(f"<{name}>\n{text}\n</{name}>" for name, text in chosen)

This is deliberately simple. The important idea is explicit allocation. Do not let context grow accidentally.

Order matters

A practical ordering:

1. System instructions
2. Stable tool definitions or policies
3. Long-term memory and durable facts
4. Less important history or examples
5. Retrieved evidence
6. Current user request
7. Final short reminder of the task/output format

The most important stable instructions go first. The most relevant task-specific evidence goes near the end. The middle is where lower-priority content goes.

Conversation history should decay

Most previous turns are not equally important. Keep recent turns verbatim. Summarize older turns. Retrieve old facts only when relevant.

Three memory types:

Memory Storage Use
Short-term current context recent conversation
Long-term database/profile stable user/project facts
Episodic embeddings similar past interactions

An application that includes the entire conversation forever will eventually become slow, expensive, and confused. Summarize or retrieve.

Dynamic context beats static context

Do not include every tool, every policy, every document, and every example on every call. Select based on the task.

If the user asks a coding question, include code tools and relevant files. If the user asks a billing question, include billing docs. If the user asks for a JSON extraction, include schema examples. Static context wastes tokens and makes the model worse.

The dynamic context loop:

classify intent -> select tools -> retrieve docs -> choose examples -> assemble context -> call model

That loop is the heart of many serious LLM products.

Context assembly is a ranked merge

The hard part is not counting tokens. The hard part is choosing what gets cut when the budget is tight.

Treat each candidate context item as a record:

@dataclass
class CandidateContext:
    name: str
    text: str
    relevance: float
    authority: float
    recency: float
    token_cost: int

Then rank by value per token:

def context_value(item: CandidateContext) -> float:
    return (
        0.55 * item.relevance
        + 0.30 * item.authority
        + 0.15 * item.recency
    ) / max(item.token_cost, 1)

This is a simple heuristic, but it encodes the right idea. A recent low-authority chat message should not beat the official policy document. A highly relevant 200-token excerpt should beat a vaguely relevant 4,000-token dump. A source with the answer should beat a source with background.

The scoring weights depend on the app:

  • legal assistant: authority dominates;
  • coding assistant: relevance and recency dominate;
  • customer support: authority and exact policy match dominate;
  • personal assistant: user memory may dominate.

Context is not a pile. It is a ranked merge under a token budget.

Tool definitions are context too

Tool schemas can quietly eat thousands of tokens. If your agent has 80 tools and every tool definition goes into every call, the model spends context on tools it will not use.

Dynamic tool selection is context engineering:

TOOLS_BY_INTENT = {
    "code": ["read_file", "search_code", "run_tests", "edit_file"],
    "calendar": ["list_events", "create_event", "update_event"],
    "email": ["search_email", "draft_email", "send_email"],
    "research": ["web_search", "open_url", "summarize_page"],
}


def select_tool_names(intent: str) -> list[str]:
    return TOOLS_BY_INTENT.get(intent, [])

Tool descriptions should be short and discriminative. The model needs to know when to use the tool, not read a product manual.

Bad:

This tool allows users to access, retrieve, inspect, and view the contents of files...

Better:

Read a UTF-8 text file by path. Use when you need exact file contents.

Good tool descriptions reduce both token cost and wrong tool calls.

Prompt caching and cost control

LLM calls are not just function calls. They are financial transactions.

The cost of a request is roughly:

input_tokens * input_price + output_tokens * output_price

Output tokens usually cost more than input tokens. Long system prompts and retrieved contexts can dominate input cost. Verbose answers dominate output cost.

The first cost rule: log tokens and dollars for every request.

MODEL_PRICES = {
    "small": {"input": 0.25, "output": 1.00},
    "large": {"input": 3.00, "output": 15.00},
}


def estimate_cost(model: str, input_tokens: int, output_tokens: int) -> float:
    price = MODEL_PRICES[model]
    return (
        input_tokens / 1_000_000 * price["input"]
        + output_tokens / 1_000_000 * price["output"]
    )

Use real provider pricing in production and update it automatically or at deploy time. Hardcoded prices age badly.

Provider prompt caching

Prompt caching reuses a stable prefix. If your system prompt, tool definitions, and examples are identical across requests, the provider can reuse the prefix computation or KV cache. You pay less and often get lower time to first token.

Cache-friendly layout:

[stable system prompt]
[stable tool definitions]
[stable examples]
[stable policy]
--- cache breakpoint ---
[retrieved context]
[conversation tail]
[current user message]

The invariant: caches match prefixes. If you put a dynamic timestamp at the top, the cache misses every time. If tool definitions reorder randomly, the cache misses. If you insert retrieved documents before stable examples, the stable examples stop caching.

Keep stable content first. Put volatile content last.

Exact cache, semantic cache, prompt cache

Three caches solve three different problems:

Cache Match type Best for
Exact cache identical prompt deterministic repeated tasks
Semantic cache similar meaning repeated user questions
Prompt cache identical prefix long stable system/context prefixes

Exact cache:

import hashlib
import json


class ExactCache:
    def __init__(self):
        self.store = {}

    def key(self, model: str, messages: list[dict], temperature: float) -> str:
        payload = json.dumps(
            {"model": model, "messages": messages, "temperature": temperature},
            sort_keys=True,
        )
        return hashlib.sha256(payload.encode()).hexdigest()

    def get(self, model, messages, temperature):
        if temperature != 0:
            return None
        return self.store.get(self.key(model, messages, temperature))

    def put(self, model, messages, temperature, response):
        if temperature == 0:
            self.store[self.key(model, messages, temperature)] = response

Semantic cache:

class SemanticCache:
    def __init__(self, threshold: float = 0.92):
        self.threshold = threshold
        self.entries = []

    def get(self, query: str):
        q = bag_of_words_embedding(query)
        best = None
        best_score = 0.0
        for entry in self.entries:
            score = cosine_sparse(q, entry["embedding"])
            if score > best_score:
                best = entry
                best_score = score
        if best and best_score >= self.threshold:
            return best["response"], best_score
        return None

    def put(self, query: str, response: str):
        self.entries.append({
            "query": query,
            "embedding": bag_of_words_embedding(query),
            "response": response,
        })

The toy semantic cache uses bag-of-words. In production, use a real embedding model and a vector index.

Model routing

Not every request deserves the strongest model.

Use a smaller model for:

  • classification;
  • extraction with a schema;
  • FAQ answers from retrieved docs;
  • rewriting;
  • short summaries;
  • cacheable deterministic transformations.

Use a stronger model for:

  • ambiguous reasoning;
  • code generation;
  • multi-document synthesis;
  • planning;
  • high-value user actions;
  • cases where smaller models fail evals.

A crude router:

def route_model(task: str, needs_reasoning: bool, risk: str) -> str:
    if risk == "high":
        return "large"
    if needs_reasoning:
        return "large"
    if "extract" in task.lower() or "classify" in task.lower():
        return "small"
    return "small"

In serious systems, replace the rules with an evaluated classifier. But start with rules. They are inspectable.

Circuit breakers

Put a budget guard around the system:

class BudgetGuard:
    def __init__(self, monthly_budget: float):
        self.monthly_budget = monthly_budget
        self.spent = 0.0

    def record(self, cost: float):
        self.spent += cost

    def mode(self) -> str:
        ratio = self.spent / self.monthly_budget
        if ratio >= 0.95:
            return "cached_only"
        if ratio >= 0.85:
            return "cheap_models_only"
        if ratio >= 0.70:
            return "warn"
        return "normal"

At 70 percent, alert. At 85 percent, route to cheaper models. At 95 percent, serve cached responses or reject low-priority requests. This sounds harsh until a bug loops over a million rows and calls an expensive model for each one.

Latency has two clocks

Users feel latency in two ways:

  • time to first token (TTFT): how long before the response starts;
  • tokens per second (TPS): how fast the response streams after it starts.

Long prompts mainly hurt TTFT because the server must prefill the prompt before decoding. Long outputs hurt total response time because every output token is generated sequentially. Prompt caching reduces TTFT for repeated prefixes. Shorter answers reduce total latency and output cost. Speculative decoding and batching help on the provider side, but your application still controls prompt length and output length.

For user-facing apps, log:

request_id
model
input_tokens
output_tokens
cached_input_tokens
ttft_ms
total_latency_ms
cost_usd
cache_status
route

Then look at percentiles, not averages. A mean latency of 900 ms can hide a p99 of 12 seconds. LLM products live or die in the tail.

Compression before generation

The cheapest token is the one you do not send.

Prompt compression can be manual or model-assisted. Manual compression means cutting vague wording, repeated rules, and examples that do not improve evals. Model-assisted compression means asking a model to rewrite context while preserving facts needed for a task.

Use compression for:

  • verbose system prompts;
  • repeated policy text;
  • long conversation histories;
  • retrieved chunks with boilerplate;
  • documents where only a few facts matter.

Do not compress:

  • legal text where exact wording matters;
  • code snippets where small changes break meaning;
  • evidence spans used for citations;
  • schemas and tool definitions that must remain exact.

A compression prompt:

Compress the following context for use in an LLM prompt.

Rules:
- Preserve all facts needed to answer the user question.
- Preserve exact numbers, dates, names, and policy conditions.
- Remove examples, boilerplate, and repeated language.
- Do not add new facts.

User question:
{question}

Context:
{context}

Always evaluate compression. A compressed context that saves 60 percent of tokens and drops the answer is worse than no compression.

Cost is a product feature

Unit economics shape product behavior. If your app costs $0.08 per request and users pay $20/month, power users can destroy margins. If you cut cost to $0.006 per request, the same product becomes viable.

Cost controls are not just finance:

  • routing lets simple requests feel faster;
  • caching makes repeated questions instant;
  • shorter prompts reduce latency;
  • structured outputs reduce retry loops;
  • evals prevent expensive regressions;
  • batch processing makes offline jobs affordable.

The best LLM products feel better because they are cheaper to run. Cost work and UX work are often the same work.

Build a minimal LLM application layer

Now combine the pieces into a small provider-agnostic application layer. The code below uses a fake model call so the architecture is clear. Swap fake_llm_call for your provider SDK.

from pydantic import BaseModel


class LLMRequest(BaseModel):
    task: str
    user_input: str
    context: list[str] = []
    examples: list[str] = []
    output_format: str = "text"
    risk: str = "low"
    needs_reasoning: bool = False


class LLMResponse(BaseModel):
    text: str
    model: str
    input_tokens: int
    output_tokens: int
    cost: float
    cache_hit: bool = False

Prompt builder:

def build_messages(req: LLMRequest) -> list[dict]:
    system = """
You are a reliable LLM application component.
Follow the task exactly.
If context is provided, use it before relying on general knowledge.
If the answer is not supported by context, say so.
"""

    parts = [f"Task:\n{req.task}"]

    if req.examples:
        parts.append("Examples:\n" + "\n\n".join(req.examples))

    if req.context:
        joined_context = "\n\n".join(
            f"<doc id='{i}'>\n{text}\n</doc>"
            for i, text in enumerate(req.context)
        )
        parts.append("Context:\n" + joined_context)

    parts.append("User input:\n" + req.user_input)

    if req.output_format == "json":
        parts.append("Return only valid JSON. Do not wrap it in markdown.")

    user = "\n\n".join(parts)
    return [
        {"role": "system", "content": system.strip()},
        {"role": "user", "content": user},
    ]

Fake model call:

def fake_llm_call(model: str, messages: list[dict]) -> tuple[str, int, int]:
    prompt = "\n".join(m["content"] for m in messages)
    input_tokens = estimate_tokens(prompt)
    if "Return only valid JSON" in prompt:
        text = '{"answer": "simulated", "supported_by_context": true}'
    else:
        text = "Simulated answer based on the provided context."
    output_tokens = estimate_tokens(text)
    return text, input_tokens, output_tokens

Application client:

class LLMAppClient:
    def __init__(self, monthly_budget: float = 100.0):
        self.exact_cache = ExactCache()
        self.semantic_cache = SemanticCache()
        self.budget = BudgetGuard(monthly_budget)

    def run(self, req: LLMRequest) -> LLMResponse:
        cached = self.semantic_cache.get(req.user_input)
        if cached:
            text, _score = cached
            return LLMResponse(
                text=text,
                model="cache",
                input_tokens=0,
                output_tokens=0,
                cost=0.0,
                cache_hit=True,
            )

        model = route_model(req.task, req.needs_reasoning, req.risk)
        if self.budget.mode() == "cheap_models_only":
            model = "small"
        if self.budget.mode() == "cached_only":
            raise RuntimeError("Budget exhausted: cached-only mode and no cache hit")

        messages = build_messages(req)
        exact = self.exact_cache.get(model, messages, temperature=0.0)
        if exact:
            return LLMResponse(
                text=exact,
                model=model,
                input_tokens=0,
                output_tokens=0,
                cost=0.0,
                cache_hit=True,
            )

        text, input_tokens, output_tokens = fake_llm_call(model, messages)
        cost = estimate_cost(model, input_tokens, output_tokens)
        self.budget.record(cost)

        self.exact_cache.put(model, messages, temperature=0.0, response=text)
        if req.risk == "low":
            self.semantic_cache.put(req.user_input, text)

        return LLMResponse(
            text=text,
            model=model,
            input_tokens=input_tokens,
            output_tokens=output_tokens,
            cost=cost,
        )

Use it:

client = LLMAppClient(monthly_budget=10.0)

request = LLMRequest(
    task="Answer the support question from the provided context.",
    user_input="How long do refunds take?",
    context=[
        "Refunds are processed within 5-7 business days after approval.",
        "Shipping refunds are handled separately.",
    ],
    output_format="json",
)

response = client.run(request)
print(response.model, response.cost, response.text)

This is small, but it has the right shape:

  • prompts are assembled, not handwritten each time;
  • context is explicit;
  • output format is explicit;
  • models are routed;
  • cache is checked before calling the model;
  • costs are recorded;
  • budget state can degrade behavior.

That is the application layer you build on.

The eval loop that keeps it honest

An LLM app without evals is a regression machine. Every prompt change, model upgrade, retrieval tweak, schema change, and cache policy can alter behavior.

Build three layers of evals.

Unit evals

Small deterministic tests for local components:

  • prompt builder includes the required sections;
  • schema parser rejects invalid outputs;
  • context budget never exceeds the token limit;
  • router sends extraction tasks to the small model;
  • cache ignores nonzero-temperature calls.

Example:

def test_context_budget_reserves_output_space():
    budget = ContextBudget(max_input_tokens=1000, output_reserve=200)
    budget.add(ContextPart("a", "word " * 1000, priority=1))
    assembled = budget.assemble()
    assert estimate_tokens(assembled) <= 800

These tests do not call the LLM. They keep your scaffolding sane.

Golden evals

Curated examples with expected behavior. These call the model.

GOLDEN_CASES = [
    {
        "name": "refund policy negative case",
        "question": "Can I get a refund after 45 days?",
        "context": "Refunds are available within 30 days of purchase.",
        "must_contain": ["30 days"],
        "must_not_contain": ["yes"],
    },
    {
        "name": "missing answer",
        "question": "Do you ship to Nepal?",
        "context": "We ship to the United States and Canada.",
        "must_contain": ["does not specify"],
        "must_not_contain": ["yes"],
    },
]

Golden evals should include failures from production. Every time the app gives a bad answer, turn that case into an eval. That is how the system gets harder to break.

Online evals

Production monitoring catches drift:

  • schema failure rate;
  • retry rate;
  • cache hit rate;
  • average and p95 cost;
  • answer thumbs-up/down;
  • human escalation rate;
  • retrieval recall on labeled queries;
  • tool call error rate;
  • refusal rate;
  • hallucination reports.

Offline evals tell you whether you should ship. Online evals tell you whether what you shipped is still working.

Model upgrades require migration tests

Do not swap models because a benchmark improved. Run your evals.

Model upgrades can change:

  • instruction following;
  • JSON reliability;
  • verbosity;
  • refusal behavior;
  • tool call eagerness;
  • citation style;
  • sensitivity to examples;
  • cost and latency;
  • output distribution under the same temperature.

The migration process:

  1. Run old model and new model on the same golden set.
  2. Compare task metrics and cost.
  3. Inspect disagreements manually.
  4. Update prompts only if the new model requires it.
  5. Roll out behind a percentage flag.
  6. Watch online metrics.

The model provider’s eval is not your eval. Your app has its own failure modes.

Failure modes

LLM application failures are boringly repeatable.

The prompt has no contract. The model is asked to “help” and produces whatever it thinks helpful means. Fix: define task, constraints, and output format.

The output is prose but the application expects data. Fix: use structured outputs and validation.

The context is a dump. The model receives ten irrelevant chunks and one relevant chunk. Fix: retrieval evaluation, reranking, and context budgets.

The model is used for memory. The app expects the model to remember facts not in the prompt. Fix: long-term memory and retrieval.

The same expensive request runs repeatedly. Fix: exact, semantic, and prompt caching.

The strongest model handles every request. Fix: model routing and evals.

No one logs cost. The invoice is the first observability tool. Fix: token and dollar logging per request.

Validation happens only in the prompt. “Return valid JSON” is not validation. Fix: parse, validate, repair once, then fail closed.

Few-shot examples drift from production data. The prompt examples were written once and never updated. Fix: mine real failures into new examples.

Long context is trusted without tests. The model accepts 200k tokens, so the team assumes it uses them well. Fix: long-context evals with distractors and answer-bearing chunks at different positions.

What you’d actually use

The boring default for most LLM apps:

Need Default
General text generation clear prompt contract, temperature 0.3-0.7
Classification/extraction structured output with schema validation
Multi-step reasoning reasoning model or explicit scratchpad, measured against evals
Repeated answers exact cache for deterministic calls, semantic cache for FAQ-like queries
Repeated long prefixes provider prompt caching
External knowledge embeddings plus retrieval, then reranking
Long conversation recent turns verbatim, older turns summarized
Cost control token logging, model routing, budget circuit breaker
Reliability eval suite before prompt changes ship

Prompting is still important. It is just not the whole job. The whole job is controlling the interaction between model, context, output contract, retrieval, cache, and cost.

If you remember one rule from this chapter, make it this:

Every LLM feature needs an interface contract.

The contract says what the model sees, what it should do, what it may output, how the output is validated, and what happens when validation fails. Without that contract, you have a demo. With it, you have the beginning of a system.

Where this goes next

This chapter gave you the everyday mechanics of working with LLMs: prompts, examples, schemas, embeddings, context, and caching. Chapter 22 turns those pieces into retrieval-augmented generation and tool use. That is where the application stops relying on the model’s memory and starts grounding itself in external systems: vector databases, documents, APIs, function calls, and tool execution loops.

The progression matters. You cannot build good RAG if you do not understand embeddings. You cannot build reliable tool use if you do not understand structured outputs. You cannot build agents if you do not understand context budgets. The next chapters stack these primitives into larger systems.

For now, the practical stance is simple: do not worship the prompt, and do not dismiss it. Treat it as one component in a larger engineered interface. The model is powerful, but the application is your responsibility.

Chapter 22 - RAG and Tool Use

The base model is not your database.

It does not know your refund policy unless that policy was in its training data. It does not know the current status of order ORD-9241. It does not know what changed in your codebase this morning. It cannot check a calendar, query Postgres, send an email, run a test suite, or call a payment API. It can only predict tokens.

That sounds limiting until you build the missing layer.

Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) lets the model read external knowledge at request time. Tool use lets the model ask your application to perform actions. RAG gives the model memory outside its weights. Tools give it hands outside its text stream.

Almost every serious LLM application is some combination of these two ideas:

retrieve facts -> put facts in context -> let the model reason
choose tool -> execute tool in code -> put result in context -> let the model answer

This chapter is the bridge from “working with LLMs” to “building agents.” We are not building autonomous agents yet. That comes later. Here we build the two primitives that agents are made of: grounding and action.

The practical stance:

  • Use RAG when the model needs private, fresh, or auditable knowledge.
  • Use tools when the model needs current data, computation, or side effects.
  • Use fine-tuning when you need behavior, style, or task adaptation baked into the model.
  • Do not use the model’s memory as your source of truth.

If Chapter 21 was about the interface contract around one LLM call, this chapter is about connecting that call to the outside world.

Why RAG exists

Suppose a user asks:

What is the refund policy for enterprise annual plans?

The model can answer from general knowledge:

Enterprise annual plans usually have custom refund terms. Contact sales or support.

That answer is plausible, polite, and useless. Your actual policy may say:

Enterprise annual plans are eligible for a prorated refund within 60 days of purchase.
Refunds after 60 days require VP approval.

The model cannot infer that policy from vibes. You must provide it.

Fine-tuning seems tempting: train the model on the company wiki. But fine-tuning is the wrong default for knowledge.

Need Fine-tuning RAG
Fresh documents stale until retrained update index immediately
Source citations hard natural
Private docs baked into weights kept in data store
Corrections retrain or patch update document
Per-user permissions difficult filter retrieval
Cost to update high low

Fine-tuning changes the model. RAG changes the context.

For factual knowledge, context is usually the right place. The model should not “know” your refund policy in its weights. It should read the current policy when answering the question.

The RAG loop

The basic RAG loop has two phases.

Offline indexing:

documents -> clean -> chunk -> embed -> store

Online answering:

query -> embed -> retrieve -> rerank -> build prompt -> generate answer

The simplest useful RAG prompt:

Answer the user's question using only the provided sources.
If the sources do not contain the answer, say that the sources do not specify.
Cite the source IDs you used.

Sources:
{retrieved_chunks}

Question:
{question}

That is the entire pattern. Production RAG is the same pattern with better chunking, better retrieval, better ranking, better prompting, permission filters, evals, and observability.

Do not let the simplicity fool you. Most RAG failures come from one of these steps being sloppy.

Ingestion: garbage in, hallucinations out

RAG starts before embeddings. It starts with documents.

Real documents are messy:

  • PDFs with broken reading order;
  • HTML pages with navigation menus;
  • tables split across pages;
  • duplicated docs;
  • old versions next to new versions;
  • screenshots with embedded text;
  • Slack threads with missing context;
  • code files with generated artifacts;
  • policies with footnotes that matter.

If your ingestion pipeline turns these into noisy chunks, no embedding model will save you.

The ingestion pipeline:

load -> normalize -> clean -> split -> attach metadata -> index

Load

Load documents with the best parser for the format. Plain text and markdown are easy. PDFs are not. HTML needs boilerplate removal. Tables need structure preservation. Code needs language-aware splitting.

For a practical system, store the raw source and the parsed text:

from dataclasses import dataclass


@dataclass
class RawDocument:
    id: str
    source_uri: str
    media_type: str
    raw_text: str
    metadata: dict

The raw document lets you reprocess later when your chunking improves.

Normalize

Normalization makes text consistent:

  • convert weird whitespace;
  • fix Unicode normalization;
  • remove duplicated headers and footers;
  • preserve headings;
  • preserve lists;
  • preserve table structure when possible.

Do not over-clean. A refund policy’s exact wording matters. A code block’s indentation matters. A table’s column names matter.

Attach metadata

Metadata is not optional. It is how you filter, cite, debug, and enforce permissions.

Each chunk should know:

  • source document ID;
  • title;
  • URL or path;
  • section heading;
  • author or owner;
  • created/updated timestamp;
  • access control labels;
  • document type;
  • chunk position;
  • version.
@dataclass
class Chunk:
    id: str
    text: str
    source_id: str
    title: str
    section: str
    position: int
    metadata: dict

Without metadata, every retrieval bug becomes archaeology.

Chunking: retrieval starts with the cut

Chunking decides what unit can be retrieved.

If chunks are too small, they match precisely but lack context:

Refunds after 60 days require approval.

Approval from whom? For which plan? Under what conditions?

If chunks are too large, they contain enough context but match poorly:

Twenty pages of billing policy, cancellation policy, tax handling, renewal terms,
payment failure behavior, regional terms, and refund rules.

The retrieval score gets diluted by unrelated text.

The boring default is 256-512 tokens with overlap. That is a starting point, not a law.

Fixed-size chunking

Simple and often good enough:

def chunk_words(text: str, chunk_size: int = 350, overlap: int = 60) -> list[str]:
    words = text.split()
    chunks = []
    step = chunk_size - overlap
    for start in range(0, len(words), step):
        chunk = " ".join(words[start:start + chunk_size])
        if chunk:
            chunks.append(chunk)
    return chunks

Use it for plain prose, logs, transcripts, and early prototypes.

Structure-aware chunking

For markdown, docs, and policies, split by headings first:

def split_markdown_sections(text: str) -> list[tuple[str, str]]:
    sections = []
    current_title = "Untitled"
    current_lines = []

    for line in text.splitlines():
        if line.startswith("#"):
            if current_lines:
                sections.append((current_title, "\n".join(current_lines).strip()))
            current_title = line.lstrip("#").strip()
            current_lines = []
        else:
            current_lines.append(line)

    if current_lines:
        sections.append((current_title, "\n".join(current_lines).strip()))

    return sections

Then apply token limits inside each section. This keeps headings attached to their content. The heading often carries the key meaning: “Enterprise Refunds”, “Data Retention”, “Regional Exceptions.”

Parent-child chunking

Parent-child chunking solves the precision-context tradeoff.

Index small child chunks. Return larger parent chunks.

Parent: full section, 800 tokens
Children: overlapping 150-token windows

Search child -> retrieve child ID -> send parent to model

Implementation shape:

@dataclass
class ParentChildIndex:
    parents: dict[str, Chunk]
    children: dict[str, Chunk]
    child_to_parent: dict[str, str]

At query time:

child_hits = vector_search(query, child_embeddings, top_k=20)
parent_ids = dedupe([child_to_parent[hit.id] for hit in child_hits])
parents = [parent_index[parent_id] for parent_id in parent_ids[:5]]

This is one of the highest-return RAG improvements. Search small, answer with enough context.

Late chunking and contextual chunks

Basic chunking splits documents before embedding. Late chunking embeds with broader context, then slices representations into chunks. Contextual retrieval prepends each chunk with a short description of where it came from:

This chunk is from the Enterprise Billing Policy, section "Refunds",
describing prorated refund rules for annual plans.

{chunk_text}

That summary helps the embedding model understand what pronouns and local references mean. A chunk saying “This applies only after day 60” becomes more retrievable when the context says it is about refunds.

The principle: a chunk should be understandable on its own. If it is not, add context or change the chunk boundary.

Embedding and indexing

Chapter 21 covered embeddings. In RAG, embeddings are the retrieval index.

Each chunk gets a vector:

chunk text -> embedding model -> vector

The query gets a vector in the same space:

user question -> embedding model -> query vector

Nearest chunks are retrieved by vector similarity.

A minimal in-memory index:

import math
from collections import Counter


def sparse_embed(text: str) -> dict[str, float]:
    counts = Counter(text.lower().split())
    norm = math.sqrt(sum(v * v for v in counts.values()))
    if norm == 0:
        return {}
    return {k: v / norm for k, v in counts.items()}


def cosine(a: dict[str, float], b: dict[str, float]) -> float:
    return sum(a.get(k, 0.0) * b.get(k, 0.0) for k in set(a) | set(b))


class SimpleVectorStore:
    def __init__(self):
        self.rows = []

    def add(self, chunk: Chunk):
        self.rows.append({
            "chunk": chunk,
            "embedding": sparse_embed(chunk.text),
        })

    def search(self, query: str, top_k: int = 5, where: dict | None = None):
        query_embedding = sparse_embed(query)
        scored = []
        for row in self.rows:
            chunk = row["chunk"]
            if where:
                if any(chunk.metadata.get(k) != v for k, v in where.items()):
                    continue
            scored.append((cosine(query_embedding, row["embedding"]), chunk))
        scored.sort(key=lambda item: item[0], reverse=True)
        return scored[:top_k]

This uses sparse bag-of-words embeddings so the code is readable. Production uses dense embedding models and a vector database. The interface is the same.

Metadata filtering before search

Always filter before vector search when you can.

If the user can only access documents in workspace acme, retrieval must filter to workspace_id = acme. If the user asks about the latest policy, filter out old versions. If the question is about billing, filter to billing docs when the category is reliable.

Metadata filters improve:

  • security;
  • speed;
  • relevance;
  • debuggability.

Do not rely on the model to ignore documents the user should not see. They should never enter the context.

Permissions are part of retrieval

Access control is not a post-processing step.

Bad pipeline:

search all documents -> retrieve top chunks -> ask model not to reveal unauthorized ones

Good pipeline:

compute user's allowed scopes -> filter index -> search allowed documents only

The model should never see unauthorized text. If the text enters the context, you have already lost the security boundary. A later instruction saying “do not reveal this” is not a permission system.

A chunk should carry access metadata:

@dataclass
class Access:
    workspace_id: str
    allowed_roles: set[str]
    allowed_user_ids: set[str]


def can_read(user: dict, access: Access) -> bool:
    if user["workspace_id"] != access.workspace_id:
        return False
    if user["id"] in access.allowed_user_ids:
        return True
    return bool(set(user["roles"]) & access.allowed_roles)

Then retrieval filters before scoring:

def permitted_chunks(user: dict, chunks: list[Chunk]) -> list[Chunk]:
    return [
        chunk for chunk in chunks
        if can_read(user, chunk.metadata["access"])
    ]

In production, this belongs in the database query or vector store filter, not in Python after retrieval. If the vector database supports metadata filters, use them. If it does not support your permission model, put an authorization layer in front of it or choose a different store.

The same rule applies to multi-tenant systems: tenant ID is a retrieval filter, not a prompt instruction.

Vector search is not enough

Semantic similarity is not relevance.

Query:

What was revenue last quarter?

Vector search might retrieve a chunk about “revenue strategy” because it is semantically close. The correct chunk might say:

Q3 earnings were $47.2M, up 12% quarter over quarter.

It does not use the word “revenue.” Pure vector search may miss it.

This is why production retrieval is usually hybrid.

Hybrid search: dense plus sparse

Dense vector search understands paraphrases. Sparse keyword search catches exact terms, IDs, codes, names, and numbers.

Use both.

vector search top 50
BM25 keyword search top 50
merge with reciprocal rank fusion
rerank top 50
send top 5 to model

BM25 in plain English

BM25 scores documents higher when they contain query terms, especially rare terms. It avoids over-rewarding repeated words and normalizes for document length.

You do not need to memorize the formula. You need to know its personality:

  • great for exact identifiers;
  • great for rare words;
  • weak for paraphrases;
  • fast and reliable;
  • still useful after decades.

If your corpus contains product IDs, error codes, legal terms, API names, or version numbers, pure embedding search is irresponsible. Add keyword search.

Reciprocal Rank Fusion

Dense scores and BM25 scores are not directly comparable. RRF merges rankings instead of raw scores:

RRF(d)=∑r1k+rankr(d)\text{RRF}(d) = \sum_r \frac{1}{k + \text{rank}_r(d)} RRF(d)=​r​∑​​​k+rank​r​​(d)​​1​​

This says: a document gets points for ranking high in each retrieval list. A document that ranks well in both dense and sparse search rises to the top.

Code:

def reciprocal_rank_fusion(rankings: list[list[str]], k: int = 60) -> list[str]:
    scores = {}
    for ranking in rankings:
        for rank, doc_id in enumerate(ranking, start=1):
            scores[doc_id] = scores.get(doc_id, 0.0) + 1.0 / (k + rank)
    return [
        doc_id for doc_id, _score in
        sorted(scores.items(), key=lambda item: item[1], reverse=True)
    ]

RRF is boring and strong. Use it before inventing a learned fusion model.

Reranking: retrieve broad, choose carefully

The first retriever should optimize recall. It should find candidates that might contain the answer. The reranker optimizes precision. It decides which candidates are most useful.

The pattern:

retrieve top 50 -> rerank -> keep top 5

A bi-encoder embeds query and document separately. Fast, precomputable, approximate.

A cross-encoder reads query and document together. Slower, more accurate.

For reranking, the model sees:

Query: What is the enterprise refund window?
Document: Enterprise annual plans are eligible for prorated refunds within 60 days...
Score relevance from 0 to 1.

Because the query and document are processed together, the reranker can catch exact answerability better than vector similarity.

You can use hosted rerankers, open rerankers, or an LLM judge. The LLM judge is the most flexible and usually the most expensive.

A simple LLM-style rerank prompt:

Score whether the document can answer the query.

Score:
0 = irrelevant
1 = related but cannot answer
2 = partially answers
3 = directly answers

Query:
{query}

Document:
{document}

Return only JSON: {"score": 0|1|2|3, "reason": "..."}

Use it for debugging even if production uses a cheaper reranker. It teaches you what your retriever is doing.

Query transformation

Sometimes retrieval fails because the user’s query is bad.

User:

what about enterprise tho

That may be a follow-up to:

What is the refund policy for annual plans?

The real search query should be:

enterprise annual plan refund policy

Query transformation rewrites the user input into retrieval-friendly queries.

Conversation-aware rewriting

Use the recent conversation:

def rewrite_followup_prompt(history: str, question: str) -> str:
    return f"""
Rewrite the user's latest question as a standalone search query.

Conversation:
{history}

Latest question:
{question}

Return only the rewritten query.
"""

This is mandatory for chat-based RAG. Without it, follow-up questions retrieve nonsense.

Multi-query retrieval

Generate several search queries and merge results:

User question:
How do I undo a mistaken invoice payment?

Generated queries:
1. mistaken invoice payment reversal
2. refund accidentally paid invoice
3. void payment after invoice paid
4. billing correction overpayment policy

Different queries catch different document wording. Retrieve for all, then fuse.

HyDE

Hypothetical Document Embeddings (HyDE) generate a fake answer, embed that fake answer, and retrieve real documents similar to it.

Why it works: questions and answers live in different language shapes. A hypothetical answer often sits closer to the real answer chunks than the question does.

HyDE prompt:

Write a short hypothetical documentation paragraph that would answer this question.
Do not mention uncertainty.

Question:
{question}
