        trace = RequestTrace(context.request_id)
        started = time.time()

        span = trace.start_span("input_guardrails")
        injection = check_prompt_injection(user_input)
        pii = redact_pii(user_input)
        trace.end_span(span, injection_passed=injection.passed, pii=pii.reason)

        if not injection.passed:
            return LLMAppResponse(
                request_id=context.request_id,
                text="I cannot help with that request.",
                model="none",
                prompt_fingerprint=self.manifest.prompt.fingerprint,
                input_tokens=0,
                output_tokens=0,
                latency_ms=round((time.time() - started) * 1000, 2),
                cost_usd=0.0,
                blocked=True,
                block_reason=injection.reason,
                risk_level=RiskLevel.HIGH,
            )

        effective_input = pii.modified_text or user_input
        key = self.cache_key(context, effective_input)
        if key in self.cache:
            cached = self.cache[key]
            cached.cache_hit = True
            return cached

        span = trace.start_span("retrieve")
        docs = self.retrieve(context, effective_input)
        trace.end_span(span, documents=[doc.doc_id for doc in docs])

        span = trace.start_span("model_call", model=self.manifest.prompt.model)
        prompt = self.render_prompt(effective_input, docs)
        text, input_tokens, output_tokens = self.call_model(prompt)
        trace.end_span(span, input_tokens=input_tokens, output_tokens=output_tokens)

        span = trace.start_span("output_guardrails")
        output_pii = redact_pii(text)
        safe_text = output_pii.modified_text or text
        trace.end_span(span, pii=output_pii.reason)

        cost = self.estimate_cost(input_tokens, output_tokens)
        response = LLMAppResponse(
            request_id=context.request_id,
            text=safe_text,
            model=self.manifest.prompt.model,
            prompt_fingerprint=self.manifest.prompt.fingerprint,
            input_tokens=input_tokens,
            output_tokens=output_tokens,
            latency_ms=round((time.time() - started) * 1000, 2),
            cost_usd=cost,
            citations=[doc.doc_id for doc in docs if doc.doc_id in safe_text],
            risk_level=RiskLevel.LOW,
        )

        self.cost_ledger.add(CostRecord(
            request_id=context.request_id,
            user_id=context.user_id,
            org_id=context.org_id,
            behavior_id=self.manifest.behavior_id,
            model=response.model,
            input_tokens=input_tokens,
            output_tokens=output_tokens,
            cache_hit=False,
            retries=0,
            cost_usd=cost,
        ))

        self.cache[key] = response
        print(json.dumps(trace.summary(), indent=2))
        return response

And a runnable example:

prompt = PromptSpec(
    name="support_refund_answer",
    version="v3",
    template=(
        "You are a support assistant. Answer using only the retrieved context.\n"
        "If the context is insufficient, say you do not have enough information.\n\n"
        "Retrieved context:\n{retrieved_context}\n\n"
        "User question:\n{user_input}\n\n"
        "Answer with a concise response and cite source IDs."
    ),
    model="fast-general-model",
    temperature=0.2,
    max_output_tokens=300,
)

manifest = BehaviorManifest(
    app_name="support_assistant",
    app_version="2026.05.09",
    prompt=prompt,
    retrieval_index_version="support-index-2026-05-01",
    retrieval_config={"top_k": 5, "rerank": True},
    tool_schema_version="support-tools-v4",
    guardrail_policy_version="support-policy-v7",
    judge_rubric_version="support-judge-v5",
)

app = LLMApplication(manifest)
ctx = app.make_context(
    user_id="user_123",
    org_id="org_acme",
    surface="support_chat",
    permissions={"support:read"},
)
response = app.handle(ctx, "Can I get a refund for an annual enterprise plan?")
print(response.text)

This toy app has many missing pieces: no real model API, no async streaming, no actual vector search, no real judge, no OpenTelemetry exporter, no database. That is fine. The architecture is the point. You can replace each stub with a production implementation without changing the contract.

From scratch means implement it once, then use the library for the rest of your life.

Streaming is part of UX and observability

Streaming is usually discussed as a frontend feature. It is also a backend design choice.

Without streaming, a user waits for the full answer. With streaming, they see the answer begin quickly. Total generation time may be identical, but perceived latency improves. For chat, time to first token often matters more than total time.

Track both:

Metric Meaning
Time to first token how long before the user sees progress
Total latency how long until the response completes
Tokens per second generation throughput
Stream interruption rate how often streams fail

Streaming complicates validation. If you validate only after the full answer, unsafe content may already have reached the user. If you validate every token, latency and complexity explode.

Use risk-based streaming:

  • Low-risk general chat: stream immediately, validate final output for logging and future tuning.
  • Medium-risk enterprise assistant: stream sentence chunks after lightweight checks.
  • High-risk regulated output: generate fully, validate, then display.
  • Tool actions: never stream execution as approval. Stream explanation, but gate the action.

For structured outputs, streaming is often the wrong default. If the caller needs valid JSON, wait for the full object, validate it, and return it. Streaming partial JSON is possible, but many clients handle it badly.

Tool actions need transaction boundaries

Tool use turns text into action. That is where product risk jumps.

A read-only tool is usually safe:

get_order_status(order_id)

A write tool needs controls:

refund_payment(order_id, amount)
delete_account(user_id)
send_email(to, subject, body)
run_shell_command(command)

The model can propose a tool call. The application decides whether to execute it.

Use four classes of tools:

Class Example Approval
Read search_docs, get_order_status automatic if authorized
Compute calculate_tax, parse_pdf automatic with resource limits
Draft draft_email, prepare_refund automatic, no side effect
Write send_email, issue_refund explicit approval or policy gate

Add idempotency keys to side-effecting tools. If a retry happens, you must not refund twice.

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ToolPolicy:
    name: str
    required_permission: str
    side_effecting: bool
    max_risk: RiskLevel
    requires_human_approval: bool


def authorize_tool_call(context: RequestContext, policy: ToolPolicy) -> GuardrailResult:
    if policy.required_permission not in context.permissions:
        return GuardrailResult(
            passed=False,
            category="tool_authorization",
            reason=f"missing permission: {policy.required_permission}",
            severity=RiskLevel.HIGH,
        )

    if policy.side_effecting and policy.requires_human_approval:
        return GuardrailResult(
            passed=False,
            category="tool_approval_required",
            reason=f"tool requires approval: {policy.name}",
            severity=RiskLevel.MEDIUM,
        )

    return GuardrailResult(True, "tool_authorization", "authorized")

Do not hide tool failures. If the order API is down, the assistant should not invent an order status. It should say the order system is unavailable and suggest a next step. Tool failures are facts the model must respect.

Feedback becomes data only after labeling

Thumbs-up and thumbs-down buttons are not evals. They are weak signals.

A thumbs-down can mean:

  • answer was wrong
  • answer was too long
  • answer was too short
  • answer was rude
  • answer refused incorrectly
  • answer did not use the right source
  • answer was correct but the user disliked the policy
  • user clicked accidentally

Use feedback as triage. Sample negative feedback into review. Label the failure type. Add recurring failures to evals.

Useful feedback schema:

@dataclass
class UserFeedback:
    request_id: str
    user_id: str
    rating: Literal["positive", "negative"]
    reason: str | None
    free_text: str | None
    created_at: str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat())


@dataclass
class LabeledFailure:
    request_id: str
    label: Literal[
        "incorrect",
        "unsupported",
        "unsafe",
        "bad_refusal",
        "missing_citation",
        "tool_error",
        "latency",
        "tone",
    ]
    notes: str
    should_add_to_eval: bool

The product loop is:

production request -> user feedback -> human label -> eval case -> prompt/system fix -> deployment gate

If feedback never becomes evals, the same bug returns.

Grounding is a contract

RAG is not automatically grounding. Retrieval only puts evidence near the model. Grounding means the answer is actually supported by that evidence.

That distinction matters. A model can retrieve the right document and still answer from memory. It can cite a source that does not contain the claim. It can combine two facts into a conclusion that no source supports. It can use one sentence from a document and ignore the next sentence that changes the meaning. It can cite a policy document while inventing the exception.

So production RAG needs a grounding contract:

If the answer contains a factual claim about private or fresh information,
that claim must be supported by retrieved evidence visible to the model.

Not every answer needs citations. “How do I reset my password?” may be fine without visible citations in a consumer UI. But the system should still know which source supported the answer. For enterprise, legal, finance, healthcare, research, and developer tools, citations are often part of the product.

There are three levels of grounding.

Level Meaning When to use
Source-level answer cites documents general support and search
Passage-level answer cites chunks or lines policy, technical docs, legal review
Claim-level each factual claim maps to evidence high-risk domains

Source-level grounding is easy and weak. Passage-level grounding is the boring default for serious RAG apps. Claim-level grounding is expensive but valuable when correctness matters more than speed.

The application should validate citations, not just ask the model to provide them.

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Citation:
    doc_id: str
    quote: str | None = None


def extract_citation_ids(answer: str) -> set[str]:
    import re
    return set(re.findall(r"\[([a-zA-Z0-9_\-]+)\]", answer))


def validate_citations(answer: str, docs: list[RetrievedDocument]) -> GuardrailResult:
    cited = extract_citation_ids(answer)
    available = {doc.doc_id for doc in docs}
    unknown = cited - available

    if unknown:
        return GuardrailResult(
            passed=False,
            category="citation_validation",
            reason=f"unknown citations: {sorted(unknown)}",
            severity=RiskLevel.MEDIUM,
        )

    if docs and not cited:
        return GuardrailResult(
            passed=False,
            category="citation_validation",
            reason="answer used retrieved context but provided no citations",
            severity=RiskLevel.MEDIUM,
        )

    return GuardrailResult(True, "citation_validation", "citations valid")

This only checks that cited documents exist. It does not check that the cited documents support the claims. For that, use a second pass.

A simple support checker splits the answer into claims and asks whether each claim is entailed by the retrieved context. In a production system, you may use an LLM judge, a natural language inference model, or a domain-specific verifier. The cheap version is still useful:

@dataclass
class ClaimSupport:
    claim: str
    supported: bool
    evidence_doc_ids: list[str]
    reason: str


def rough_claims(answer: str) -> list[str]:
    parts = [part.strip() for part in answer.replace("\n", " ").split(".")]
    return [part for part in parts if len(part.split()) >= 5]


def lexical_support(claim: str, docs: list[RetrievedDocument]) -> ClaimSupport:
    claim_terms = {w.lower().strip(".,:;()[]") for w in claim.split() if len(w) > 3}
    matches = []

    for doc in docs:
        doc_terms = {w.lower().strip(".,:;()[]") for w in doc.text.split() if len(w) > 3}
        overlap = len(claim_terms & doc_terms) / max(len(claim_terms), 1)
        if overlap >= 0.35:
            matches.append(doc.doc_id)

    return ClaimSupport(
        claim=claim,
        supported=bool(matches),
        evidence_doc_ids=matches,
        reason="lexical overlap check",
    )

That checker is crude. It misses paraphrases and can be fooled by shared vocabulary. But crude checks catch crude failures, and crude failures are common. A model that says “refunds are available within 90 days” when the retrieved policy says “60 days” should fail a stronger verifier, but even a lexical checker can flag unsupported sentences when no relevant words appear in context.

Grounding also means saying “I do not know” when evidence is missing. This is harder than it sounds because base models are trained to continue text, not to admit absence. Your prompt, evals, and guardrails must reward abstention.

Add explicit no-answer cases to evals:

Question: What is the refund policy for lifetime plans?
Retrieved context: annual plan refund policy only
Expected behavior: say the context does not contain lifetime plan refund information

If your eval suite has only answerable questions, the model learns that every question has an answer. Production users will ask questions your corpus cannot answer. The app must handle that without hallucinating.

Grounding has a cost. More retrieval, reranking, citation validation, and support checking adds latency. For low-risk chat, you may choose lighter checks. For enterprise search, support assistants, and regulated workflows, grounding is the feature. If the answer cannot be trusted, the app is decorative.

Privacy and data retention are architecture

LLM applications move sensitive text through more systems than most teams expect.

A single request may touch the web server, prompt renderer, embedding model, vector store, model provider, guardrail API, logging pipeline, tracing backend, analytics warehouse, eval dataset, human review tool, and cache. Each hop is a data exposure decision.

Ask these questions before launch:

  • What raw user text do we store?
  • What model provider receives it?
  • Is data used for provider training?
  • How long do we retain prompts and responses?
  • Who can read raw conversations?
  • Are logs redacted?
  • Are eval cases anonymized?
  • Can a user delete their data?
  • Are embeddings considered sensitive in our policy?
  • Can cached responses leak across tenants?

Do not answer these in a prompt. Answer them in architecture.

The safe default is data minimization:

Data Default handling
Raw prompt store only when needed, redact sensitive fields
Raw response store for limited retention if debugging requires it
Request metadata store structured fields by default
Retrieved docs store IDs and versions, not full text
Tool results store summaries and IDs, not secrets
Embeddings treat as derived sensitive data
Eval samples anonymize and label
Human review restrict access and audit reads

Embeddings deserve special mention. Engineers sometimes treat embeddings as harmless because they are vectors. They are not raw text, but they can leak information. Membership inference and nearest-neighbor search can reveal sensitive content. If the source text is private, the embedding should be protected like private derived data.

Logs are another trap. Debug logging is useful during development and dangerous in production. A single logger.info(prompt) can send customer secrets to a logging vendor with broad internal access.

Use redaction before logging:

def safe_log_payload(context: RequestContext, response: LLMAppResponse) -> dict[str, Any]:
    return {
        "request_id": context.request_id,
        "user_id_hash": hashlib.sha256(context.user_id.encode()).hexdigest()[:12],
        "org_id_hash": hashlib.sha256(context.org_id.encode()).hexdigest()[:12],
        "surface": context.surface,
        "model": response.model,
        "prompt_fingerprint": response.prompt_fingerprint,
        "input_tokens": response.input_tokens,
        "output_tokens": response.output_tokens,
        "latency_ms": response.latency_ms,
        "cost_usd": response.cost_usd,
        "cache_hit": response.cache_hit,
        "blocked": response.blocked,
        "block_reason": response.block_reason,
        "citations": response.citations,
    }

Notice what is missing: raw prompt, raw response, full user ID, full organization ID, retrieved text, tool secrets. You can keep a separate secure debug store for sampled requests if the product requires it. Make that an explicit decision with retention and access controls.

Privacy also affects evals. Production samples are valuable, but you cannot casually paste private conversations into a permanent eval file. Build an anonymization step. Replace names, emails, account IDs, phone numbers, addresses, secrets, and document IDs where possible. Keep source references when needed for debugging, but control access.

The practical rule:

Assume every prompt and response will eventually appear in a dashboard,
ticket, eval file, trace, cache, or screenshot unless the system prevents it.

Design the prevention.

Incidents need runbooks

An LLM incident is any production event where model behavior causes or risks user harm, business harm, policy violation, data leakage, major cost spike, or severe reliability degradation.

Examples:

  • the assistant gives wrong refund instructions to hundreds of users
  • a prompt injection leaks hidden policy text
  • a cache bug returns one customer’s answer to another customer
  • a model upgrade doubles cost
  • a guardrail outage lets unsafe outputs through
  • a tool bug sends duplicate emails
  • retrieval starts returning stale policy documents
  • a prompt change makes the assistant refuse valid requests

When this happens, you do not want to invent the response process in chat.

Write runbooks before launch.

The first runbook is rollback:

1. Identify behavior_id currently serving traffic.
2. Disable candidate rollout or set rollout_percent=0.
3. Restore previous prompt/model/retrieval/guardrail manifest.
4. Purge unsafe cache entries if cache may contain bad responses.
5. Confirm metrics return to baseline.
6. Preserve traces and samples for investigation.

The second runbook is containment:

1. Determine affected tenants, users, and product surfaces.
2. Stop side-effecting tools if actions may be unsafe.
3. Fail closed on high-risk outputs.
4. Disable semantic cache if leakage is suspected.
5. Add temporary guardrail rule if abuse pattern is clear.
6. Escalate to security, legal, support, or compliance based on severity.

The third runbook is analysis:

1. Reconstruct requests from traces and behavior IDs.
2. Identify prompt, model, retrieval, tool, cache, or policy cause.
3. Create minimal reproduction cases.
4. Add those cases to evals.
5. Fix the system.
6. Run regression and red-team suites.
7. Roll out through shadow and canary.

The postmortem should not end with “the model hallucinated.” That is not a root cause. Ask why the system allowed the hallucination to become a user-visible failure.

Better causes:

  • retrieval returned stale documents
  • prompt did not require abstention on missing evidence
  • eval suite lacked no-answer cases
  • output grounding validator was not enabled for this surface
  • cache key omitted policy version
  • rollout skipped shadow mode
  • judge rubric rewarded confident answers

Those causes are fixable.

Assign severity levels:

Severity Example Response
P0 data leak, unsafe side effect, legal exposure immediate rollback and incident response
P1 widespread wrong answers in important workflow rollback or disable feature
P2 localized quality regression canary stop and fix
P3 minor tone or formatting issue backlog or prompt patch

Every P0/P1 should produce new eval cases. If the same class of incident can happen again with no test failing, the postmortem is incomplete.

Common failure modes

The failures repeat across companies.

The invisible prompt change. Someone edits a prompt in the dashboard. Quality shifts. Nobody knows why. Fix: prompt changes go through version control and produce behavior IDs.

The context dump. Retrieval returns too many chunks. The model gets buried in irrelevant text and misses the answer. Fix: improve chunking, reranking, and prompt context layout. More context is not always better.

The unsafe cache hit. A cached answer crosses tenant, user, permission, locale, or time boundaries. Fix: cache scope includes every correctness-changing factor.

The judge that rewards verbosity. The eval judge gives higher scores to long answers. The product becomes bloated. Fix: rubric penalizes unnecessary length, and human calibration catches judge bias.

The blocked power user. Guardrails block legitimate technical, medical, legal, or security discussion because keywords look scary. Fix: classify intent and domain, not just words.

The tool with too much power. A model gets a write-capable tool for a task that only needed read access. Fix: least privilege and explicit approval for side effects.

The retry multiplier. Provider instability triggers retries across all services, tripling cost and worsening latency. Fix: capped retries, jitter, circuit breakers, and fallback behavior.

The dashboard without quality. The team tracks latency and cost but not answer quality. The system gets faster and cheaper while becoming less useful. Fix: eval metrics next to operational metrics.

The eval suite that fossilizes. The eval set never changes after launch. Production moves on. Fix: sample real traffic and add labeled failures continuously.

The one-number release gate. Average score improves while safety or high-value workflows regress. Fix: category-level gates and severity-weighted blocking.

What you’d actually use

For evals, start with a small in-repo eval runner if the team is early. A JSONL file of cases, a script that runs the current behavior, a judge prompt, and a comparison report will beat a fancy dashboard nobody trusts. As the system grows, use tools such as promptfoo, Braintrust, LangSmith, DeepEval, or Arize Phoenix. The tool matters less than the discipline: eval cases, versioned behavior, regression gates, and failure review.

For tracing, use OpenTelemetry. Put spans around retrieval, tools, cache lookup, model calls, guardrails, validation, and streaming. Export to whatever observability stack your company already uses. Do not make the LLM app a special island.

For guardrails, combine layers. Use cheap deterministic checks for length, schema, permissions, and obvious injection strings. Use safety classifiers for abuse and policy categories. Use PII detectors such as Presidio where privacy matters. Use output schema validation for structured responses. Use human review for high-risk actions. Do not expect one moderation endpoint to secure the whole application.

For prompt and behavior management, keep prompts in version control unless there is a strong reason not to. If you use a hosted prompt platform, mirror the important metadata: owner, version, behavior ID, eval scores, rollout state, and rollback path.

For cost, build token and spend tracking before launch. Add budget alerts. Add per-tenant limits. Add model routing. Add cache metrics. Most teams only get serious about cost after the first ugly invoice. That is late.

For deployment, use shadow mode and canaries. Do not replace production behavior for everyone because a prompt looked better in a notebook. Run the candidate on real traffic, score it, watch cost and latency, then roll forward.

The boring default stack:

FastAPI or your existing web framework
OpenTelemetry for traces
structured JSON logs
prompt/version files in git
JSONL eval cases in repo
promptfoo or Braintrust-style eval runner
provider SDK behind your own model gateway
Presidio or equivalent PII detection
safety classifier for input/output policy
Redis for rate limits and exact cache
vector store for RAG
feature flags for rollout

Use managed tools when they save time. Keep ownership of the behavior contract. Vendors can host traces, prompts, evals, and guardrails. They cannot decide what your product should mean by “correct.”

The checklist

Before an LLM feature goes live, ask these questions:

Area Question
Behavior Is the prompt versioned and tied to a behavior ID?
Evals Does the feature have golden, adversarial, and production-sampled eval cases?
Release Are there explicit pass/fail deployment gates?
Safety Are input and output guardrails active?
Permissions Does retrieval filter by user permissions?
Tools Are side-effecting tools gated by policy or approval?
Cache Is the cache scoped by tenant, permissions, and behavior version?
Reliability Are timeouts, retries, fallbacks, and degradation paths defined?
Observability Can you trace one request end to end?
Cost Do you record input tokens, output tokens, retries, and cost per request?
Privacy Are prompts and responses logged safely with retention rules?
Rollout Can you shadow, canary, and roll back?
Feedback Does user feedback become labeled eval data?

If the answer is no for a low-risk internal feature, maybe you ship anyway and accept the risk. If the answer is no for a customer-facing feature that touches money, health, legal, identity, or security, you are not ready.

Where this goes next

This chapter turned LLM calls into an engineered application. You now have the control plane: evals, guardrails, observability, cost, reliability, rollout, and feedback. Chapter 24 moves from text-only applications into multimodal systems, where the same engineering rules apply but the inputs and outputs become images, audio, video, and structured sensor data. The surface gets wider. The need for clear contracts gets sharper.

Chapter 24 - Multimodal AI

Text was the easy modality.

That sounds wrong if you spent the last chapters building transformers, aligning language models, serving inference, wiring tools, and building production evals. Text is not easy. But text has one huge advantage: it is already a sequence of symbols. A language model can treat a document as a row of tokens and learn the next-token game directly.

The physical world is not a row of tokens.

An image is a grid of pixels. A PDF is text plus layout plus tables plus charts plus font size plus whitespace. A video is a sequence of images where the answer may depend on what happened three minutes ago. Audio is a pressure wave where meaning lives across time, frequency, speaker identity, silence, rhythm, and noise. A robot action is not a word; it is a control signal sent into motors. A computer-use agent does not just read a sentence; it looks at a screen, finds a button, clicks it, waits, and adapts.

Multimodal AI is the engineering project of turning all of that into model-readable structure without destroying the information that matters.

The naive version is simple:

image -> caption -> text model
audio -> transcript -> text model
PDF -> OCR -> text model
video -> sampled captions -> text model

That pipeline still ships. You should not be embarrassed to use it. For many production systems, the boring pipeline is the right answer. If you need to search invoices, summarize support calls, or answer questions over scanned documents, a strong OCR or speech-to-text model plus a normal LLM may beat an elegant end-to-end multimodal system on cost, observability, and reliability.

But the pipeline loses information. Captions throw away spatial detail. Transcripts throw away tone and background sound. OCR loses layout. Frame sampling loses motion. A screenshot caption may say “there is a form” but not tell you the exact coordinates of the disabled submit button. A chart OCR pass may extract numbers but miss the trend. A video transcript may say “the user opened the door” but not show that they used their elbow because both hands were full.

Multimodal models exist because sometimes the non-text part is the task.

This chapter is about the core ideas that made multimodal AI work:

pixels become tokens
image-text pairs become shared embedding spaces
vision encoders become inputs to LLMs
connectors align visual features with language
high-resolution inputs become token-budget problems
video becomes temporal grounding
audio becomes time-frequency reasoning
documents become layout-aware evidence
actions become output modalities

The trick is not “add images to an LLM.” The trick is deciding what information from the world should become tokens, which model should process those tokens, which losses should train them, and where the production system should draw the boundary between perception, reasoning, retrieval, and action.

The multimodal problem

All modern multimodal systems answer the same question:

How do you put different kinds of information into a shared computational space?

Text tokens live in a vocabulary. Image patches live in a grid. Audio frames live in time-frequency space. Video frames live in time plus two spatial dimensions. Robot actions live in continuous control space. UI actions live in coordinates and element trees.

The transformer gives you a useful common machine: it consumes a sequence of vectors and lets every vector attend to other vectors. If you can turn the modality into vectors, you can usually make the transformer do something with it.

That leads to four design patterns.

Pattern Core idea Example
Encode then connect use a specialist encoder, then map features into an LLM CLIP encoder + projector + LLM
Cross-attend keep vision features separate and let text attend to them Flamingo-style gated cross-attention
Early fusion tokenize every modality and train one sequence model Chameleon-style token-only models
Hybrid generation use different losses for different modalities Transfusion-style text plus diffusion

Most shipped vision-language models use encode then connect. It is simple, cheap enough, and strong. You take a vision encoder, extract visual features, pass them through a connector, insert them into a language model’s context, and train the system to answer visual instructions.

image -> vision encoder -> visual tokens -> connector -> LLM -> answer

That architecture is why the field moved fast. You do not need to train a frontier language model from scratch. You can reuse a strong LLM and teach it to read visual tokens.

The rest of the chapter explains each part of that pipeline and the newer systems that try to unify everything more deeply.

Images as tokens

The first move is almost embarrassingly simple: cut the image into patches.

A 224 by 224 RGB image has shape:

height=224, width=224, channels=3

If you split it into 16 by 16 patches, you get:

14 x 14 = 196 patches

Each patch contains:

16 x 16 x 3 = 768 pixel values

Flatten each patch, multiply it by a learned projection matrix, and now every patch is a vector with the same dimension as a transformer token embedding. That is the Vision Transformer (ViT) move [Dosovitskiy et al., 2020].

image pixels -> patches -> patch embeddings -> transformer

Here is the shape in code. images has shape (batch, channels, height, width).

import torch
import torch.nn as nn


class PatchEmbed(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, image_size=224, patch_size=16, in_channels=3, dim=768):
        super().__init__()
        self.image_size = image_size
        self.patch_size = patch_size
        self.grid_size = image_size // patch_size
        self.num_patches = self.grid_size * self.grid_size
        self.proj = nn.Conv2d(
            in_channels=in_channels,
            out_channels=dim,
            kernel_size=patch_size,
            stride=patch_size,
        )

    def forward(self, images):
        x = self.proj(images)
        x = x.flatten(2)
        x = x.transpose(1, 2)
        return x


images = torch.randn(2, 3, 224, 224)
patcher = PatchEmbed()
tokens = patcher(images)
print(tokens.shape)

The output shape is:

(2, 196, 768)

Two images. One hundred ninety-six visual tokens per image. Seven hundred sixty-eight dimensions per token.

That is the bridge. Once an image is a sequence of vectors, the transformer does not care whether those vectors came from words or patches. Attention can compare patch 17 with patch 93 just like it compares token 17 with token 93.

The patch size controls the trade-off:

Patch size Tokens for 224x224 What happens
32x32 49 cheap, loses detail
16x16 196 common default
14x14 256 better detail, more compute
8x8 784 high detail, expensive

Smaller patches preserve more detail and cost more attention. Since transformer attention scales roughly with sequence length squared, doubling resolution can hurt quickly.

Positional information matters. A bag of image patches is not an image. The model needs to know which patch is top-left, which is bottom-right, and which patches are neighbors. Early ViTs used learned absolute position embeddings. Modern systems use more flexible position schemes because images arrive at different resolutions and aspect ratios.

The conceptual point is simple:

A patch token means "there is some visual evidence at this location."

That is enough to make language-style sequence modeling work for vision.

The image encoder

A ViT is not automatically a good multimodal encoder. It has to learn useful visual representations.

There are several pretraining strategies.

Strategy Training signal What it learns
Supervised classification image label category-level visual features
Contrastive image-text matched image/caption pairs alignment with language
Masked image modeling reconstruct missing patches visual structure
Self-distillation match teacher features robust general features

Classic supervised pretraining teaches the model to classify images. That gives useful features, but the labels are narrow. If the label is “dog”, the model may not learn that the dog is wearing a red collar beside a blue bicycle.

Contrastive pretraining teaches the model to align images with text. That matters for multimodal AI because the language model downstream thinks in words. You want image features that already point toward language.

Masked image modeling teaches structure. Hide patches, ask the model to reconstruct or predict them. This is closer to BERT-style pretraining, but for pixels or visual tokens.

Self-distillation trains a student model to match a strong teacher’s features. DINO-style models became popular because their visual features often work well for segmentation, retrieval, and dense perception without labels.

In production, the vision encoder is a major choice. A stronger encoder can matter more than a fancier connector. If the encoder misses small text in a screenshot or fine details in a medical image, the LLM cannot recover that information by being clever. The visual evidence never reached it.

The production rule:

Do not expect the LLM to reason about visual details the encoder did not preserve.

That is why multimodal performance often improves when you upgrade the vision encoder, increase image resolution, or preserve more visual tokens.

CLIP: the shared embedding trick

CLIP’s idea is clean: train an image encoder and a text encoder so matching image-text pairs land near each other in embedding space [Radford et al., 2021].

Given a batch of images and captions:

image_1 <-> caption_1
image_2 <-> caption_2
...
image_n <-> caption_n

The model learns:

similarity(image_i, caption_i) should be high
similarity(image_i, caption_j) should be low when i != j

This is contrastive learning. The batch supplies positive pairs and negative pairs. You are not asking the model to generate a caption. You are asking it to arrange images and text in a shared space.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F


def clip_loss(image_embeds, text_embeds, temperature=0.07):
    image_embeds = F.normalize(image_embeds, dim=-1)
    text_embeds = F.normalize(text_embeds, dim=-1)
    logits = image_embeds @ text_embeds.T
    logits = logits / temperature
    labels = torch.arange(logits.shape[0], device=logits.device)
    image_to_text = F.cross_entropy(logits, labels)
    text_to_image = F.cross_entropy(logits.T, labels)
    return (image_to_text + text_to_image) / 2

If the batch has 256 image-text pairs, each image has one correct caption and 255 incorrect captions. That turns ordinary web image-caption data into a strong supervision signal.

The result is a shared embedding space.

"a photo of a golden retriever" -> vector
image of golden retriever -> nearby vector

That enables zero-shot classification. To classify an image, you embed candidate labels as prompts:

"a photo of a dog"
"a photo of a cat"
"a photo of a truck"

Then choose the text embedding closest to the image embedding.

CLIP changed the field because it made vision models language-aware. It also made image retrieval and cross-modal search practical. Search by text, retrieve images. Search by image, retrieve captions. Use the image encoder as the visual front end for a VLM.

CLIP also has limits.

It is strong at global semantic matching. It is weaker at precise counting, reading small text, spatial relations, and detailed grounding. If the caption says “a man standing next to a car”, CLIP does not need to learn the exact pixel boundary of the man or the car. It only needs enough representation to match the caption.

That is why CLIP is a foundation, not the whole system.

From CLIP to VLMs

A vision-language model (VLM) is usually an LLM with visual inputs. The simplest version has three parts:

vision encoder -> connector -> language model

The vision encoder turns the image into visual features. The connector maps those features into the LLM’s embedding space. The LLM consumes visual tokens plus text tokens and generates text.

image
  -> ViT / CLIP / SigLIP encoder
  -> visual features
  -> projector / Q-Former / resampler
  -> LLM token embeddings
  -> answer

The connector exists because the vision encoder and LLM were trained separately. Their vector spaces do not naturally match. A visual feature dimension does not mean the same thing as a language embedding dimension. The connector learns a translation.

There are three common connector families.

Connector Idea Trade-off
Linear or MLP projector map each visual token directly simple, cheap, strong baseline
Q-Former learn query tokens that extract visual information compresses vision, more complex
Perceiver resampler cross-attend from fixed latents to visual features handles variable visual inputs

The important question is not “which connector is most elegant?” The question is:

How much visual information should enter the LLM, and in what form?

Too few tokens, and the model misses detail. Too many tokens, and the LLM context is flooded with expensive visual evidence. A 1,000-token image is not free. It competes with user text, retrieved context, tool results, and output budget.

BLIP-2 and the Q-Former

BLIP-2 introduced one of the cleanest connector ideas: use a small transformer with learnable query tokens to extract the visual information the LLM needs [Li et al., 2023].

The image encoder is frozen. The LLM is frozen. The Q-Former learns the bridge.

image -> frozen image encoder -> many visual features
learned queries -> Q-Former attends to visual features -> compact visual tokens
compact visual tokens -> LLM

Think of the Q-Former as a set of learned questions:

What objects are here?
What text is visible?
What relationships matter?
What scene context matters?

The queries are not literal English questions. They are learned vectors. But the intuition is useful: instead of dumping every patch into the LLM, the Q-Former asks the image encoder for a compact summary.

That buys parameter efficiency. You can train a relatively small bridge while reusing large frozen models. This mattered when training a full multimodal model was too expensive for most teams.

The trade-off is compression. If the Q-Former outputs 32 query tokens, all visual information must pass through 32 vectors. That may be fine for captioning. It may not be enough for dense document understanding, small UI elements, charts, or detailed visual reasoning.

The lesson:

Connectors are information bottlenecks.

Every bottleneck is a bet about what the downstream task needs.

Flamingo and cross-attention

Flamingo took a different path. Instead of turning visual features into ordinary prefix tokens and feeding them into the LLM, it inserted gated cross-attention layers into the language model [Alayrac et al., 2022].

The LLM remains mostly frozen. Visual features are processed by a Perceiver-style resampler into a fixed number of visual latents. Then, at certain layers, the language model can cross-attend to those visual latents.

text tokens attend to text history
text tokens cross-attend to visual latents
gate controls how much visual information enters

The gate matters. At initialization, the model should behave like the original language model. Training gradually opens the visual pathway. That makes adaptation more stable.

Flamingo also handled interleaved image-text examples:

<image> question answer
<image> question answer
<image> new question -> model answers

That gave it few-shot multimodal ability. You could show examples with images and text inside the prompt, and the model learned the pattern.

The practical idea that survived:

You can keep a strong language model mostly intact and add visual access through controlled cross-attention.

Later systems made different engineering choices, but Flamingo established a core recipe: frozen LLM, visual encoder, resampler, and interleaved multimodal context.

LLaVA and visual instruction tuning

LLaVA made the community recipe simple enough to copy.

Architecture:

CLIP vision encoder -> MLP projector -> Vicuna/LLaMA-style LLM

Training:

Stage 1: align visual features to language space
Stage 2: instruction-tune on image-question-answer data

That is it. No Q-Former. No gated cross-attention. No complex resampler. Take image features, project them into the LLM embedding dimension, and train on visual conversations.

Why did this work?

Because the LLM already knew how to follow instructions. The missing skill was not general conversation. The missing skill was grounding conversation in visual tokens. A projector plus visual instruction tuning was enough to unlock useful behavior.

A typical visual instruction example looks like:

User: <image>
What is unusual about this image?

Assistant:
The dog is sitting in the driver's seat of the car, which is unusual because dogs do not drive.

The model learns to treat image tokens as part of the conversation. The same chat formatting issues from Chapter 18 still apply. A bad chat template can break a VLM just as easily as it breaks a text-only model.

LLaVA’s influence came from its parameter economy and recipe clarity:

  • use an open vision encoder
  • use an open LLM
  • train a small projector
  • create synthetic visual instruction data with a stronger model
  • fine-tune end to end or partially

This made VLM development accessible. It also produced a generation of systems that were good at visual chat but weaker at high-resolution documents, detailed grounding, and video. Those weaknesses drove the next set of architectures.

The training curriculum

Multimodal training is not one training run. It is usually a curriculum.

The model has to learn several skills that are related but not identical:

see visual features
align visual features with language
follow visual instructions
handle high-resolution detail
reason across multiple images
reason across time
refuse unsafe visual requests
use tools or cite sources when needed

If you throw all of that into one soup from the beginning, the model can learn, but it is harder to debug. A staged curriculum gives each part of the system a job.

The common VLM curriculum:

Stage What trains Data Goal
1. Vision pretraining vision encoder images, labels, image-text pairs, masked images strong visual features
2. Connector alignment connector, maybe small adapter image-caption pairs map visual features into language space
3. Visual instruction tuning connector + LLM adapters or full model image QA, conversations follow instructions about images
4. High-resolution tuning vision path and connector OCR, screenshots, documents preserve small details
5. Multi-image/video tuning full multimodal path image sequences, videos reason across visual contexts
6. Safety/eval tuning policy layers, refusals, preferences red-team and preference data behave safely in product

Stage 1 is where the visual encoder learns to see. If you use an existing encoder, this stage already happened elsewhere.

Stage 2 is alignment. The model learns that a region of visual feature space corresponds to language concepts. This stage is often cheap compared with pretraining. You can freeze the vision encoder and LLM, then train only a projector. That gives the LLM a visual prefix it can at least interpret.

Stage 3 is where the system becomes useful as an assistant. Captions alone do not teach dialogue. The model needs examples of users asking questions, pointing at images, requesting extraction, asking for comparisons, and expecting grounded answers.

Stage 4 fixes the “looks good in demos, fails on screenshots” problem. Ordinary image-caption data does not contain enough small text, tables, receipts, UI elements, equations, and dense diagrams. If your product involves documents or screens, you need this data.

Stage 5 teaches composition across visual inputs. A model trained only on single images often treats multiple images as a pile of unrelated visual facts. It needs examples where the answer depends on comparing images, tracking changes, or following temporal order.

Stage 6 is where Chapter 23 comes back. Safety and evals are not separate from multimodal training. The model must learn when not to identify a person, when not to infer sensitive attributes, when to say an image is too unclear, when to ask for a better scan, and when the visual evidence does not support the requested conclusion.

The important engineering habit is to know which stage a failure belongs to.

Failure Likely layer
cannot recognize object vision encoder or data
sees object but cannot answer connector or instruction tuning
misses small text resolution policy or OCR/document data
confuses image order multi-image formatting or data
wrong timestamp video sampling or temporal encoding
hallucinates visual detail grounding, data, or eval gap
unsafe face/person inference safety policy and training

“The VLM is bad” is not a diagnosis. Find the layer.

Data is the real architecture

Multimodal papers often present architecture diagrams. Production quality often comes from data.

For image-text systems, useful data includes:

  • natural image captions
  • dense captions
  • region descriptions
  • visual question answering
  • OCR-heavy images
  • charts and plots
  • screenshots
  • documents
  • diagrams
  • multi-image comparisons
  • videos with timestamps
  • audio with transcripts and sound labels
  • user-interface action traces

The model learns what the data rewards. If training captions say only “a dog in a park”, the model may not learn “a brown dog wearing a red harness beside a stroller.” If visual QA data asks mostly object questions, the model may fail at spatial relations. If document data is clean, the model may fail on phone photos of receipts. If video data contains short clips, the model may fail on hour-long recordings.

Synthetic data is common. A stronger model captions images, generates questions, writes answers, or converts dense annotations into conversations. This works, but synthetic data has a smell. It can be too neat, too verbose, too balanced, and too aligned with the teacher model’s blind spots.

Human data is expensive but often better for hard domains. A human can write a question that depends on the weird corner of a chart, the checkbox in a form, or the difference between two nearly identical UI states. That kind of data is gold.

The practical recipe:

Use broad web-scale data for general perception.
Use synthetic data for cheap instruction coverage.
Use human-labeled data for your high-value failure modes.
Use production failures to keep the eval and training sets alive.

Do not confuse benchmark data with product data. A model can score well on general VQA and still fail at your invoices. A model can answer celebrity-photo questions and still fail at warehouse shelf images. Your domain distribution is the distribution that matters.

High resolution is a token-budget problem

The original ViT setup assumes a fixed square image such as 224 by 224. Real images are not like that.

A screenshot might be 1440 by 900. A scanned PDF page might be 2550 by 3300. A phone photo might be vertical. A chart might contain small text in one corner. A UI screenshot might require reading a tiny disabled button. If you resize everything to 224 by 224, you erase the evidence.

The brute-force solution is to use higher resolution. But visual tokens explode.

For patch size 14:

Resolution Grid Tokens
224x224 16x16 256
448x448 32x32 1,024
896x896 64x64 4,096
1344x1344 96x96 9,216

Nine thousand visual tokens for one image is expensive. It may be necessary for a dense page, but you cannot casually stuff multiple high-resolution images into the context and expect latency to behave.

Modern systems use several strategies.

Any-resolution tiling. Split the image into tiles. Encode each tile. Add a global low-resolution view so the model knows the whole layout. This helps with screenshots and documents because small details survive.

Patch packing. Instead of padding every image to a fixed square, pack variable-sized patch sequences efficiently. This saves compute when images have different aspect ratios.

Dynamic resolution. Allocate more visual tokens to images that need detail and fewer to simple images. A photo of one large object does not need the same budget as a dense spreadsheet.

Spatial position encoding. Use position embeddings that preserve two-dimensional layout across variable resolutions. Qwen-style multimodal rotary position embeddings split position into temporal, height, and width components.

Here is the production version of the decision:

For every visual input, decide how much detail is worth paying for.

A user profile avatar can be low resolution. A contract scan needs high resolution. A chart needs enough resolution to read labels. A UI screenshot needs enough resolution to locate controls. A photo for broad captioning can be compressed aggressively.

You should not use one image preprocessing policy for every product surface.

Here is a small preprocessing policy that shows the shape. The numbers are not universal. The point is that the application chooses a budget based on the task.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class VisualBudget:
    max_images: int
    max_pixels: int
    tile_size: int
    allow_ocr: bool
    preserve_aspect_ratio: bool


def choose_visual_budget(task: Literal["avatar", "photo_chat", "screenshot", "document", "chart"]) -> VisualBudget:
    if task == "avatar":
        return VisualBudget(1, 512 * 512, 224, False, True)
    if task == "photo_chat":
        return VisualBudget(4, 1024 * 1024, 336, False, True)
    if task == "screenshot":
        return VisualBudget(3, 1600 * 1200, 448, True, True)
    if task == "document":
        return VisualBudget(10, 2200 * 3000, 672, True, True)
    if task == "chart":
        return VisualBudget(3, 1800 * 1400, 672, True, True)
    raise ValueError(f"unknown task: {task}")

The model call should know why it is receiving the image. A chart, a screenshot, and a landscape photo should not be squeezed through the same preprocessing path.

Position, masks, and modality boundaries

A multimodal model needs to know three things about each token:

what modality it came from
where it came from
which other tokens it may attend to

For text, position is one-dimensional:

token 0, token 1, token 2, ...

For images, position is two-dimensional:

row, column

For video, position is three-dimensional:

time, row, column

For audio, position is time, often after conversion into frames or spectrogram patches.

This is why multimodal position encoding becomes messy. If you flatten an image into a sequence of patches, you can use ordinary 1D positions, but the model has to infer the 2D layout indirectly. Better systems preserve explicit height and width structure. Video systems add time.

Qwen-style multimodal rotary position embeddings split the position signal across dimensions. The intuition:

some channels encode time
some channels encode height
some channels encode width

A patch token from frame 8, row 12, column 20 carries a different position from a patch token at frame 2, row 12, column 20. That lets the model reason about both spatial and temporal relationships.

Attention masks matter too. In a decoder-only LLM, text tokens usually use causal attention: token t can see earlier tokens, not later ones. Image tokens are different. When an image is provided as input, all patch tokens are already known. There is no reason patch 10 should be forbidden from attending to patch 50 inside the same image encoding step.

Different systems make different masking choices:

Token type Common attention pattern
input image tokens bidirectional among visual tokens
input text tokens causal or full depending on architecture
generated text causal
generated image tokens causal, masked diffusion, or bidirectional denoising
video tokens spatial attention plus temporal strategy

Hybrid systems exist because one attention pattern is not ideal for every modality. Text generation naturally wants left-to-right causality. Image understanding benefits from seeing the whole image. Image generation often benefits from parallel refinement.

Modality boundary tokens are the simple but critical piece:

<image_start> visual tokens <image_end>
<audio_start> audio tokens <audio_end>
<video_start> frame tokens <video_end>

These markers tell the LLM where one modality begins and ends. They also help prompt templates stay stable. If you accidentally change image marker formatting between training and inference, performance can collapse in the same way a wrong chat template breaks an aligned LLM.

The modern VLM recipe

By the time open-weight VLMs matured, the recipe had become a design space with five major axes.

Axis Choices What matters
Vision encoder CLIP, SigLIP, EVA, DINO-style, custom visual quality and resolution
Connector projector, Q-Former, resampler bottleneck and compute
LLM 7B, 13B, 34B, MoE, frontier API reasoning ceiling
Data captions, OCR, VQA, instruction data, documents, video task coverage
Resolution schedule low-to-high, dynamic, tiled detail vs cost

The unglamorous finding from many controlled comparisons: the connector is often less important than the encoder, data, and LLM. A simple projector can be strong if the encoder is good, the LLM is good, and the training data matches the target task.

Do not overfit to architecture diagrams. A clever connector will not save bad data. A huge LLM will not recover tiny text erased by preprocessing. A high-resolution encoder will not help if your evals only test broad captions.

The production picker:

Need Default
visual chat over ordinary images open VLM or hosted frontier VLM
screenshots and UI understanding high-resolution VLM with coordinate grounding
document QA OCR/text RAG first, vision-native RAG when layout matters
charts and diagrams VLM with high-res crop/tiling and citation support
video summaries sampled frames plus transcript first
long video reasoning video-specific model or retrieval over clips
low latency smaller VLM, lower resolution, cascaded pipeline
high accuracy stronger model, more visual tokens, better evals

The boring default is not always the most advanced model. It is the simplest system that preserves the evidence required for the task.

Modern model families

The model families in Phase 12 are not random names. They represent design pressures.

LLaVA-style models represent the simple adapter recipe:

strong vision encoder + projector + instruction-tuned LLM

They are good at visual chat, approachable to train, and easy to understand. Their weakness is that the basic recipe has to be extended for high resolution, video, and dense grounding.

LLaVA-OneVision-style models represent curriculum unification:

single image + multi-image + video in one training path

The bet is that visual reasoning transfers across scenarios if the model sees them together. A model that learns to compare two images may become better at comparing frames. A model that learns temporal order may become better at multi-step visual reasoning.

Qwen-VL-style models represent engineering around resolution and time:

native resolution + multimodal position encoding + dynamic video sampling

The practical insight is that visual tokens need good position metadata. A screenshot, document page, and video clip are not just bags of patches. The model needs location and time.

InternVL-style models represent native multimodal pretraining:

train language and vision together more deeply instead of only bolting vision onto a finished LLM

The advantage is stronger integration. The cost is training complexity. Native multimodal pretraining requires data, compute, and careful balancing so language ability does not degrade while visual ability improves.

Chameleon-style models represent early fusion:

turn text and images into tokens, train one autoregressive transformer

This is elegant because there is one sequence model. It is difficult because image tokenization quality and training stability become load-bearing.

Emu-style models push next-token prediction further into image and video generation. The bet is that a single next-token objective can handle understanding and generation if the tokenizer and scale are good enough.

Transfusion-style models represent hybrid loss design:

autoregressive text + diffusion image loss inside one transformer system

The bet is that text and images should share context but not necessarily share the same generation loss.

Show-o-style systems represent discrete diffusion unification:

autoregressive language + masked discrete diffusion for visual tokens

The bet is that image tokens should be generated with parallel masked refinement rather than strict left-to-right prediction.

Janus-style systems represent decoupled visual pathways:

one visual encoder for understanding
another pathway for generation
shared reasoning body

The bet is that understanding and generation want different visual representations.

MIO and any-to-any systems represent modality-complete interaction:

text, image, audio, video in
text, image, audio, video out

The bet is that users do not want separate models for separate modes. They want one assistant that can listen, look, talk, write, and create.

Embodied VLA systems represent action grounding:

vision + language + state -> action

The bet is that language and visual pretraining can help models act in the world.

You do not need to memorize every family. You need to recognize the design question each family answers:

How much do we fuse modalities?
How much do we preserve resolution?
How do we represent time?
Do we generate with tokens or diffusion?
Do we act, or only answer?

Those questions stay relevant after specific model names change.

Single-image, multi-image, and video

Early VLMs handled one image. Real tasks often need more:

  • compare two product photos
  • inspect before-and-after images
  • answer over a PDF page sequence
  • understand a video
  • follow a UI workflow across screenshots
  • compare multiple charts

LLaVA-OneVision-style systems treat single-image, multi-image, and video tasks as one curriculum. The model learns that visual context can arrive as one image, many images, or sampled frames. That matters because the same reasoning skill transfers.

For multi-image input, the model needs to know which tokens belong to which image:

<image_1> tokens...
<image_2> tokens...
Question: What changed?

For video, the model also needs time:

frame at 0s
frame at 2s
frame at 4s
...

The simplest video model is a VLM with sampled frames. That works for broad summaries:

Sample 1 frame per second -> encode frames -> ask model for summary

It fails when the answer depends on short events, ordering, timing, or motion. If someone picks up a key for half a second, one-frame-per-second sampling may miss it. If a person enters before another person leaves, order matters. If a UI spinner appears for 300ms before an error, sparse frames may miss the cause.

Video is not many images. Video is images plus time.

Frame sampling is the first engineering decision. Here is a basic sampler:

def sample_frame_times(duration_seconds: float, mode: str, max_frames: int) -> list[float]:
    if max_frames <= 0:
        return []

    if mode == "uniform":
        if max_frames == 1:
            return [duration_seconds / 2]
        step = duration_seconds / (max_frames - 1)
        return [round(i * step, 2) for i in range(max_frames)]

    if mode == "start_middle_end":
        points = [0.05, 0.5, 0.95]
        return [round(duration_seconds * p, 2) for p in points[:max_frames]]

    if mode == "dense_short_clip":
        fps = max_frames / max(duration_seconds, 1.0)
        return [round(i / fps, 2) for i in range(max_frames)]

    raise ValueError(f"unknown sampling mode: {mode}")

This code does not detect scene changes or motion. It just makes the decision explicit. Production systems should choose sampling based on the question:

User asks Sampling policy
“Summarize this video” uniform or scene-change sampling
“What happens at the end?” sample later frames more densely
“When does the error appear?” query-guided retrieval over frames
“Did the user click checkout?” UI-event or motion-aware sampling
“Compare the first and last state” start/end plus keyframes

Sampling is a retrieval problem. You are retrieving visual evidence from time.

Temporal grounding

Temporal grounding means mapping language to moments in video.

Example:

Question: When does the user click the checkout button?
Answer: 01:14.2 to 01:15.0

The output is not just text. It is a time span. That makes evaluation stricter and more useful. You can compare predicted time intervals with ground truth intervals.

Video systems use several strategies:

Strategy Idea Good for
Uniform sampling take frames at fixed intervals broad summaries
Scene-change sampling sample when visuals change edited videos
Motion-aware sampling sample around movement physical actions
Query-guided sampling retrieve frames relevant to question long videos
Hierarchical clips summarize chunks, then reason over summaries long context

Temporal position encoding matters. The model needs to know not only where a patch is in the frame, but when that frame occurred. Some systems extend rotary position embeddings into time, height, and width. The idea is direct: every visual token carries a location in space-time.

For production, use a two-pass approach for long videos:

pass 1: index video into shots, transcripts, frames, objects, and summaries
pass 2: retrieve relevant segments and ask a VLM over those segments

Do not push a two-hour video into a model because the context window technically allows it. Long context is not the same as reliable attention. Retrieval still matters.

Long video and million-token context

There are four paths to long-video understanding.

Path Idea Trade-off
Brute context put huge visual context into a long-context model simple, expensive
Efficient attention use ring attention or sparse attention infrastructure complexity
Token compression pool, summarize, or compress visual tokens may lose details
Agentic retrieval search through video segments as needed more system complexity

Brute context is attractive because it feels like the model can see everything. But attention over huge contexts is expensive, and models still struggle to use every token reliably. A million-token context can hold a lot of video metadata, but the relevant moment may still be hard to find.

Token compression is necessary. You can compress per frame, per scene, per object track, or per transcript segment. Each compression choice defines what can be recovered later. If you compress away small objects, no downstream reasoning step will restore them.

Agentic retrieval is often the pragmatic answer. The system builds an index:

video -> shots -> keyframes -> transcript -> audio events -> object tags -> embeddings

Then a model can search:

Find segments where the speaker mentions refund policy.
Find the moment where the user opens the settings page.
Find frames similar to this reference image.

This is multimodal RAG over time.

Audio is not just transcription

The simplest audio pipeline is:

audio -> speech-to-text -> LLM

This is often the right production default. Whisper-style speech recognition is strong, cheap enough, and easy to inspect. If the task is meeting summarization, call-center QA, or voice dictation, a transcript plus a text model gets you far.

But audio contains more than words:

  • speaker identity
  • emotion and prosody
  • pauses and interruptions
  • background noise
  • music
  • alarms
  • sound events
  • timing
  • overlapping speech

If the task needs those signals, transcription is lossy.

Speech models usually convert audio into a spectrogram: a time-frequency representation. A log-Mel spectrogram says, roughly, how much energy exists at each frequency band over time.

waveform -> log-Mel spectrogram -> audio encoder -> tokens

Whisper uses an encoder-decoder transformer over audio features. Audio-language models adapt that pattern in different ways:

  • use Whisper encoder features as audio tokens
  • use audio-specific encoders such as BEATs
  • use an audio Q-Former to bridge audio features into an LLM
  • combine speech recognition with audio event reasoning
  • train end-to-end systems that answer directly from audio

Cascaded systems are easier:

speech-to-text -> text LLM -> text-to-speech

End-to-end audio-language systems can preserve more signal:

audio tokens -> multimodal model -> text or audio response

The trade-off is observability. A transcript is easy to inspect, redact, search, and cite. Latent audio tokens are harder. In production, the cascade often wins until the task truly needs non-text audio reasoning.

Omni models and real-time interaction

An omni model handles multiple input and output modalities in one interaction loop:

text + image + audio + video -> model -> text + audio + actions

The user does not experience this as “modalities.” They experience it as a conversation where they can talk, point the camera, show a screen, interrupt, and receive spoken answers.

The engineering challenge is latency.

Voice interaction has a tight budget. If the user stops speaking and the assistant waits three seconds before responding, the interface feels broken. Text chat can tolerate seconds. Voice needs turn-taking.

A real-time assistant needs:

  • voice activity detection
  • streaming speech recognition or audio tokens
  • incremental reasoning
  • streaming speech synthesis
  • interruption handling
  • low-latency safety checks
  • memory of the visual scene

Some omni architectures split the system into a thinker and a talker. The thinker handles understanding and reasoning. The talker handles low-latency speech generation. That split exists because deep reasoning and smooth speech synthesis have different latency profiles.

Thinker:

multimodal understanding -> plan -> answer content

Talker:

answer content -> streaming speech tokens -> audio

The split is practical. You do not want a heavy reasoning path to block every audio token. The assistant should begin speaking naturally while the system manages the reasoning and response plan.

The hard part is interruption. Humans interrupt. If the user says “wait, no, I meant the chart on the right”, the assistant must stop speaking, update context, and respond to the correction. That is not a model-only problem. It is a state-machine problem around the model.

Unified generation: tokens, diffusion, and hybrids

Understanding images is one side. Generating images, video, or audio is another.

There are three broad approaches to unified multimodal generation.

Approach Idea Example family
Discrete token generation tokenize images and predict image tokens like text Chameleon, Emu-style systems
Diffusion generation denoise continuous visual latents diffusion and flow models
Hybrid generation autoregressive for text, diffusion for images Transfusion-style systems

Discrete image tokenization uses a VQ-style tokenizer. The image becomes a grid of discrete codes:

image -> visual tokenizer -> code_1, code_2, ..., code_n

Then a transformer can predict mixed sequences:

text tokens, image tokens, text tokens, image tokens

This is elegant because everything is next-token prediction. One model can read and generate mixed media. The downside is reconstruction quality. The image tokenizer sets a ceiling. If the tokenizer cannot represent fine texture or text accurately, the generator cannot produce it.

Diffusion models generate images by denoising. They are strong at visual quality because they operate in continuous latent spaces and refine the image over steps. But they do not look like normal text autoregressive models.

Hybrid systems try to keep the best of both:

text: autoregressive next-token loss
image: diffusion or flow-matching loss
shared transformer body

This is where models such as Transfusion-style architectures fit. The transformer handles mixed context, but visual generation uses a loss better suited to images.

Show-o-style discrete diffusion systems add another variation: use masked discrete diffusion for image tokens and autoregressive modeling for text. Instead of generating image tokens strictly left to right, the model fills masked visual tokens in parallel over steps. That can be more natural for images, where the whole scene structure matters at once.

Janus-style systems make a different point: understanding and generation may want different visual encoders. The best representation for recognizing an image is not necessarily the best representation for generating one. Decoupling encoders lets the model use specialized visual pathways while sharing a reasoning core.

The engineer’s takeaway:

Text wants sequence prediction. Images often want spatial refinement.
Unified models are useful, but the loss function still matters.

Documents and diagrams

Documents are where naive multimodal systems go to suffer.

A document page is not just text. It has layout:

  • columns
  • headings
  • tables
  • footnotes
  • checkboxes
  • signatures
  • stamps
  • diagrams
  • equations
  • reading order
  • page numbers
  • visual grouping

OCR gives you text. It may not give you structure.

Consider a table:

Plan        Refund window       Approval
Monthly     14 days             automatic
Annual      60 days             finance review after threshold
Enterprise  custom              contract-specific

Bad OCR may return the words in a flat sequence. The model then has to reconstruct rows and columns from damaged text. Sometimes it can. Sometimes it invents a relationship.

There have been three eras.

First, OCR pipelines:

image/PDF -> OCR -> layout parser -> text chunks -> text RAG

This is still the default for many production systems because it is debuggable. You can inspect OCR output, fix extraction, preserve page numbers, and index text.

Second, OCR-free document models:

document image -> model -> answer / extracted fields

These models read the page visually and avoid an explicit OCR step. They can preserve layout better, but they may be harder to inspect.

Third, VLM-native document understanding:

page image -> high-resolution VLM -> grounded answer

This works increasingly well for forms, screenshots, diagrams, and mixed text-visual pages. But it costs more and needs careful evaluation.

The production rule:

Use OCR when text is the source of truth.
Use vision-native models when layout or visual structure is the source of truth.
Use both when the document matters.

For diagrams, the challenge is relational. The model must understand arrows, boxes, labels, grouping, and direction. A diagram is a graph drawn as an image. A good system may convert it into structured representation:

nodes, edges, labels, regions

Then the LLM can reason over that structure. Do not force a language model to infer a complex graph from a paragraph caption if you can extract the graph directly.

Vision-native document RAG

Text RAG works when text extraction preserves the evidence. It fails when the answer depends on layout, scanned quality, handwriting, diagrams, charts, or visual formatting.

Vision-native document RAG indexes pages as images.

The ColPali family is the reference idea. It combines a VLM-like page encoder with late interaction retrieval inspired by ColBERT. Instead of representing a page as one vector, it represents the page as many token-level vectors. A query can match different visual/textual parts of the page.

The rough flow:

PDF page -> page image -> visual-language encoder -> token embeddings
query -> text encoder -> query token embeddings
late interaction score(query tokens, page tokens)
retrieve page images
send relevant pages to VLM

Late interaction matters because one vector is a harsh bottleneck for a dense page. A page may contain many facts. Token-level matching lets the query find the part of the page that matters.

Storage cost is the trade-off. Multi-vector page indexes are larger than single-vector indexes. If you have millions of pages, the storage and retrieval infrastructure matters.

When text RAG still wins:

  • clean digital PDFs
  • plain text documents
  • source code
  • documentation pages
  • logs
  • low-latency search
  • cheap retrieval at scale

When vision-native RAG wins:

  • scanned PDFs
  • forms
  • tables with complex layout
  • diagrams
  • charts
  • handwritten notes
  • screenshots
  • documents where reading order is ambiguous

The best production systems often use hybrid retrieval:

OCR text index + page image index + metadata filters + reranker

Then the answer generator sees both extracted text and page images. This gives the model multiple routes to the evidence.

Multimodal RAG

Multimodal RAG generalizes the Chapter 22 RAG loop.

Text RAG:

text query -> retrieve text chunks -> generate answer

Multimodal RAG:

text/image/audio/video query
  -> retrieve text/images/pages/clips/audio segments
  -> reason over mixed evidence
  -> generate grounded answer

Cross-modal retrieval is the key. A text query can retrieve an image. An image query can retrieve text. A screenshot can retrieve similar UI states. An audio clip can retrieve transcript segments. A diagram can retrieve related design docs.

Fusion strategies:

Strategy How it works Trade-off
Early fusion combine modalities before retrieval powerful, complex
Late fusion retrieve per modality, merge rankings practical, debuggable
Rerank fusion retrieve candidates, use multimodal reranker strong but slower
Agentic retrieval model decides which modality/index to search flexible, harder to control

Late fusion is the boring default. Search the text index, image index, and metadata index separately. Merge candidates. Rerank. Send the top evidence to the generator.

For example, a travel planner over multimodal data:

User: Find a quiet hotel near the museum with rooms like this photo.

Inputs:
- text query: "quiet hotel near the museum"
- image query: reference room photo

Retrieval:
- text search over hotel descriptions and reviews
- image search over room photos
- geo filter near museum
- rerank by combined score

Generation:
- compare candidates
- cite review snippets and photos
- explain trade-offs

Evaluation must check both retrieval and generation:

  • Did retrieval find the right modality?
  • Did the answer use the evidence?
  • Are visual claims supported by images?
  • Are citations valid?
  • Did the model ignore relevant non-text evidence?
  • Did it hallucinate details not visible?

The failure mode is familiar: the answer sounds good but is grounded in the wrong evidence. Multimodal systems make this worse because evidence is harder to inspect.

Embodied models and actions

Vision-language-action models (VLAs) add another output type: action.

For a robot, the model may receive:

camera image + language instruction + robot state

and output:

end-effector movement, gripper command, or action tokens

RT-2-style systems showed that web-scale vision-language knowledge can transfer into robotics by expressing actions as tokens. OpenVLA-style systems made open references for robot policies. Flow-matching action models such as pi0-style systems treat actions as continuous trajectories rather than discrete words. Humanoid systems add another layer: whole-body control with safety constraints.

The conceptual shift:

The model is no longer only describing the world.
It is changing the world.

That means failures become physical. A wrong caption is embarrassing. A wrong robot action can break equipment or hurt someone.

Use action limits:

  • workspace boundaries
  • speed limits
  • force limits
  • forbidden zones
  • human proximity checks
  • emergency stop
  • simulator validation
  • human approval for high-risk tasks

The model should not be the only safety system. Robotics has control stacks for a reason. The VLA can propose high-level or mid-level actions, but low-level controllers and safety monitors enforce constraints.

The same idea applies to computer-use agents. A screenshot-based agent can click buttons, type text, submit forms, send messages, or change settings. That is action. Treat it with the same respect.

Computer-use agents

A computer-use agent sees a screen and acts through a UI.

Inputs:

screenshot
accessibility tree, when available
cursor position
recent actions
user instruction

Outputs:

click(x, y)
type(text)
scroll(direction)
press_key(key)
wait()
finish(result)

The primitive is GUI grounding: mapping language to screen coordinates or UI elements.

Example:

Instruction: Click the export button.
Visual task: find the export button on the screenshot.
Action: click(x=1120, y=84)

Screenshot-only agents are general because every UI can be screenshotted. But screenshots are visually ambiguous. Accessibility trees provide structure: button labels, roles, input fields, focus state, hierarchy. The best systems use both when possible.

screenshot tells you what it looks like
accessibility tree tells you what it is

Computer-use agents fail in predictable ways:

  • click the wrong similar-looking button
  • miss disabled state
  • fail after layout shift
  • type into the wrong field
  • lose track after navigation
  • ignore modal dialogs
  • loop on the same action
