Rule 1: separate description from execution. The model can read a tool description. It cannot be allowed to execute code directly. The host or server validates and executes. This is the boundary that lets a probabilistic planner use deterministic systems.

Rule 2: keep IDs stable. Tool call IDs, task IDs, trace IDs, session IDs, resource URIs, agent task IDs, and artifact IDs exist because distributed systems finish out of order. If you rely on list position or message order, parallelism will break you.

Rule 3: make every action observable. A tool call without an audit event is a liability. A model call without token accounting is a cost leak. A task without state is a support ticket waiting to happen. Observability is not an add-on; it is part of the protocol contract.

Rule 4: distinguish protocol errors from domain errors. Unknown JSON-RPC method is a protocol error. “Note not found” is a tool-level error. A model can recover from a domain error if you feed it back as an observation. It cannot recover from a broken wire protocol.

Rule 5: declare capabilities. Clients and servers should say what they support before using it. Capability negotiation is what lets old clients talk to new servers without mystery failures.

Rule 6: treat descriptions as untrusted input. Tool descriptions, resource descriptions, prompt templates, Agent Cards, and skill files are all text the model may read. Text can carry instructions. Anything that enters the prompt is part of the attack surface.

Rule 7: bind authority to audience and scope. A token for one resource should not work on another. A read token should not write. A local root should not silently expand. A tool should not inherit more permission than the user approved.

Rule 8: push long work into tasks. If work can outlive a connection, give it a task ID and a state machine. Do not hold the whole user interaction hostage to one long process.

Rule 9: preserve opacity where it is useful. Tool calls should be transparent because the caller needs to understand what happened. Agent-to-agent delegation can be opaque because the called agent may own its internal reasoning. Use MCP when you need transparency. Use A2A when you need task delegation.

Rule 10: make unsafe actions harder than safe actions. Read tools should be easy. Write tools should be gated. Destructive tools should require confirmation, stronger scopes, idempotency, and audit. If deleting a production database is as easy as searching documentation, your interface is broken.

These rules apply even if you never use MCP. They apply to homegrown tool routers, browser agents, robotics policies, plugin systems, workflow automation, and agent SDKs.

Whenever you see a new agent protocol, evaluate it with these questions:

Question What you are checking
How are capabilities discovered? registry and negotiation
How are calls correlated? IDs and ordering
Who executes actions? trust boundary
How are arguments typed? schema and validation
How are errors represented? recoverability
How is authority granted? auth, scopes, roots
How are long tasks handled? task lifecycle
How is user input requested mid-flight? elicitation or equivalent
How is behavior traced? observability
How are malicious integrations blocked? supply-chain security

A protocol that cannot answer these is not production-ready. It may still be useful for demos. Just do not confuse it with an integration layer you can bet a product on.

What you’d actually use

For a single product feature, start with provider-native function calling and strict structured outputs. Keep tools atomic. Use JSON Schema. Add retries and validation. Add confirmation for consequential tools.

For local developer tools, use MCP over stdio. It fits IDEs, local files, local repos, and desktop clients.

For SaaS integrations, use MCP over Streamable HTTP with OAuth. Do not ship remote MCP without proper auth, Origin checks, session IDs, and token audience validation.

For enterprise, put a gateway in front. Centralize RBAC, audit, registry approval, tool hash pinning, rate limits, and credential vaulting.

For long-running tools, use tasks. For user disambiguation or confirmation, use elicitation. For server-side reasoning without server-side model keys, use sampling carefully. For interactive outputs, use MCP Apps with a strict sandbox.

For agent-to-agent delegation, use A2A when you want task-level collaboration and opacity. Use MCP when you want transparent tool invocation.

For observability, instrument with OpenTelemetry GenAI conventions from the beginning. Add trace context across model calls, MCP calls, tools, gateways, and sub-agents.

For reusable workflows, package AGENTS.md, SKILL.md, and MCP servers together. Tools give the model hands. Skills teach it the work.

The boring default sequence:

1. provider function calling
2. strict schemas and validation
3. MCP server for reusable tools
4. MCP gateway for shared deployment
5. OAuth and registry controls
6. OTel tracing
7. skills for workflows
8. A2A only when task delegation is truly needed

Where this goes next

This chapter gave you the protocol layer: tools, schemas, MCP, security, auth, gateways, A2A, observability, routing, and skills. The next chapter uses this layer to build agents. Tools let a model act. Protocols let those tools scale. Agents decide when to act, how to plan, how to recover, how to remember, and how to stop before the loop runs away.

Chapter 26 - Agent Loops and Reasoning Patterns

An agent is not a smarter prompt.

That is the first mistake. A developer takes a normal chat prompt, adds “you are an autonomous agent”, gives the model a few tools, and expects magic. The model calls a search tool, reads the result, calls the wrong tool, gets an error, apologizes to itself, repeats the same call, burns through the token budget, and finally tells the user it completed the task. It did not.

Nothing about the word “agent” fixes control flow.

An agent is a loop around a model. The model observes the current state, decides what to do next, requests an action, reads the observation, and repeats until a stop condition fires. That is the core. Everything else in agent engineering is scaffolding around that loop: planning, memory, reflection, search, tool validation, workflow graphs, state checkpoints, safety policy, evals, and observability.

This chapter is the first of three agent chapters because Phase 14 is too important to compress.

The split is:

Chapter 26: loops, reasoning patterns, planning, tool-use behavior
Chapter 27: memory, frameworks, orchestration, production runtimes
Chapter 28: benchmarks, observability, safety, failure modes, eval-driven agents

Here we focus on the thinking and acting patterns:

  • ReAct: observe, think, act, observe
  • ReWOO and plan-and-execute: plan first, execute second
  • Reflexion: learn from failure in words
  • Self-Refine and CRITIC: generate, verify, improve
  • Tree of Thoughts and LATS: deliberate search
  • HTN and evolutionary planning: symbolic plans and machine-checkable optimization
  • workflow patterns: when not to use an agent at all

If Chapter 25 gave the model hands, this chapter gives you the control loop that decides when to use them.

What an agent actually is

A useful definition:

An agent is a model-driven control loop that can choose actions, observe results,
and continue until a task is complete or a stop condition is reached.

The model is not the agent. The model is one component. The agent is the whole runtime:

state
  -> model call
  -> tool/action decision
  -> validation and execution
  -> observation
  -> updated state
  -> next model call

That distinction matters because most agent failures happen outside the model. The model may choose a tool that does not exist. The runtime may execute a destructive action without confirmation. The observation may hide an error. The loop may have no turn budget. The state may contain poisoned tool output. The final answer may claim success after a failed API call.

The model produces tokens. The agent runtime decides what those tokens mean.

The minimal agent needs five ingredients:

Ingredient Purpose
State conversation, task, memory, tool results, budgets
Model proposes next step or final answer
Tools actions the model may request
Observation formatter turns tool results/errors into model-readable context
Stop condition prevents endless work and false completion

Without tools, it is a chatbot. Without state, it is a stateless completion. Without observations, it cannot learn from the world. Without stop conditions, it is a billing incident.

An agent is not automatically autonomous in the grand sense. Most production agents are bounded workers:

fix this bug
summarize this repo
triage this ticket
fill this form
research this topic
book this meeting

The task can be open-ended, but the runtime should not be. It needs budgets, permissions, timeouts, approval gates, and a definition of done.

Workflows before agents

Before you build an agent, ask whether you need one.

A workflow is an engineer-owned graph:

step 1 -> step 2 -> step 3 -> done

An agent is a model-owned graph:

model decides next step -> tool -> model decides next step -> ...

Workflows are easier to test, cheaper to run, safer to audit, and simpler to debug. Agents are useful when the next step depends on observations you cannot predict ahead of time.

Use a workflow when:

  • the steps are known
  • the task is short
  • compliance needs a readable graph
  • failure must be easy to reproduce
  • cost must be bounded tightly
  • every input follows a predictable shape

Use an agent when:

  • the task is open-ended
  • the next step depends on tool results
  • the environment is partially observable
  • the number of steps varies widely
  • the system must recover from unexpected states
  • exploration is part of the work

The mistake is using an agent because the word sounds more advanced. Many high-value AI products are workflows with an LLM inside. A support classifier that routes tickets to five fixed chains is a workflow. An invoice extractor with OCR, schema validation, and a repair loop is a workflow. A summarizer that chunks, summarizes, and combines is a workflow.

Agents earn their cost when the path cannot be known ahead of time.

Anthropic’s five workflow patterns are a good starting set:

Pattern Shape Use when
Prompt chaining output of one call feeds the next clean linear decomposition
Routing classifier picks handler inputs belong to different categories
Parallelization fan out and aggregate independent sections or voting
Orchestrator-workers orchestrator assigns subtasks subtasks vary per request
Evaluator-optimizer propose, evaluate, refine quality can be iteratively improved

These patterns are not less serious than agents. They are the boring default. Reach for a dynamic agent only when a static workflow cannot express the task.

The ReAct loop

ReAct means reasoning plus acting. The classic format:

Thought: I need the current package version.
Action: search_docs({"query": "current package version"})
Observation: The current package version is 2.4.1.
Thought: I can answer now.
Action: finish({"answer": "The current package version is 2.4.1."})

Modern systems may not expose Thought: text directly. Some models use a separate reasoning channel. Some encrypt reasoning across turns. Some frameworks hide it behind event streams. The visible transcript changes. The control flow does not:

observe -> think -> act -> observe -> think -> act -> stop

The observation is load-bearing. It is how the model learns what happened after an action. If the tool result is vague, the next decision is worse. If errors are hidden, the agent hallucinates success. If the observation includes untrusted instructions, the agent may obey them.

A good observation is:

  • concise
  • factual
  • structured where possible
  • explicit about failure
  • clear about what changed
  • stripped of irrelevant noise

Bad observation:

Error.

Good observation:

Tool notes_delete failed. Reason: missing confirmation. The note was not deleted.
To delete it, call notes_delete again with confirm=true after user approval.

The model can recover from the second. It cannot do much with the first.

The trajectory is the artifact

For a normal LLM call, the output is the artifact.

For an agent, the trajectory is the artifact.

A trajectory is the sequence of states, model decisions, tool calls, observations, retries, failures, and final answer. If a user says “the agent deleted the wrong note”, you cannot debug that from the final answer. You need the trajectory:

turn 1: model chose notes_search("TPS report")
observation: found note-3, note-7, note-14
turn 2: model chose notes_delete(note_id="note-3", confirm=false)
observation: confirmation required
turn 3: model chose notes_delete(note_id="note-3", confirm=true)
observation: deleted note-3
final: "Deleted the old TPS report note."

Now you can see the bug: the agent picked one of three matches without asking the user. The final answer hides the failure. The trajectory exposes it.

A production trajectory should record:

Field Why
run ID correlate user report to one run
task what the agent was asked to do
model which model made each decision
prompt/behavior version reproduce behavior
available tools understand selection context
tool calls actions requested
tool observations what the agent saw
approvals which actions the user approved
stop reason why the run ended
cost and latency operational debugging
final answer user-visible result

Do not rely on chat transcripts alone. The transcript may omit hidden tool metadata, correlation IDs, reasoning summaries, retries, validation errors, and approval decisions. Store an event log.

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class AgentEvent:
    run_id: str
    turn: int
    kind: str
    payload: dict[str, Any]
    timestamp_ms: int


class AgentEventLog:
    def __init__(self, run_id: str):
        self.run_id = run_id
        self.events: list[AgentEvent] = []

    def append(self, turn: int, kind: str, **payload: Any) -> None:
        self.events.append(AgentEvent(
            run_id=self.run_id,
            turn=turn,
            kind=kind,
            payload=payload,
            timestamp_ms=int(time.time() * 1000),
        ))

    def by_kind(self, kind: str) -> list[AgentEvent]:
        return [event for event in self.events if event.kind == kind]

Event kinds might include:

run_started
model_called
tool_call_requested
tool_call_validated
tool_call_blocked
tool_call_executed
observation_added
approval_requested
approval_granted
approval_denied
replan_requested
reflection_written
guardrail_triggered
run_finished

This event log becomes the source for debugging, eval trajectories, observability spans, and incident review. Chapter 28 will use this idea heavily.

The practical rule:

If you cannot replay the trajectory, you cannot debug the agent.

Observation design

Observation formatting is a quiet superpower.

The model does not see the real world. It sees your serialization of the real world. If a database query returns 1,000 rows and you dump them raw, the model gets noise. If a shell command fails and you show only exit 1, the model gets no repair path. If a browser action returns a screenshot with no element metadata, the model has to infer too much. If a retrieval tool returns untrusted web text with hidden instructions, the model may treat the text as instructions.

An observation should answer four questions:

What was attempted?
Did it succeed?
What changed?
What should the agent know next?

For read tools:

Tool search_docs succeeded.
Query: "refund annual enterprise"
Found 3 documents.
Top result: refund_policy.md, score 0.88.
Relevant excerpt: "Annual enterprise plans are eligible for prorated refunds within 60 days."

For write tools:

Tool notes_create succeeded.
Created note_id=note-184.
No other notes were modified.

For blocked tools:

Tool send_email was not executed.
Reason: user approval required for external email.
Next step: ask the user to approve the draft before calling send_email again.

For failed tools:

Tool github_create_issue failed.
Error type: permission_denied.
Required scope: issues:write.
No issue was created.

Notice the phrase “No issue was created.” This prevents false completion. Agents often hallucinate success after failed actions because the observation says only “permission denied” and the model optimistically continues. Say what did not happen.

Observation format should be stable. If one tool returns plain text, another returns JSON, another returns stack traces, and another returns markdown tables, the agent has to learn every style. Use a common envelope:

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Observation:
    tool_name: str
    success: bool
    summary: str
    data: dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
    no_change: bool = False
    next_step_hint: str | None = None

    def to_model_text(self) -> str:
        lines = [
            f"Tool: {self.tool_name}",
            f"Status: {'success' if self.success else 'failed'}",
            f"Summary: {self.summary}",
        ]
        if self.no_change:
            lines.append("State change: none.")
        if self.data:
            lines.append("Data:")
            lines.append(json.dumps(self.data, indent=2, sort_keys=True))
        if self.next_step_hint:
            lines.append(f"Next step hint: {self.next_step_hint}")
        return "\n".join(lines)

Do not overdo next-step hints. The observation should not become a hidden planner. But for validation errors, permission failures, and confirmation requirements, a repair hint helps the model recover safely.

A minimal agent loop

Here is a small agent loop. It uses a scripted model so the code is runnable without an API. The point is the runtime shape.

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any, Callable
import json
import time
import uuid


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class AgentTool:
    name: str
    description: str
    schema: dict[str, Any]
    executor: Callable[[dict[str, Any]], Any]
    consequential: bool = False


@dataclass
class AgentState:
    task: str
    messages: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list)
    observations: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    tool_calls: int = 0
    turns: int = 0
    done: bool = False
    final_answer: str | None = None


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class AgentDecision:
    kind: str
    tool_name: str | None = None
    arguments: dict[str, Any] | None = None
    final_answer: str | None = None

The scripted “model” reads state and returns a decision:

class ScriptedAgentModel:
    def decide(self, state: AgentState, tools: dict[str, AgentTool]) -> AgentDecision:
        if not state.observations:
            return AgentDecision(
                kind="tool",
                tool_name="search_notes",
                arguments={"query": state.task},
            )

        last = state.observations[-1]
        if "Found" in last:
            return AgentDecision(
                kind="finish",
                final_answer=f"Based on the notes: {last}",
            )

        return AgentDecision(
            kind="finish",
            final_answer="I could not complete the task with the available tools.",
        )

The runtime owns validation, execution, observations, and stop conditions:

class AgentLoop:
    def __init__(self, model: ScriptedAgentModel, tools: list[AgentTool], max_turns: int = 8):
        self.model = model
        self.tools = {tool.name: tool for tool in tools}
        self.max_turns = max_turns

    def run(self, task: str) -> AgentState:
        state = AgentState(task=task)
        state.messages.append({"role": "user", "content": task})

        while not state.done and state.turns < self.max_turns:
            state.turns += 1
            decision = self.model.decide(state, self.tools)

            if decision.kind == "finish":
                state.done = True
                state.final_answer = decision.final_answer or ""
                state.messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": state.final_answer})
                break

            if decision.kind != "tool":
                state.observations.append(f"Unknown decision kind: {decision.kind}")
                continue

            observation = self.execute_tool(decision)
            state.tool_calls += 1
            state.observations.append(observation)
            state.messages.append({
                "role": "tool",
                "name": decision.tool_name,
                "content": observation,
            })

        if not state.done:
            state.done = True
            state.final_answer = "Stopped: turn budget exhausted before completion."

        return state

    def execute_tool(self, decision: AgentDecision) -> str:
        tool = self.tools.get(decision.tool_name or "")
        if tool is None:
            return f"Tool failed. Unknown tool: {decision.tool_name}."

        if tool.consequential:
            return f"Tool {tool.name} was not executed because it requires approval."

        try:
            result = tool.executor(decision.arguments or {})
            return f"Tool {tool.name} succeeded. Result: {json.dumps(result)}"
        except Exception as exc:
            return f"Tool {tool.name} failed. {type(exc).__name__}: {exc}"

Run it:

def search_notes(args):
    query = args["query"]
    return {"matches": 2, "summary": f"Found notes related to {query}"}


agent = AgentLoop(
    model=ScriptedAgentModel(),
    tools=[
        AgentTool(
            name="search_notes",
            description="Use when the user asks to search notes.",
            schema={"type": "object", "required": ["query"]},
            executor=search_notes,
        )
    ],
)

result = agent.run("agent loop")
print(result.final_answer)

This is not impressive. That is the point. Strip away the framework and an agent is a loop. If you understand this loop, frameworks become implementation choices instead of magic boxes.

Agent state is not just messages

Most tutorials make agent state look like a chat history:

user message
assistant tool call
tool result
assistant message

That is necessary, but not sufficient.

An agent state has several layers:

Layer Example
Conversation user and assistant messages
Working memory current task, partial plan, extracted facts
Tool state last results, open task IDs, files touched
Runtime budgets turns left, cost left, time left
Permissions scopes, approvals, roots
Control state current phase: planning, executing, refining
External state references issue IDs, note IDs, document IDs

If you store all of that as free-form text, you make the model responsible for state management. That is fragile. Keep structured state in code and render only the relevant parts into the model context.

Example:

@dataclass
class AgentRunState:
    run_id: str
    task: str
    phase: str = "start"
    messages: list[dict[str, Any]] = field(default_factory=list)
    plan: Plan | None = None
    completed_steps: set[str] = field(default_factory=set)
    open_tasks: dict[str, str] = field(default_factory=dict)
    approvals: set[str] = field(default_factory=set)
    facts: dict[str, Any] = field(default_factory=dict)
    turns_used: int = 0
    tool_calls_used: int = 0
    cost_usd: float = 0.0


def render_state_for_model(state: AgentRunState) -> str:
    visible = {
        "task": state.task,
        "phase": state.phase,
        "completed_steps": sorted(state.completed_steps),
        "known_facts": state.facts,
        "turns_remaining": max(0, 12 - state.turns_used),
        "tool_calls_remaining": max(0, 30 - state.tool_calls_used),
    }
    return json.dumps(visible, indent=2, sort_keys=True)

The model does not need every internal field on every turn. It needs the state relevant to the decision. Rendering state is context engineering.

This is where frameworks such as LangGraph become useful: they treat state as a typed object that moves through nodes. But the idea does not require a framework. Keep state structured, then decide what to show.

The deeper lesson:

The agent runtime owns state.
The model receives a view of state.

That view may be incomplete by design. A model deciding whether to search documentation does not need raw OAuth tokens. A model drafting an email does not need full user profile data unless the task requires it. A model deciding the next browser click does not need every prior screenshot if the current UI state is enough.

Good state design reduces cost, improves reliability, and shrinks the prompt-injection surface.

Stop conditions

An agent without a stop condition is a bug with a budget.

Stop conditions include:

Stop condition Meaning
explicit finish model calls or emits final answer
no tool calls model answered directly
max turns runtime budget exhausted
max tool calls action budget exhausted
max wall-clock timeout
max cost spend budget exhausted
repeated state loop detected
guardrail trip unsafe behavior
human cancel user stops run

Use more than one. Do not trust the model to stop itself correctly every time.

Loop detection is underrated. If the agent calls the same tool with the same arguments three times and receives the same observation, it is probably stuck.

def repeated_call_guard(calls: list[tuple[str, str]], limit: int = 3) -> bool:
    if len(calls) < limit:
        return False
    return len(set(calls[-limit:])) == 1

The key is to feed the stop reason back truthfully:

Stopped after 12 turns because the same tool call repeated 3 times.
No external changes were made after the stop.

Users can tolerate failure. They cannot tolerate fake completion.

Tool use inside agents

Chapter 25 covered tools as contracts. Inside an agent, tool use becomes a policy problem.

The model chooses tools under uncertainty. It may call a tool too early, too often, with wrong arguments, or when no tool fits. Your runtime must handle all four.

Tool-call failure categories:

Failure Runtime response
unknown tool return observation naming valid tools
invalid arguments return schema error with example
permission denied block and explain required permission
confirmation required pause for user approval
timeout return timeout observation
transient error retry if safe
consequential side effect require idempotency and audit

Argument repair is useful. If the model emits "limit": "5" and the schema expects integer, coercion is fine when unambiguous. If the model emits "delete": "yes" for a destructive action, do not coerce that into approval. Safety-sensitive fields should be strict.

Inside agents, tool descriptions become routing policy. If two tools overlap, the model will make mistakes. Prefer tools that are:

  • atomic
  • clearly named
  • tightly scoped
  • explicitly described
  • safe by default
  • versioned

An agent with 80 ambiguous tools is worse than an agent with 12 good tools and a search/discovery path.

Plan first or react step by step

ReAct interleaves planning and acting:

think -> act -> observe -> think -> act -> observe

Plan-and-execute separates them:

plan -> execute steps -> synthesize

The difference matters.

ReAct is flexible. It can adapt after every observation. It works well when the environment is unknown, tools are unreliable, or the path depends heavily on feedback.

Plan-and-execute is efficient. It can reduce repeated reasoning, execute independent steps in parallel, and make the plan auditable before action.

Use ReAct when:

  • each observation changes the next step
  • environment state is uncertain
  • tools may fail in surprising ways
  • task is short enough that token growth is tolerable

Use plan-and-execute when:

  • steps are mostly predictable
  • subtasks can run independently
  • you need an auditable plan
  • token cost matters
  • you can validate the plan before execution

The simplest plan object:

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class PlanStep:
    step_id: str
    tool_name: str
    arguments: dict[str, Any]
    depends_on: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Plan:
    steps: list[PlanStep]


def validate_plan(plan: Plan, tools: set[str]) -> list[str]:
    errors = []
    ids = {step.step_id for step in plan.steps}
    for step in plan.steps:
        if step.tool_name not in tools:
            errors.append(f"{step.step_id}: unknown tool {step.tool_name}")
        for dep in step.depends_on:
            if dep not in ids:
                errors.append(f"{step.step_id}: unknown dependency {dep}")
    return errors

The plan should be validated before execution. If a planner invents a tool, reject the plan. If a step depends on a missing step, reject the plan. If the plan includes a consequential action, ask for approval before execution.

ReWOO: reasoning without observations

ReWOO splits the agent into three roles:

Planner -> Workers -> Solver

The planner writes a plan. Workers fetch evidence. The solver composes the final answer.

The important idea is that the planner does not see observations while planning. It emits evidence slots:

E1 = search("capital of France")
E2 = search("population of #E1")
Answer using E1 and E2.

Then workers execute the evidence steps. The solver sees the original question, the plan, and the evidence.

Why this helps:

  • fewer repeated tokens
  • independent evidence steps can run in parallel
  • failures localize to plan nodes
  • the plan can be inspected before execution
  • planner can be smaller than solver

Why it hurts:

  • static plan may be wrong
  • unexpected observations cannot influence earlier planning
  • dependencies must be tracked
  • planner mistakes can poison the whole run

ReWOO is excellent for research-style tasks where evidence gathering is predictable:

find entity -> find attributes -> compare -> synthesize

It is weaker for interactive environments where each action changes the world.

DAG execution and parallel workers

The useful version of ReWOO is not a list. It is a directed acyclic graph.

Some steps depend on earlier evidence:

E1: search("capital of France")
E2: search("population of #E1")

Some steps are independent:

E1: search("pricing policy")
E2: search("refund policy")
E3: search("enterprise contract terms")

Independent steps can run in parallel. Dependent steps must wait.

Represent this explicitly:

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class EvidenceStep:
    step_id: str
    tool_name: str
    arguments: dict[str, Any]
    depends_on: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)


def ready_steps(steps: list[EvidenceStep], completed: set[str]) -> list[EvidenceStep]:
    return [
        step for step in steps
        if step.step_id not in completed and all(dep in completed for dep in step.depends_on)
    ]

The executor runs waves:

def execute_plan_waves(steps: list[EvidenceStep], execute_one) -> dict[str, Any]:
    completed: set[str] = set()
    results: dict[str, Any] = {}

    while len(completed) < len(steps):
        wave = ready_steps(steps, completed)
        if not wave:
            missing = {step.step_id for step in steps} - completed
            raise ValueError(f"plan has unresolved cycle or dependency: {sorted(missing)}")

        for step in wave:
            resolved_args = {}
            for key, value in step.arguments.items():
                if isinstance(value, str) and value.startswith("#"):
                    resolved_args[key] = results[value[1:]]
                else:
                    resolved_args[key] = value

            results[step.step_id] = execute_one(step.tool_name, resolved_args)
            completed.add(step.step_id)

    return results

This sequential implementation is easier to read. In production, each wave can run concurrently. The important part is dependency order and reference substitution.

Plan validation should happen before execution:

  • every tool exists
  • every dependency exists
  • no cycles
  • no consequential action without approval
  • no step exceeds budget
  • arguments validate after reference substitution

The model can write the plan. The runtime owns the DAG.

This is a recurring theme. The model proposes flexible structure. Code validates and executes it.

Replanning

Pure plan-and-execute assumes the plan is good enough. Real tools disagree.

A practical agent adds replanning:

make plan
execute step
if step succeeds, continue
if step fails, replan from current state

Replanning should be bounded. If the agent replans forever, you have moved the infinite loop from actions to plans.

Good replanning triggers:

  • unknown tool
  • missing permission
  • no results found
  • tool timeout
  • state changed
  • user changed goal
  • plan dependency failed

Bad replanning trigger:

the model feels like trying a different approach

Make replanning explicit:

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class ReplanRequest:
    original_task: str
    current_plan: Plan
    completed_steps: list[str]
    failed_step: str
    failure_observation: str
    remaining_budget: int

Then the planner receives the failure context and produces a revised plan. This is how you keep agency without giving up control.

Reflexion: learning from failure in words

Reflexion adds a trial loop around the agent.

One attempt fails. The agent, evaluator, or separate reflector writes a natural-language reflection:

I failed because I searched for "refund policy" but the question asked about enterprise annual plans.
Next time, search for the exact product tier and contract term.

That reflection is stored and included in the next attempt.

No weights change. The learning is verbal. The model conditions on memory.

The components:

Component Job
Actor runs the task
Evaluator scores success/failure
Self-reflector writes what to change
Episodic memory stores reflections for future attempts

Reflexion works when there is a useful failure signal. Unit tests are excellent. Tool errors are useful. Human feedback can work. Pure self-judgment is weaker.

Code shape:

@dataclass
class ReflectionMemory:
    items: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    max_items: int = 5

    def add(self, reflection: str) -> None:
        self.items.append(reflection)
        self.items = self.items[-self.max_items:]

    def context(self) -> str:
        if not self.items:
            return "No prior reflections."
        return "\n".join(f"- {item}" for item in self.items)

The failure mode is memory rot. If you store every reflection forever, the prompt fills with stale advice. Reflections need TTL, deduplication, and periodic cleanup. Chapter 27 covers memory systems in depth. For now, the principle is enough:

Failure can become prompt-level learning if the failure signal is real.

Evaluators are the learning signal

Every reasoning pattern in this chapter depends on the same hidden question:

How does the agent know whether it is getting better?

If the answer is “the model decides,” you have a weak system. Sometimes that is enough. A model can often notice that an answer is too vague, too long, or missing a required section. But the model is also the thing that made the mistake. Asking it to grade itself is useful, not authoritative.

A stronger evaluator comes from outside the model:

Evaluator Strong for Weak for
unit tests code behavior requirements not covered by tests
type checker interface mistakes runtime logic
linter style and simple defects semantic correctness
calculator arithmetic problem framing
SQL query database truth stale or incomplete data
exact match known-answer tasks open-ended answers
schema validator output structure content quality
retrieval check citation support synthesis quality
human review judgment-heavy work speed and cost
LLM judge rubric scalable preference checks bias, verbosity reward, self-preference

The evaluator determines how aggressive the agent can be.

If the evaluator is a unit test suite, the agent can try many candidate patches. Bad candidates are rejected mechanically. If the evaluator is “does this marketing copy feel better,” the loop needs a small budget and probably a human checkpoint. If the evaluator is a vague judge prompt that says “rate this from 1 to 10,” search may simply learn to satisfy the judge’s quirks.

This is the core reason coding agents improved faster than many other agents. Code has unusually good evaluators:

  • tests
  • compilers
  • type checkers
  • linters
  • formatters
  • runtime errors
  • benchmark scores
  • diffs

The agent can make a change, run the evaluator, observe failure, and try again. The environment gives concrete feedback. The same pattern is much harder for strategy memos, medical advice, financial plans, or policy recommendations. Those domains have evaluators, but many are delayed, expensive, incomplete, or unsafe to use as live feedback.

You should classify evaluators by strength before choosing an agent pattern.

Hard evaluators return an objective pass/fail or score. Unit tests, JSON schema validation, exact-match answers, and arithmetic checks are hard evaluators.

Soft evaluators return judgment. Human ratings, preference models, rubric judges, and self-critiques are soft evaluators.

Delayed evaluators only become available later. Did the customer churn? Did the user like the workflow? Did the recommendation help? These are valuable for product learning but dangerous as inner-loop signals.

Proxy evaluators measure something near the target but not the target. Click-through rate is a proxy for satisfaction. Length may be a proxy for completeness. Tool success may be a proxy for task success. Proxy evaluators are useful until the agent learns to game them.

The practical rule is simple:

Strong evaluator -> you can spend more search.
Weak evaluator -> keep the loop short and keep humans closer.

This is why “agentic reasoning” is not magic. It is optimization. The model generates candidates. The evaluator pushes the search toward better candidates. If the evaluator points in the wrong direction, a more capable agent gets worse faster.

When you design an agent, write the evaluator before you write the loop. At minimum, write down:

  • what success means
  • what failure means
  • which failures are recoverable
  • which failures should stop immediately
  • which checks are automatic
  • which checks require human approval
  • which metric could be gamed

For example, a support refund agent should not use “customer satisfaction” as its only evaluator. The agent can maximize satisfaction by approving every refund. It needs policy checks, account checks, fraud checks, and escalation rules. A coding agent should not use “tests pass” as its only evaluator if the tests are weak. It also needs diff review, scope checks, and sometimes generated regression tests.

The evaluator is the steering wheel. The model is the engine. Do not build a bigger engine before you know where the steering points.

Self-Refine and CRITIC

Self-Refine is a loop:

generate -> feedback -> refine -> feedback -> refine -> stop

One model can play all roles, but the prompts should be different. If the critic prompt sounds like the generator prompt, the model rubber-stamps itself.

CRITIC strengthens the feedback step with tools:

generate answer
verify with search/tests/calculator/linter
critique using tool results
refine answer

This matters because LLMs are unreliable at self-verification. A model that hallucinated a fact may also judge the hallucinated fact as plausible. External tools ground the critique.

Use Self-Refine when:

  • task is subjective or stylistic
  • no external verifier exists
  • iteration cost is low

Use CRITIC when:

  • facts can be checked
  • code can be tested
  • math can be calculated
  • outputs can be validated
  • retrieval can confirm claims

A useful loop:

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Critique:
    passed: bool
    issues: list[str]


def refine_loop(generate, critique, refine, task: str, max_iters: int = 3) -> str:
    output = generate(task)
    history = []

    for _ in range(max_iters):
        result = critique(task, output)
        if result.passed:
            return output
        history.append({"output": output, "issues": result.issues})
        output = refine(task, output, result.issues, history)

    return output

The stop condition is important. More refinement is not always better. Agents can overfit to the critic, add unnecessary caveats, or make correct answers worse. Most production systems budget one to three refinement passes.

Evaluator-optimizer workflows are this pattern in production language: evaluator checks, optimizer improves. Use it when you can define quality criteria.

Tree of Thoughts

Chain-of-thought is one path. If the first step is wrong, the whole path may stay wrong.

Tree of Thoughts turns reasoning into search:

state -> generate candidate thoughts -> score candidates -> expand best candidates

Instead of one trajectory:

A -> B -> C -> answer

You get a tree:

        A
      / | \
     B  C  D
    /      | \
   E       F  G

Each node is a partial reasoning state. The model proposes expansions. A value function scores them. Search keeps promising branches.

This helps on tasks where backtracking matters:

  • puzzles
  • math search
  • code generation with tests
  • planning with multiple possible routes
  • research with competing hypotheses

It is expensive. Generating and scoring branches can multiply token cost by 10, 100, or more. Do not use Tree of Thoughts for simple Q&A or extraction.

The search pattern:

@dataclass
class ThoughtNode:
    text: str
    score: float
    depth: int
    parent: "ThoughtNode | None" = None


def beam_search(root: ThoughtNode, expand, score, beam_size: int, max_depth: int) -> ThoughtNode:
    frontier = [root]
    best = root

    for _ in range(max_depth):
        candidates = []
        for node in frontier:
            for thought in expand(node):
                child = ThoughtNode(
                    text=thought,
                    score=score(thought),
                    depth=node.depth + 1,
                    parent=node,
                )
                candidates.append(child)
                if child.score > best.score:
                    best = child

        frontier = sorted(candidates, key=lambda n: n.score, reverse=True)[:beam_size]
        if not frontier:
            break

    return best

The hard part is the score. If your value function is noisy, search optimizes noise. A unit test is a strong value function. A vague “rate this reasoning from 1 to 10” prompt is weaker.

Search is only as good as its evaluator.

Search budgets and when search pays off

Search feels intelligent because it gives the model chances to recover. A single completion commits early. Search keeps alternatives alive.

But search is not free. If you generate 4 candidate thoughts at each depth and search 4 levels, the full tree has:

1 + 4 + 16 + 64 + 256 = 341 nodes

If each node requires one model call to generate and one model call to score, you have hundreds of calls for one answer. Most production systems do not do full tree search. They use a budgeted approximation:

  • beam search keeps only the top k candidates per depth
  • best-first search expands the currently highest-scoring candidate
  • depth limits stop runaway exploration
  • time limits bound latency
  • evaluator thresholds stop when an answer is good enough
  • caching avoids scoring the same state twice

This is why the search budget is a product decision, not only an algorithm decision. A research assistant running in the background can spend two minutes exploring query paths. A chat assistant in front of a user cannot. A coding agent running overnight can try twenty candidate patches. An agent handling a live checkout flow should not.

Think in budgets:

Budget What it limits
depth how many reasoning/action steps a branch can take
width how many alternatives survive at each step
rollouts how many full attempts the system can try
tool calls how much external work the agent can do
tokens cost and context growth
wall-clock time user-visible latency
side effects how many real-world changes are allowed

Search pays off when three things are true.

First, early mistakes are common. If the model usually gets the answer right in one pass, search only adds cost.

Second, mistakes are detectable. Search needs a signal that one branch is better than another. Tests, calculators, retrieval checks, and simulators are useful. Vibes are not enough.

Third, alternatives are meaningfully different. If every branch is a paraphrase of the same idea, search is theater. A good expansion prompt should force diversity:

Generate three different solution strategies:
1. the direct approach
2. the conservative approach with fewer assumptions
3. the approach that uses external verification

For code, diversity might mean different implementation plans. For research, it might mean different query strategies. For planning, it might mean different orderings of subtasks. For math, it might mean algebraic, geometric, and computational attacks.

Search fails when the model samples shallow variations and the evaluator prefers confident nonsense. That failure is common. You see it in agents that produce five plans, score all five with the same model, pick the most verbose one, and call that deliberation.

A practical search loop should separate roles:

  • generator proposes candidates
  • evaluator scores candidates using as much external evidence as possible
  • controller decides what to expand next
  • executor performs only approved actions

Do not let the generator decide everything. It will tend to protect its own first idea. The controller can still be implemented with an LLM, but the prompt and inputs should be different from the generator prompt.

For most engineering systems, a small beam is the boring default:

beam_size = 2 or 3
max_depth = 3 to 5
max_wall_time = depends_on_task
hard_stop_on_dangerous_action = true

That gives the model room to recover without turning every request into a science project.

LATS: search plus acting

LATS combines Tree of Thoughts, ReAct, and Reflexion with Monte Carlo Tree Search.

The agent does not just search over thoughts. It searches over trajectories:

state -> action -> observation -> state -> action -> observation

The model proposes actions. The environment returns observations. A value function scores partial trajectories. Failed rollouts can produce reflections that improve later rollouts.

Monte Carlo Tree Search has four steps:

Step Meaning
select choose promising node using exploration/exploitation
expand generate candidate children
simulate roll out from a child
backpropagate update ancestor values

This is powerful when:

  • actions are reversible or safe to simulate
  • evaluator is reliable
  • correctness matters more than cost
  • the task benefits from exploring alternatives

It is dangerous when:

  • actions have real side effects
  • evaluator is weak
  • budget is tight
  • environment cannot be reset

For production, LATS-style search is most natural in coding agents with tests, research agents with multiple query paths, and planning subgraphs where actions are simulated before execution. Do not run tree search over real payment APIs.

Simulated actions versus real actions

Agents become dangerous when they cannot tell the difference between exploring an option and doing it.

In ordinary reasoning, the model can say:

Maybe I should delete the stale records and rebuild the index.

That sentence is harmless. In a tool-using agent with a database tool, the same thought can become a real mutation. The controller must separate simulated actions from executable actions.

Use three action classes:

Class Meaning Example
thought action reasoning only “consider deleting stale rows”
dry-run action tool call with no mutation EXPLAIN, --dry-run, validation endpoint
live action real side effect delete rows, send email, charge card

Search belongs mostly in the first two classes. The agent can search over thoughts. It can search over dry runs. It should not search by trying live actions against production systems.

This is not only about safety. It is about learning. A dry-run action gives observation without damage. A database EXPLAIN plan can reveal cost. A Terraform plan can reveal infrastructure changes. A test suite can reveal code behavior. A payment API sandbox can reveal request shape. These observations make the next step better while keeping the real world untouched.

The live action boundary should be explicit in code:

class ActionKind(str, Enum):
    THINK = "think"
    READ = "read"
    DRY_RUN = "dry_run"
    MUTATE = "mutate"


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class PlannedAction:
    tool: str
    args: dict[str, Any]
    kind: ActionKind
    reason: str

Then the executor can enforce policy:

def require_approval(action: PlannedAction) -> bool:
    return action.kind == ActionKind.MUTATE

That looks simple because it is. The point is to make the dangerous boundary impossible to miss. If a tool can mutate state, it should be marked that way in the registry. If an action crosses a money, identity, security, legal, or customer-communication boundary, require approval or a deterministic policy check.

Advanced agent papers often focus on reasoning quality. Production agent systems live or die on action boundaries. The agent can be brilliant and still be unusable if every branch of its reasoning can hit a live API.

HTN planning

Hierarchical Task Network (HTN) planning is older than LLMs and still useful.

An HTN has:

  • compound tasks
  • primitive tasks
  • methods that decompose compound tasks
  • operators that execute primitive tasks
  • preconditions
  • effects
  • state

Example:

task: onboard_employee
method:
  create_account
  assign_laptop
  add_to_payroll
  schedule_orientation

Each primitive operator has preconditions and effects:

operator: assign_laptop
precondition: employee_has_account, laptop_available
effect: employee_has_laptop

The planner searches for a decomposition that is valid by construction. This matters for compliance workflows, robotics, scheduling, and policy-bound automation.

LLMs can help HTN planners by suggesting decompositions when the method library is incomplete. But the symbolic planner must validate the suggestion. The LLM proposes. The planner proves.

That is the right split:

LLM expands possibilities
symbolic layer enforces correctness

If you let the LLM directly edit the plan without precondition/effect validation, you no longer have HTN soundness. You have prose wearing a planner costume.

Evolutionary search

Evolutionary search is for optimization when you have a machine-checkable fitness function.

Loop:

seed program
  -> model proposes mutations
  -> evaluator scores candidates
  -> keep best
  -> mutate again

This is not ordinary agent planning. It is search over artifacts, usually code.

It works when:

  • candidates can be generated cheaply
  • evaluator is deterministic
  • evaluator is fast enough
  • better/worse is measurable
  • failures are safe

Examples:

  • optimize a function against tests
  • improve a SQL query plan
  • search compiler transformations
  • tune prompts against eval score
  • generate scheduling heuristics

It does not work when the evaluator is “ask the LLM if this is better.” That collapses into self-judgment. Evolutionary search needs a real fitness signal.

Minimal skeleton:

def evolutionary_search(seed, mutate, evaluate, generations: int, population_size: int):
    population = [seed]
    best = seed
    best_score = evaluate(seed)

    for _ in range(generations):
        candidates = []
        for item in population:
            candidates.extend(mutate(item, population_size))

        scored = [(evaluate(candidate), candidate) for candidate in candidates]
        scored.sort(reverse=True, key=lambda x: x[0])
        population = [candidate for _, candidate in scored[:population_size]]

        if scored and scored[0][0] > best_score:
            best_score, best = scored[0]

    return best, best_score

Again, the evaluator is everything. With unit tests, benchmark scores, or formal checks, evolutionary search can be powerful. With vibes, it is expensive noise.

The five workflow patterns in practice

The workflow patterns are not separate from agents. They are the pieces you should use before letting the model own the whole graph.

Prompt chaining:

extract -> normalize -> summarize -> format

Routing:

classify request -> billing chain or technical chain or sales chain

Parallelization:

summarize each document chunk in parallel -> synthesize

Orchestrator-workers:

orchestrator decides which specialist workers to run -> workers return -> orchestrator synthesizes

Evaluator-optimizer:

draft -> critique -> revise -> pass

These patterns are easier to test than agents because the graph is mostly known. They also compose with agents. For example, an agent may use an evaluator-optimizer subroutine for final answer quality, or a ReWOO planner may execute worker calls in parallel.

The best systems are rarely “pure agents.” They are controlled workflows with agentic sections where dynamic decision-making is valuable.

Choosing the right pattern

Use this decision table.

Task shape Pattern
one obvious answer, no tools direct model call
fixed sequence of transformations prompt chain
input categories require different paths routing
independent chunks or votes parallelization
variable subtasks but bounded graph orchestrator-workers
output can be checked and improved evaluator-optimizer / CRITIC
unknown next step after each observation ReAct
known evidence-gathering plan ReWOO
plan mostly known but needs repair plan-and-execute with replanning
hard reasoning needs backtracking Tree of Thoughts / LATS
plan must satisfy formal constraints HTN
artifact can be machine-scored evolutionary search

The more dynamic the pattern, the more observability and evals you need.

Direct call is easiest to test. Prompt chain is still easy. ReAct is harder. LATS is much harder. A multi-agent search system with memory and tools is not something you debug with print statements.

Complexity must buy capability.

Combining patterns without making a mess

Real systems usually combine patterns. The mistake is combining them by accident.

A good agentic product often looks like this:

workflow controller
  -> route request
  -> retrieve context
  -> run bounded agent loop
  -> verify output
  -> ask for approval if needed
  -> execute final action
  -> log trajectory

Only one part of that graph is a true agent loop. The rest is workflow. That is healthy. The workflow provides rails. The agent provides judgment where the path is not known in advance.

Take a support debugging assistant. A messy version says:

Here are 18 tools. Figure out the problem and fix it.

The clean version composes patterns:

route issue type
  -> ReWOO planner creates evidence plan
  -> parallel workers gather logs, config, recent deploys
  -> ReAct loop investigates unknowns
  -> CRITIC verifier checks final diagnosis against evidence
  -> human approves any customer-facing action

That system uses routing, ReWOO, parallelization, ReAct, CRITIC, and approval gates. It is not chaotic because each pattern has a job.

The composition rule:

One control owner per layer.

At the top level, the workflow owns the lifecycle. Inside a bounded node, the agent owns local decisions. Inside a verifier node, the evaluator owns pass/fail. Inside an executor, policy owns side effects.

What you should avoid is nested control without ownership:

agent calls agent calls agent calls tools calls another planner

This produces failures nobody can explain. Did the planner choose the wrong subtask? Did the worker misunderstand the task? Did the tool observation hide the error? Did the final synthesizer ignore a warning? Without clean boundaries, trace analysis becomes archaeology.

Use handoff contracts between patterns. Every node should say what it consumes and produces.

Example:

@dataclass(frozen=True)
class EvidencePlan:
    questions: list[str]
    searches: list[str]
    required_sources: list[str]


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class EvidenceBundle:
    claims: list[str]
    citations: list[str]
    missing: list[str]


@dataclass(frozen=True)
class VerifiedAnswer:
    answer: str
    passed: bool
    issues: list[str]

Once you have explicit handoff objects, composition becomes normal software engineering. You can test each node. You can replay a failed trajectory. You can swap a ReAct investigator for a ReWOO planner without rewriting the whole system.

The deep lesson is that agents should not dissolve architecture. They should sit inside architecture.

Prompting agents without hiding the control flow

Agent prompts tend to become large because they carry instructions, tool descriptions, policies, memory, examples, output schemas, and task context. Large prompts are not automatically bad. Hidden prompts are.

The reader should think of an agent prompt as a runtime configuration file. It defines behavior. It should be versioned, reviewed, tested, and logged.

A usable agent prompt has clear layers:

role and mission
behavior rules
tool-use policy
available tools
state summary
task
output format

The role should be specific but not theatrical:

You are a support investigation agent. Your job is to identify the most likely cause of a customer issue using the available diagnostic tools. Do not perform customer-facing actions.

The behavior rules should control process:

- Prefer read-only tools first.
- Ask for missing identifiers instead of guessing.
- Stop after 8 tool calls unless the controller grants more budget.
- If evidence conflicts, report the conflict.
- Do not call mutation tools.

The tool-use policy should be concrete:

Use get_recent_deploys when the issue started within the last 48 hours.
Use search_logs only after you have a service name and time window.
Use check_customer_plan before diagnosing entitlement issues.

Weak agent prompts say “use tools when helpful.” Strong prompts teach tool selection.

The state summary should be compact. Do not dump the full trajectory back into the model forever. Summarize what matters:

Known:
- customer: acme-corp
- issue: webhook delivery failing since 09:30 UTC
- recent deploy: billing-api v42 at 09:05 UTC

Open:
- whether failures are all endpoints or only one endpoint
- whether retry queue is growing

This is not cosmetic. Context is a working memory with limited attention. If you fill it with logs, stale attempts, and repeated instructions, the model loses the thread. Chapter 27 covers memory compression in depth, but the local rule belongs here:

Keep the current state small enough that the next action is obvious.

Finally, require structured action outputs. Free-form “I will search logs now” is harder to execute safely than:

{
  "action": "search_logs",
  "args": {
    "service": "billing-api",
    "since": "2026-05-09T09:00:00Z",
    "query": "webhook delivery failed"
  },
  "reason": "Webhook failures started shortly after the billing-api deploy."
}

The prompt should not hide the architecture. It should expose the architecture to the model in the smallest useful form.

Practical design rules

Start with the simplest system that can solve the task.

Add tools only when the model needs external state, computation, or action.

Add a loop only when the model needs to use observations to choose later steps.

Add planning only when repeated step-by-step reasoning is wasteful or the plan must be inspected.

Add reflection only when failures recur and you have a useful failure signal.

Add search only when the evaluator is strong enough to guide search.

Add symbolic planning only when correctness constraints matter.

Add evolutionary search only when candidates can be scored mechanically.

In production, every agent run should record:

  • task
  • model
  • prompt/behavior version
  • tools available
  • tool calls
  • observations
  • stop reason
  • turn count
  • cost
  • latency
  • final answer
  • errors

Chapter 28 will go deep on observability and evals. For now, remember that an agent trajectory is the unit of debugging. A final answer without its trajectory is not enough.

Common early mistakes

Calling a workflow an agent. If the steps are fixed, call it a workflow. This clarifies ownership.

No turn budget. The model gets stuck and keeps trying.

Bad observations. Tool errors are hidden or vague, so the model cannot recover.

Too many tools. The registry is large and ambiguous, so selection accuracy drops.

Unsafe consequential tools. The model can mutate state without confirmation or scope checks.

Search without evaluator. Tree search amplifies weak self-judgment.

Reflection without pruning. Memory fills with stale advice.

Planning without validation. The model invents tools and impossible steps.

Premature framework adoption. The team debugs framework behavior before understanding the loop.

These are boring mistakes. That is why they are common.

Agent design review checklist

Before an agent goes near users, run a design review. You are not looking for perfect architecture. You are looking for uncontrolled ambiguity.

Ask these questions.

What is the task boundary?

If the task is “help the user,” the boundary is too broad. If the task is “compare three invoices against purchase orders and flag mismatches,” the boundary is usable. Agents perform better when the task has a recognizable shape.

Can this be a workflow?

If the steps are known, use a workflow. If the steps are mostly known, use a workflow with one agentic node. Reach for a full loop only when observations genuinely change the next action.

What tools can the agent use?

Every tool should have a name, schema, description, examples, timeout, error contract, permission level, and side-effect class. If a tool cannot be described clearly, the model will use it inconsistently.

What observations does the agent receive?

A tool result should tell the agent what happened in language it can act on. Raw stack traces, giant JSON blobs, and vague "failed" strings are bad observations. The executor should normalize tool errors into recoverable messages.

What are the stop conditions?

There should be limits for turns, tool calls, tokens, cost, wall-clock time, repeated actions, and repeated failures. There should also be positive stop conditions: answer found, verifier passed, user approval required, or escalation needed.

What actions require approval?

Live mutations need policy. Sending messages, changing permissions, deleting data, moving money, filing tickets under a user’s name, and calling external systems should not be treated like search queries.

What is the evaluator?

Name the evaluator. If it is a test suite, say which tests. If it is a rubric, write the rubric. If it is a human, define what the human reviews. If there is no evaluator, keep the agent simple.

Can you replay a failed run?

If a user reports a bad result, you need the trajectory: prompts, model version, tool calls, observations, state summaries, costs, stop reason, and final output. Without replay, you have anecdotes instead of debugging.

What memory enters the prompt?

Memory should be selected, summarized, and bounded. Long-term memory is not a junk drawer. Reflections, user preferences, retrieved facts, and prior trajectories have different lifetimes and trust levels.

What happens when the agent is unsure?

A good agent can say:

I do not have enough evidence.
I need approval.
The tools disagree.
This request is outside my scope.
I should escalate.

That is not weakness. That is operational maturity.

What is the abuse case?

Assume a user tries to prompt-inject the agent through a document, tool result, webpage, email, ticket, or file. Which instructions are trusted? Which are data? Chapter 28 goes deep on agent safety, but the design review should flag the boundary now.

This checklist turns agent design from vibes into engineering. If you cannot answer these questions, the agent is not ready. It may still be a useful prototype. It is not a production system.

What you’d actually use

For most product features, start with a workflow: direct call, prompt chain, routing, parallelization, or evaluator-optimizer. Add tools through the Chapter 25 contract. Add strict schemas. Add evals.

For open-ended tool use, use a ReAct-style agent loop with hard budgets, good observations, and approval gates for consequential tools.

For research tasks, use plan-and-execute or ReWOO when evidence gathering is predictable. Add replanning when tools fail.

For code tasks, use CRITIC-style verification with tests, linters, type checkers, and execution sandboxes. Use search only when tests provide a strong value function.

For compliance or robotics-style planning, use symbolic structure: HTN, state machines, preconditions, effects. Let the LLM propose, not prove.

For optimization, use evolutionary search only when the evaluator is real.

Use frameworks after you understand the runtime shape you need. LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI, OpenAI Agents SDK, Claude Agent SDK, Agno, and Mastra all wrap these same ideas. Chapter 27 covers them. Do not outsource your mental model to a framework.

Where this goes next

This chapter covered how agents think and act: loops, planning, reflection, refinement, search, and workflow patterns. The next chapter adds the machinery agents need to persist and scale: memory systems, skill libraries, stateful graphs, actor runtimes, role-based crews, SDKs, orchestration patterns, and production runtimes. The loop is the core. The runtime is what keeps it alive.

Chapter 27 - Agent Memory, Frameworks, and Runtimes

An agent without memory is a talented contractor with amnesia. It can solve the task in front of it, but every new session starts from zero. It forgets user preferences, forgets what tools failed last time, forgets the shape of the codebase, forgets the facts it already paid to retrieve, and forgets the mistakes it promised not to repeat.

An agent without a runtime is even worse. It can think, but it cannot survive. A browser closes. A worker restarts. A tool call times out on step 38. A human approval sits pending overnight. A queue spikes. A model provider returns a transient error. The agent’s reasoning pattern from Chapter 26 is still correct, but the system around it is too fragile to carry that reasoning into production.

This chapter is about the machinery around the loop.

Chapter 26 covered how an agent decides: observe, think, act, plan, reflect, refine, search. Chapter 27 covers how an agent persists and runs: memory tiers, memory blocks, sleep-time consolidation, hybrid retrieval, skill libraries, state graphs, actor runtimes, role-based crews, provider SDKs, orchestration topologies, and production runtime shapes.

The mental model is simple:

agent = loop + memory + tools + runtime + observability

The loop gives agency. Memory gives continuity. Tools give contact with the world. Runtime gives durability. Observability gives accountability.

Leave any one out and the system may still demo well. It will not operate well.

Memory is not context

The first mistake people make is treating a larger context window as memory.

It is not.

A context window is working memory. It is the stuff the model can currently attend to. It is finite, expensive, and fragile. You can make it larger, but you do not change its nature. A 128k context window is a bigger desk, not a filing cabinet. You can spread more papers on it, but the desk still gets cluttered, and tomorrow it is empty unless something persisted the papers.

Agent memory has to solve three different problems:

Problem What breaks
overflow the conversation or trace no longer fits
dilution too much irrelevant context weakens attention
persistence a new session starts with no history

Overflow is obvious. The agent runs for many turns, calls tools, reads files, retrieves documents, and eventually the prompt cannot hold everything.

Dilution is subtler. Even if the prompt fits, stuffing everything into it makes the important parts harder to use. Long context does not mean uniform attention over every token. Models still lose facts in the middle, over-weight recent text, and get distracted by irrelevant retrieved chunks.

Persistence is the production killer. A support agent that remembers a user’s billing constraints across sessions is useful. A coding agent that remembers project conventions is useful. A research agent that remembers which sources were already ruled out is useful. A stateless agent repeats itself.

So memory is not “put more into the prompt.”

Memory is a storage and retrieval system that decides:

  • what to keep
  • where to keep it
  • when to retrieve it
  • how much to retrieve
  • how to summarize it
  • how to invalidate it
  • how to cite it
  • how to keep poisoned content from becoming trusted instruction

That is engineering, not prompt decoration.

The memory lifecycle

Memory is easier to understand when you stop thinking of it as a database table and start thinking of it as a lifecycle.

Every useful memory goes through stages:

observe -> decide -> write -> index -> retrieve -> use -> update -> expire

The agent observes something. The runtime decides whether it is worth remembering. The memory system writes it to one or more stores. Indexing makes it retrievable. Later, retrieval brings it back. The agent uses it. New evidence may update it. Eventually it expires, gets invalidated, or gets compacted into a summary.

Most memory bugs come from skipping one of those stages.

If you skip the decide stage, the agent writes everything. The store fills with trivia:

User asked about lunch.
User said "thanks."
Assistant apologized.
Tool returned 200.

None of that is durable memory. It is transcript noise.

If you skip indexing, the memory exists but cannot be found. This is common when teams write facts into a SQL table and later bolt on vector search. The fact is stored, but the retrieval path does not know which fields matter.

If you skip update and invalidation, the memory becomes stale. Old addresses, old project decisions, old preferences, old bug diagnoses, old tool failures. The agent sounds confident because memory makes it confident. Confidence plus stale facts is worse than forgetfulness.

If you skip expiry, you create a retention problem. Not every memory should live forever. Some memory is useful for one turn. Some for one session. Some for one project. Some for one user across years. Some must be deleted because law or policy says so.

A practical memory record should carry lifecycle metadata:

@dataclass
class DurableMemory:
    id: str
    text: str
    scope: str
    source: str
    confidence: float
    importance: float
    created_at: datetime
    last_read_at: datetime | None = None
    expires_at: datetime | None = None
    valid: bool = True

That looks heavy until you debug your first memory incident. Then every field earns its place.

Scope prevents leakage. Source supports audit. Confidence helps ranking. Importance prevents critical facts from being buried. Timestamps support recency and expiry. Validity supports contradiction without deletion.

Memory writes should be gated by policy. The model can propose writes, but the runtime should decide.

def should_write_memory(text: str, scope: str, confidence: float) -> bool:
    if confidence < 0.7:
        return False
    if scope not in {"user", "session", "project", "agent"}:
        return False
    if len(text.split()) < 4:
        return False
    return True

This toy policy is not enough, but it shows the boundary. The model proposes. The runtime disposes.

In production, the write policy might reject:

  • greetings
  • one-off task details
  • facts already present
  • unsupported claims
  • tool outputs marked untrusted
  • sensitive data without consent
  • instructions found inside retrieved content
  • facts with no source citation

The read policy matters too. Memory retrieval should not dump everything relevant into the prompt. It should retrieve, filter, rank, and compress.

retrieve 50 candidates
filter by scope and permissions
rank by relevance + importance + recency
deduplicate
compress if needed
inject top records with citations

The final injection should be explicit:

Retrieved memory:
- [user:pref:42] User prefers concise direct explanations. Source: session 18 turn 4.
- [project:book:9] Manuscript lives in book_codex, not book. Source: session 21 turn 1.

Do not blend retrieved memory into the prompt as if it were system truth. Label it. Cite it. Let the model know where it came from.

That gives you a chance to recover when memory is wrong:

The retrieved memory says X, but the current user message says Y. Ask for clarification.

Without labels, the model cannot tell whether a statement came from the developer, the user, an old memory, or an untrusted webpage.

Virtual context: the MemGPT pattern

MemGPT gave the field the right analogy: operating-system virtual memory.

In an operating system, RAM is fast and limited. Disk is larger and slower. Programs behave as if they have more memory than physical RAM because the OS moves pages between RAM and disk. A page fault means the program needs something that is not currently in RAM, so the OS loads it.

Agent memory has the same shape:

OS concept Agent concept
RAM main context
disk external memory store
page fault memory tool call
kernel agent runtime
process agent loop

The model’s prompt is RAM. External memory is disk. Memory tools are syscalls. The runtime is the kernel that executes those calls and splices results back into the next turn.

This is the core MemGPT idea. You do not pretend the prompt is infinite. You give the agent tools to manage memory explicitly.

A minimal memory tool surface looks like this:

core_memory_append(section, text)
core_memory_replace(section, old, new)
archival_memory_insert(text, metadata)
archival_memory_search(query, top_k)
conversation_search(query, top_k)

The agent sees these as tools. When it realizes it needs an old fact, it calls archival_memory_search. When it learns a durable fact, it calls archival_memory_insert. When it needs to edit always-visible state, it modifies core memory.

That turns memory into part of the action loop:

user asks question
agent notices missing context
agent calls memory.search
runtime returns relevant records
agent answers using retrieved records
agent writes new durable fact if needed

The important part is not the exact API. The important part is the separation:

main context = what the model sees right now
external memory = what the model may retrieve when needed

If you keep that separation clean, you can swap the memory backend later. If you blur it, every prompt becomes a junk drawer.

Building a two-tier memory

A two-tier memory system needs a main context and an archival store.

The main context holds pinned facts and recent conversation. The archival store holds searchable facts with citations.

Here is the shape:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from datetime import datetime
from typing import Any


@dataclass
class MemoryRecord:
    id: str
    text: str
    metadata: dict[str, Any]
    created_at: datetime
    source_session: str
    source_turn: int


@dataclass
class MainContext:
    core: dict[str, str] = field(default_factory=dict)
    messages: list[dict[str, str]] = field(default_factory=list)
    max_messages: int = 20

    def append_message(self, role: str, content: str) -> None:
        self.messages.append({"role": role, "content": content})
        self.messages = self.messages[-self.max_messages:]

    def render(self) -> str:
        core_text = "\n".join(f"{k}: {v}" for k, v in self.core.items())
        turns = "\n".join(f"{m['role']}: {m['content']}" for m in self.messages)
        return f"Core memory:\n{core_text}\n\nRecent turns:\n{turns}"

A toy archival store can start with token overlap. Production uses embeddings, BM25, graph search, or a hybrid.

class ArchivalStore:
    def __init__(self) -> None:
        self.records: list[MemoryRecord] = []

    def insert(self, record: MemoryRecord) -> None:
        self.records.append(record)

    def search(self, query: str, top_k: int = 5) -> list[MemoryRecord]:
        q = set(query.lower().split())

        def score(record: MemoryRecord) -> int:
            terms = set(record.text.lower().split())
            return len(q & terms)

        ranked = sorted(self.records, key=score, reverse=True)
        return [r for r in ranked[:top_k] if score(r) > 0]

That is not a good retrieval system. It is a good teaching scaffold. It shows the control flow without hiding it behind a vector database.

The production version adds:

  • embeddings or BM25 for retrieval
  • source citations on every record
  • user/session/agent scope
  • write policies
  • deduplication
  • invalidation
  • retention rules
  • prompt-injection scanning on retrieved content
  • evaluation queries for recall quality

The citation field matters more than people expect. “The agent remembers that the user prefers quarterly reports” is not enough. You need to know which turn produced that memory. Otherwise a poisoned webpage, a misunderstood sentence, or an old preference becomes impossible to audit.

Every durable memory should answer:

Who wrote this?
When was it written?
From what source?
Who is allowed to read it?
Is it still valid?

If your memory record cannot answer those questions, it is not production memory. It is a sticky note.

Memory blocks

Flat memory stores are easy to build and hard to operate. Everything becomes a record. User preferences, agent persona, safety rules, task state, project conventions, and random conversation facts all sit in the same pile.

Memory blocks fix that by making the always-visible memory structured.

A block is a typed, persistent, editable section of core memory. It has a label, value, limit, and description.

@dataclass
class MemoryBlock:
    label: str
    value: str
    limit: int
    description: str
    version: int = 1

    def append(self, text: str) -> None:
        next_value = f"{self.value}\n{text}".strip()
        if len(next_value) > self.limit:
            raise ValueError(f"{self.label} block is over its limit")
        self.value = next_value
        self.version += 1

The classic blocks are:

Block Purpose
Human facts about the user
Persona the agent’s role, tone, and constraints
Task current objective
Project durable facts about a codebase or domain
Safety hard boundaries and escalation rules

The Human block might say:

User is Sajak. Prefers direct practical explanations. Is writing a three-volume AI engineering book. Wants Volume 2 drafted first.

The Persona block might say:

You are a practical AI engineering assistant. Be direct. Use code when it clarifies. Do not use marketing language.

The Project block might say:

Book manuscript lives in book_codex. Volume 2 chapter files use continuous numbering. Chapters should distill phase content, not copy it.

These blocks are small enough to stay in the prompt. They are important enough to be always visible. They are structured enough to edit safely.

The mistake is appending forever. A block is not a log. It is a compact current state. If a block grows without bound, it stops being core memory and becomes archival memory pretending to be core memory.

Good block systems need:

  • character or token limits
  • append and replace operations
  • version history
  • diffs
  • summaries before overflow
  • permissions for sensitive blocks
