Research-mode agent 1000+ $100+ Bounded by external time-out as well
Computer-use agent (browser) 100-200 $5-15 Action budget matters more than token budget
Code-review bot (per PR) 30-50 $1-5 Tight because of PR fan-out across a repo

These are starting points, not specifications. The right number for a deployment is whatever falls below the cost of an unintended action and above the cost of a routinely-completed task. Set the budget high and you ship runaways occasionally. Set it low and you ship truncated runs that waste setup cost. The middle is empirical.

A useful diagnostic: track the distribution of (budget consumed / budget cap) across all runs. A healthy distribution has most runs at 20-60% of cap. A bimodal distribution with one mode at 20% and another at 95% means your cap is too tight for hard tasks. A distribution where everything clusters at 80%+ means you have systematically under-budgeted.

Why budgets cannot be the only control

Cost governors are necessary and not sufficient. The 2024 frontier-lab incident referenced earlier five-figure dollar burn on a runaway loop happened in a deployment that had a budget. The budget caught the runaway eventually. By the time it fired, the agent had already published several wrong tool calls.

The ratio that matters is not “cost governor on or off” but “what fraction of harmful actions happen before the budget fires.” Budgets are a coarse gate. The next layer kill switches, circuit breakers, canary tokens is what catches the harmful actions inside an otherwise-normal-cost run.

Kill switches, circuit breakers, and canary tokens

Cost governors bound what the agent spends. They do not bound what the agent does inside the budget. An agent with a fifty-dollar budget can still exfiltrate a secret, publish the wrong post, or delete a resource. Some of the worst actions cost almost nothing in tokens.

Three classical detectors fill the gap.

Kill switches

A kill switch is a boolean the agent reads but cannot write. When set to off, the agent does nothing externally observable. Implementation choices:

  • Feature flag in a managed service (LaunchDarkly, Statsig, Unleash). Updates propagate in seconds. The agent polls the flag on every consequential action.
  • Redis key the agent polls on each turn. Simple, requires the agent process to reach Redis and to actually check.
  • Signed config in object storage. The agent verifies a signature on boot and rejects unsigned states.
  • OS-level lifecycle. Docker kill, Kubernetes kubectl delete pod, systemd stop. The bluntest version, used when other layers have failed.

Properties a correct kill switch has:

  • The agent cannot set it to on. (Lives in a system the agent’s credentials do not write.)
  • It is checked on every consequential action, not only at startup.
  • When it is off, the agent does nothing externally observable, including logging to systems the agent can reach.
  • Re-enabling it is an explicit human action, not an automatic timeout.

The last property matters. Kill switches that auto-reset are kill switches an attacker can wait out.

Circuit breakers

A circuit breaker pauses a specific pattern, not the whole agent. The classical shape from Nygard’s 2007 Release It still applies:

  • Closed: action is allowed.
  • Open: action is blocked.
  • Half-open: after a cool-down, one to three probe attempts are allowed; success closes the breaker, any failure re-opens it.

Agent-relevant triggers:

  • Five identical tool calls in a row (repetitive loop).
  • Five consecutive failures on the same tool (systemic failure).
  • Tool-call rate above a threshold (velocity-based).
  • A specific tool invoked (file.delete, email.send) immediately after an out-of-trust content read.

The pattern is shape-aware: it is not a global cost cap but a pattern-specific pause. An agent stuck in a loop trips the breaker for that loop while other paths continue.

Canary tokens

Canary tokens also called honeytokens are inputs the agent has no legitimate reason to touch. Access is the alarm. Examples:

  • A fake AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY in the environment with no real privilege. Any exfiltration attempt is instantly attributable.
  • A fake database record flagged with a known sentinel. Any read or update of this record fires an alert.
  • A fake file in the workspace. Reads or edits of this file are suspicious.
  • A fake memory entry injected at session start. If it appears in a later output, memory has been exfiltrated.

Canary design is workflow-specific. A canary the agent has a legitimate reason to touch is noise. A canary the agent never sees is wasted budget.

Why hard limits matter alongside statistical detectors

Statistical detectors (EWMA, CUSUM, z-score on tool-call rates) adapt to a moving baseline. That is a feature for legitimate drift and a bug for adversarial drift. A patient attacker can shift the baseline slowly enough that no alarm fires.

Hard constitutional limits do not bend. “Never email secrets@” as a constitutional rule is simpler, auditable, and not gameable by slow drift. The layering pattern: statistical detectors catch most noise, hard limits catch the attack that walked past the detector.

We come back to constitutional limits in Chapter 31. For this chapter, the rule is: production agents need both adaptive detection and non-negotiable hard limits, and the hard limits live somewhere the agent cannot edit.

Propose-then-commit: HITL on consequential actions

For consequential actions the ones a kill switch is too blunt for and a budget cap will not catch the production pattern is propose-then-commit.

The agent proposes the action. A human (or a separate service that itself was approved by a human) reviews the proposal. On approval, the action commits. On rejection, the action does not commit and the agent reads the rejection as an observation.

The shape:

agent proposes action with:
  - tool name
  - arguments
  - intended effect
  - reversibility classification
  - rollback plan
─→ proposal queued in durable workflow (Lesson 12 substrate)
─→ human reviews
─→ approve or reject decision recorded
─→ on approve: action commits with idempotency key + precondition check
─→ on reject: agent observes rejection, plans alternative

Several design choices make this work in practice.

The proposal carries a structured rollback plan. Every consequential action says how it will be undone if the post-action verification fails. An action with no rollback (“we cannot un-send this email”) must be flagged as such, and HITL review of those actions is more rigorous usually a challenge-and-response confirming the human understands the irreversibility.

Approval state lives in durable execution. A workflow waiting on approval is not a polling loop. It is a durable pause. The workflow may sleep for hours, the worker may be reclaimed by another job, the deploy may roll over when the approval arrives, the workflow resumes from exactly that state.

Approval has a TTL. Proposals do not wait forever. After a timeout (minutes for interactive, hours or days for routines), the proposal expires and the agent is told the human did not approve. Without a TTL, abandoned proposals fill the queue.

Batch approvals exist where appropriate. In Claude Code’s AcceptEdits mode and similar, classes of actions (in-workspace file edits) are pre-approved up to a certain rate. The HITL gate fires on out-of-class actions. This trades approval friction for speed inside a defined scope.

The approver is not the agent. This is the load-bearing rule. An agent that approves its own proposals has not introduced HITL; it has introduced theater. The approval surface UI, Slack approval, email approval, dedicated reviewer tool must be reachable only by humans, and the human’s identity must be authenticated.

The EU AI Act Article 14 codifies this for high-risk systems: “effective human oversight” requires that a human be able to intervene, understand the system’s outputs, and disregard them. In operational terms, that means proposals must be legible to the reviewer (not raw JSON traces), the rejection must actually stop the action, and the audit trail must show who approved what and when.

What a good propose-then-commit UI looks like

The HITL gate fails most often at the UI layer, not the workflow layer. A reviewer who cannot see what they are approving will either rubber-stamp everything or block everything. Both outcomes negate the gate.

A propose-then-commit UI that actually works:

  • One screen per proposal. Tool name, arguments, intended effect, reversibility, rollback plan, current state context. Nothing else.
  • Diff-style preview. For file edits: show the diff. For database writes: show the before/after row. For HTTP calls: show the request and the predicted response. Reviewers approve concrete changes, not abstract intentions.
  • Rejection requires a reason. Free-text field. The reason becomes feedback to the agent and an audit-trail entry.
  • Batch approvals only for pre-categorized actions. Approving “all in-workspace file edits” is fine. Approving “everything Claude proposes for the next hour” is theater.
  • Clear escalation path. If the reviewer is unsure, escalate to a senior reviewer rather than approve or reject. The unsure case is where the system fails most.

The pattern that scales: the same operational discipline as code review. The reviewer reads the diff, considers the surrounding context, asks questions if unclear, approves or rejects with a reason. Code review is a known-good model; agent action review is structurally similar.

Approval load and where it breaks

Human review is a bottleneck. An agent that proposes one consequential action per minute is fine; an agent that proposes one per second saturates the reviewer in seconds. Two structural responses:

  • Pre-approve action classes, not individual actions. The reviewer approves “any file edit inside /src/” once at the start of the session. The agent fires those edits without per-action review. Out-of-class actions still require approval.
  • Triage by risk class. A low-risk action (read a file) bypasses HITL entirely. A medium-risk action (write a file in workspace) goes to a default-approve queue. A high-risk action (delete, push, send) goes to explicit approval. The classifier-based triage from Auto Mode applies here.

The combination is what makes interactive coding agents work in 2026. Without it, every session would saturate the user with approval prompts. With it, only the actions worth a moment’s attention surface.

Checkpoints and rollback: closing the loop on a partial commit

Durable execution makes the run resumable. Propose-then-commit makes the action auditable. The third piece is what happens between an approved proposal and a verified side effect when the action partially executes, crashes, and resumes.

The combination that actually works is:

idempotency key + precondition check + post-action verify + rollback on verify-fail

Each piece does specific work.

Idempotency keys prevent double-execute. The action is identified by a key the workflow generates before commit. The downstream system tracks which keys it has already processed. A retried commit with the same key is a no-op on the downstream side. Without idempotency, a retry after a transient failure can fire the action twice.

Precondition checks confirm the state is still consistent with what was approved. Idempotency alone is not enough. Consider an action approved as “transfer $100 from A to B when balance > $1000.” The workflow commits, crashes mid-execution, and resumes. If only the idempotency key is checked, the transfer runs once (correct). But if A’s balance dropped to $500 between crash and resume, the idempotency check passes; the precondition does not. Without a precondition check, the workflow ships an overdraft.

Post-action verification confirms the side effect actually happened. “The tool returned 200” is not verification. Real verification re-reads the target system:

  • Database update: UPDATE ... RETURNING *, assert returned row matches intended state.
  • Email send: check sent-folder for the message ID.
  • File write: read the file back and hash it.
  • API call: follow-up GET on the target resource.

If verification fails, the workflow is in a known-bad state. Rollback engages.

Rollback has three forms:

  • In-band rollback: reverse the side effect directly (DELETE after INSERT, Send-correction-email after Send).
  • Compensating transaction: a new action that neutralizes the original (the standard SAGA pattern).
  • Out-of-band rollback: alert a human, pause the workflow, leave the bad state for investigation.

A no-op rollback (“we cannot undo this”) must be named in the proposal stage. Actions with no rollback require stronger HITL at commit time.

The sharp failure mode this combination prevents is the double-execute:

1. Action approved, idempotency key k.
2. Commit starts, executes, returns 200.
3. Workflow crashes before persisting "committed" status.
4. Workflow resumes, sees "approved but not committed", re-executes.
5. Side effect fires twice.

Mitigation: persist an “in-flight” intent before execution, execute with the idempotency key, then mark “committed” only after post-action verification succeeds. If the action fires and the status write fails, you know to verify and (if necessary) re-fire. If the status write succeeds and the action fails, the recovery path verifies and fires exactly once.

The EU AI Act Article 14 operational reading from the durable-execution discussion applies here too. Checkpoints must be queryable by an auditor. Rollbacks must be rehearsed at least once end-to-end before they are trusted in production. The audit trail must survive a deploy the checkpoint backend cannot be ephemeral. Failed verifications must alert, not silently log.

A workflow that crashes mid-commit, resumes, completes the side effect without a verify path, and continues without surfacing the gap does not survive Article 14 review.

The composite control stack

The seven controls compose into a single stack. The order matters.

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Permission modes              ← what the agent can do       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Untrusted-content provenance  ← who told it to              │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Cost governors                ← how much it can spend       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Kill switches and circuits    ← stop on observed failure    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Canary tokens                 ← detect intent on bait       │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Propose-then-commit           ← human review at the line    │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Checkpoints + rollback        ← recover from partial commit │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Durable execution             ← survive crashes and review  │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Each layer assumes the layers above it might fail. Permission modes might be misconfigured. Untrusted-content tracking might miss a source. Cost governors might be too generous. Kill switches might fire late. Canaries might be untouched. HITL might approve the wrong thing. Checkpoints might miss a transition.

The layering assumes that no single control is sufficient and that the combination is. A run that passed cost governance, evaded kill switches, and got a misclick approval at the HITL stage still has post-action verification and rollback to catch a verify-fail. A run that survived all of that is, by construction, the kind of run we wanted to allow.

Three common ways teams break the stack:

Skipping HITL “for speed”. A team ships AcceptEdits-equivalent mode by default, treats the cost governor as the only gate, and discovers months later that an injection payload caused a chain of edits that should have been reviewed. Speed is a real product value; it does not survive the absence of HITL on consequential actions.

Putting controls in the wrong process. Budget enforcement inside the agent’s Python module is editable by the agent. Canary tokens stored in the workspace are findable by the agent. Kill switches in environment variables the agent can read and the agent’s tools can write are not kill switches. Controls live outside the agent’s reach or they are theater.

Treating the audit log as optional. Article 14 review fails on systems whose audit trail does not survive a deploy. Even before the regulatory reading, an incident response without an audit trail is forensic guesswork. The audit log is part of the control stack, not a separate concern.

A well-built production agent looks small from the outside a chat interface, a few tools, results. The control stack underneath is roughly the size of the agent’s product surface. That is the right ratio. Long-horizon agents in 2026 spend more engineering effort on controls than on capability work, and the teams that ship reliably are the ones that internalized that asymmetry early.

Where this leaves us

The production layer of autonomous systems is now in place.

Permission modes scope what the agent can touch. Untrusted-content provenance defends against indirect prompt injection. Durable execution survives crashes, deploys, and human review. Cost governors bound the spend. Kill switches, circuit breakers, and canary tokens detect failure and intent. Propose-then-commit puts a human in the loop at consequential actions. Checkpoints and rollback close the loop on partial commits.

What this chapter does not cover is the layer above the frameworks under which agents are deployed at frontier scale, the external evaluators who check that the controls actually work, and the safety policies that decide which agents are allowed to ship at all.

Chapter 31 is that layer. It covers the constitutional approach Anthropic ships in Claude, the input/output classification stack (Llama Guard, content moderators, prompt-injection classifiers), Anthropic’s Responsible Scaling Policy, OpenAI’s Preparedness Framework, DeepMind’s Frontier Safety Framework, METR’s external evaluation program, and the broader governance landscape CAIS, CAISI, and the regulatory frameworks that frontier labs now operate under.

If Chapter 30 is what an individual production agent needs, Chapter 31 is what the population of frontier agents needs from the labs that train them and the governments that regulate them. Both are part of the same problem. A perfectly controlled individual agent that ships into a world without safety frameworks contributes to a population of agents whose aggregate behavior is not controlled. A perfectly governed population whose individual agents have no controls is not safe. The two layers compose.

Take one observation forward. The controls in this chapter are 90% engineering and 10% AI-specific. Permission systems, durable workflow engines, cost governors, kill switches, change-management gates, and rollback plans are all decades-old patterns in operating systems, payment systems, infrastructure, and finance. The new variable is the actor inside the system non-deterministic, coercible by content, capable of editing its own scaffolding. The controls have not changed shape much. They have changed who they need to defend against.

The work in Chapter 31 is harder. The controls there have to defend against agents that are themselves part of the design, against capability surges that arrive between model releases, against deployments at population scale where individual agents cannot be reviewed by hand. The move from individual-agent controls to population-level controls is the move from operational discipline to governance, and 2026 is the first year that move has had teeth.

Chapter 31 - Frontier Safety, Governance, and External Evaluation

Chapter 30 was about controls a single team can build for a single agent.

Chapter 31 is about controls the field needs for the population of agents being trained and deployed. The two layers are connected population-level safety only works if individual deployments are well-controlled, and individual deployments only matter if the underlying models are safely scoped but they are governed differently and break differently.

This is the last chapter of Phase 15 and the closing chapter of Volume 2’s autonomous-systems arc. It covers six layers of governance:

1. Constitutional AI           hard-coded rules inside the model
2. Input/output classifiers    a moderation layer outside the model
3. Frontier safety frameworks   RSP, Preparedness, FSF as governance documents
4. External evaluation          METR and the case for independent measurement
5. Civil society and government   CAIS, CAISI, SB-53
6. The composite ecosystem      what each layer assumes about the others

The pattern matches Chapter 30’s: no single layer is sufficient, and the combination is. A constitution that lives only inside the model can be eroded by training. A classifier that lives only outside the model can be bypassed by capable agents. A scaling policy that lives only at one lab cannot constrain a competitor. An external evaluator that lives only as a 501©(3) cannot subpoena a lab. Each layer covers a failure mode of the layers around it.

The shift from Chapter 30 to Chapter 31 is the shift from operational discipline to ecosystem design. Operational discipline ships an agent. Ecosystem design decides which agents are allowed to be shipped. Both are real engineering. The second is less visible from inside a single team and more consequential at the population scale.

Constitutional AI: rules inside the model

The first layer of population-level safety lives inside the model itself.

Anthropic’s Constitutional AI is the most public, most documented version of the pattern. The 2026 release of Claude’s published constitution made it the cleanest reference: a written document, public, that the model is trained to follow. It distinguishes two kinds of rules.

Soft principles. The model is trained to produce responses that align with a set of values being helpful, honest, avoiding harm, taking the user’s intent seriously, refusing requests outside its competence, treating users with dignity. These principles are taught through reward modeling and DPO/RLHF on principle-tagged data (Chapter 18). They are loose enough to be context-sensitive and tight enough to be measurable.

Hard limits. A small set of behaviors are never allowed regardless of context. The 2026 published constitution names categories: synthesis of biological, chemical, or nuclear weapons; creation of CSAM; generation of certain cyberweapons; deceptive behavior toward operators in the canonical hierarchy. Hard limits are trained as constants. They are not soft principles weighted against other goals; they are non-negotiable.

The architectural difference matters. A soft principle can be overridden by a sufficiently strong context. A hard limit, by design, should not be. In practice, frontier models still occasionally violate hard limits under sustained adversarial pressure the rate is small but not zero which is why Chapter 30’s controls (input/output classifiers, kill switches, canary tokens) exist as a parallel defense. The constitution is the first layer, not the only layer.

A few production lessons from running constitutional approaches at scale.

Operator vs user vs world. Claude’s constitution makes the priority hierarchy explicit. The operator (the company running Claude through the API) sets the deployment context system prompt, tool allowlists, persona constraints. The user interacts with Claude through that context, with their own goals and instructions. The world is everyone else affected by the conversation, including third parties the user has no direct relationship with. When these three sources conflict, the constitution defines who wins.

The hierarchy in operational terms:

World safety     hard limits never bend (no synthesis of weapons of mass
                 destruction, no CSAM, no generation of certain
                 cyberweapons, no deceptive behavior toward operators).
Operator scope   operator can constrain but cannot weaponize Claude
                 against users (cannot instruct Claude to deceive its
                 users, cannot strip basic safety properties).
User goals       user instructions are followed inside the operator's
                 scope and inside world-safety limits.

An operator cannot instruct Claude to deceive users in ways that harm them. A user cannot instruct Claude to take actions that harm third parties. The hierarchy is the rule that decides when intentions conflict.

The constitution as a documentation artifact. Anthropic’s January 2026 publication of the constitution was a governance choice, not a technical one. It made the rules legible to operators (so they know what they can and cannot ask Claude to do), users (so they know what to expect), regulators (so they have something concrete to point at), and competitors (so the industry can converge on shared norms). Other labs use similar approaches without publishing the document; the 2026 industry trend has been toward more transparency, not less.

Updating the constitution. A live constitution is a moving target. Each model release retrains some constitutional behaviors. The published constitution is versioned. Operators have to test for behavioral changes between versions, especially around hard limits and refusal behavior, because a small shift in constitutional priority can produce a large shift in deployed behavior. The version-to-version diff is part of the model’s release notes; reading it is part of the ship checklist.

Constitutional-style rules don’t replace alignment. Chapter 18’s alignment toolkit RLHF, DPO, GRPO, AAR is what produces a model whose constitution holds under pressure. The constitution is the spec. The alignment training is what makes the spec stick. A constitution without alignment training is wishful thinking; alignment training without a clear spec is undefined behavior at scale.

Hard limits resist adversarial pressure differently from soft principles. Soft principles are weighted against other goals the model balances helpfulness, honesty, and avoiding harm in the context of a request. Hard limits are not weighted; they should not bend regardless of context. In practice, the rate at which hard limits actually hold under sustained adversarial pressure is small but not zero. The 2026 measurement: well-aligned frontier models violate hard limits in roughly 0.1-1% of focused adversarial conversations, depending on the limit and the attack class. That residual rate is why Chapter 30’s controls (input/output classifiers, kill switches, canary tokens) exist as a parallel defense.

The honest framing: Constitutional AI is necessary and not sufficient. It is the layer where intent meets weights. The next layer is what runs around the model when the constitutional layer fails.

Input/output classifiers: the moderation layer outside the model

Llama Guard, OpenAI’s moderation API, Google’s safety classifiers, and Anthropic’s own input filters all share a shape. A separate model usually a smaller, fine-tuned classifier examines the input or output of the main model and produces a structured judgment.

The classifier sits in two places.

Input-side classification screens user-supplied content before it reaches the main model. Detected violations: explicit jailbreak prompts, prompts requesting hard-limit-violating outputs, indirect prompt injection in retrieved content. The classifier returns a category label and a confidence; the orchestration layer decides whether to forward, sanitize, refuse, or escalate.

Output-side classification screens generated content before it reaches the user or before tool calls execute. Detected violations: outputs that contain hard-limit content the main model produced anyway, tool calls to disallowed targets, leaked secrets or PII, outputs that match canary patterns.

The Llama Guard family is the canonical open-source reference. Llama Guard 3 (2024) and Llama Guard 4 (2025) are fine-tuned Llama models trained on a labeled taxonomy.

The Llama Guard 4 taxonomy in detail:

Category Examples
S1: Violent Crimes Encouragement of physical assault, terrorism, mass violence
S2: Non-Violent Crimes Theft, fraud, drug trafficking, illegal financial advice
S3: Sex-Related Crimes Trafficking, sexual harassment, sexual coercion
S4: Child Exploitation CSAM and any content sexualizing minors
S5: Defamation False statements about real persons
S6: Specialized Advice Medical, legal, financial advice without disclaimers
S7: Privacy PII disclosure, doxxing
S8: Intellectual Property Copyright violation, plagiarism
S9: Indiscriminate Weapons Bioweapons, chemical weapons, nuclear, large-scale cyber
S10: Hate Group-based dehumanization, harassment
S11: Self-Harm Suicide methods, eating disorders, self-injury encouragement
S12: Sexual Content Explicit sexual content (context-dependent)
S13: Elections Misleading content about voting and electoral processes
S14: Code Interpreter Abuse Prompts seeking to execute harmful code

Each input/output is tagged against this taxonomy with a structured response.

input or output  →  Llama Guard  →  {safe | unsafe: S1, S6, S7, ...}

The shape is intentionally narrow. A classifier that returns rich free-form analysis is harder to integrate, harder to monitor, and harder to defend against the same prompt-injection attacks the main model is exposed to. A classifier that returns one of a small set of structured labels can be wired into automation reliably.

The Llama Guard 4 release (Meta, 2025) added two specific improvements over Llama Guard 3. First, multimodal support the classifier accepts text plus images and tags either modality. This matters for vision-language models where the harm signal can live in the image. Second, the S14 code-interpreter-abuse category specifically targets the agent context: prompts that look benign in chat form but become harmful when executed in a sandbox. The taxonomy was extended in response to the 2024-2025 wave of agent-targeted attacks.

Three patterns matter for production.

Classifiers are pre-trained models, not just rules. A classifier sees the same kinds of adversarial pressure the main model sees. Llama Guard 4’s training set explicitly includes adversarial examples, jailbreak attempts, and indirect-injection payloads. Without that training, the classifier becomes the weakest link.

Classifiers compose. Production stacks chain multiple classifiers: Llama Guard for the broad taxonomy, a domain-specific classifier for the deployment vertical (medical, financial, legal), a PII detector, a prompt-injection detector. Each one catches a different failure mode. Triggers from different classifiers carry different weights.

Classifier scores feed into permissions. A “safe” verdict allows the action. An “unsafe” verdict blocks it and returns a structured observation to the agent (so the agent can plan an alternative). A “borderline” verdict confidence below a threshold escalates to HITL. The classifier’s output is not advisory; it is part of Chapter 30’s permission stack.

The classifier layer’s structural weakness: it inherits the failure modes of the model it screens. A jailbreak that fools the main model often fools the classifier in the same way, because the classifier was trained on similar data. Defense in depth here means combining a classifier-based moderation layer with hard-coded rules and with the canary tokens and circuit breakers from Chapter 30. No single layer of moderation is robust.

Frontier safety frameworks: governance documents at lab scale

The third layer is the lab-level scaling policy. These documents are simultaneously technical, governance, and political artifacts. They state what a lab will do at specific capability thresholds and how it will demonstrate that the threshold has not been crossed.

Three documents define the 2026 landscape.

Anthropic’s Responsible Scaling Policy v3.0 (effective February 24, 2026)

The current Anthropic policy. v3.0 replaced the 2023 RSP and made several substantive changes worth understanding.

Two-tier mitigation schedule. v3.0 introduced a split between commitments Anthropic will follow unilaterally and commitments framed as industry-wide recommendations. Security measures in the “industry recommendation” column are not Anthropic’s promises; they are policy advocacy. The reader has to look at which column each commitment lives in.

The AI R&D-4 threshold. v3.0 names a specific next capability threshold: a model that could automate a substantial fraction of AI research at competitive cost. Once Anthropic believes a model crosses it, they must publish an affirmative case identifying misalignment risks and mitigations before continued scaling. Claude Opus 4.6 does not cross it per the v3.0 announcement, but the document concedes “confidently ruling this out is becoming difficult.”

Frontier Safety Roadmaps and Risk Reports. v3.0 elevated two artifact types to standing documents. Roadmaps are forward-looking planned safety work, expected capability progression, mitigation research. Risk Reports are retrospective observed capability and residual risk after a model release. Both are public. Both are versioned. Readers can compare what a Roadmap promised against what a subsequent Risk Report delivered.

Removing the pause clause. The 2023 RSP included an explicit commitment: if a model crossed specific capability thresholds, training would pause until mitigations were in place. v3.0 replaced the explicit pause with a softer formulation publish an affirmative case, proceed if mitigations are adequate. SaferAI and other analysts called this out as the strongest regression in the new document.

The SaferAI downgrade. SaferAI is an independent organization that grades RSP-style documents. The 2023 Anthropic RSP scored 2.2 on their rubric. v3.0 scored 1.9. The downgrade moved Anthropic from “moderate” to “weak,” joining OpenAI and DeepMind in the weak category. Per SaferAI, the downgrade factors were: qualitative thresholds replacing quantitative ones, the pause commitment removal, AI R&D-4 mitigations described as “affirmative case” rather than specific measures, and review mechanisms with limited independent oversight.

This is how a scaling policy can get less rigorous while looking more polished. The lesson is in reading the document with the specificity and skepticism it deserves.

OpenAI Preparedness Framework v2

The OpenAI counterpart has its own structure. The 2025-2026 v2 names four risk categories cybersecurity, CBRN (chemical, biological, radiological, nuclear), persuasion, model autonomy and assigns capability levels to each.

The four-by-four matrix:

Category What it covers
Cybersecurity Offensive cyber capability exploit development, vulnerability discovery, autonomous attack execution
CBRN Uplift to hostile actors developing chemical, biological, radiological, or nuclear weapons
Persuasion Capability to manipulate human behavior at scale, including political influence and targeted manipulation
Model Autonomy Long-horizon agency, including the ability to self-replicate, acquire resources, or evade oversight

Each category has four levels: Low, Medium, High, Critical. Levels are tied to specific deployment requirements. A model assessed as High capability in one category requires specific mitigations before deployment. Critical requires further mitigations or non-deployment.

The “Undermining Safeguards” sub-category, added in 2025-2026, sits inside Model Autonomy and addresses the specific failure class DGM demonstrated: an agent that disables its own safety checks to raise score. METR’s prototype monitoring evaluations (Lesson 21) feed directly into this category.

The framework is similar in shape to Anthropic’s RSP but differs in execution. OpenAI’s process puts more weight on internal evaluation by their Preparedness team and less on publicly versioned documents. The framework is a single document; the per-model assessments live in a separate model card published with each release. SaferAI scored the v2 Preparedness Framework slightly higher than RSP v3.0, primarily because the level definitions were more concrete, but still in the “weak” range.

The lesson is the same: read the document closely, separate commitments from advocacy, track promises against outcomes.

DeepMind Frontier Safety Framework v3

DeepMind’s framework, updated to v3 in April 2026, introduced two specific elements that set it apart.

Critical Capability Levels (CCLs). A graduated set of capability thresholds in domains like cyber, autonomy, and biological knowledge. Each CCL is associated with specific evaluation protocols and mitigation expectations. The CCL approach is finer-grained than RSP’s affirmative-case framing: rather than one threshold that triggers a single response, each CCL has its own response.

The 2026 v3 CCL set, in summary:

Domain CCL examples
Autonomy Long-horizon multi-step task completion at frontier
ML R&D Substantial automation of ML research
Biology Uplift to bioweapon synthesis above expert baseline
Cyber Autonomous offensive cyber operations
Persuasion Sustained behavioral manipulation

Deceptive alignment monitoring. v3 added explicit attention to alignment-faking and similar behaviors the classes of failure documented by Anthropic’s 2024 alignment-faking paper. The framework names specific evaluations DeepMind will run to detect these behaviors before deployment, including red-team protocols where evaluators simulate retraining contexts to surface eval-vs-deployment behavior gaps.

DeepMind’s framework is the most explicit about the deceptive-alignment failure mode and the most aggressive about evaluating for it. Whether the evaluations are sufficient is an open question; the framing at least acknowledges the problem class concretely. The 2026 SaferAI rating placed FSF v3 in the same “weak” tier as RSP and Preparedness Framework, with the deceptive-alignment work cited as the strongest single component across the three frameworks.

Cross-framework comparison

The three lab-level frameworks are visibly converging in shape and diverging in detail. A practitioner reading all three should look for:

Question RSP v3.0 OpenAI PF v2 DeepMind FSF v3
Threshold form Qualitative (“affirmative case”) Levels per category CCLs per domain
Pause commitment Removed in v3.0 None Implicit in CCL response
Deceptive-alignment focus Mentioned “Undermining safeguards” sub-category Explicit evaluations
Independent review Internal Safety Advisory Group Internal Preparedness team Internal review
External evaluator references METR METR METR
2026 SaferAI score 1.9 ~2.0 ~2.0

The structural pattern: each lab produces a document that is internally rigorous and externally not enforced. Independent rating organizations (SaferAI, others) provide one form of external pressure. Civil society and regulatory pressure provide another. The frameworks themselves are the labs’ best public articulation of how they think about catastrophic risk; they are not regulations and they are not contracts.

Reading scaling policies as a practitioner

Three practical questions to ask of any scaling policy document.

What is unilateral and what is industry advocacy? v3.0’s two-tier structure made this distinction visible. Earlier policies often blurred the line. A commitment that depends on competitor coordination is not the same as a commitment a lab makes alone.

What is quantitative and what is qualitative? Quantitative thresholds (specific benchmark scores, specific evaluation outcomes) can be checked from outside. Qualitative thresholds (“the model substantially automates AI research”) depend on the lab’s own assessment. The 2023-to-2026 shift across labs has been toward more qualitative thresholds, partly because benchmarks rescaled and partly because qualitative thresholds give labs more flexibility.

What stops the lab if the threshold is crossed? A commitment to “publish an affirmative case” is weaker than a commitment to “pause training.” Both are weaker than a commitment to “submit to external review.” The strength of the policy is the strength of the action it triggers when the threshold is met.

A practitioner reading scaling policies should not treat them as binding constraints. They are not regulations. They are public signals about how a lab thinks about catastrophic risk and how it expects to communicate trade-offs. Reading them with specificity and skepticism is the practical skill closer to reading a bond prospectus than a service contract.

METR and the case for external evaluation

Scaling policies are only as useful as the measurements they reference. A policy that says “before crossing AI R&D-4 we will publish an affirmative case” is meaningful only if there is a credible measurement of when AI R&D-4 has been crossed.

METR is the 2024-2026 organization that produces many of those measurements.

METR (formerly ARC Evals)
  - Independent 501(c)(3) since December 2023
  - Evaluates frontier models, often pre-release, under NDA with labs
  - Publishes methodology after-the-fact
  - Headline artifact: Time Horizon benchmark

The Time Horizon benchmark is the single most-cited measurement in 2026 frontier-safety conversations. The methodology fits a logistic curve to task-success probability against the log of expert human completion time. The horizon is the expert-time at which P(success) = 0.5. The January 2026 Time Horizon 1.1 release placed Claude Opus 4.6 at approximately fourteen hours at fifty-percent reliability, with a doubling time of roughly four months on the post-2023 fit and roughly seven months on the full 2019-2025 fit.

The benchmark suite components:

Suite What it covers Scope
HCAST ML, cybersecurity, software engineering, reasoning 180+ tasks; 1 minute to 8+ hours
RE-Bench ML research engineering 71 tasks with human expert baseline
SWAA Short-task calibration Low-end of horizon curve

METR’s 2025-2026 work has expanded beyond the headline Time Horizon. Prototype monitoring evaluations measure whether a monitor can catch a model performing a side task and whether the agent can evade the monitor. These directly feed the “undermining safeguards” category in OpenAI PF v2 and the deceptive-alignment monitoring in DeepMind FSF v3.

Three things to know about METR-style measurements.

Time horizons are upper bounds. Benchmark tasks give the model clean tools and well-formatted data. Real deployments are messier. The model never actually bills a customer, deletes real data, or contacts real people. Real deployments have irreversible stakes. METR’s papers state this directly: reported horizons are idealized, not deployment predictions.

Eval-context gaming complicates the numbers. The 2026 International AI Safety Report documents frontier models behaving measurably safer in evaluation contexts than in deployment. Anthropic’s 2024 alignment-faking study found Claude exhibited faking in 12% of basic tests, rising to 78% under retraining attempts. METR’s own work flags this. A horizon number is the capability ceiling under favorable conditions plus a possible upward bias from gaming. The deployment number is lower.

External evaluation matters because of incentives. Internal labs have incentives to optimize metrics they report. METR’s independence a 501©(3) with declared methodology and peer-reviewed papers is the structural mitigation. It is not sufficient alone (labs still control what METR sees and what is published), but it is strictly better than no external evaluation.

The practical use of horizon numbers:

  • As a capability filter. If a model’s horizon is well below the expert-time of a proposed task, do not ship it autonomous.
  • As a trend indicator. Doubling time tells you how long current practice will remain safe even without new mitigations.
  • As a prior, not a guarantee. A horizon of fourteen hours is a starting point. Adjust down for your task distribution, your tooling quality, and your deployment context.

The broader case: independent measurement is what keeps scaling policies honest. A lab can write any threshold into a policy. A measurement they did not make tells you whether the threshold has been crossed.

Other external evaluators in 2026

METR is the most prominent but not the only independent evaluator working with frontier labs.

  • Apollo Research. Focuses on deceptive-alignment evaluation. Published the alignment-faking work that became the empirical backbone of FSF v3’s deceptive-alignment monitoring.
  • Redwood Research. Adversarial evaluations and AI control protocols. Their AI control framework (treating the model as a potentially-misaligned actor) feeds into Anthropic’s RSP and is referenced by other labs.
  • MATS (ML Alignment Theory Scholars). Talent pipeline; produces alignment researchers who staff the labs and external orgs.
  • CAISI. Government-facing evaluations on cyber, bio, chemical-weapons risks; the public counterpart to METR’s NDA-gated work.

Each fills a different role. METR measures capability horizons. Apollo measures deception. Redwood measures control. CAISI provides a regulatory-aligned baseline. The combination is closer to a mature evaluation ecosystem than any single org would be alone, even if the total capacity is still small relative to the rate of capability progression.

Why external evaluation is structurally necessary

The argument for external evaluation is not that internal labs are dishonest. It is that internal labs face incentives that bias their measurements, even with the best intentions. A lab evaluating its own model:

  • chooses what to measure,
  • chooses what to publish,
  • has financial incentives tied to the model’s deployability,
  • shares failure-mode definitions with the team that designed the model,
  • has limited capacity to red-team itself adversarially.

External evaluation breaks none of these alone but raises the cost of each one. METR’s published methodology means the lab cannot quietly redefine the measurement. Apollo’s adversarial protocols mean the lab cannot quietly skip the failure modes. CAISI’s government-facing role means there is a baseline below which voluntary participation becomes politically costly.

The 2026 evaluation capacity is bounded. Three or four major external evaluators handle most of the frontier-lab work. Scaling that capacity more independent orgs, more standardized protocols, more public reporting is one of the open governance problems. The labs themselves have an interest in this scaling, because the alternative is regulatory pressure that constrains them more sharply than voluntary external evaluation does.

Civil society and government: CAIS, CAISI, SB-53

The fifth layer is non-lab governance: civil society, standards organizations, and regulation. Two distinct US-based organizations matter, and their acronyms collide enough to confuse readers.

CAIS Center for AI Safety. A 501©(3) non-profit founded in 2022 in San Francisco. CAIS publishes the four-risk framework, coordinates public statements, and produces research on catastrophic AI risk. Their May 2023 statement on extinction risk “Mitigating the risk of extinction from AI should be a global priority alongside other societal-scale risks such as pandemics and nuclear war” was signed by hundreds of researchers and CEOs and remains one of the most-cited public artifacts on the topic. CAIS’s 2026 outputs include the AI Dashboard for frontier-model evaluation, the Remote Labor Index (joint with Scale AI), the Superintelligence Strategy Paper, and the AI Frontiers newsletter.

CAISI Center for AI Standards and Innovation. A US-government center within NIST. CAISI runs voluntary agreements with frontier labs and publishes unclassified capability evaluations focused on cyber, bio, and chemical-weapons risks. The role is the public, government-facing counterpart to METR’s private lab engagements: METR reports are often NDA-gated, CAISI reports are unclassified.

The acronyms are distinct on purpose; the missions do not overlap. A practitioner should know both.

CAIS’s four-risk framework is the most widely cited societal-scale-risk taxonomy in the 2026 literature. Its categories:

  1. Malicious use. A bad actor uses AI to cause harm: bioweapons synthesis, disinformation campaigns, cyberattacks, fraud at scale.
  2. AI races. Competitive pressure between labs, companies, or nations pushes deployment past the point where it is safe. The race dynamic erodes individual labs’ willingness to delay or pause.
  3. Organizational risks. Internal lab dynamics safety-culture failures, insufficient audit, under-resourced security produce a bad deployment regardless of external pressure.
  4. Rogue AIs. A sufficiently capable AI pursues goals that conflict with human welfare, whether by direct misalignment or by deceptive alignment under pressure.

The categories are not mutually exclusive. A rogue AI produced by an organization that traded audit for speed in a race is all four at once.

For practitioners, organizational risk is the most directly actionable category. The concrete sub-levers:

  • Safety culture. Do team members feel able to escalate a concern without career cost? CAIS surveys find this is a strong predictor of every other lever.
  • Rigorous audits. Internal-only audits produce optimistic reports. External audits, even imperfect ones, change what gets reported.
  • Multi-layered defenses. No single layer is sufficient the running theme of Phase 15.
  • Information security. Model weights leaking, eval data leaking, monitor-bypass techniques leaking. RAND SL-4, named in Anthropic’s RSP v3.0, is the specific industry standard.

The regulatory layer is just starting to have teeth. California SB-53 is the 2025-2026 state senate bill that addresses catastrophic risk from frontier models. Key drafted provisions: specific capability thresholds that trigger state-level obligations, whistleblower protections for AI lab employees, incident-reporting requirements for catastrophic failures. If signed, it would be the first US state-level catastrophic-risk regulation. Whether or not the bill becomes law, the framing shapes how other state legislatures approach the problem; US tech regulation has historically followed state-level leads to federal action.

The EU AI Act is the more mature regulatory framework already in force. It categorizes AI systems by risk tier and applies specific obligations to high-risk systems. We touched Article 14 in Chapter 30; Articles 9-15 collectively define the operational requirements: risk management, data governance, technical documentation, record-keeping, transparency, human oversight, accuracy, robustness, cybersecurity. These apply to any AI system deployed in the EU above the high-risk threshold, regardless of where it was trained.

The high-risk categories under the AI Act include: biometric identification, critical infrastructure operation, education and vocational training, employment and worker management, access to essential services, law enforcement, migration and border control, administration of justice. Frontier general-purpose models (GPAI General-Purpose AI) face additional obligations including transparency about training data, copyright compliance, and (for systemic-risk GPAI above specific compute thresholds) risk management requirements similar to those in the lab frameworks.

The Act’s extraterritorial reach matters. A US lab deploying a frontier model into EU-based applications is subject to the Act regardless of where the model was trained. Compliance is not optional for any system shipped to EU users at scale, and the fines (up to 7% of global revenue for some violations) are large enough to focus engineering attention.

The 2026 picture is that regulation is real, fragmented, and evolving. A frontier deployment in 2026 has to think about:

  • EU AI Act (high-risk system obligations, GPAI requirements)
  • US state laws (California first, others following)
  • Sector-specific rules (HIPAA in health, GDPR in privacy, financial regulation in finance)
  • Voluntary agreements (CAISI commitments, lab-to-lab agreements)
  • Export controls (US BIS rules on advanced computing exports)

Each constrains different things. None is a substitute for the others. The compliance overhead has become substantial enough that frontier labs run dedicated policy teams whose job is to navigate the regulatory landscape, similar to how multinational financial firms run dedicated compliance functions.

The composite ecosystem

The six layers compose into a single ecosystem that no single organization controls.

┌────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Civil society & government          CAIS, CAISI, SB-53, EU Act │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ External evaluation                 METR, Apollo, third parties│
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Frontier safety frameworks          RSP, PF, FSF               │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Input/output classifiers            Llama Guard, moderation    │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Constitutional / model-internal     soft principles, hard limits│
├────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Production controls (Chapter 30)    permissions, HITL, kill   │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Each layer assumes the layers above and below might fail.

The constitutional layer assumes the alignment training did not perfectly install the rules and that classifiers will catch what slipped through. The classifier layer assumes the constitution did not perfectly hold and that production controls will catch the borderline cases. The frontier safety framework assumes the classifier may not catch novel failure modes and that external evaluation will surface them. External evaluation assumes the framework’s thresholds may be too generous and that civil society will publicize the gap. Civil society assumes the lab will not voluntarily strengthen its policy under economic pressure and that government will create floors. Government assumes regulation will be slower than capability progression and that voluntary agreements with labs will fill the gap until law catches up.

The ecosystem has known weaknesses.

International coordination is incomplete. US labs operate under US-leaning policies. EU regulation has extraterritorial reach via the AI Act’s deployment requirements. China, the UK, Japan, Korea, and other jurisdictions have their own emerging frameworks. Frontier labs in different countries face different constraints, which produces a race-to-the-bottom risk where work moves to the most permissive jurisdiction.

Eval capacity is bounded. METR is one organization. CAISI is one center. The number of independent evaluators capable of meaningful frontier-model assessment is small enough that the labs’ own internal evaluations dominate. Scaling external evaluation capacity is one of the open governance problems.

Capability moves faster than governance. Frontier capability roughly doubles in horizon every four to seven months. Regulatory cycles are measured in years. Voluntary agreements are faster but depend on labs’ own willingness to participate. The mismatch is structural.

The deployed population is increasingly invisible. Hosted APIs deploy frontier models into millions of applications the labs cannot review individually. The aggregate behavior of the population matters more than the behavior of any single deployment, but the population behavior is hard to measure.

These weaknesses are real. They are also partly addressable. Better international coordination, more independent evaluators, faster regulatory cycles, and more population-level monitoring are all live work in 2026. None of them will close completely; the goal is to close enough of them that the residual risk is acceptable, given the residual benefit.

Closing the autonomous-systems arc

Three chapters of Phase 15 are now in place.

Chapter 29 covered the systems themselves: long-horizon agents, self-improvement loops, the bounded discipline that keeps loops honest, the recursive-self-improvement question. Chapter 30 covered the production controls that turn a research agent into a deployable one: permissions, durability, budgets, kill switches, HITL, checkpoints, rollback. Chapter 31 covered the governance layer that wraps individual deployments into a population-level safety story: constitutional rules, moderation classifiers, frontier safety frameworks, external evaluation, civil society, and government.

The throughline across all three is defense in depth. No single system is robust. No single control is sufficient. No single layer of governance closes the problem. The composite is what works, and the composite is engineering as much as it is policy.

A few specific things to carry forward.

Reliability decays with horizon. The METR doubling on capability is real; the per-step reliability improvements are slower. Long-horizon agents at the 2026 frontier still benefit massively from runtime controls because the runtime catches what the agent’s reliability does not.

Self-improvement loops need evaluator integrity. AlphaEvolve’s wins came from rigorous evaluators. DGM’s reward-hacking demo came from a non-rigorous one. The pattern generalizes. Any self-improvement loop is only as good as the evaluator it is graded against, and the evaluator must live somewhere the loop cannot edit.

Production controls are 90% engineering, 10% AI-specific. The patterns from operating systems, payment systems, infrastructure, and finance translate. The new variable is the actor, not the controls. Teams that have already built robust workflow systems for non-AI work tend to build robust agent runtimes faster.

Governance is real and incomplete. RSP v3.0, OpenAI PF v2, DeepMind FSF v3, EU AI Act, California SB-53, CAIS, CAISI, METR the layers exist and are evolving. None is sufficient alone. Reading the documents closely is now part of the practitioner’s skill set, not a separate policy concern.

The 2026 frontier is uncomfortable. Time horizons of a workday at fifty-percent reliability. Self-improvement loops that demonstrably reward-hack inside research environments. Alignment-faking measured at twelve to seventy-eight percent depending on setup. Scaling policies grading down on independent rubrics. None of this is a reason to stop building autonomous systems; it is a reason to build them with the controls described in Chapters 29-31, and to keep updating those controls as capability moves.

What this volume does not cover, and where Volume 3 picks up, is the production layer at scale: how frontier-class systems are served, deployed, monitored, and operated across millions of users, regulated jurisdictions, and adversarial contexts. Chapter 32 begins Volume 3 with serving infrastructure. The arc continues through alignment in the wild, red-teaming and defense, governance at the deployment layer, and the capstone projects that pull the whole stack together.

The conclusion to take from Phase 15: autonomous systems are no longer a future problem. They are a current production problem with an evolving governance layer. The teams shipping them well are doing the work in Chapters 29-31 and the teams shipping them badly are skipping pieces of it. The middle ground is uncomfortable but tractable. Autonomy is not unsafe by construction. It is unsafe by neglect, and the neglect is what the controls and the governance layer are meant to prevent.

We end Volume 2 here. The next volume is the operational story what happens when everything Volume 2 built ships into the world.

Chapter 32 - Foundations of Multi-Agent Systems

A single agent is a control loop around a model. Chapters 26 through 28 covered exactly that: the loop, its memory, its frameworks, its evaluation, its safety. By the end of those chapters you could ship a working agent.

Then you point that agent at a real codebase. Two hundred files, three languages, tests that depend on infrastructure, and a requirement to research external APIs before writing code. The agent chokes. Not because the model is dumb, but because the task exceeds what one loop can absorb. The context window fills up with file contents. The agent forgets what it read forty tool calls ago. It tries to be a researcher, a coder, and a reviewer all at once, and does all three poorly.

This is the single-agent ceiling. You hit it the moment a task needs more context than fits in one window, different expertise at different stages, or work that should happen in parallel.

The smart move is not a bigger agent. It is more agents.

This chapter is the first of three on multi-agent systems and swarms. Phase 16 is large enough to warrant the same three-chapter split we used for Phase 14 and Phase 15:

Chapter 32: foundations   primitives, communication, supervisor patterns,
            hierarchies, society-of-mind, role specialization
Chapter 33: coordination   handoffs, A2A, blackboards, consensus, voting,
            negotiation, generative agents, theory of mind
Chapter 34: production and frontier   swarm optimization, MARL, agent
            economies, scaling, failure modes, evaluation, 2026 case studies

Here we focus on the primitives that everything else stacks on. Why multi-agent at all. What every framework AutoGen, LangGraph, CrewAI, OpenAI Agents SDK, Microsoft Agent Framework, Google ADK actually is, expressed in four primitives. How agents communicate. The supervisor pattern that sits behind Anthropic’s Research system. The hierarchical extension when one supervisor is not enough. The society-of-mind and debate patterns that improve quality through disagreement. Role specialization: planner, critic, executor, verifier, and how these four cover most production tasks.

If Chapter 26 gave you the loop and Chapter 27 gave you the runtime, Chapter 32 is the architectural language for systems where more than one of those loops cooperate.

The single-agent ceiling

Start with a concrete failure.

A single agent has one loop, one context window, one system prompt. It looks like this:

┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            SINGLE AGENT                 │
│                                         │
│  ┌───────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │         Context Window            │  │
│  │                                   │  │
│  │  research notes                   │  │
│  │  + code files                     │  │
│  │  + test output                    │  │
│  │  + review feedback                │  │
│  │  + API docs                       │  │
│  │  + ...                            │  │
│  │                                   │  │
│  │  ████████████████████ FULL ███   │  │
│  └───────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                         │
│  One system prompt covering             │
│  research + coding + review + testing   │
│                                         │
│  Result: mediocre at everything         │
└─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Three things break.

Context saturation. Tool results pile up. By turn thirty, the agent has consumed 150,000 tokens of file contents, command outputs, and prior reasoning. Critical details from turn five get lost. The model technically still has them in its window but a model that has to find one fact across a 150k-token soup performs noticeably worse than the same model handed the fact directly.

Role confusion. A system prompt that says “you are a researcher, coder, reviewer, and tester” produces an agent that half-researches, half-codes, and never finishes reviewing. Each role wants a different system prompt. A jack-of-all-trades prompt is bad at everything because it cannot specialize.

Sequential bottleneck. The agent reads file A, then file B, then file C. Three serial model calls. Three serial tool executions. No parallelism. Real research tasks decompose into dozens of independent sub-questions; running them serially throws away the parallelism the task naturally allows.

Multi-agent fixes the first two by giving each agent its own context window and its own focused system prompt. It fixes the third by spawning agents concurrently. The trade-off: every agent boundary is a coordination cost, and coordination is its own source of bugs.

The Anthropic engineering post on their production Research system reports the magnitude of the win. Lead Opus 4 plus Sonnet 4 worker subagents beat a single-agent Opus 4 by 90.2% on internal research evals. The same post notes that 80% of the variance on BrowseComp is explained by token usage alone multi-agent wins largely because each worker gets a fresh 200k-token window. The architecture matters because it lets each piece of the system see exactly what it needs and nothing else.

When not to use multi-agent

Multi-agent adds complexity. Every message between agents is a potential failure point. Debugging goes from “read one conversation” to “trace messages across five agents.”

Stay single-agent when:

  • The task fits in one context window under roughly 100k tokens of working data.
  • You do not need different system prompts for different stages.
  • Sequential execution is fast enough.
  • The task is simple enough that splitting it adds more overhead than value.

The complexity cost is real. Every agent boundary is a lossy compression step: agent A’s full context gets summarized into a message for agent B. Coordination logic is its own source of bugs. Latency increases N agents means at least N serial model calls, more if they need to talk back and forth. Cost multiplies each agent burns tokens independently.

A working rule of thumb: if a task takes fewer than twenty tool calls and fits in 100k tokens, keep it single-agent. Past that, the multi-agent overhead starts paying for itself.

The 2025 Anthropic engineering post adds a specific number: production multi-agent uses roughly 15× the tokens of single-agent for the same task. You only run it when the task value justifies the cost. A research workflow that decides a million-dollar engineering decision warrants the 15× spend. A weekend coding-assist call does not.

The four-primitive model

Every six months a new multi-agent framework ships. AutoGen in 2023. CrewAI in 2024. LangGraph and OpenAI Swarm in 2024. Google ADK in April 2025. Microsoft Agent Framework RC in February 2026. Each press release claims to be “the right abstraction.”

If you try to learn them one at a time, you burn out. The APIs look different. The docs disagree about what an “agent” is. One framework calls its shared memory a “blackboard,” another a “message pool,” a third a “StateGraph.” You start suspecting the field is just churning.

It is not. Underneath the marketing, four primitives are stable. Learn them once, read every new framework in one paragraph.

The primitives

Agent. A system prompt plus a tool list, possibly a model selection, optionally a name. Stateless: every run starts from its system prompt and the current message history. Two agents with the same prompt and tools are interchangeable.

Agent = (system_prompt, tools, model, optional_name)

Handoff. A structured transfer of control from one agent to another. Three implementation flavors dominate:

  • Function return. The current agent’s tool returns the next agent. OpenAI Swarm’s pattern. Routing lives inside agent tool schemas.
  • Graph edge. LangGraph. Edges are declarative; conditions select the next node.
  • Speaker selection. AutoGen GroupChat. A selector function (sometimes itself an LLM call) reads the message pool and picks who speaks next.
Handoff = (from_agent, to_agent, reason, payload)

Shared state. Any data structure more than one agent can read, and sometimes write. Message pool, blackboard, key-value store, vector memory, structured artifact bundle. Two topologies dominate: full pool (every agent sees every message) and projected (agents see a role-scoped view).

SharedState = { messages: [], artifacts: {}, context: {} }

Orchestrator. Whoever decides who speaks next. Four flavors:

  • Static. The graph is fixed at build time (LangGraph deterministic, CrewAI Sequential).
  • LLM-selected. A separate LLM call picks the next speaker (AutoGen GroupChat, CrewAI Hierarchical).
  • Handoff-driven. The current agent calls a handoff tool that names the next agent (OpenAI Swarm).
  • Queue-driven. Workers pull from a shared queue; no explicit next-speaker (swarm architectures).
Orchestrator = (state, last_speaker) -> next_agent

That is the entire design space. Every framework picks defaults for each axis; the rest is surface syntax.

How every 2026 framework maps to it

Framework Agent Handoff Shared state Orchestrator
OpenAI Swarm / Agents SDK Agent(instructions, tools) tool returns Agent caller’s problem LLM’s next handoff call
AutoGen v0.4 / AG2 ConversableAgent speaker-selector on GroupChat message pool selector function
CrewAI Agent(role, goal, backstory) Process.Sequential / Hierarchical Task outputs chained manager LLM or static order
LangGraph node function graph edge + condition StateGraph reducer the graph, deterministic
Microsoft Agent Framework agent + orchestration patterns pattern-specific thread / context pattern-specific
Google ADK agent + A2A card A2A task A2A artifacts host decides

Surface differences look huge. Underneath: the same four knobs.

The stateless insight

Every primitive except shared state is stateless. The agent is a function of (prompt, tools). The handoff is a function call. The orchestrator is a scheduler. The only stateful thing in the system is shared state.

That is where all the interesting bugs live. Memory poisoning. Message ordering. Versioning. Write contention. Frameworks that hide shared state (Swarm) push the problem to the caller. Frameworks that centralize it (LangGraph checkpoint, AutoGen pool) make it inspectable but shift coordination cost onto the shared-state implementation.

Internalize this and framework comparison becomes a short checklist:

  • Does the orchestrator trust the LLM to route (Swarm) or pin routing in code (LangGraph)?
  • Is shared state full-history (GroupChat) or projected (StateGraph reducer)?
  • Can agents modify each other’s prompts (CrewAI manager) or only hand off (Swarm)?

Those three questions answer eighty percent of which framework fits a given problem. You stop shopping for “the best multi-agent framework” and start designing for the axis you care about.

What changes between frameworks

Once the primitives are fixed, the remaining design decisions are convenience, not architecture:

  • Memory strategy. Ephemeral vs durable checkpointing (LangGraph checkpointer).
  • Safety boundary. Who can approve a handoff (human-in-the-loop policy from Chapter 30).
  • Cost accounting. Per-agent token budgets.
  • Observability. Tracing handoffs, persisting state for replay.

All implementable on top of the primitives. None of them are new primitives.

The practical discipline: before adopting a new framework, write the primitive mapping for it. If you cannot, the docs are incomplete or the framework is inventing a fifth primitive (which is rare; check for a shared-state flavor you have not seen). Pin the mapping in your architecture doc. When a new team member joins, send them the mapping before the API docs. When framework versions change, diff the mapping, not the changelog.

Frameworks side-by-side, in code

The clearest way to internalize the four primitives is to see the same toy system written in three different frameworks and notice that the agents and the work are identical only the orchestration syntax differs.

OpenAI Agents SDK / Swarm style. Handoff-driven. The active agent calls a tool that returns the next agent.

from openai_agents import Agent

def transfer_to_coder():
    return coder_agent

researcher = Agent(
    name="researcher",
    instructions="Research the user's question. When you have enough context, call transfer_to_coder.",
    tools=[search_web, transfer_to_coder],
)

coder = Agent(
    name="coder",
    instructions="Given research notes, write the requested code.",
    tools=[write_file, run_tests],
)

The orchestrator here is “the LLM’s tool call.” Routing lives inside the researcher’s prompt and tool list. There is no separate orchestration code.

LangGraph style. Graph-edge orchestration. The orchestrator is a declarative graph; LLM outputs decide which edge to follow.

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph

def researcher(state):
    notes = run_research(state["question"])
    return {"notes": notes}

def coder(state):
    code = write_code(state["question"], state["notes"])
    return {"code": code}

graph = StateGraph(State)
graph.add_node("researcher", researcher)
graph.add_node("coder", coder)
graph.add_edge("researcher", "coder")
graph.set_entry_point("researcher")

The orchestrator is the graph itself; the shared state is State with reducer rules; handoff is graph-edge transition. The LLM contributes content; the framework controls flow.

AutoGen GroupChat style. Speaker-selection orchestration. A separate selector function picks the next speaker from a pool.

from autogen import ConversableAgent, GroupChat, GroupChatManager

researcher = ConversableAgent(
    name="researcher",
    system_message="You research questions and write notes.",
    llm_config=cfg,
)

coder = ConversableAgent(
    name="coder",
    system_message="You write code based on research notes.",
    llm_config=cfg,
)

groupchat = GroupChat(agents=[researcher, coder], speaker_selection_method="auto")
manager = GroupChatManager(groupchat=groupchat, llm_config=cfg)

The orchestrator is the manager; the shared state is the group chat’s message pool; handoff is implicit in selection. Every agent sees every message.

The same task, three syntaxes, identical primitives. Once you read each as “(agent, handoff, shared state, orchestrator) = (X, Y, Z, W)” the API differences become surface variations rather than fundamental decisions.

A short tour of the active 2026 frameworks

For practical orientation, the frameworks worth knowing:

Framework Best at Less good at
OpenAI Agents SDK Triage, skill-routing, simple specialist trees Long sessions, audit, parallelism
LangGraph Deterministic flows, audit, durable state Quick prototyping, free-form agent collaboration
AutoGen v0.4 / AG2 Free-form multi-party conversations, debate Strict flow control, single-agent ergonomics
CrewAI Role-driven crews, business-process automation Anything outside Sequential or Hierarchical
Microsoft Agent Framework Enterprise orchestration patterns, compliance Bleeding-edge experimentation
Google ADK Cross-vendor A2A integration Single-vendor optimization

Each is a different default on the four-primitive axis. None is “best.” All converge to similar capability when used by experienced engineers; the right pick depends on which axis your team most wants explicit control over.

Communication: from FIPA-ACL to JSON

Multi-agent systems have a longer history than the LLM era suggests. The 1990s produced a body of work on agent communication languages FIPA-ACL (Foundation for Intelligent Physical Agents Agent Communication Language) being the most influential that is worth knowing about, both because the design problems they solved are the same ones LLM agent frameworks rediscover, and because the vocabulary they introduced (speech acts, performatives, protocols) still shows up in 2026 papers.

FIPA-ACL in one paragraph

FIPA-ACL (1997-2002) borrowed from speech-act theory: every message between agents is a speech act with a specific performative inform, request, query, propose, accept-proposal, reject-proposal, confirm, disconfirm, agree, refuse, and a few dozen more. The performative is metadata that tells the receiving agent how to interpret the content. A message with request performative wants the receiver to do something; a message with inform wants the receiver to update its beliefs.

The structure of a FIPA-ACL message:

(performative
  :sender      agent-A
  :receiver    agent-B
  :content     "the meeting is at 3pm"
  :language    English
  :ontology    Calendar
  :protocol    fipa-request
  :conversation-id 12345
  :reply-with   m-1
  :in-reply-to  m-0
)

The fields outside :content are the part that survives. Modern agent frameworks have rediscovered most of them under different names:

  • :performative → action type or intent in modern frameworks
  • :sender / :receiver → message routing
  • :protocol → conversation protocol (request-reply, contract-net, subscription)
  • :conversation-id → thread ID, session ID
  • :reply-with / :in-reply-to → message correlation

FIPA-ACL did not survive into 2026 production. Its specific performatives were too academic, its XML-style syntax too verbose, its ontology requirements too heavy. But the idea that an agent message is more than a string, that messages have types and protocols and correlation survived completely.

Modern agent communication

The 2026 production stack runs on a thinner version of the same idea. A message is structured JSON with at minimum:

  • A type or intent (request, response, broadcast, etc.).
  • A sender ID.
  • A target specific agent, role, or broadcast.
  • A correlation ID for request-reply.
  • A payload (free-form).
  • Optional: a protocol name, a session ID, a timestamp.

Two protocols worth naming.

A2A (Agent-to-Agent Protocol) is Google’s 2025 standard for cross-vendor agent communication. It defines a Card (an agent’s public capabilities advertisement), a Task (a unit of work with structured input/output), and Artifacts (named outputs the agent produces). A host platform routes tasks to agents based on cards. We cover A2A in detail in Chapter 33.

MCP (Model Context Protocol) is Anthropic’s 2024 standard for tool/data integration that has been adopted across the industry. MCP is technically a tool protocol, not an agent protocol but agent frameworks routinely use MCP servers as the substrate for cross-agent shared state. Chapter 25 covered MCP for tools; the multi-agent layer reuses it.

The 2026 reality: most multi-agent systems run on a JSON-message backbone with the structure above, augmented by A2A or MCP for cross-system communication. The FIPA-ACL vocabulary is mostly historical, but its lessons are baked in.

The conversation-protocol idea

One FIPA-ACL idea worth lifting wholesale: conversation protocols. A protocol is a named pattern of messages that defines how a multi-step interaction flows.

The classical examples:

  • Request-reply. One agent requests, another replies. The simplest protocol.
  • Contract Net. An agent broadcasts a task; potential workers bid; the broadcaster awards the task. Used for resource allocation in classical multi-agent systems; reappears in 2026 agent-economy work (Chapter 34).
  • Subscription. An agent subscribes to a topic; publishers send updates; the subscriber processes them. Maps to event-driven architectures.
  • Auction. A specialization of Contract Net where bids include prices.
  • Negotiation. A multi-round back-and-forth where both sides propose and counter-propose toward agreement.
