Modern frameworks rarely give protocols first-class names, but the patterns are everywhere. AutoGen GroupChat is essentially an open multi-party request-reply. CrewAI Sequential is a chain of request-replies. The supervisor pattern is request-reply with the supervisor as both initial requester and final receiver. A2A’s Task structure is a request-reply with a Card as advertisement.

When you design a multi-agent system, naming the protocol is part of the design. “These two agents have a request-reply relationship” is a useful sentence. “These three agents participate in a contract-net protocol with a coordinator” is a useful sentence. Without protocol names, the design defaults to “agents talk to each other somehow,” which is unimplementable.

Supervisor / orchestrator-worker pattern

If you only learn one multi-agent pattern, learn this one. It is the workhorse behind production research systems, complex coding agents, and the majority of “agentic” products that ship in 2026.

The structure:

                 ┌──────────────┐
                 │   Lead       │  plans, decomposes,
                 │  (Opus 4)    │  synthesizes
                 └──┬────┬───┬──┘
                    │    │   │
            ┌───────┘    │   └───────┐
            ▼            ▼           ▼
      ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐  ┌─────────┐
      │ Worker1 │  │ Worker2 │  │ Worker3 │
      │(Sonnet) │  │(Sonnet) │  │(Sonnet) │
      └─────────┘  └─────────┘  └─────────┘
         fresh       fresh        fresh
         context     context      context

A lead agent plans the work, decomposes it into sub-tasks, delegates each to a worker, and synthesizes the workers’ results. Workers run concurrently with fresh context windows. The lead never sees the raw materials the workers consume it only sees the workers’ summaries.

Why it wins

Three mechanisms.

Fresh context per subagent. A worker exploring “FIPA-ACL heritage” does not carry the 40k tokens the lead spent planning. It gets a 200k window for one question. The Anthropic post quantified this: 80% of the BrowseComp variance is explained by total token usage. Multi-agent wins largely because each worker has more useful context room.

Specialization via prompt. The lead’s system prompt is “decompose and synthesize,” not “research.” Each worker’s prompt is narrow: “find what changed in X.” Focused prompts produce focused outputs. A jack-of-all-trades prompt is the single-agent failure mode reappearing inside a worker if you let it.

Parallelism. Workers run concurrently. Wall-clock time is roughly max(worker_times) + plan + synthesis, not sum(worker_times). For a research task with eight sub-questions, this is the difference between a thirty-second response and a four-minute response.

Production lessons from Anthropic’s Research system

The 2025 Anthropic engineering post on their multi-agent research system is the closest thing to a production reference for this pattern. Several of its lessons are worth carrying.

Scale effort to query complexity. Simple queries: one agent, three to ten tool calls. Complex queries: ten or more agents. The lead must estimate this from the query, not the caller. A “what is the capital of France” query that spawns ten workers is a budget incident.

Broad then narrow. Decompose into broad sub-questions first. Spawn more workers per sub-question only if the answer warrants depth. This avoids the failure mode where the lead tries to predict every sub-sub-question up front and produces a brittle plan.

Rainbow deployments. Long-running stateful agent systems do not blue-green well. Old runs are still in flight when you want to swap in a new version. Anthropic uses rainbow deployments: new version traffic ramps up while old-version runs drain naturally. Standard SRE deployment patterns adapted for the long-tail latency profile of agent workloads.

Token usage dominates cost. Multi-agent systems use roughly 15× the tokens of single-agent for the same task. Only run it when the task value justifies the cost. The math shows up in production budgets quickly; we covered the per-workload defaults in Chapter 30.

The lead’s three responsibilities

A well-designed lead does three things and nothing else.

Plan. Decompose the query into sub-questions. The plan is structured: a list of sub-tasks with names, input scope, and expected output shape. The plan is logged before execution.

Delegate. For each sub-task, instantiate a worker with a focused system prompt and the sub-task as input. The worker does not see the plan or sibling sub-tasks; it only sees what it needs.

Synthesize. Receive the workers’ outputs. Combine them into the final answer. Note any disagreements. The synthesis prompt is a different system prompt than the planning prompt the lead is two roles, sequenced.

What a lead must not do: do the work itself. The moment the lead reads a primary source, it has consumed the context window the worker was supposed to have. The discipline of “the lead never sees raw materials” is what makes the pattern work.

Failure modes

The lead hallucinates the plan. If the lead generates sub-questions that do not actually decompose the real question, workers do precise research on the wrong target. The mitigation is a plan-review step either a human reviewing the plan before workers spawn, or a critic agent that grades the plan against the original query.

Workers over-explore. Without explicit scope boundaries, workers drift beyond their assigned sub-question and pollute the synthesis step. The mitigation is in the worker’s system prompt: explicit “do not answer questions outside the scope of [sub-question]” instructions, plus a length cap on the worker’s output.

Synthesis silently picks one side. Two workers return contradictory facts. The lead must surface the disagreement explicitly, not silently pick one. A lead that quietly chooses is the worst failure mode in the pattern: the user never knows disagreement happened.

When supervisor is wrong

  • Sequential tasks. If step 2 literally needs step 1’s output, parallelism buys nothing. Use a pipeline (CrewAI Sequential, LangGraph linear graph) instead.
  • Simple queries. Single-agent handles them faster and cheaper. Always run the lead’s “scale effort” check before spawning workers.
  • Strict determinism. Supervisor uses LLM-selected delegation. Static graphs are better when audit and replay matter more than adaptability.
  • Tightly-coupled tasks. Tasks where workers’ decisions depend on what other workers found mid-task are not supervisor-shaped. They are negotiation-shaped (Chapter 33).

The deployment checklist before shipping a supervisor pattern:

  • Model pairing. Lead on a reasoning-tier model (Opus class). Workers on a faster, cheaper model (Sonnet class).
  • Worker timeout. Any worker exceeding 2× median runtime gets killed; the lead either re-spawns with narrower scope or proceeds without it.
  • Token cap per worker. Hard limit (10× expected synthesis input) prevents a runaway worker from blowing the budget.
  • Observability. Trace the lead’s plan, each worker’s tool calls, and the synthesis. This is the basis for any post-hoc debugging.
  • Rainbow rollout. Stateful long-running agents need versioned drain-and-replace, not hot swap.

Hierarchical architecture: when one supervisor is not enough

Supervisor scales to one level of decomposition. For tasks where the sub-tasks themselves need decomposition, you stack supervisors.

              ┌──────────────────────┐
              │   Top-level lead     │
              │  (decomposes into    │
              │   broad domains)     │
              └──┬────┬─────────┬────┘
                 │    │         │
        ┌────────┘    │         └────────┐
        ▼             ▼                  ▼
  ┌──────────┐  ┌──────────┐       ┌──────────┐
  │ Sub-lead │  │ Sub-lead │       │ Sub-lead │
  │ (domain  │  │ (domain  │       │ (domain  │
  │   A)     │  │   B)     │       │   C)     │
  └─┬───┬────┘  └─┬───┬────┘       └─┬───┬────┘
    │   │         │   │              │   │
    ▼   ▼         ▼   ▼              ▼   ▼
   W1  W2        W3  W4             W5  W6

Each sub-lead is itself a supervisor over its own workers. The top-level lead sees only the sub-leads’ summaries; the sub-leads see only the workers’ outputs. The hierarchy can extend further if the task warrants three levels, four levels though in practice three is the most you should need.

When to use hierarchical

The case for a hierarchical structure is when the natural decomposition has more sub-tasks than one lead can supervise meaningfully. A research project on “the state of multi-agent systems in 2026” might decompose into seven domains (frameworks, communication protocols, coordination patterns, production systems, evaluation, safety, frontier research). Seven is at the edge of what one lead can keep in plan-context. Push to fifteen sub-tasks and the lead’s planning capacity breaks down.

The hierarchical fix: the top lead decomposes into three or four broad domains, hands each domain off to a sub-lead, and the sub-leads further decompose within their domain. The total worker count can scale without any single lead managing more than four or five direct reports.

CrewAI ships this pattern as Process.Hierarchical. LangGraph supports it through nested graphs. AutoGen supports it through nested GroupChats. The pattern is generic; each framework implements it slightly differently.

Decomposition drift

The classical failure mode of hierarchical systems is decomposition drift: the top-level lead’s intent gets diluted as it passes through each level of decomposition. The top-level lead asks “what changed in multi-agent systems between 2023 and 2026.” The sub-lead for “frameworks” asks “what frameworks exist in 2026.” A worker under that sub-lead asks “what is CrewAI.” By the time the worker answers, the original “what changed” framing is gone.

The mitigation is to pass the original question through every level. Every sub-lead and every worker sees the top-level question in their context, alongside the specific sub-question they are answering. The synthesis at each level reframes the answer in the original question’s terms before passing up.

This is the same discipline as keeping a north-star metric in product management. The sub-task is the immediate work; the original question is what the sub-task ultimately serves.

Hierarchical observability

Hierarchical systems are harder to debug than flat supervisor systems. A failure at the bottom of the tree manifests as a missing or wrong piece of the final answer at the top. To find which worker failed, you have to walk the tree.

Production hierarchical systems run with structured trace logging at every level: every plan, every delegation, every worker output. The trace is a tree, not a flat list. Tools that visualize multi-level agent traces (Langfuse, Phoenix, Weights & Biases agent tracing) are part of the production stack for these systems, not optional add-ons.

Cost compounding in hierarchies

A specific cost trap to know about. Each level of the hierarchy multiplies the agent count. A top-level lead with four sub-leads, each managing four workers, runs sixteen workers. With five levels of hierarchy and a fan-out of four at each level, the system has 1024 leaves. Each leaf is a model call. The token-cost ratio of a hierarchical system can grow well past the 15× single-agent baseline.

The mitigation is dynamic depth. The top-level lead decides how deep to go based on query complexity, just as the supervisor pattern’s “scale effort” rule scales worker count to query complexity. Easy queries should not provoke a five-level hierarchy. Complex queries that genuinely warrant decomposition can have it.

The discipline shows up in production as a pre-flight cost estimate. Before the top-level lead spawns sub-leads, it estimates the total agent count and cost based on its plan. If the estimate exceeds a budget threshold, the lead falls back to a flatter approach or escalates for approval. This is the multi-agent equivalent of the action-budget pattern from Chapter 30.

Society of mind and debate

Marvin Minsky’s The Society of Mind (1986) argued that intelligence emerges from many specialized agents disagreeing and resolving disagreements. The 2023-2024 multi-agent literature ran experiments to see whether the pattern works for LLMs.

It does. Multi-agent debate, where two or more LLM agents argue about an answer over multiple rounds, consistently improves quality on reasoning tasks. The Du et al. 2023 paper “Improving Factuality and Reasoning in Language Models through Multiagent Debate” showed measurable gains on math, knowledge, and reasoning benchmarks. Subsequent work refined the pattern.

The basic debate setup

Round 1:
  Agent A produces an answer to the question.
  Agent B produces an answer to the question.

Round 2:
  Agent A sees Agent B's answer and either agrees, disagrees, or refines.
  Agent B sees Agent A's answer and either agrees, disagrees, or refines.

Round 3 (and onward):
  Same pattern. Continue until convergence or a fixed round count.

Final:
  A judge agent (or a vote) selects the final answer.

The mechanism is intuitive. A single agent can be confidently wrong and have no signal to update. Two agents producing the same wrong answer produce no signal either. But two agents producing different answers, then forced to read each other’s reasoning, often converge on the correct one the contradicting positions surface a flaw one of them was overlooking.

What debate is good for

  • Math problems. Reported gains of 5-15 percentage points on GSM8K and MATH compared to single-agent reasoning.
  • Factuality. Two agents independently checking each other’s claims catch each other’s hallucinations more often than a single agent self-checks.
  • Multi-step reasoning. Long chains of inference benefit because each step gets debated.

What debate is bad for

  • Subjective tasks. “Write a poem about autumn” debated by three agents produces a worse poem, not a better one. Debate works when there is a fact of the matter to converge on.
  • Tasks the model already nails. Easy questions where the single-agent answer is reliably correct waste debate compute.
  • Speed-critical tasks. Debate is at least 3-5× the latency of single-agent because of the multiple rounds.

The Society of Mind pattern, generalized

Beyond two-agent debate, the broader Society of Mind pattern uses a heterogeneous ensemble of agents with different specializations. A math problem might be debated by a “calculation” agent, a “constraint-checking” agent, and a “common-sense reasoning” agent. The diversity is the point: agents with the same training run produce correlated errors; agents with different specializations catch each other’s blind spots.

In 2026 production, debate is most often used as a layer on top of supervisor patterns: the supervisor’s synthesis is itself produced by a small debate, with two synthesizer agents disagreeing-then-converging.

The cost-quality trade-off is real. A two-agent two-round debate roughly quadruples the cost of single-agent. The quality lift varies by task; for high-stakes reasoning the trade is usually worth it, for routine generation it is not.

Heterogeneous models matter for debate

The most reliable way to extract quality from debate is to use different base models on each side. Two Claude agents debating tend to converge fast and agree on shared blind spots. A Claude agent debating a GPT agent debating a Gemini agent produces more diverse reasoning paths because each model has different training data, different alignment training, and different failure modes. The diversity is the whole point debate works because each agent catches what others miss, and agents from the same model miss similar things.

The 2025-2026 production pattern: when running debate at scale, route different agents to different model providers. The cost is operational complexity (managing multiple API integrations, handling provider-specific behaviors). The benefit is meaningfully better debate outcomes than any single-provider ensemble.

A specific failure mode to avoid: temperature-induced “fake diversity.” Running the same model three times at temperature 0.9 produces three different outputs but does not give you what diverse debate needs. The samples are correlated they share the model’s biases, hallucinations, and reasoning patterns. Real debate diversity requires actual model diversity, not just sampling variance.

Role specialization: planner, critic, executor, verifier

Most production multi-agent systems converge on a small set of roles. The four-role taxonomy that emerged from 2024-2026 work planner, critic, executor, verifier covers the majority of practical systems.

┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                      Task                                    │
│                       │                                      │
│                       ▼                                      │
│                  ┌─────────┐                                 │
│                  │ Planner │  decompose, prioritize,         │
│                  └────┬────┘  output a plan                  │
│                       ▼                                      │
│                  ┌─────────┐                                 │
│                  │  Critic │  challenge the plan,            │
│                  └────┬────┘  flag risks                     │
│                       ▼                                      │
│                  ┌──────────┐                                │
│                  │ Executor │  do the work                   │
│                  └────┬─────┘                                │
│                       ▼                                      │
│                  ┌──────────┐                                │
│                  │ Verifier │  check the result              │
│                  └────┬─────┘                                │
│                       ▼                                      │
│                  Final output                                │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Each role has a specific system prompt and a specific job.

Planner

System prompt: “You are a planner. Given a task, decompose it into ordered steps with explicit success criteria for each step. Do not execute. Output only the plan.”

The planner does not do the work. It is responsible for the structure. A good planner produces a plan that is concrete enough that the executor can follow it without re-planning, and abstract enough that the executor has freedom to handle local details.

The single most common planner failure mode is vague plans “write the code, then test it” instead of “implement function X with signature Y, then run tests A, B, C.” Vague plans produce executors that have to re-plan, which means the planner’s job was incomplete.

Critic

System prompt: “You are a critic. Given a plan, identify what could go wrong, what assumptions are unjustified, and what risks are unstated. Do not propose alternatives. Output only the critique.”

The critic argues against the plan. Its job is to surface flaws before execution, not to fix them. The pattern is borrowed from the Society of Mind work and from human engineering review: a separate role, with a separate prompt, dedicated to finding problems.

A critic that proposes alternatives is doing the planner’s job. A critic that approves everything is failing. The most useful critics are the ones tuned to be moderately adversarial pessimistic enough to surface real risks, not so pessimistic that they reject every plan.

Executor

System prompt: “You are an executor. Given a plan, execute each step using the provided tools. Report what you did and what the tools returned. Do not deviate from the plan without flagging the deviation.”

The executor is where most of the actual work happens. It takes the plan, runs the tools, produces the artifacts. Its system prompt is narrow because its job is narrow: faithful execution, not creativity.

A good executor reports both successes and failures clearly. A failed step should be reported as a failed step, not papered over. The verifier downstream will catch the gap, but earlier surfacing is cheaper.

Verifier

System prompt: “You are a verifier. Given a task and the executor’s output, determine whether the output actually completes the task. Cite specific evidence. Do not propose fixes. Output a structured verification report.”

The verifier closes the loop. It is the role that catches when the executor reported success but did not actually achieve the task. The pattern matches Chapter 30’s post-action verification: re-read the target state, confirm the side effect actually happened.

Verifier failure modes are subtle. A verifier that simply re-runs the executor’s logic catches nothing it is verifying with the same blind spots. Good verifiers come from a different angle: if the executor wrote code, the verifier runs the tests rather than re-reads the code; if the executor sent an email, the verifier checks the sent folder rather than re-reads the request.

The four-role pipeline

Wired together:

Task → Planner → Critic → (revised plan) → Executor → Verifier → Done
                    │                                      │
                    └─── if critique blocks ───┐           │
                                               ▼           │
                                          back to Planner  │
                                                           │
                                    if verification fails  │
                                               │           │
                                               ▼           │
                                          back to Executor ┘

The roles compose into a feedback loop. The critic’s veto sends the planner back to revise. The verifier’s veto sends the executor back to retry. Both loops have iteration caps to prevent infinite cycles.

This four-role pipeline maps cleanly onto:

  • Coding agents. Planner = task decomposer. Critic = design reviewer. Executor = code writer. Verifier = test runner.
  • Research agents. Planner = sub-question decomposer. Critic = methodology reviewer. Executor = researcher (the supervisor pattern’s worker). Verifier = fact-checker.
  • Customer support agents. Planner = case triage. Critic = policy compliance. Executor = response writer. Verifier = quality reviewer.

The roles are general because the underlying decomposition (think, challenge, do, check) is general. Most agentic workflows fit it. Tasks that resist this decomposition either fit a different pattern (negotiation, debate) or are too small to need multi-role decomposition at all.

Combining role specialization with the supervisor pattern

In production, role specialization and the supervisor pattern compose. The supervisor’s lead acts as the planner. The supervisor’s workers act as executors. A separate critic agent reviews the lead’s plan before workers spawn. A separate verifier agent checks each worker’s output before synthesis.

The composite system is:

Lead (planner) → Critic → Lead (revised plan) → Workers (executors) →
   Verifier (per worker) → Lead (synthesis) → Verifier (final)

That is roughly the architecture of a high-end production research agent in 2026. Six logical roles, two of which (lead pre- and post-synthesis) share an agent identity. Five distinct system prompts. The cost is high; the quality on hard tasks is meaningfully better than any single-role decomposition.

Common multi-agent failures and what catches them

Before we move to Chapter 33’s coordination patterns, name the failures the foundations chapter itself can teach you to recognize. These are the bugs every team building multi-agent systems hits in the first month, and they map cleanly onto the primitives we just covered.

Context bleed. Agent A’s full context leaks into Agent B’s input because the message-passing layer concatenates rather than projects. Symptom: B starts behaving as if it were A. Mitigation: explicit input contracts; B’s input includes only the fields B needs.

Role drift. Over many turns, an agent’s responses start covering territory outside its system prompt. Symptom: a “researcher” starts writing code, a “critic” starts proposing fixes. Mitigation: every N turns, re-anchor the agent’s prompt; cap conversation length per role.

Lost handoffs. An agent intends to hand off but the routing layer misses the signal. Symptom: a worker terminates without its output reaching the supervisor. Mitigation: explicit handoff schemas with required fields; the orchestrator validates the schema and treats malformed handoffs as failures.

Orchestrator hallucination. The orchestrator (especially in LLM-selected and handoff-driven flavors) routes to an agent that doesn’t exist or to itself. Symptom: infinite loops or broken runs. Mitigation: validate handoff targets against a registered agent list before executing; loop-detection on repeated speaker selection.

Shared-state corruption. Two agents write to shared state concurrently and one overwrites the other. Symptom: lost work, stale references. Mitigation: append-only message logs (most frameworks default to this); explicit reducers (LangGraph) that resolve concurrent writes deterministically.

Synthesis silent failure. The supervisor reports success when one or more workers actually failed. Symptom: the final answer is plausible but missing pieces. Mitigation: each worker reports a structured status; the supervisor synthesizes only across workers that reported success and explicitly notes which sub-tasks failed.

Cost runaway from spawning. A poorly-bounded supervisor spawns workers indefinitely. Symptom: a single user query consumes hours of compute. Mitigation: hard caps on worker count per session, propagated from Chapter 30’s cost-governor stack.

These are the failures of foundations. The failures of coordination what happens when peers disagree, when consensus drifts, when negotiation deadlocks, when an adversary crashes the protocol are Chapter 33.

Where this leaves us

The foundations of multi-agent systems are now in place.

Single agents hit a ceiling at context, role, and parallelism. Multi-agent fixes all three at the cost of coordination complexity. The four primitives (agent, handoff, shared state, orchestrator) describe every framework in the field. Communication runs on a JSON-message backbone whose lessons trace to FIPA-ACL even when the syntax does not. The supervisor pattern is the workhorse: a lead plans and synthesizes, workers execute in fresh contexts, parallelism wins. Hierarchical extensions stack supervisors when one is not enough. Society-of-mind and debate improve quality through structured disagreement. Role specialization decomposes the work into planner, critic, executor, verifier four roles that cover most production tasks.

What this chapter does not cover is the harder coordination problems. How agents hand off control to each other in flight. How they share state efficiently across long-running runs. How they reach consensus when they disagree. How they vote, negotiate, simulate societies, model each other’s intentions. How they communicate across organizational boundaries via standardized protocols like A2A.

Chapter 33 is exactly that. We move from the single-supervisor-with-workers shape into the genuinely multilateral patterns: handoffs without supervisors, blackboards for shared state, Byzantine fault tolerance for adversarial coordination, voting and self-consistency for ensemble reasoning, negotiation between agents with different goals, generative agents simulating populations, theory of mind for agents reasoning about other agents.

The pattern in Chapter 32 was one director, many workers. The pattern in Chapter 33 is many peers, with all the coordination problems that implies. The cost goes up; the capability goes up; the engineering discipline that keeps it tractable is what we cover next.

For now: take the four primitives with you. Whenever you read a new framework’s docs, write the primitive mapping first. Whenever you design a new multi-agent system, name the orchestrator type and the shared-state shape before writing any code. Whenever a multi-agent system fails in production, the failure is almost always in the orchestrator or the shared state the agents themselves are usually fine. The architecture is where the engineering lives.

Chapter 33 - Coordination, Communication, and Coalitions

Chapter 32 was about one director and many workers.

Chapter 33 is about many peers. It is harder.

The supervisor pattern works because there is a single point of decision. The lead plans, delegates, synthesizes, and the workers do not need to talk to each other. Coordination cost stays bounded by the lead’s planning capacity. The trade is real: any task that decomposes into independent sub-tasks fits the pattern, and many real tasks do.

But many real tasks do not. A negotiation between a buyer agent and a seller agent has no supervisor. A blackboard-style research pipeline where multiple specialists pull and post incrementally has no fixed director. A consensus over disagreeing experts produces a single answer from many voices, and the protocol that produces that answer is itself the coordination.

This chapter covers the patterns that show up when there is no single director when peers must communicate, share state, vote, negotiate, and reason about each other. The structure:

Handoffs and routines         control passes between peers
A2A protocol                  cross-vendor coordination at the wire level
Shared memory and blackboard   peers read and write a common workspace
Group chat and speaker selection   many participants, structured turn-taking
Consensus and BFT             agreement under disagreement and adversarial pressure
Voting, self-consistency, debate topology   quality through structured disagreement
Negotiation and bargaining    agents with different goals reaching agreement
Generative agents and simulation   populations of agents producing emergent behavior
Theory of mind               agents modeling other agents

Each pattern is a different trade-off between coordination overhead and capability. Each one solves a problem the others cannot. The boring rule from Chapter 30 holds: no single layer is sufficient, and a real system stacks several.

If Chapter 32 was the architectural language, Chapter 33 is the coordination language. The vocabulary you build here is what lets you describe complex multi-agent systems in a paragraph rather than a diagram.

Handoffs and routines: stateless orchestration

The simplest peer-to-peer pattern is the handoff. One agent finishes its part of the work and passes control to another agent not by reporting up to a supervisor, but directly.

OpenAI’s Swarm (October 2024) distilled this to two primitives: routines (an agent’s instructions plus tools, treated as a system prompt) and handoffs (a tool that returns another Agent). No state machine, no branching DSL the LLM routes by calling the right handoff tool. The OpenAI Agents SDK (March 2025) is the production successor, and the pattern is now widely copied.

def transfer_to_refunds():
    return refund_agent

triage_agent = Agent(
    name="triage",
    instructions="Route the user to the right specialist.",
    functions=[transfer_to_refunds, transfer_to_sales, transfer_to_support],
)

That is the entire abstraction. The triage agent’s system prompt makes it choose the right handoff based on the user message. The LLM’s tool-calling does the routing. The runtime detects the agent return value and switches the active agent.

Why the pattern is viral

Three reasons.

Small API. Two concepts to learn. Compare to AutoGen’s GroupChat or LangGraph’s StateGraph: those abstractions are good but require absorbing a vocabulary before you can write a useful system. Handoffs use the tool-calling capability the model already has.

Uses what the model already does well. Tool calling is production-grade across providers in 2026. Wrapping handoffs as tools means routing reliability tracks tool-calling reliability, which is generally good.

No state-machine burden. You do not describe the graph; the agents’ prompts describe who they hand off to. Adding a new specialist agent is a matter of writing one more agent and exposing one more handoff tool. The system grows by addition, not by restructuring.

The stateless trade-off

Swarm is explicitly stateless between runs. The framework keeps message history during a single run but does not persist anything across runs. Memory, continuity, long-running tasks all the caller’s problem.

In production (OpenAI Agents SDK, March 2025), this changed: the SDK adds session management, guardrails, and tracing while keeping the handoff primitive. The lesson generalizes: handoffs are the right primitive for routing; durable state is a separate concern that lives in shared state (next section) or in the durable-execution layer from Chapter 30.

When handoffs fit

  • Triage patterns. Front-line agent routes user to a specialist.
  • Skill-based routing. “If the task needs code, call the coder; if it needs research, call the researcher.”
  • Short, bounded conversations. Customer support, FAQ-to-ticket, simple workflows.

When handoffs struggle

  • Long sessions with shared memory. Each handoff resets context to the new agent’s prompt plus minimal history. Persistent state across agents requires caller-managed memory.
  • Parallel execution. Handoff is one-at-a-time the active agent switches sequentially. Parallelism requires the caller orchestrating multiple runs.
  • Audit and replay. Stateless runs are hard to replay exactly; the LLM’s handoff choice is non-deterministic.

Production checklist

  • Handoff logging. Every handoff writes a trace event with from-agent, to-agent, and a context snapshot.
  • Context transfer rules. Decide what moves on handoff: full history (expensive), last N messages, or a summary. The right answer depends on whether the new agent needs the prior agent’s reasoning or just the conclusion.
  • Guardrail on handoff. A handoff to a specialist with different tool permissions must be authenticated. Otherwise prompt injection can force unwanted handoffs into privileged agents.
  • Loop detection. Two agents handing back and forth is a common failure; detect with a simple last-K ring check.
  • Fallback agent. If a handoff target does not exist, fall back to a safe default rather than crashing the run.

The deeper point: handoffs are the multi-agent equivalent of subroutine calls. They are simple, composable, and most systems use them somewhere. They are not, by themselves, sufficient for complex coordination that is what the rest of this chapter is about.

A2A: cross-vendor coordination at the wire level

The handoff pattern works inside a single framework. But the 2025-2026 reality is that production systems often span multiple vendors and frameworks: a Claude-based research agent calling an OpenAI-based coding agent calling a Gemini-based browser agent. A2A Agent-to-Agent Protocol, Google’s 2025 standard is the wire-level protocol designed for cross-vendor coordination.

A2A defines three primitives.

Card. A public advertisement of an agent’s capabilities what tasks it accepts, what artifacts it produces, what authentication it requires. Cards are JSON documents, typically served at a well-known URL. A host platform discovers agents by reading their cards.

Task. A unit of work with structured input and an expected structured output. A task has an ID, an input payload, status (pending, running, complete, failed), and produces zero or more artifacts. Tasks can be long-running; the protocol supports streaming progress updates.

Artifact. A named output the agent produces. A research agent might produce a “summary” artifact; a coding agent might produce a “patch” artifact. Artifacts are typed and addressable, which means downstream agents can request specific artifacts by name without parsing free-form responses.

A typical A2A interaction:

1. Host reads Agent A's Card (capability discovery).
2. Host creates a Task on Agent A with structured input.
3. Agent A processes the task, possibly producing intermediate artifacts.
4. Agent A's status transitions to "complete" with final artifacts.
5. Host reads the artifacts and routes them to Agent B (or to the user).

Why A2A matters

Three things become possible.

Cross-framework coordination. A LangGraph workflow can call a CrewAI crew can call an OpenAI Agents SDK pipeline, all through A2A. The frameworks no longer need to share an SDK to interoperate.

Capability discovery. A host platform can query “which agents can produce a ‘patch’ artifact” and route work accordingly. Agent registries become possible. Marketplace-style agent ecosystems become possible (Chapter 34’s agent economies).

Audit and observability across vendors. Because A2A standardizes task lifecycle and artifact naming, a single tracing layer can observe interactions across multiple agents from different vendors. This was not possible with framework-specific SDKs.

A2A vs MCP

A2A is sometimes confused with MCP (Model Context Protocol) because they share the goal of standardization. They solve different problems.

Protocol Standardizes Used for
MCP Tool and data integration An LLM accessing tools and data sources
A2A Agent-to-agent communication Multi-agent systems coordinating across vendors

In practice, both ship together. An A2A-coordinated multi-agent system uses MCP for the individual agents’ tool integrations. A2A is the layer above MCP agents talking to agents, where each agent uses MCP for its own tools.

The 2026 trajectory: A2A is becoming the assumed substrate for any multi-agent system that crosses organizational boundaries. Internal multi-agent systems often skip it (they can use a framework-specific protocol), but anything shipped to external customers or coordinating across teams converges to A2A.

Shared memory and the blackboard pattern

Handoffs pass control. Shared memory passes state. The two are independent: a system can have one without the other, and most production systems have both.

The classical formalization is the blackboard pattern, originally from 1970s expert systems. The structure: a central data store (the “blackboard”) that all agents read and selectively write to. Specialist agents observe the blackboard, recognize when their expertise applies, contribute their part, and the system’s solution emerges from the accumulated contributions.

                ┌────────────────────────────────┐
                │         Blackboard             │
                │                                │
                │  - Problem statement           │
                │  - Researcher notes            │
                │  - Code draft                  │
                │  - Test results                │
                │  - Review comments             │
                │  - Open questions              │
                └─┬───────┬──────┬──────┬────────┘
                  │       │      │      │
            ┌─────┘       │      │      └──────┐
            ▼             ▼      ▼             ▼
      ┌─────────┐  ┌──────────┐ ┌────────┐ ┌─────────┐
      │Researcher│  │  Coder   │ │ Tester │ │Reviewer │
      └─────────┘  └──────────┘ └────────┘ └─────────┘

Each specialist watches the blackboard for state it can act on. The researcher acts when the problem statement is incomplete. The coder acts when the research is ready. The tester acts when code is posted. The reviewer acts when tests pass. No central scheduler tells anyone when to act agents trigger themselves on the state they observe.

What changes when LLMs replace classical experts

The 1970s blackboard had hand-coded specialists with brittle pattern-matching triggers. The 2026 blackboard has LLM-based specialists that can read free-form blackboard state and decide whether to contribute. The pattern is more flexible and more failure-prone:

  • Flexibility. An LLM specialist can recognize subtle conditions that a hand-coded trigger would miss.
  • Failure mode. An LLM specialist can hallucinate the trigger condition, contributing when it shouldn’t.

The mitigation is explicit blackboard schemas. Rather than a free-form text blackboard, structure the blackboard as a typed key-value store: problem_statement, research_notes (list), code_draft (string), test_results (struct), review_comments (list). Each specialist reads only the keys it needs and writes only the keys it owns.

Full pool versus projected views

Two topologies for shared state, with different scaling properties.

Full pool. Every agent sees every message. Simple to implement (concatenate all messages, send as context). AutoGen GroupChat is a canonical example. The trade-off: at large agent counts, every agent’s context fills up with messages it does not care about. Token cost grows with the square of the number of agents.

Projected. Each agent sees only a role-scoped view. A “researcher” sees the problem statement and research notes; a “coder” sees the research notes and code draft; a “tester” sees the code draft and test results. LangGraph’s StateGraph reducer pattern formalizes this: each node declares which fields it reads and writes, and the framework enforces the projection.

class State(TypedDict):
    question: str
    notes: list[str]
    code: str | None
    tests: dict | None

def researcher(state: State) -> State:
    new_notes = run_research(state["question"])
    return {"notes": new_notes}  # only updates 'notes'

def coder(state: State) -> State:
    code = write_code(state["question"], state["notes"])
    return {"code": code}  # reads 'notes', only updates 'code'

Projected views scale better and force the system designer to think about which agents need which information. The cost is upfront schema design; the benefit is that adding a new specialist becomes a matter of declaring its read and write set rather than worrying about context bloat.

In 2026, projected views are the dominant pattern in production. Full pools survive in research prototypes and in genuinely small systems (three or four agents).

Memory poisoning

Shared state has a security concern that handoffs do not. Anything one agent writes, every other agent reads. If one agent gets compromised by indirect prompt injection, by adversarial input, by a hallucinated step its corrupt output enters every downstream agent’s context.

The mitigation pattern from Chapter 30 carries over: provenance tagging on every blackboard entry, content-class permissions that gate what downstream agents do with untrusted content, and canary tokens in the blackboard to detect when a poisoned write has reached an agent that should not have read it.

The strongest discipline is to treat the blackboard as untrusted by default. Every agent reading from the blackboard validates inputs before acting on them, the way a service treats inputs from an external API. The blackboard is a shared workspace, not a shared trust boundary.

Group chat and speaker selection

When the right pattern is “many agents in conversation,” the coordination question becomes who speaks next. AutoGen’s GroupChat is the canonical reference; CrewAI’s hierarchical process and Microsoft Agent Framework’s group orchestration patterns are variations.

The structure: a pool of agents, a shared message log, and a selector that picks the next speaker on each turn.

turn N:    selector reads message pool → picks agent X
turn N+1:  X produces a message → goes into the pool
turn N+2:  selector reads the updated pool → picks agent Y
...

Selection strategies

Five selectors come up in practice, with different trade-offs.

Round robin. Agents speak in fixed order. Simplest, deterministic, fair. Bad when some agents have nothing to add and burn turns. Good for protocols where every agent must contribute (initial proposals, voting rounds).

Random. Pick uniformly from the pool. Fair on average; introduces variance that helps avoid echo chambers. Bad when convergence matters and you want repeatable behavior.

Manual. A human picks the next speaker. Useful for debugging and demos; not viable for production scale.

Rule-based. A policy decides based on the last message: “if the last message asked a coding question, pick the coder; if it asked a math question, pick the mathematician.” Predictable and auditable. Bad when rules cannot capture nuance.

LLM-selected. A separate LLM call reads the message pool and picks the next speaker. Most flexible. Most expensive. Most failure-prone the selector itself can be wrong, can loop, can pick agents that have nothing useful to add.

In 2026 production, LLM-selected is the dominant choice for free-form collaboration; rule-based is used inside structured workflows; round robin survives for voting and consensus rounds.

Speaker-selection failure modes

The selector loops. Two agents keep getting picked alternately, neither making progress. Mitigation: per-pair handoff caps (no more than three exchanges between A and B without C entering).

The selector starves. Some agents never speak because the selector consistently picks others. Mitigation: a fairness term in the selector’s prompt or a hard rule that every agent speaks at least once per N turns.

The pool fills up. With long conversations, every agent’s context fills with messages it does not care about. Mitigation: projected views (above) or a summarization step that compresses old messages into a shorter recap.

Speaker-selection adversarial. A malicious agent crafts messages designed to manipulate the selector into picking it again. Mitigation: cap turn count per agent; alternate between LLM-selected and rule-based selection on adjacent turns.

When to pick group chat

Group chat is the right pattern when the work is genuinely collaborative every agent’s input matters and the order of contributions is not known in advance. Examples: brainstorming, design review, complex debugging where multiple specialists need to weigh in.

It is the wrong pattern when the work is sequential or hierarchical. Use a pipeline (Chapter 32’s role-specialized chain) for sequential work. Use a supervisor for hierarchical decomposition. Group chat is the freer-form alternative, paid for in coordination overhead.

Termination conditions

A subtle but production-critical question: when does a group chat end? Three common termination patterns.

Explicit terminator agent. One agent’s role is to detect convergence and emit a special “DONE” message. The selector watches for this signal. Useful when the task has a clear completion criterion (a finished design, a resolved decision, a verified plan).

Round cap. The conversation runs for at most N rounds, then the orchestrator picks the best message as the answer. Useful when convergence is uncertain and you need bounded latency.

Quality-detector termination. A separate agent reads the conversation periodically and decides whether the answer is “good enough.” This is the most flexible but most expensive every round costs an extra evaluation.

In practice most production group chats use a combination: round cap as a hard limit, terminator agent for early stopping, quality-detector for borderline cases. Without a termination strategy, group chat runs to the orchestration layer’s hard timeout, which is the worst form of termination because it produces unfinished work without warning.

Consensus and Byzantine fault tolerance

You have N agents producing answers. They disagree. Majority vote picks the wrong one because two agents are correlated. Or one agent lies. Or three share a hallucination because they share training data. How do you produce a single answer from many voices?

Classical distributed systems gave this problem its formal treatment. Practical Byzantine Fault Tolerance (PBFT, Castro and Liskov, OSDI 1999) tolerates f < n/3 Byzantine nodes nodes that can lie, drop messages, fail silently, do anything except crash safely. The protocol has three phases (pre-prepare, prepare, commit) and two primitives (signed messages, quorum certificates). Agreement on a single value among n >= 3f + 1 honest-or-malicious nodes.

PBFT is correct. It is also incomplete for LLM agents. The classical assumptions break:

  1. Independent faults. Byzantines do not coordinate. LLM agents running the same base model share faults; their errors are correlated.
  2. Honest nodes are truly honest. PBFT only aligns disagreement; it assumes “honest output” is correct. An “honest” LLM still hallucinates.
  3. The question has a ground-truth answer. Consensus on a wrong fact is still consensus.

Three LLM-specific attack patterns

Byzantine lie. One agent outputs a deliberately wrong answer with high confidence. Classical BFT handles this if f < n/3.

Sycophantic conformity. One agent reads others’ answers before voting and aligns with whoever spoke first or loudest. Not malicious, but correlates with the loudest voice. Classical BFT does not prevent it because the sycophantic agent passes every signature check.

Correlated-error monoculture. Three agents share a base model. They hallucinate the same wrong answer. The “majority” is wrong by construction. Classical BFT does not help because all three “honestly” agree.

The 2025-2026 responses

Three research directions extend BFT to handle these patterns.

CP-WBFT (Confidence-Probed Weighted BFT). Each voter attaches a confidence probe to its answer a self-reported probability or a separate calibration model’s prediction. Vote weights scale with confidence. Reported +85.71% BFT improvement on complete graphs. Mitigates sycophantic conformity, since conforming agents tend to have low confidence on their volunteered position.

DecentLLMs. Leaderless. Worker agents propose in parallel; evaluator agents score proposals; the final answer is the geometric median of scored positions. Robust when f < n/2. Mitigates Byzantine lie and correlated errors geometric median is robust to outliers and pulls toward the dense cluster, not the model-biased average.

WBFT (Weighted BFT with Hierarchical Structure Clustering). Vote weights assigned by response quality plus a trust score learned from history. Cluster agents into Core and Edge; Core agents must achieve consensus first, Edge agents follow. Mitigates scalability Core consensus is small and fast and partially monoculture (Core can be chosen for diversity).

A minimal protocol

The core BFT round for LLM agents, stripped to essentials:

1. Task arrives; each agent i produces answer a_i.
2. Each agent attaches confidence probe c_i in [0, 1].
3. Aggregator collects (a_i, c_i) from all n agents.
4. Aggregator groups by semantic cluster (equivalent answers).
5. Aggregator computes weight per cluster: w(C) = sum c_i over i in C.
6. Winner = cluster with max weight, if max > threshold * sum(c_i).
   Else: retry or escalate.
7. Minority clusters logged with provenance for post-hoc audit.

The semantic clustering step is the LLM-specific twist. Two answers “the study reports 4.2%” and “4.2% improvement” should cluster together. A naive string-equality check misses this. In production, use a cheap embedding model or explicit canonicalization.

The threshold parameter decides when to accept. Too low: you accept weak majorities. Too high: you never accept anything. Empirical range: 0.5 to 0.67 for n = 5-7 agents, higher for smaller n. Below threshold, escalate to a human or to a different agent ensemble.

What consensus does not give you

The empirical finding from “Can AI Agents Agree?” (arXiv:2603.01213) is uncomfortable. Even with no adversaries, LLM agents disagree on scalar questions at rates above 30% on many benchmarks. A single agent adopting a deceptive persona can pull a Mixture-of-Agents consensus 40+ percentage points off the honest baseline.

The takeaway: BFT gives you machinery to align outputs. It does not tell you whether the aligned output is right. Combine consensus with verification (Chapter 32’s verifier role), diversity (heterogeneous models), and an evaluator agent that grades the consensus output independently of the agents that produced it.

The discipline:

Agreement is not correctness.
Verify the consensus output independently before trusting it.

Voting, self-consistency, and debate topology

Consensus’s lighter cousin is plain voting. Run the same query through N agents, take the most common answer. The mechanism is older than computer science; the question is when it works.

Self-consistency (Wang et al., 2022) was the first systematic empirical result for LLMs. Run the same prompt N times with sampling temperature, take the majority answer. Reported gains on math benchmarks of 5-15 percentage points over single-shot. The mechanism: stochastic sampling produces diverse reasoning paths; correct answers are converged on by multiple paths while wrong answers are scattered across many incorrect paths. Majority vote separates them.

The 2024-2025 literature extended this with debate topology varying the structure of the inter-agent communication.

Topologies that have been studied

Topology Structure Best for
Independent N agents answer separately, vote at the end Tasks where diverse reasoning paths help
Sequential Agent k+1 sees agent k’s answer Tasks where refinement helps
Star Each agent sees only the moderator’s summary Tasks where context size is constrained
Complete graph Every agent sees every other agent Free-form debate; expensive
Hierarchical Lower-tier agents debate; tier propagates up Decomposable tasks

Independent and self-consistency are simple and surprisingly effective. They scale linearly with agent count. They benefit most from heterogeneous models, since diversity is the source of the lift.

Sequential and complete-graph topologies allow agents to influence each other. They can lift quality further but introduce the conformity bias we covered in consensus: agents reading each other’s answers tend to converge regardless of correctness, especially when models are correlated.

Debate quality drops with conformity

The empirical finding across multiple 2024 papers: debate quality peaks at two to three rounds. Past that, agents converge on each other regardless of truth. The mechanism is straightforward each round of “see my answer, refine yours” rewards consistency over correction. After enough rounds, agents reach an attractor that may or may not be the correct answer.

Practical rule: bound debate rounds at two or three. If consensus has not formed, escalate to a different protocol (verification, human review) rather than running more rounds.

Negotiation and bargaining

When agents have different goals not just different perspectives on the same goal coordination becomes negotiation.

A buyer agent wants a low price; a seller agent wants a high price. A scheduling agent for team A wants morning meetings; the scheduling agent for team B wants afternoons. A task-allocator wants minimum cost; a worker wants maximum reward. Each side has preferences the other does not share.

The classical models:

Single-issue negotiation. Two parties, one variable (price, quantity, deadline). Bargaining theory gives concrete protocols: alternating offers (Rubinstein 1982), with convergence properties depending on each party’s discount factor and patience.

Multi-issue negotiation. Multiple variables, possibly with trade-offs. Pareto frontier and Nash bargaining solution apply. Implementation is harder; agents must articulate preferences over bundles, not just individual items.

Coalition formation. N parties, subsets can form coalitions with different payoffs. Shapley value and core solutions from cooperative game theory are the formal references. In practice, coalition formation among LLM agents is a research area more than a production pattern.

LLM-specific negotiation patterns

The 2024-2025 work on LLM negotiation found a few specific patterns.

Self-disclosure asymmetry. LLM agents often disclose their preferences too readily. A buyer agent that says “I’d really pay up to $500” gives the seller a clear ceiling. The mitigation is prompt engineering: instructing the agent to negotiate from positions, not interests; not disclosing reservation values without consideration.

Anchoring. First offers strongly influence final prices, even between LLMs. Negotiation prompts that emphasize “make a strong first offer” routinely win against prompts that emphasize “be reasonable.”

Conformity collapse. Two LLM agents from the same base model often converge to a 50/50 split regardless of their stated preferences, because they share the same training data on what “fair” looks like. Heterogeneous model pairings (Claude vs GPT vs Gemini) produce more varied outcomes.

Long-horizon stalling. Agents instructed to be patient can deadlock. Bound negotiations to a fixed number of rounds; if no agreement, default to a fallback (split the difference, defer to a third agent, escalate to a human).

When negotiation is the right pattern

Negotiation fits when:

  • Agents have explicit, different goals (not just different views).
  • A specific outcome variable can be the subject of agreement (price, allocation, schedule).
  • Both sides have something to give and something to gain.

Negotiation is the wrong pattern when:

  • The “agents” actually share a goal use consensus.
  • The decision is one-shot and time-critical use a simple rule or a supervisor.
  • One side has unilateral authority use it.

In 2026 production, negotiation shows up in marketplaces (agent buyers and agent sellers), in scheduling between organizations, and in resource allocation systems. It is rarer than consensus or supervisor patterns because most production tasks have a single objective.

A worked negotiation example

Concrete example: two agents negotiating a project deadline.

Agent A represents the engineering team; its objective is the latest possible deadline. Agent B represents the customer; its objective is the earliest possible deadline. Both have private valuations A would accept anything ≥ 8 weeks; B would accept anything ≤ 12 weeks. The Pareto-optimal range is 8 to 12 weeks; any agreement in that range is rational for both sides.

A naive prompt-driven negotiation produces:

A: "I need at least 12 weeks."  (over-discloses upper bound)
B: "I can wait at most 6 weeks." (false anchor below true range)
A: "That's impossible. 12 weeks is firm."
B: "I'll go to 8."
A: "Fine, 12."
B: "Fine, 10."
[deadlock]

A negotiation-trained or carefully-prompted version produces:

A: "Looking at our roadmap, we'd propose 14 weeks." (anchor high)
B: "That's longer than we'd hoped. We were thinking 6." (counter-anchor)
A: "We can try to compress. What constraints are driving 6?" (probe interests)
B: "We need it before our Q1 review on March 15."
A: "If we deliver a usable beta by March 15 and full release later, would 10 weeks for full release work?"
B: "Yes, that works."
[agreement at 10 weeks with phased delivery]

The second negotiation surfaces interests behind positions, expands the trade space (phased delivery), and reaches an outcome both sides prefer to deadlock. This is the difference between negotiation as theater and negotiation as problem-solving. Frontier models can execute the second pattern with appropriate prompting; the prompts are the difference.

Generative agents and emergent simulation

The 2023 Park et al. paper “Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior” showed something different from any pattern we have covered so far. Twenty-five LLM agents in a simulated village, each with a persona, memory, and daily plan. The researchers gave them a starting prompt and let the simulation run.

Emergent behavior appeared. Without being told to, the agents organized a Valentine’s Day party. They invited each other. They formed friendships and conflicts. They updated their daily plans based on rumors and observations. The simulation produced patterns no individual agent’s prompt described.

This is generative agent simulation, and it is a different pattern from anything else in this chapter. There is no shared task. There is no convergence. The “output” is the emergent dynamics of a population of agents over time.

The architecture

Each agent has:

  • A persona (system prompt describing who they are).
  • A memory stream (a log of observations, experiences, and reflections, retrieved by relevance).
  • A planner (decomposes daily intentions into hourly actions).
  • A reflection step (periodically summarizes recent memories into higher-level insights).

The simulation runs in discrete time steps. Each step, every agent observes its environment, retrieves relevant memories, decides on an action, and updates its memory. The environment is shared; the agents’ actions affect each other through the environment.

What generative agents are good for

  • Social science simulation. Studying how rumors spread, how norms form, how cooperation emerges.
  • Game design and worldbuilding. Populating a game world with agents that have plausible behavior.
  • Multi-stakeholder system testing. Simulating users, operators, adversaries simultaneously to stress-test a system.

What generative agents are not for

  • Task completion. They are not optimizing toward a goal; emergence is the point.
  • Production decision-making. Their behavior is too variable to drive critical decisions.
  • Cost-sensitive applications. Each agent runs many model calls per simulated day; populations of dozens to hundreds of agents over weeks of simulated time become expensive.

Generative agents are a research and design tool. The patterns they reveal (rumor propagation, norm formation, group dynamics) inform production systems indirectly, but the simulations themselves are not products. The 2026 frontier work in this area focuses on faithful simulation of human populations for policy testing, rather than as a basis for autonomous decision-making.

Theory of mind and agent coordination

The most subtle coordination requirement is for agents to model each other. Not to share state or vote, but to anticipate what other agents will do based on what those agents know and intend.

The classical formalization is theory of mind the cognitive ability to attribute mental states to other agents and to reason about their behavior accordingly. In humans, this develops around age four and is what makes complex social coordination possible.

For LLM agents, theory of mind matters in three concrete cases.

Cooperation under partial observation. Agent A knows something Agent B does not. A’s optimal action depends on whether A can communicate this to B before acting. Modeling B’s current beliefs is part of choosing the right action.

Anticipating adversaries. Agent A is negotiating with B. A’s best move depends on what B is likely to do next. If A models B’s reasoning, A can anticipate and plan for it.

Avoiding redundant work. In a multi-agent team, A and B might both try the same approach without coordination. If A models B’s likely action, A can choose a different approach.

What 2024-2025 research showed

Frontier models exhibit measurable theory-of-mind capabilities on benchmarks like ToMi, the Sally-Anne test, and false-belief tasks. The capabilities are not human-level but are above the level of the 2022-era LLMs.

In multi-agent contexts, the practical application is prompting agents to explicitly reason about other agents’ beliefs and intentions before acting. The pattern:

"Before responding, briefly consider:
1. What does the other agent currently believe?
2. What is the other agent likely to do next?
3. How should that affect my response?"

Adding this prompt structure to agents in negotiation, debate, and cooperation tasks produces measurable improvements on coordination benchmarks. The mechanism is the same as chain-of-thought: forcing the model to surface its reasoning improves the reasoning’s quality.

Limitations

Theory of mind in LLM agents is not robust. It works on clear cases (the Sally-Anne test, where the answer is unambiguous) and fails on subtler ones (multi-step belief revision, deception detection). For production multi-agent systems, theory-of-mind prompting is a quality lift, not a guarantee. Combine it with explicit communication and explicit shared state rather than relying on agents to infer each other’s mental states.

The deception-detection asymmetry

A specific failure mode worth naming: LLM agents are systematically worse at detecting deception than at executing it. Frontier models can produce convincing deceptive personas; the same models often fail to recognize when another agent is being deceptive.

The asymmetry has practical consequences. In any multi-agent system where one agent might be compromised by prompt injection or where one agent acts adversarially (red-team scenarios), the other agents will likely not catch the attack via theory-of-mind reasoning alone. Defenses must be architectural provenance tagging, content-class permissions, canary tokens not behavioral.

The 2026 reality: theory of mind is a useful prompting pattern for cooperation, not a defense against adversaries. For adversarial robustness, fall back on the Chapter 30 control stack.

Where this leaves us

The coordination patterns are now in place.

Handoffs pass control between peers; A2A standardizes the wire format across vendors. Shared memory and the blackboard pattern let peers coordinate through observable workspace state, with full pools and projected views as the two main topology choices. Group chat with speaker selection handles free-form multi-party conversation. Consensus and BFT produce single answers from many voices, with confidence weighting and clustering as the LLM-specific extensions. Voting and debate topology handle quality-through-disagreement at lower coordination cost. Negotiation handles agents with different goals. Generative agents simulate populations and produce emergent behavior. Theory of mind lets agents reason about each other’s beliefs and intentions.

Each pattern solves a problem the others cannot. A production multi-agent system in 2026 typically uses several: a supervisor pattern at the top level, with handoffs between specialists, a blackboard for shared artifacts, voting on key decisions, and theory-of-mind prompts for coordination-sensitive sub-tasks.

What this chapter does not cover is the production layer: how multi-agent systems scale to thousands of agents, how MARL lets agents learn coordination policies from experience, what failure modes show up in production deployments, how to evaluate multi-agent systems honestly, and what the 2026 case studies look like end-to-end.

Chapter 34 is exactly that. Swarm optimization (PSO, ACO) borrows from biology to coordinate large populations. Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MADDPG, QMIX, MAPPO) trains coordination policies rather than hand-designing them. Agent economies, token incentives, and reputation systems give populations of agents reasons to cooperate. Production scaling queues, checkpoints, durable execution from Chapter 30 applied at multi-agent scale. Failure modes (MAST, groupthink, monoculture, cascading failures). Evaluation benchmarks specific to multi-agent. And case studies of 2026 state-of-the-art systems.

The pattern in Chapter 32 was one director, many workers. The pattern in Chapter 33 is many peers. The pattern in Chapter 34 is populations and economies many peers at scale, with the engineering machinery to keep them tractable in production.

For now, take three rules from this chapter.

First, agreement is not correctness. Every coordination pattern produces an output. None of them, by themselves, tell you the output is right. Verify with an independent agent or evaluator before trusting consensus.

Second, shared state is where the bugs live. Handoffs are stateless and easy to reason about. Shared memory is stateful and hard. Most multi-agent system failures, especially the subtle ones, trace to shared-state issues corruption, poisoning, race conditions, missing projection. Engineer shared state with the same rigor you would engineer a database schema.

Third, the right pattern depends on the problem. Supervisor for decomposable tasks. Handoff for routing. Blackboard for incremental collaboration. Group chat for free-form discussion. Consensus for quality through disagreement. Negotiation for different-goal coordination. Generative agents for simulation. Theory of mind as a quality lift. Pick the pattern that matches the structure of the problem; do not try to make every problem fit one pattern.

Multi-agent coordination is a vocabulary as much as a toolkit. Once you can name what you are doing “this is a supervisor with role-specialized workers, projected blackboard, and a verifier on the synthesis” the engineering becomes tractable. Without the names, you are just hoping the agents work it out.

Chapter 34 - Production Multi-Agent Systems and the 2026 Frontier

The first two chapters of this trilogy were about patterns. This one is about the systems that ship those patterns at scale.

A research demo runs three agents in a notebook. A production multi-agent system runs hundreds or thousands of agents under live traffic, with adversarial inputs, partial failures, cost constraints, and audit requirements. The patterns from Chapters 32 and 33 still apply. The engineering around them is what separates a working prototype from a deployable product.

This chapter covers the production layer:

Swarm optimization (PSO, ACO)          biological inspirations for large-scale coordination
Multi-Agent RL (MADDPG, QMIX, MAPPO)   learning coordination policies from experience
Agent economies                         token incentives, reputation, marketplaces
Production scaling                      queues, checkpoints, durable execution at MAS scale
Failure modes (MAST, Groupthink)        the 2026 taxonomy and what to do about it
Evaluation and coordination benchmarks   how to measure multi-agent quality honestly
Case studies and 2026 SOTA              what the field actually shipped

Each section is one piece of what makes a multi-agent system survive production. The three-chapter arc closes here. By the end, you should be able to look at a 2026 multi-agent product and understand both why it works architecturally and why it does not collapse under load.

The throughline: multi-agent systems fail more often than they succeed in production, and the ones that succeed are the ones whose builders treated failure modes as first-class engineering targets. The 2025 measurement (Cemri et al., NeurIPS) put the failure rate of off-the-shelf multi-agent systems at 41 to 86.7 percent. That number is uncomfortable. It is also instructive: the gap between “works in a notebook” and “works in production” is bigger for multi-agent than for any other category we have covered in this volume.

Swarm optimization: biological inspirations

When the agent count grows past a small team into the hundreds or thousands, hand-designed coordination stops scaling. The 1990s and 2000s produced a body of work on biologically-inspired optimization that fits naturally Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO, Kennedy and Eberhart 1995) and Ant Colony Optimization (ACO, Dorigo 1992) are the two canonical references.

The 2026 question is what these classical algorithms contribute when the “agents” are LLMs rather than scalar particles or simulated insects.

Particle Swarm Optimization, in one paragraph

PSO models a population of particles moving through a search space. Each particle has a position and a velocity. Each remembers the best position it has visited. The whole swarm shares the best position any particle has visited. On each update, each particle’s velocity is influenced by three terms: its current direction (inertia), a pull toward its personal best, and a pull toward the global best. Over many iterations, the swarm converges on high-fitness regions of the search space.

v_i(t+1) = w * v_i(t)
         + c1 * r1 * (personal_best_i - x_i(t))
         + c2 * r2 * (global_best - x_i(t))
x_i(t+1) = x_i(t) + v_i(t+1)

The algorithm is forty years old and still works. It is robust to non-differentiable objectives, parallelizes naturally, and produces interpretable trajectories.

PSO for LLM coordination

The 2024-2025 literature applied PSO to populations of LLM agents in two ways.

Prompt search. Treat each agent as a “particle” whose “position” is its system prompt. Run all agents in parallel on a benchmark. Score each. Update prompts via PSO-style movement: each agent’s next prompt is a blend of its previous prompt, its best historical prompt, and the swarm’s best historical prompt. The blending is implemented through a meta-LLM that generates a new prompt given the three reference prompts.

Tool composition. Treat each agent as a particle whose position is a configuration over available tools (which subset to expose, in what order, with what parameters). PSO drives the swarm toward configurations that maximize task success on a benchmark.

The honest assessment: PSO-style optimization of LLM agents works, but the gains over random search are modest unless the search space is structured well. The bigger contribution from the swarm-optimization literature is conceptual the idea that a population of agents with private and shared memory can collectively explore a space without central coordination.

Ant Colony Optimization

ACO is the other canonical swarm algorithm. Ants leave pheromone trails as they explore; trails on shorter paths get reinforced more often (because ants traverse them faster); over time, the colony converges on near-optimal paths through a graph.

For LLM agents, the analog is stigmergy coordination through traces left in the environment. A research swarm that writes notes to a shared blackboard is doing stigmergic coordination: later agents’ search behavior is influenced by what earlier agents left behind. The 2026 use of ACO-inspired patterns in MAS is mostly through this analog rather than through literal pheromone updates.

Stigmergy and the practical pattern

Stigmergy coordination through traces left in the environment is the pattern that survives most cleanly into 2026 LLM systems even when the specific algorithms (PSO, ACO) do not. The pattern shows up as soon as you have a shared blackboard (Chapter 33) and multiple agents observing it.

A research swarm exhibits stigmergy when:

  • Agent A writes notes to the blackboard.
  • Agent B reads A’s notes, decides to focus on a different angle, writes its own notes.
  • Agent C reads both, fills in a gap, writes its own notes.
  • Without explicit coordination, the swarm’s collective coverage of the question grows.

This is genuinely emergent behavior no agent planned the collective trajectory, but the trajectory is more comprehensive than any individual agent’s plan. The cost is that swarm-coordinated work tends to be slower than supervisor-coordinated work; the win is that swarms scale to many more agents without any planning bottleneck.

In production, stigmergic patterns are most common in long-horizon research and exploration tasks where the search space is large enough that supervisor planning becomes brittle. They are uncommon in tightly-time-constrained tasks where convergence speed matters.

When swarm optimization is the right fit

Swarm optimization fits when:

  • The search space is large and irregular.
  • Evaluation is cheap relative to the value of finding a good solution.
  • Parallelism is available and underused.
  • You do not need a guaranteed global optimum.

It is the wrong fit when:

  • The search space has rich structure that gradient methods can exploit.
  • Evaluation is expensive (every “particle” position costs a benchmark run).
  • The system needs to converge fast (swarm methods are batch-iterative).

In 2026 production, swarm optimization is rarely the dominant pattern. It shows up in specific niches: prompt engineering at scale, agent-system architecture search, and resource allocation in agent marketplaces. The conceptual import populations exploring jointly through shared traces survives more broadly than the specific algorithms.

Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning

The other classical machinery that bears on multi-agent LLMs is Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL). Where Chapter 16 covered single-agent RL PPO, DPO, GRPO MARL extends to the case where multiple agents learn simultaneously in a shared environment.

The defining difficulty is that the environment is non-stationary from any single agent’s perspective. Agent A’s reward depends on what Agent B does. As B learns, the dynamics A faces change. Convergence guarantees that hold for single-agent RL break in the multi-agent case.

Three algorithms dominate the 2024-2026 MARL literature.

MADDPG (Multi-Agent Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient). Centralized training, decentralized execution. During training, each agent’s critic sees every agent’s observation and action. The critics learn joint Q-functions. At deployment, each agent acts on its own observation only. The critic-time information sharing addresses non-stationarity; the actor-time independence allows scalable execution.

QMIX. A monotonic mixing network combines individual agent Q-values into a joint Q-value. The monotonicity constraint guarantees that improving any individual agent’s Q-value cannot hurt the joint Q-value, which lets the system decompose joint optimization into individual updates while keeping cooperative incentives aligned.

MAPPO (Multi-Agent PPO). PPO extended to multi-agent. Each agent has its own policy; a centralized critic estimates joint value. Surprisingly competitive with more elaborate methods on standard benchmarks.

MARL for LLM coordination

The 2025-2026 application of MARL to LLM agents is in early days. Two threads worth knowing.

Coordination policy fine-tuning. Train a population of LLM agents in a multi-agent simulation environment using MARL. The agents learn coordination policies when to communicate, when to defer, when to insist that emerge from the training rather than being prompt-engineered. Reported results on collaborative coding benchmarks show modest but consistent gains over heterogeneous-prompt baselines.

Negotiation training. Two LLM agents fine-tuned via MARL on bargaining tasks learn negotiation strategies that outperform their pre-training defaults. The trained policies tend to be more patient (delay disclosure of reservation values) and more strategic (anchor with strong first offers).

The structural challenge is data and compute. MARL training is sample-expensive. For LLM agents at frontier scale, the cost of running enough simulation episodes to converge is substantial. The 2026 frontier work is on simulation environments that produce useful learning signal at much lower episode counts.

Where MARL is genuinely necessary

For most production multi-agent systems, MARL is overkill. Hand-designed coordination via prompts and shared state works for the cases we have covered. MARL becomes necessary when:

  • The optimal coordination policy cannot be hand-designed (the search space is too large).
  • The coordination needs to adapt to environmental changes the prompt cannot anticipate.
  • The system has access to a fast simulator and a reward signal.
